# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The questions below strictly follow the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern**: Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks with **internal choice**). Each sub-question is modeled to the typical KTU Part B split: **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Full mark-allocations and complete model solutions are provided.

---

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Direct / Short Answer)

### Question 1 (3 marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> *List any three essential properties required of a cutting tool material, with a one-line justification for each.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Hot Hardness (HRC at elevated T):** Allows the tool to retain a sharp cutting edge at the high temperature of the shear zone (often 600–1000 °C) without plastic deformation. (1 mark)
2. **Wear Resistance:** Reduces abrasion, adhesion, and diffusion wear at the tool–chip interface, prolonging tool life. (1 mark)
3. **Toughness ($K_{IC}$):** Resists fracture and chipping under mechanical shock, especially during interrupted cuts (milling, drilling). (1 mark)

*(Optional additional properties for full marks: thermal conductivity, chemical stability, low coefficient of friction.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

> *Why is High-Speed Steel (HSS) called "high speed"? What is the role of vanadium in HSS?*

**Model Answer:**

HSS is called "high speed" because it retains its hardness (≥ HRC 60) at **red-hot temperatures (~600 °C)**, enabling cutting speeds **2–4× higher** than the older carbon-tool steels used before 1900 — when F.W. Taylor and M. White developed the first HSS (1.5 marks).

**Role of Vanadium:** Vanadium forms extremely hard, stable **vanadium carbide (VC, HV ~2800)** particles. These carbides are the hardest microstructural constituent in HSS and dramatically improve **wear resistance** without significantly reducing toughness. (1.5 marks)

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 2 Choice A]**

> *a) Classify cutting tool materials. Explain the composition, microstructure, properties, and applications of **High-Speed Steel (HSS)** with reference to its grades T1, M2, and M42. (7 marks)*
>
> *b) Discuss the **composition, manufacture, and ISO classification of cemented carbides**. Why is TiC added in the P-series, and what is the role of Co content? (7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**1. Classification of Tool Materials (2 marks):**

The four principal families are:
- **High-Speed Steels (HSS)**
- **Cemented Carbides**
- **Ceramics (Al$_2$O$_3$, Si$_3$N$_4$, Sialons)**
- **Diamond tools (MCD, PCD, CVD)**

(Plus: cermets, cubic boron nitride CBN, ultra-hard materials.)

**2. Composition of HSS (2 marks):**

HSS is an **iron-base alloy** containing the following key elements:

- **W (12–18 %)** in T-type; **Mo (3–10 %)** in M-type — form hard M$_6$C carbides for hot hardness.
- **Cr (~4 %)** — improves hardenability and corrosion resistance; forms M$_{23}$C$_6$ carbides.
- **V (1–5 %)** — forms the hardest VC carbides for wear resistance.
- **Co (0–10 %)** — improves red-hardness in M35, M42 grades.
- **C (0.7–1.5 %)** — primary carbide former.

**3. Microstructure of HSS (1.5 marks):**

After quenching and tempering:
- **Martensite matrix** (tough, ~70 %).
- **Primary carbides** (M$_6$C, M$_{23}$C$_6$) — undissolved, ~15 %.
- **Secondary carbides** (fine, precipitated during tempering) — ~10 %.
- **Retained austenite** (transformed during multiple tempering cycles) — ~5 %.

**4. Key Properties of HSS (1 mark):**

- Hot hardness up to **600 °C**, room hardness **HRC 62–68**.
- High toughness ($K_{IC}$ ≈ 25–30 MPa·√m) — best of all high-hardness tool materials.
- Excellent grindability (can be resharpened easily).
- Lower cost than carbide/ceramic.

**5. Grades and Applications (0.5 mark):**

| Grade | Composition (%) | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **T1 (18-4-1)** | 18 W, 4 Cr, 1 V | General-purpose turning, form tools, taps |
| **M2 (6-5-4-2)** | 6 Mo, 5 W, 4 Cr, 2 V | Drills, endmills, milling cutters, broaches (workhorse grade) |
| **M42 (with 8 % Co)** | 6 Mo, 5 W, 4 Cr, 2 V, 8 Co | High-performance taps, difficult-to-machine alloys, high-speed cutters |

**HSS Heat Treatment Cycle:** Pre-heat (850 °C) → Austenitize (1230 °C) → Quench (oil) → Temper (550 °C, 2–3 times) → Final HRC 62–64.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**1. Composition of Cemented Carbides (2 marks):**

Cemented carbides are **powder-metallurgical composites** of:
- **WC (Tungsten Carbide)** — primary hard phase (HV 1800–2400), 70–97 % by weight.
- **Co (Cobalt)** — ductile binder (3–25 %), provides toughness by bridging WC grains.
- **TiC, TaC, NbC** — added in P- and M-series to resist crater wear.

**2. Manufacture (1.5 marks):**

- **Step 1:** Ball-mill WC, Co, and (for P-series) TiC/TaC powders to submicron size.
- **Step 2:** Press into desired shape (uniaxial / cold isostatic pressing).
- **Step 3:** Sinter in vacuum or hydrogen at **1300–1500 °C** — Co melts and wets WC grains, densifying the compact.
- **Step 4:** Optional HIP (Hot Isostatic Pressing) to remove residual porosity.
- **Step 5:** Grind to final dimensions; coat if required.

**3. ISO Classification (2 marks):**

| Code | Color | Workpiece | Composition |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P** | Blue | Steel (long chips) | WC + TiC + TaC + Co |
| **M** | Yellow | Stainless steel (austenitic) | WC + TiC + Co |
| **K** | Red | Cast iron, non-ferrous (short chips) | WC + Co (no TiC) |
| **N** | Green | Aluminum, plastics | WC + Co (no TiC) |
| **S** | Orange | Superalloys (Ni, Ti) | Coated carbide / Si$_3$N$_4$ |
| **H** | Gray | Hardened steel (> 45 HRC) | Coated carbide / ceramic |

**4. Role of TiC in P-Series (1 mark):**

TiC forms a **TiO$_2$ protective oxide layer** on the rake face at high cutting temperatures. This **oxide skin acts as a diffusion barrier**, preventing carbon and tungsten atoms from the tool from diffusing into the flowing steel chip. It therefore **suppresses crater wear** — the principal wear mode in steel turning.

**5. Role of Co Content (0.5 mark):**

- **Higher Co (10–20 %)** → Higher **toughness**, lower hardness. Used for **roughing** and interrupted cuts.
- **Lower Co (3–6 %)** → Higher **hardness and wear resistance**, lower toughness. Used for **finishing** and continuous cuts.

**Rule of thumb:** Co content trades off **toughness ↔ wear resistance**. A typical P30 insert has 10 % Co; a K10 insert has 6 % Co.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 2 Choice B]**

> *a) What are ceramics? Compare **alumina-based and silicon-nitride-based** ceramic tool materials in terms of composition, properties, and applications. Why is Si$_3$N$_4$ preferred for milling cast iron? (7 marks)*
>
> *b) Describe the **types of diamond tools (MCD, PCD, CVD)**. Why is diamond not used for machining ferrous metals? With neat sketches, discuss the layered structure of a modern coated carbide insert. (7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**1. Definition (1 mark):**

Ceramics are **inorganic, non-metallic, crystalline tool materials** with high hardness, chemical inertness, and excellent hot-hardness retention up to 1200 °C. They contain no metallic binder (in the pure form) or only minor sintering aids.

**2. Alumina-Based Ceramics (2.5 marks):**

- **Pure Alumina (Al$_2$O$_3$):** Monolithic, cold-pressed and sintered at 1600 °C. Hardness HV 1800. Brittle, low thermal-shock resistance. Used for **high-speed finishing of cast iron** at $V_c$ = 400–800 m/min.
- **Alumina + TiC (Al$_2$O$_3$-TiC composite, 70:30):** Improved toughness and thermal-shock resistance over pure Al$_2$O$_3$. Hardness HV 1900. Used for **finish machining of steels** and **high-speed semi-finishing of cast iron**.
- **Alumina + SiC Whisker (Al$_2$O$_3$ + 20–30 % SiC whiskers):** Whiskers bridge cracks and deflect them → **toughness increases 2–3×** over pure Al$_2$O$_3$. Hardness HV 2000. Used for **high-speed roughing of Ni-based superalloys (Inconel, Waspaloy)** at $V_c$ = 200–500 m/min.

**3. Silicon-Nitride Based Ceramics (2 marks):**

- **Si$_3$N$_4$ + Y$_2$O$_3$ + Al$_2$O$_3$ (sintering aids):** Hot-isostatic-pressed (HIP) to full density. Hardness HV 1700. **High toughness** ($K_{IC}$ ≈ 7 MPa·√m, ~2× alumina). Low thermal expansion coefficient ($\alpha \approx 3 \times 10^{-6}$/K) and high thermal conductivity.
- **Sialon (Si-Al-O-N):** Solid-solution of Al$_2$O$_3$ in Si$_3$N$_4$. Hardness HV 1800, higher oxidation resistance. Used for **high-speed machining of Ni-superalloys**.

**4. Why Si$_3$N$_4$ for Cast Iron Milling? (1.5 marks):**

Milling of cast iron involves:
- **Discontinuous chips** → repeated mechanical shock on the tool edge.
- **High local temperatures** at each tooth engagement → rapid thermal cycling.
- **Abrasive carbides** in gray cast iron → flank wear.

Si$_3$N$_4$ survives these conditions because:
- **Low thermal expansion** → small dimensional change on heating → less thermal-stress cracking.
- **High thermal conductivity** ($k \approx 30$ W/m·K) → heat is conducted away from the cutting edge → temperature gradients are gentler.
- **High toughness** ($K_{IC}$ ≈ 7 MPa·√m) → absorbs the mechanical shock of interrupted cuts.
- **Chemical inertness** to iron → no diffusion wear.
- Result: Si$_3$N$_4$ allows **3–5× higher cutting speeds** than carbide in cast iron milling, with 5–10× longer tool life.

**5. Comparison Table (additional 0.5 mark):**

| Property | Al$_2$O$_3$ | Al$_2$O$_3$ + SiC whisker | Si$_3$N$_4$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hardness (HV) | 1800 | 2000 | 1700 |
| Toughness (MPa·√m) | 4 | 8 | 7 |
| Thermal shock resistance | Poor | Good | Excellent |
| Best workpiece | Cast iron finishing | Ni-superalloys | Cast iron milling |

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**1. Types of Diamond Tools (3 marks):**

**a) Monocrystalline Diamond (MCD):**
- A single, gem-quality natural diamond crystal.
- Cleaved along (111) planes and mounted on a tool shank.
- Hardness HV 10000; sharpest possible edge (radius < 50 nm).
- **Application:** Ultra-precision machining of optical components (Cu, Al mirrors), lens turning, ruling engines. *Very expensive, cannot be re-sharpened.*

**b) Polycrystalline Diamond (PCD):**
- Sintered mass of randomly-oriented diamond micro-grains (5–30 µm) bonded with a small amount of Co or SiC binder.
- Manufactured at **60 kbar and 1500 °C** (high-pressure high-temperature, HPHT).
- Produced as a **thick layer (0.5–3 mm)** brazed onto a tungsten-carbide substrate.
- Hardness HV 8000–9000; isotropic properties; can be re-sharpened by EDM/laser.
- **Application:** **Aluminum alloys (Al-Si pistons, Al alloys for aerospace), copper, brass, wood, CFRP composites, ceramics, plastics.**

**c) CVD Diamond (Coated Diamond):**
- A **polycrystalline diamond film (10–30 µm thick)** grown on a carbide insert by **chemical vapor deposition** (CH$_4$ + H$_2$ plasma, 800–1000 °C).
- **Chemically pure** (no metallic binder), therefore **sharper edge** than PCD.
- **Application:** Micro-machining, high-end turning of Al-Si alloys, drilling of CFRP, machining of graphite electrodes.

**2. Why Diamond is NOT Used for Ferrous Metals (2 marks):**

Above ~700 °C, diamond is destroyed by:

**a) Atomic Diffusion / Solubility:** Carbon (diamond) is **soluble in iron (γ-Fe austenite)**. At cutting temperatures, carbon atoms from the diamond diffuse rapidly into the steel chip — leading to severe **diffusion wear**. The tool wears as fast as a soft steel tool.

**b) Graphitization + Catalysis:** Iron, cobalt, and nickel are **catalysts for the conversion of diamond → graphite**. At high temperatures in the presence of these metals, the diamond tool's atoms rearrange into the much softer graphite structure (HV ~10). The tool loses its hardness catastrophically.

Therefore, **diamond tools are reserved exclusively for non-ferrous metals (Al, Mg, Cu, Zn, Ti-free brasses), composites (CFRP, MMC), plastics, wood, and graphite.**

**3. Layered Structure of Modern Coated Carbide Insert (2 marks):**

A modern multi-coated carbide insert typically has **4 to 6 layers**, each optimized for a specific function. From outside (rake face) to inside:

| Layer | Material | Thickness | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (outermost) | **TiN** (gold) | 1–3 µm | Anti-friction, wear indicator, prevents BUE |
| 2 | **TiCN** | 4–8 µm | High wear resistance, low friction |
| 3 | **Al$_2$O$_3$** (Alumina) | 3–5 µm | Thermal barrier, chemical inertness |
| 4 | **TiN or TiCN** | 1–2 µm | Adhesion layer between coating and substrate |
| 5 (substrate) | **WC-Co with surface gradient** | bulk | Toughness, bulk support |

The substrate itself often has a **Co-enriched surface layer** (cobalt enrichment by selective decarburization) to improve coating adhesion and surface toughness.

**Why this layered design works:**
- The **outer TiN** (low friction) reduces adhesion of workpiece material to the insert.
- The **Al$_2$O$_3$** thermal barrier keeps the substrate cooler, so its hot hardness is preserved.
- The **WC-Co substrate** absorbs mechanical shock, preventing brittle fracture of the ceramic coatings.

This architecture gives coated inserts a **3–10× productivity advantage** over uncoated carbide.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing PCD with CVD diamond** — PCD has binder + thickness 0.5–3 mm; CVD is binder-free film 10–30 µm. Examiners will deduct **1 full mark** if these are swapped.
> 2. **Writing "diamond is hard" without the reactivity reason** for why diamond fails on ferrous metals. The full justification (diffusion + catalytic graphitization) is needed for **2 marks**.
> 3. **Mixing up ISO P/K color codes** — P = blue (steel), K = red (cast iron), M = yellow (stainless). Examiners test this every year.
> 4. **Forgetting the formula $T = C / V_c^n$** when discussing tool material performance. Always anchor qualitative statements to Taylor's equation.
> 5. **Not specifying units** in numerical answers — C has units of m/min · min$^n$; n is dimensionless. Markers deduct **0.5 mark** for unitless answers.
> 6. **Missing heat treatment steps in HSS** — preheat + austenitize + quench + **multiple tempering** (2–3 cycles at 550 °C) is mandatory for full marks.
> 7. **Stating ceramics cannot be used for steel** — false! **Whisker-reinforced Al$_2$O$_3$** is specifically designed for high-speed steel machining. Read the question carefully.
> 8. **Stating "titanium carbide makes tools harder"** — incomplete. The **TiO$_2$ oxide skin** formed in service is the actual mechanism that reduces **diffusion/crater wear**.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

### ✅ Cutting Tool Materials — Key Takeaways

- **Tool material selection** is governed by **hot hardness, wear resistance, toughness, and chemical stability** of the material.
- **HSS** retains hardness up to **600 °C**; **Carbide** up to **900 °C**; **Ceramic** up to **1200 °C**; **Diamond** decomposes above 700 °C in presence of Fe.
- **HSS grades:** T1 (18 W, 4 Cr, 1 V) and M2 (6 Mo, 5 W, 4 Cr, 2 V) are the most common. **M42 (with 8 % Co)** is the premium grade.
- **HSS heat treatment:** Austenitize at 1230 °C, quench, **double/triple temper at 550 °C** → secondary hardening peak.
- **Cemented Carbide** = WC grains (80–95 %) + Co binder (5–20 %), made by **powder metallurgy** (cold press + liquid-phase sinter at 1300–1500 °C).
- **ISO Carbide Classification:** **P = steel** (blue, has TiC), **M = stainless** (yellow), **K = cast iron** (red, no TiC), **N = non-ferrous** (green), **S = superalloy** (orange), **H = hardened steel** (gray).
- **Coated Carbides** have 3–5 layers (TiN + TiCN + Al$_2$O$_3$ + TiN adhesion + WC-Co substrate). Productivity gain: 3–10× over uncoated.
- **Higher Co content** = higher toughness, lower wear resistance (use for roughing). **Lower Co content** = higher hardness (use for finishing).
- **Alumina (Al$_2$O$_3$)** ceramics: pure (cast iron finishing), TiC-composite (steel), SiC-whisker (superalloys). Brittle, avoid interrupted cuts.
- **Silicon Nitride (Si$_3$N$_4$)** ceramics: low thermal expansion + high thermal conductivity → **excellent thermal shock resistance** → ideal for **cast iron milling**.
- **Sialon (Si-Al-O-N):** Solid-solution ceramic for superalloys and cast iron at high $V_c$.
- **Diamond types:** MCD (natural, ultra-precision optics), **PCD (sintered, Al/Cu/wood/CFRP)**, **CVD (coated film, micro-machining, Al-Si)**.
- **Diamond is FORBIDDEN for ferrous metals** due to **C diffusion into Fe + catalytic graphitization by Fe/Ni/Co**.
- **Hot hardness decays exponentially:** $H(T) = H_0 e^{-\beta(T - T_0)}$. Smallest $\beta$ = best hot hardness.
- **Taylor's Tool Life Equation:** $T = C / V_c^n$ — and its log-linear form is a **straight line on log-log plot**, with slope $-1/n$.
- **Optimum cutting speed (cost-based):** $V_{c,\text{opt}} = \left( C \cdot C_m \cdot L / (n \cdot C_t \cdot t_m) \right)^{1/(n+1)}$.
- **Productivity optimum** is at $V_c$ where $T = t_m$ (single-part machining time). **Cost optimum** is at lower $V_c$.
- **Wear mechanisms** vs temperature: adhesion (low T) → abrasion (all T) → diffusion & oxidation (high T). **Diamond fails by chemical/graphitization wear; ceramic excels at resisting diffusion wear.**
- **SiC whisker-reinforced Al$_2$O$_3$** is the only ceramic recommended for **high-speed roughing of Ni-superalloys** (Inconel, Waspaloy).
- **Coated inserts — CVD vs PVD:** CVD is high-T (1000 °C) thick coating, lower cost, may have eta-phase brittleness at interface. PVD is low-T (500 °C) thin sharp coating, retains substrate toughness, sharper edge.
- **Cubic Boron Nitride (CBN)** — although not the focus of this module, recall that CBN is the second-hardest material and is used for **hardened steel (HRC > 50) and cast iron** — the alternative to diamond for ferrous materials.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "HSSC CD":** **H**ot-hardness, **S**econdary hardening, **S**intering, **C**oating layers, **C**eramic whisker, **D**iamond reactivity. These six keywords cover 80 % of KTU Module 2 questions on tool materials.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Properties and Applications of Different Tool Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET522 - MECHANICS OF METAL CUTTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Properties and Applications of Different Tool Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tool Material (KTU Definition):** A *manufactured composite* (typically a metal, ceramic, cermet, or superhard phase) shaped into a cutting edge, engineered to resist the combined *thermal, mechanical, tribological, and chemical* loads generated at the tool–chip–workpiece (TCW) interface during a material-removal operation. Performance is judged by **hot hardness, toughness, wear resistance, thermal stability, and chemical inertness** at the operating temperature of the cut.

A *cutting tool* in the KTU 2024 scheme is no longer treated merely as a "hard piece of steel." It is a **multi-functional tribological system** — the cutting edge must simultaneously:
1. Penetrate the workpiece plastically (requiring **hardness > workpiece hardness**).
2. Withstand the cutting force $F_c$ (requiring **toughness / transverse rupture strength**).
3. Survive the chip-tool interface temperature $\theta$ (requiring **hot hardness / temper resistance**).
4. Resist the sliding abrasive/chip contact (requiring **abrasive/adhesive/diffusion wear resistance**).
5. Avoid welding with the chip (requiring **chemical stability / low solubility with the work material**).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Soldier's Armor" Analogy

Imagine a medieval soldier going into battle against different enemies (dragons = heat, swords = cutting forces, poisoned arrows = chemical diffusion, sharp rocks = abrasive wear).

- **Plain Carbon Steel** is like a thin cotton armor — cheap, flexible (tough), but melts and chips the moment a dragon (heat) or a sharp rock (abrasion) appears.
- **High Speed Steel (HSS)** is hardened alloy armor with cooling fins — tougher than carbon steel, survives moderate heat, used for the general "infantry" of machining (drills, taps, milling cutters).
- **Carbides (WC-Co)** are like plate mail with riveted ceramic plates — extremely hard, but brittle; ideal for mass-produced infantry facing a relentless enemy.
- **Ceramics (Al₂O₃, Si₃N₄)** are like pure ceramic samurai armor — incredibly hard and heat-resistant, but shatter on heavy impact; you send them only against fiery enemies (high-speed finishing of cast iron at 1000 °C).
- **CBN (Cubic Boron Nitride)** is like a meteor-forged blade — second only to diamond in hardness, immune to iron's chemical attack; you reserve it for the toughest steel boss.
- **Diamond (PCD)** is the mythical "adamant" sword — the hardest known material, but chemically reactive to iron above 700 °C and brittle under shock; it is a specialist weapon for non-ferrous monsters (aluminum, composites, copper).

### 1.3 The Five Performance Pillars (KPI Block)

> [!NOTE]
> Any tool material is evaluated on these **Five Pillars**. The KTU examiner loves asking the *trade-off* question: "Why is diamond not used to machine steel?" — the answer lies in the *Chemical Inertness* pillar.

| # | Property | Symbol / Unit | Engineering Meaning |
|---|----------|---------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Hot Hardness** | $HV$ at $\theta$ | Resistance to plastic deformation at the cutting temperature. The single most important property. |
| 2 | **Toughness / TRS** | Transverse Rupture Strength, $\text{MPa}$ | Ability to absorb mechanical shock without fracture. |
| 3 | **Wear Resistance** | — | Resistance to abrasive, adhesive, diffusion, and oxidation wear. |
| 4 | **Thermal Stability** | Max. usable $\theta$, $^\circ\text{C}$ | Temperature at which the tool loses hardness or oxidizes rapidly. |
| 5 | **Chemical Stability** | Solubility with Fe at $\theta$ | Tendency to dissolve into the chip or form built-up edge. |

### 1.4 Classification of Tool Materials (KTU Taxonomy)

The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies tool materials as follows:

$$
\text{Tool Materials} = \begin{cases}
\text{(A) High Speed Steel (HSS)} \\
\text{(B) Cast Cobalt Alloys (Stellites)} \\
\text{(C) Cemented Carbides} \begin{cases} \text{Uncoated} \\ \text{Coated (single, multi, gradient)} \end{cases} \\
\text{(D) Cermets (TiC / TiN bonded with Ni-Mo)} \\
\text{(E) Ceramics} \begin{cases} \text{Alumina (Al}_2\text{O}_3\text{)} \\ \text{SiAlON} \\ \text{Sialon / Silicon Nitride (Si}_3\text{N}_4\text{)} \end{cases} \\
\text{(F) Superhard} \begin{cases} \text{Cubic Boron Nitride (CBN)} \\ \text{Diamond (PCD / CVD / MCD)} \end{cases}
\end{cases}
$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Property–Performance Link

A KTU question will often ask: *"For a given workpiece–speed combination, which tool material would you select?"* The answer is found by chaining the five pillars into a **Performance Equation**.

#### 2.1.1 Hot Hardness vs. Temperature (the Master Curve)

The most diagnostic property is **hot hardness**, i.e., the Vickers hardness retained at the cutting temperature $\theta$. A tool material's *cutting speed capability* is directly proportional to its *temperature of equal hardness* with the workpiece.

$$
H_V(\theta) = H_{V,\text{RT}} \cdot \exp\left[-\beta \left(\theta - \theta_{RT}\right)\right]
$$

Where:
- $H_{V,\text{RT}}$ = room-temperature Vickers hardness (typically **GPa**).
- $\beta$ = thermal softening coefficient (material constant, units $^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$).
- $\theta$ = cutting temperature in $^\circ\text{C}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** $\beta$ is small for ceramics and CBN (slow softening) and large for HSS (rapid softening). A *high $\theta$ for a given $H_V$* is what makes CBN a "high-speed" tool for hardened steel.

#### 2.1.2 The Taylor Tool-Life Equation (Foundational Link)

The classic **Taylor equation** binds tool material properties to performance:

$$
V \cdot T^n = C
$$

Where:
- $V$ = cutting speed, m/min.
- $T$ = tool life, minutes.
- $n$ = Taylor exponent (material dependent; **typical values**: HSS $n \approx 0.1$–$0.15$, Carbide $n \approx 0.2$–$0.3$, Ceramic $n \approx 0.4$–$0.6$, CBN $n \approx 0.5$–$0.8$).
- $C$ = Taylor constant (related to $H_V$ and thermal stability).

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition:** A *higher* $n$ means the tool is *less sensitive* to speed changes — exactly what we observe when moving from HSS to CBN. This single equation is the bridge between material selection and process economics.

#### 2.1.3 Wear Mechanisms — the "Enemy Quartet"

A KTU 14-mark question is very likely to be: *"Explain the four major tool wear mechanisms with their governing equations."* Memorize this block.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(i) Abrasive wear rate} \quad &\frac{dW}{dt} = k_a \cdot \sigma \cdot v_s \\
\text{(ii) Adhesive wear rate} \quad &\frac{dW}{dt} = k_{ad} \cdot \frac{F_N}{H_V} \\
\text{(iii) Diffusion wear rate} \quad &\frac{dW}{dt} = D_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{Q_d}{RT}\right) \\
\text{(iv) Oxidation / chemical wear} \quad &\frac{dW}{dt} = f\!\left(\theta, p_{O_2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $D_0$ is the diffusion coefficient, $Q_d$ the activation energy for diffusion, and $R$ the universal gas constant. **This is a high-yield block.**

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU examiners test the *order of magnitude* of these numbers. The vertical bars use `\vert` and `\mid` so the table renders correctly.

| Property / Quantity | HSS (M2) | WC-Co (K20) | Cermet (TiC-TiN) | Alumina Ceramic | Si₃N₄ (Sialon) | CBN | PCD |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Vickers Hardness, $H_V$ (GPa) | $8$–$9$ | $14$–$18$ | $16$–$18$ | $18$–$22$ | $16$–$20$ | $30$–$45$ | $50$–$100$ |
| TRS, $\sigma$ (MPa) | $2500$–$4000$ | $1500$–$2400$ | $1500$–$2000$ | $400$–$800$ | $800$–$1200$ | $600$–$1100$ | $600$–$1100$ |
| Max. usable $\theta$ ($^\circ$C) | $600$ | $900$–$1000$ | $1000$–$1100$ | $1200$–$1400$ | $1300$–$1400$ | $1400$–$1500$ | $700$–$800$ |
| Thermal conductivity $k$ (W/m·K) | $30$–$50$ | $80$–$100$ | $30$–$40$ | $25$–$35$ | $25$–$35$ | $40$–$100$ | $500$–$600$ |
| Taylor exponent $n$ | $0.10$–$0.15$ | $0.20$–$0.30$ | $0.25$–$0.35$ | $0.40$–$0.60$ | $0.40$–$0.60$ | $0.50$–$0.80$ | $0.50$–$0.90$ |
| Chemical inertness to Fe | Low–Med | Medium | Medium–High | High | High | Very High | **Poor** (forms Fe₃C, dissolves) |
| Relative cost index | $1\times$ | $3$–$5\times$ | $5$–$8\times$ | $6$–$10\times$ | $6$–$10\times$ | $15$–$30\times$ | $40$–$100\times$ |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Coated carbide inserts dominate roughing of engine blocks; PCD is the unchallenged tool for high-silicon aluminum pistons and brake discs.
- **Aerospace:** Machining of titanium (Ti-6Al-4V) and Inconel superalloys uses SiAlON or whisker-reinforced alumina at low $V$, high $f$; CBN is used for finish-turning of Inconel jet-engine shafts.
- **Mold & Die:** Hardened die-steel (HRC 60+) finish-machining is the *exclusive* domain of **CBN** — uncoated carbide is simply not hard enough.
- **Electronics:** Heat-sink and connector machining on copper alloys uses **PCD** for its very high thermal conductivity (heat is evacuated through the tool).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Hot-Hardness-Governed Cutting Speed Limit

The operating cutting speed $V$ is governed by the requirement that the tool's hot hardness $H_V(\theta)$ must remain above a *critical threshold* $H_{V,\text{crit}}$ (typically $1.2 \times$ workpiece hardness, to ensure depth-of-cut penetration).

**Step 1 — Write the hot-hardness exponential decay law:**

$$
H_V(\theta) = H_{V,\text{RT}} \cdot \exp\!\left[-\beta \left(\theta - \theta_{RT}\right)\right]
$$

**Step 2 — Express the cutting temperature in terms of cutting speed.** From the KTU 2024 energy-partition model:

$$
\theta = \theta_0 + K_\theta \cdot V^{0.4} \cdot f^{0.2} \cdot t^{0.05}
$$

(Approximate, single-point tool form.) Here $\theta_0$ is the ambient temperature, $K_\theta$ the temperature coefficient, $f$ feed, $t$ depth.

**Step 3 — Impose the design constraint** $H_V(\theta) \geq H_{V,\text{crit}}$:

$$
H_{V,\text{RT}} \cdot \exp\!\left[-\beta \left(\theta - \theta_{RT}\right)\right] \geq H_{V,\text{crit}}
$$

**Step 4 — Take the natural logarithm of both sides:**

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{H_{V,\text{RT}}}{H_{V,\text{crit}}}\right) \geq \beta \left(\theta - \theta_{RT}\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the temperature expression from Step 2:**

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{H_{V,\text{RT}}}{H_{V,\text{crit}}}\right) \geq \beta \left[\theta_0 + K_\theta V^{0.4} f^{0.2} t^{0.05} - \theta_{RT}\right]
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the limiting cutting speed $V_{\text{lim}}$:**

$$
V_{\text{lim}} = \left[\frac{\theta_{RT} - \theta_0 + \dfrac{1}{\beta} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{H_{V,\text{RT}}}{H_{V,\text{crit}}}\right)}{K_\theta \cdot f^{0.2} \cdot t^{0.05}}\right]^{2.5}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The "$\dfrac{1}{\beta}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{H_{V,\text{RT}}}{H_{V,\text{crit}}}\right)$" term is the **thermal budget** of the tool material. A ceramic with a small $\beta$ (slow softening) has a *large thermal budget* and therefore permits a *much higher* $V_{\text{lim}}$ than HSS. This is the rigorous answer to *"Why can ceramics cut 10× faster than HSS?"*

### 3.2 Derivation: Coated-Tool Hot-Hardness (Effective Tool)

For a coated insert, the hot hardness is a **rule-of-mixtures** approximation of the substrate and the coating. Let:
- $H_c, H_s$ = hardness of coating and substrate at $\theta$.
- $x$ = thickness ratio of coating to substrate.

$$
H_{V,\text{eff}} = H_c \cdot x + H_s \cdot (1 - x)
$$

**Step 1 —** Identify the design target: maximize $H_{V,\text{eff}}$ at the operating $\theta$.

**Step 2 —** Differentiate with respect to $x$ to find the optimum thickness ratio:

$$
\frac{d H_{V,\text{eff}}}{dx} = H_c - H_s = 0 \implies H_c = H_s
$$

**Step 3 —** Since for a coating system $H_c > H_s$ at the coating temperature (the whole *point* of a coating), the optimum is achieved at the **maximum practically coatable $x$**, governed by adhesion and residual stress:

$$
x_{\text{opt}} = \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{H_s}{H_c - H_s} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma_s}{\sigma_c}}
$$

where $\sigma_s$ and $\sigma_c$ are the residual stresses in the substrate and coating respectively. (This is a KTU 14-mark derivation for "Coated Tools" sub-topic.)

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Material-by-Material Property Summary

This is the most examination-relevant block. Each row is a *self-contained answer* to a potential 7-mark question.

#### 3.3.1 High Speed Steel (HSS) — e.g., M2, M35, M42, T15

- **Composition:** W (6–19 %), Mo (0–9 %), Cr (4 %), V (1–5 %), Co (0–12 %), balance Fe.
- **Heat treatment:** Hardened to **HRC 62–68** by quenching from $1240$–$1280\,^\circ$C and triple-tempered at $560\,^\circ$C (secondary hardening due to Mo₂C / VC precipitation).
- **Hot hardness:** Loses hardness rapidly above $600\,^\circ$C; **M42 (8 % Co)** extends this to $650\,^\circ$C.
- **TRS:** High — $2.5$–$4.0$ GPa, makes it the most **tough** of all tool materials.
- **Application:** Drills, taps, reamers, broaches, form tools, gear hobs, milling cutters (especially with inserted tips), saw blades. Used at $V = 20$–$60$ m/min on steels, $40$–$80$ m/min on aluminum.
- **Why not carbide at low $V$?** Because at $V < 60$ m/min the chip-load vibration would shatter a carbide insert — HSS's toughness absorbs the impact.

#### 3.3.2 Cemented Carbides (WC-Co)

- **Composition:** Tungsten Carbide (WC) grains, $5$–$20\,\%$ Cobalt (Co) binder; ISO classification: **P** (steel), **M** (stainless), **K** (cast iron), **N** (non-ferrous).
- **Structure:** Two-phase composite — WC grains provide hardness, Co binder provides toughness.
- **Hot hardness:** Retains hardness up to $900$–$1000\,^\circ$C. **Cobalt content inversely proportional to hardness but directly to toughness.**
- **TRS:** $1.5$–$2.4$ GPa (lower than HSS, hence brittleness).
- **Coated variants:** $\text{TiC}, \text{TiN}, \text{TiAlN}, \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3, \text{CrAlN}$ — single, multi-layer, gradient-nano, Al-rich.
- **Application:** Lathe inserts, milling inserts, drills with carbide heads, end mills. Universal workhorse for $\mathbf{60\%}$ of all metal cutting.

#### 3.3.3 Cermets (TiC / TiN + Ni-Mo binder)

- **Composition:** $\text{TiC-TiN}$ or $\text{Ti(C,N)}$ grains in a $\text{Ni-Mo}$ binder; the **Mo** forms a tough $\text{Mo}_2\text{C}$ rim around grains.
- **Hot hardness:** Slightly higher than WC-Co at $1000$–$1100\,^\circ$C.
- **TRS:** Lower than carbide, hence used in *finishing* with light depths.
- **Surface finish:** Excellent — produces a **mirror-like finish** on steel due to low chemical affinity with Fe.
- **Application:** Finishing of steels and cast irons, semi-finishing of stainless, **high-speed turning of carbon steels** at $V = 200$–$400$ m/min.

#### 3.3.4 Ceramics

- **Alumina (Al₂O₃):** Pure white ceramic, $H_V = 18$–$22$ GPa, $T_{\max} = 1400\,^\circ$C, low thermal conductivity → poor thermal shock resistance. Used in **high-speed finishing of cast irons** ($V = 300$–$800$ m/min).
- **Silicon Nitride (Si₃N₄):** Higher TRS ($800$–$1200$ MPa) and thermal shock resistance; **machines cast iron and nickel-based superalloys** at high speeds.
- **Sialon (Si-Al-O-N):** A solid solution of Al₂O₃ in Si₃N₄. Combines hot hardness with toughness. **Used for high-V machining of Inconel and aerospace nickel alloys.**
- **Whisker-Reinforced Alumina (Al₂O₃ + SiC whiskers):** Toughness boosted $\approx 3\times$ by crack-bridging whiskers. Used for **nickel-based superalloys** (jet engine rotors).

#### 3.3.5 Cubic Boron Nitride (CBN)

- **Structure:** Cubic form of BN (zinc-blende), second only to diamond in hardness ($H_V \approx 30$–$45$ GPa).
- **Key advantage over diamond:** **Chemically inert to iron up to $1500\,^\circ$C** — does NOT dissolve into ferrous chips. (Diamond is unstable above $700\,^\circ$C in contact with Fe.)
- **Application:** Hardened steel (HRC 55–70) finish-machining replacing grinding, gray cast iron at high $V$, **Ni-Hard castings**, pearlitic rolls.
- **Limitation:** Brittle; cannot handle interruptions; expensive; reacts with water — must use **dry machining or dedicated CBN-grade emulsions**.

#### 3.3.6 Diamond (PCD, CVD, MCD)

- **Polycrystalline Diamond (PCD):** Sintered diamond particles on WC-Co substrate, $H_V = 50$–$100$ GPa.
- **CVD Diamond:** Pure diamond film deposited chemically; isotropic, sharper edges.
- **Application:** High-silicon aluminum alloys (AlSi alloys > 12 % Si — automotive pistons, engine blocks), metal-matrix composites (MMCs), carbon-fiber reinforced polymers (CFRP), copper and brass, graphite and tungsten carbide turning.
- **Forbids:** **Never machine ferrous metals** (Fe, Ni, Co) with diamond — catalytic dissolution at $\theta > 700\,^\circ$C turns diamond into graphite/iron-carbide.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Tool Material Selection Logic — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Workpiece Material Identified] --> B{Category?}
    B -->|Ferrous Steel/SS| C{Desired Output}
    B -->|Cast Iron| D[Carbide K-grade / Ceramic Al2O3]
    B -->|Aluminum/Cu/Composite| E[PCD or Uncoated Carbide N-grade]
    B -->|Hardened Steel HRC 55+| F[CBN]
    B -->|Inconel/Ti-Alloy| G[Sialon or Whisker Al2O3]
    B -->|Wood/Plastic| H[Carbide with PCD tip]

    C -->|High V finishing| I[Coated Carbide TiAlN]
    C -->|Mirror finish| J[Cermet]
    C -->|Low V interrupted cut| K[HSS M2 or M42]

    D -->|High V continuous| L[Ceramic Al2O3 or Si3N4]
    D -->|Heavy roughing| M[Coated Carbide]

    E -->|High Si Al| N[PCD]
    E -->|CFRP / MMC| O[Diamond coated or CVD]
```

### 4.2 Hot Hardness Comparison Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph RT[Room Temperature]
        A1[HSS 850 HV]
        A2[Carbide 1500 HV]
        A3[Al2O3 2000 HV]
        A4[CBN 4000 HV]
        A5[PCD 8000 HV]
    end
    subgraph HT[At 800 degrees C]
        B1[HSS 200 HV - dead]
        B2[Carbide 900 HV - fading]
        B3[Al2O3 1500 HV - ok]
        B4[CBN 3500 HV - still cutting]
        B5[PCD 2000 HV - graphitized]
    end
    A1 -.transforms.-> B1
    A2 -.transforms.-> B2
    A3 -.transforms.-> B3
    A4 -.transforms.-> B4
    A5 -.transforms.-> B5
```

> [!NOTE]
> The dashed arrows denote the **thermal transformation** of hardness at $800\,^\circ$C. Notice how the ranking completely rearranges — PCD loses its crown to CBN at high temperature. This is the KTU board's favorite conceptual figure.

### 4.3 Coated Carbide — Cross-Sectional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S1[Substrate Layer - WC-Co]
        S1A[Coarse WC grains - high toughness]
    end
    S1 --> S2
    subgraph S2[Inner Coating TiC or TiCN]
        S2A[Diffusion barrier + wear resistance]
    end
    S2 --> S3
    subgraph S3[Mid Coating Al2O3 - thermal barrier]
        S3A[Heat shield to substrate]
    end
    S3 --> S4
    subgraph S4[Outer Coating TiN or TiAlN - golden / purple]
        S4A[Lubricity + low BUE + wear indicator]
    end
```

### 4.4 Wear Mechanism Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W[Tool Wear Observed] --> Q{Dominant Mode?}
    Q -->|Flank land| R[Abrasive wear - raise H_V or use coating]
    Q -->|Crater on rake| S[Diffusion wear - reduce theta or use chemically inert tool]
    Q -->|Chatter marks| T[Chipping - increase toughness or reduce feed]
    Q -->|Built-up edge| U[Adhesive wear - use coating reduce v increase coolant]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`
**List the four major tool wear mechanisms. State one operational strategy to mitigate each.**

**Model Answer (verbatim, exam-ready):**
1. **Abrasive wear:** Hard inclusions in the workpiece (e.g., SiC, Al₂O₃, carbides) plough the tool. *Mitigation:* Use a tool with higher hardness / finer grain carbide; apply a hard coating (TiC, TiAlN).
2. **Adhesive wear:** Microwelding at the tool–chip contact followed by fracture. *Mitigation:* Apply a lubricious coating (TiN, MoS₂); use a chemically inert tool (ceramic, cermet) for the workpiece.
3. **Diffusion wear:** Mutual atomic diffusion at high $\theta$ across the tool-chip interface (e.g., W and C diffusing from WC into the Fe chip). *Mitigation:* Lower cutting speed (reduces $\theta$); use a chemically stable tool (CBN for steel).
4. **Oxidation / chemical wear:** Atmospheric or coolant-driven oxidation of tool at high $\theta$. *Mitigation:* Use a thermally stable coating (Al₂O₃, TiAlN) or a ceramic tool; switch to dry machining.

#### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]`
**Why cannot diamond (PCD) tools be used to machine steels, even though diamond is the hardest known material?**

**Model Answer:**
Although diamond has the highest hardness ($H_V \approx 50$–$100$ GPa), at cutting temperatures $\theta > 700\,^\circ$C the carbon atoms of diamond undergo **catalytic dissolution** into the iron (Fe) chip to form **iron carbides (Fe₃C)**. This is a thermodynamic instability caused by the strong affinity between C and Fe. The result is rapid **diffusive/chemical wear** that destroys the cutting edge. Therefore diamond is reserved for **non-ferrous** workpieces (aluminum alloys, copper, CFRP, MMC, graphite). For steels, the second-hardest material — **CBN** — is used, because it is chemically inert to Fe up to $\approx 1500\,^\circ$C.

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Module 2]`**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **four major tool wear mechanisms** that occur during a single-point turning operation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the properties and applications of **High Speed Steel, Cemented Carbide, and Ceramic** tool materials. Use a comparative table. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Four Wear Mechanisms — Sketches and Explanations:**

| # | Wear Mode | Location on Tool | Physical Cause | Visual Marker |
|---|-----------|------------------|----------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Flank wear** | Clearance face | Hard workpiece asperities abrade flank | Uniform land of width $VB$ |
| 2 | **Crater wear** | Rake face | Diffusion / dissolution at high $\theta$ | Concave hollow at depth $KT$ |
| 3 | **Built-up edge (BUE)** | Rake face | Microwelding of work material | Random protrusion |
| 4 | **Notch wear** | Tool tip | Oxidation at stagnation point | Sharp notch at depth $VN$ |

**Wear curves** ($VB$ vs. time) for each mode follow a characteristic three-stage pattern: (i) rapid initial wear-in, (ii) steady-state linear wear, (iii) accelerated failure. KTU expects a sketch of the tool with $VB$, $KT$, $BUE$, $VN$ clearly marked.

**(b) Comparison Table (HSS vs Carbide vs Ceramic):**

| Property | HSS (M2) | Carbide (WC-Co, K20) | Ceramic (Al₂O₃) |
|----------|----------|----------------------|-------------------|
| Hot Hardness at 600 °C | $550$ HV | $1200$ HV | $1700$ HV |
| Hot Hardness at 1000 °C | $200$ HV (dead) | $800$ HV | $1500$ HV |
| TRS (MPa) | $2500$–$4000$ | $1500$–$2400$ | $400$–$800$ |
| Toughness | Very high | Medium | Low (brittle) |
| Thermal shock resistance | Excellent | Good | Poor |
| Max. cutting speed on steel (m/min) | $30$–$60$ | $150$–$250$ | $300$–$800$ |
| Cost index | $1\times$ | $3$–$5\times$ | $6$–$10\times$ |
| Typical application | Drills, taps, broaches | Universal turning inserts | High-$V$ finishing of CI |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. Confusing **TRS (Transverse Rupture Strength)** with **Hardness** — they are *opposing* properties. Higher hardness ⇒ lower TRS. KTU deducts 1 mark for this confusion.
> 2. Writing "ceramics have high toughness" — false; ceramics are *brittle*.
> 3. Omitting the **uncoated vs coated** distinction for carbides.
> 4. Drawing the wear sketch without labeling $VB$, $KT$, and the BUE region.

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Module 2]`**

**(a)** Explain the **ISO classification of cemented carbides** (P, M, K grades). List two suitable workpieces for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **properties, advantages, and limitations of coated carbide tools**. What are the typical coating materials used, and what role does each play? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) ISO Classification (P, M, K):**

| ISO Code | Color Code | WC Grain Size | Co Content | Suitable Workpiece | Application Examples |
|----------|------------|---------------|------------|--------------------|----------------------|
| **P** (Steel) | Blue | Medium-coarse | $6$–$10\%$ | Carbon steel, alloy steel, tool steel, stainless steel | Turning, milling of structural components |
| **M** (Stainless) | Yellow | Fine-medium | $8$–$15\%$ | Austenitic SS, martensitic SS, high-temp alloys | Turning of pump shafts, valve bodies |
| **K** (Cast Iron) | Red | Coarse | $5$–$8\%$ | Gray CI, ductile CI, malleable CI | Cylinder liners, brake discs |
| **N** (Non-ferrous) | Green | Fine | $3$–$6\%$ | Al, Cu, plastics, wood | PCD alternatives, CFRP |

(For KTU 2024 scheme, the **N** class is increasingly tested.) Each code has sub-classes $01$ to $50$ indicating increasing toughness and decreasing wear resistance (e.g., **P01** = high-V finishing, **P50** = low-V heavy roughing).

**(b) Coated Carbide Tools:**

A coated carbide insert has **$3$–$20\,\mu\text{m}$** of coating applied by **CVD (Chemical Vapor Deposition)** or **PVD (Physical Vapor Deposition)**.

- **Coating materials and their roles:**

| Coating | Role | Color |
|---------|------|-------|
| **TiN** (Titanium Nitride) | Lubricity, low friction, wear indicator | Gold |
| **TiC** (Titanium Carbide) | High wear resistance, low BUE tendency | Gray |
| **TiCN** (Titanium Carbonitride) | Combines TiC + TiN properties | Blue-gray |
| **TiAlN** (Titanium Aluminum Nitride) | High-temp oxidation resistance ($800\,^\circ$C) | Purple-black |
| **Al₂O₃** (Alumina) | Thermal barrier — keeps heat in the chip | White |
| **CrAlN** | High-temp + corrosion resistance | Silver |

- **Advantages:** 2–4× tool life, higher cutting speeds (50–100 % increase), better surface finish, reduced BUE.
- **Limitations:** Higher cost, re-sharpening destroys coating (most coated tools are **inserts**, not resharpenable), coating flaking at high mechanical shock.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Confusing **CVD** (high-T, thicker, tensile stress) with **PVD** (low-T, thinner, compressive stress). PVD is preferred for sharp inserts and HSS.
> 2. Forgetting to mention that **Al₂O₃** acts as a *thermal barrier*, not a wear layer.
> 3. Saying "coated tools are 10× faster" — the realistic figure is **1.5–3× speed increase**, not 10×.
> 4. Missing the fact that **coatings are sacrificial** — once worn, tool life drops sharply.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Print-Friendly):**

- [x] The **Five Performance Pillars:** Hot Hardness, Toughness (TRS), Wear Resistance, Thermal Stability, Chemical Inertness.
- [x] **HSS (M2, M35, M42):** Toughest, low hot hardness ($T_{\max} \approx 600\,^\circ$C), used for drills/taps/broaches.
- [x] **Carbide (WC-Co):** Universal workhorse; **ISO P/M/K/N** classification; **Co-content trade-off** (more Co ⇒ tougher, less hard).
- [x] **Cermet (TiC-TiN + Ni-Mo):** Higher hot hardness than carbide; best for **mirror finishing of steel**.
- [x] **Ceramics:** Al₂O₃ for cast iron; Si₃N₄/Sialon for nickel alloys; whisker-reinforced for Inconel; **brittle — no interruptions**.
- [x] **CBN:** Second hardest; **chemically inert to Fe**; **must** for hardened-steel (HRC 55+) finishing; avoids diamond's Fe catalysis.
- [x] **PCD:** Hardest; chemically reactive to Fe at $>700\,^\circ$C; only for **Al, Cu, CFRP, MMC, graphite, wood**.
- [x] **Coatings:** TiN (lubricity), TiC (wear), TiCN (combo), TiAlN (high-T), Al₂O₃ (thermal barrier), CrAlN (corrosion).
- [x] **Taylor's $n$ values:** HSS $0.10$–$0.15$ < Carbide $0.20$–$0.30$ < Ceramic $0.40$–$0.60$ < CBN/PCD $0.50$–$0.90$.
- [x] **Wear quartet:** Abrasive, Adhesive, Diffusion, Oxidation — *each* has a counter-strategy.
- [x] **Diamond cannot machine steel** because of catalytic Fe-C reaction forming Fe₃C; **CBN can**, up to $1500\,^\circ$C.
- [x] **The trade-off curve** (Hardness ↔ Toughness) is the single most important selection graph — every tool material sits on it.

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Tool Materials. Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020 Outcome-Based pattern.**
