# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper]
**Differentiate between CVD and PVD coating techniques used on cutting tools. State two advantages of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | CVD (Chemical Vapor Deposition) | PVD (Physical Vapor Deposition) |
|---|---|---|
| Process temperature | **800–1000 °C** (high) | **400–600 °C** (low) |
| Coating thickness | **5–15 µm** (thick) | **1–5 µm** (thin) |
| Adhesion | Excellent (chemical bond) | Good (mechanical bond) |
| Substrate effect | Causes **brittle η-phase** in carbide | No η-phase, preserves toughness |
| Application | Roughing, heavy-duty | Finishing, sharp-edge tools |
| Cost | Moderate | Higher (vacuum equipment) |

**Two advantages of CVD:** Thick coatings possible, excellent adhesion, suitable for high-temperature roughing.
**Two advantages of PVD:** Low temperature preserves substrate toughness, sharper cutting edges possible, better for drills and taps.

*(3 marks: 1 for process distinction, 1 for advantages, 1 for application relevance)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**What is the significance of Taylor's tool life equation in cost benefit analysis of cutting tools?**

**Model Answer:**
Taylor's equation $VT^n = C$ provides the **mathematical link** between cutting speed $V$ and tool life $T$ using the constants $n$ and $C$, which are unique to each tool-workpiece pair. In cost benefit analysis, this equation is the **bridge** that allows one to:

1. **Predict tool life** at any chosen operating speed.
2. **Compute the number of components per tool edge** ($N = T/t_m$).
3. **Calculate the cost per piece** by substituting $T$ into the cost equation.
4. **Derive the optimum cutting speed** ($V_c$ or $V_p$) by differentiating the cost function.

Without Taylor's equation, there is no analytical way to convert a "cutting speed choice" into a "cost figure" — which is the entire purpose of CBA.

*(3 marks: 1 for stating the equation, 1 for its role, 1 for connecting to cost optimization)*

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): Comprehensive Cost Optimization Problem

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

> **(a)** A turning operation on EN8 steel uses a coated carbide insert with Taylor's constants $C = 400$ and $n = 0.30$. The tool cost is $C_t = ₹400$, tool change cost $C_h = ₹80$, and operator + machine rate is $C_o = ₹20/\text{min}$. The cutting parameters are: $D = 60$ mm, $L = 100$ mm, $f = 0.3$ mm/rev, $d = 2$ mm. Calculate the **minimum cost cutting speed** and the corresponding **tool life**. **OR** [7 marks]

> **(b)** Compare the cost per component when operating at $V = 200$ m/min versus the minimum cost speed $V_c$ found in part (a). Use the **cost-per-piece equation** for both cases and compute the percentage saving. **OR** [7 marks]

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Step 1:** Identify the minimum-cost-speed formula.

$$V_c = \frac{C}{\left[\left(\frac{1}{n} - 1\right) \cdot \frac{C_t + C_h}{C_o}\right]^n}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $1/n - 1$.

$$\frac{1}{n} - 1 = \frac{1}{0.30} - 1 = 3.333 - 1 = 2.333$$

**Step 3:** Compute $(C_t + C_h)/C_o$.

$$\frac{C_t + C_h}{C_o} = \frac{400 + 80}{20} = \frac{480}{20} = 24$$

**Step 4:** Compute the product.

$$2.333 \times 24 = 56$$

**Step 5:** Raise to power $n = 0.30$.

$$56^{0.30} = e^{0.30 \cdot \ln 56} = e^{0.30 \cdot 4.025} = e^{1.2075} \approx 3.345$$

**Step 6:** Compute $V_c$.

$$V_c = \frac{400}{3.345} \approx 119.6 \text{ m/min}$$

$$\boxed{V_c \approx 120 \text{ m/min}}$$

**Step 7:** Compute $T_c$.

$$T_c = \left(\frac{400}{120}\right)^{\frac{1}{0.30}} = (3.333)^{3.333}$$

$$(3.333)^3 = 37.04, \quad (3.333)^{0.333} = 1.494$$

$$T_c \approx 37.04 \times 1.494 \approx 55.3 \text{ minutes}$$

$$\boxed{T_c \approx 55.3 \text{ min}}$$

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Step 1:** Compute the geometric constant $K_1$.

$$K_1 = \frac{1000 L}{\pi D f} = \frac{1000 \times 100}{\pi \times 60 \times 0.3} = \frac{100000}{56.55} \approx 1768.4 \text{ m·min/rev·mm}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $t_m$ at $V = 200$ m/min.

$$t_m = \frac{K_1}{V} = \frac{1768.4}{200} = 8.842 \text{ min/piece}$$

**Step 3:** Compute $t_m$ at $V_c = 120$ m/min.

$$t_m = \frac{1768.4}{120} = 14.74 \text{ min/piece}$$

**Step 4:** Compute $T$ at $V = 200$ m/min.

$$T_{200} = \left(\frac{400}{200}\right)^{1/0.30} = (2)^{3.333}$$

$$2^{3.333} = 2^3 \times 2^{0.333} = 8 \times 1.26 = 10.08 \text{ min}$$

**Step 5:** Cost per piece at $V = 200$ m/min.

$$C_{pc,200} = C_o \cdot t_m + (C_t + C_h) \cdot \frac{t_m}{T_{200}}$$

$$= 20 \times 8.842 + 480 \times \frac{8.842}{10.08}$$

$$= 176.84 + 480 \times 0.8772 = 176.84 + 421.06 = 597.90 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 6:** Cost per piece at $V_c = 120$ m/min.

$$C_{pc,120} = 20 \times 14.74 + 480 \times \frac{14.74}{55.3}$$

$$= 294.8 + 480 \times 0.2665 = 294.8 + 127.92 = 422.72 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 7:** Percentage saving.

$$\%\text{ Saving} = \frac{597.90 - 422.72}{597.90} \times 100 = \frac{175.18}{597.90} \times 100 \approx 29.3\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{Saving at } V_c = 29.3\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit trap:** Students often confuse $C = 400$ m/min with a *dimensionless* number. The $C$ in Taylor's equation *always* carries the unit of m/min.
> 2. **Sign error in derivative:** When solving for $V_c$, students frequently write $1 - 1/n$ instead of $1/n - 1$. The correct sign must be **positive** (since $n < 1$).
> 3. **$t_m$ vs $T$ confusion:** Machining time per piece $t_m$ uses **length and feed**; tool life $T$ uses **speed and Taylor's constants**. Mixing them up costs full marks.
> 4. **Rounding:** KTU accepts answers to **2 significant figures**; do not over-round at intermediate steps (keep at least 4 digits).

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Comparative Tool Material Selection

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

> **(a)** For machining a high-volume batch of **10,000 aluminium alloy components** on a CNC turning centre, compare the following three tool options using cost benefit analysis:
>
> | Tool | C_t (₹) | n | C at 200 m/min | Recommended V (m/min) |
> |---|---|---|---|---|
> | Uncoated HSS | 150 | 0.10 | 90 | 60 |
> | Coated Carbide (TiN) | 500 | 0.25 | 350 | 250 |
> | PCD | 6000 | 0.40 | 800 | 600 |
>
> Use $C_o = ₹25/\text{min}$ and machining time $t_m = 2.0$ min/piece (at recommended V). State which tool gives the **lowest cost per piece**. [7 marks]

> **(b)** Discuss the **technical justification** (not just the cost) for choosing the winning tool from part (a), considering (i) built-up edge formation in aluminium, (ii) chemical affinity to iron, (iii) achievable surface finish, and (iv) tool change frequency. [7 marks]

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Cost per piece equation:**

$$C_{pc} = C_o \cdot t_m + (C_t + C_h) \cdot \frac{t_m}{T}$$

Assume $C_h = ₹50$ for all three tools (typical KTU assumption).

**Step 1:** HSS (V = 60, n = 0.10, C = 90).

$$T_{HSS} = \left(\frac{90}{60}\right)^{1/0.10} = (1.5)^{10}$$

$1.5^2 = 2.25$ ; $1.5^4 = 5.0625$ ; $1.5^8 = 25.629$ ; $1.5^{10} = 1.5^8 \times 1.5^2 = 25.629 \times 2.25 = 57.66$

$$T_{HSS} \approx 57.7 \text{ min} \quad\Rightarrow\quad N = 57.7/2.0 = 28.8 \text{ pieces/edge}$$

$$C_{pc,HSS} = 25 \times 2.0 + 200 \times \frac{2.0}{57.7} = 50 + 6.93 = ₹56.93$$

**Step 2:** Coated Carbide (V = 250, n = 0.25, C = 350).

$$T_{CC} = \left(\frac{350}{250}\right)^{1/0.25} = (1.4)^4$$

$1.4^2 = 1.96$ ; $1.4^4 = 3.8416$

$$T_{CC} \approx 3.84 \text{ min} \quad\Rightarrow\quad N = 3.84/2.0 = 1.92 \text{ pieces/edge}$$

$$C_{pc,CC} = 25 \times 2.0 + 550 \times \frac{2.0}{3.84} = 50 + 286.5 = ₹336.5$$

**Step 3:** PCD (V = 600, n = 0.40, C = 800).

$$T_{PCD} = \left(\frac{800}{600}\right)^{1/0.40} = (1.333)^{2.5}$$

$1.333^2 = 1.778$ ; $1.333^{0.5} = 1.155$ ; $1.333^{2.5} = 1.778 \times 1.155 = 2.053$

$$T_{PCD} \approx 2.05 \text{ min} \quad\Rightarrow\quad N = 2.05/2.0 = 1.03 \text{ pieces/edge}$$

$$C_{pc,PCD} = 25 \times 2.0 + 6050 \times \frac{2.0}{2.05} = 50 + 5902.4 = ₹5952.4$$

**Step 4:** Re-evaluation — these values are wildly off. The issue is that for HSS, the recommended V is 60 m/min but the $t_m = 2$ min/piece was given "at recommended V" of each tool, so we must assume $t_m$ already differs. Let me correct with **fixed $V$ per tool** in the cost equation.

> [!WARNING]
> **This is a teaching correction:** The CBA should be performed at the **recommended speed for each tool**, not at a common speed. The table's column "Recommended V" was the operational speed; $t_m$ must therefore be computed per tool. Re-derive using $t_m = L/(f \cdot N_{rpm})$ with $D = 50$ mm and $f = 0.2$ mm/rev for consistency.

**Corrected Step (using $D = 50$ mm, $f = 0.2$ mm/rev, $L = 100$ mm):**

$$K_1 = \frac{1000 \times 100}{\pi \times 50 \times 0.2} = \frac{100000}{31.42} = 3183 \text{ m·min/rev·mm}$$

**HSS at V = 60:** $t_m = 3183/60 = 53.05$ min. $T = 57.7$ min. $N = 57.7/53.05 = 1.09$ pieces. $C_{pc} = 25(53.05) + 200(53.05/57.7) = 1326.3 + 183.9 = ₹1510$.

**Carbide at V = 250:** $t_m = 3183/250 = 12.73$ min. $T = 3.84$ min. **N = 3.84/12.73 = 0.30 — less than 1 piece!** This means the tool fails mid-cut. **Carbide is unsuitable at this feed/speed combination.**

**PCD at V = 600:** $t_m = 3183/600 = 5.31$ min. $T = 2.05$ min. $N = 0.39$ — also less than 1 piece. **PCD is unsuitable.**

**Conclusion:** The given parameters are incompatible — the cutting length $L$ is too long for the tool lives. A real KTU exam would either provide consistent numbers or expect the student to identify this inconsistency. The *pedagogical* answer is: **For aluminium at high V, PCD wins if the tool life is adequate; for low V, HSS wins.** Cost-benefit analysis only works when both the tool and the parameters are matched.

**Final answer (corrected interpretation):**

The student should state: *"For the given parameters, **none** of the three tools produce even one full component per edge at the recommended speeds, indicating an infeasible combination. A re-design of cutting parameters (lower V or higher n) or a different workpiece is required. If we relax the constraint and assume $t_m = 2$ min as given, then the cost per piece would be: HSS = ₹56.93, Carbide = ₹336.5, PCD = ₹5952.4. HSS wins on raw cost, PCD wins on cycle time. For a 10,000-piece batch, total HSS cost = 10,000 × 56.93 ≈ ₹5.69 lakh; total PCD cost = ₹59.5 lakh — HSS is the choice."*

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Discussion (each point 1.5 marks, total 7):**

(i) **Built-Up Edge (BUE) in Aluminium:** Aluminium has high chemical affinity to ferrous tools. HSS and carbide (both contain Fe) suffer severe BUE formation at low-to-moderate speeds, causing poor surface finish and sudden tool failure. PCD is **chemically inert** to aluminium — no BUE — giving stable cutting and mirror finish.

(ii) **Chemical Affinity to Iron:** Ferrous tools (HSS, carbide) and aluminium have a strong **atomic-scale adhesion tendency** that causes crater wear and *diffusion wear*. PCD eliminates this because carbon (diamond) does not diffuse into aluminium.

(iii) **Surface Finish:** HSS turning of aluminium typically gives **Ra 1.6–3.2 µm**; coated carbide gives **Ra 0.8–1.6 µm**; PCD gives **Ra 0.2–0.4 µm** (mirror finish). For 10,000 components, the lower scrap rate of PCD compensates for higher tool cost in many applications (e.g., piston machining).

(iv) **Tool Change Frequency:** HSS would need change every ~1 piece (T = 2 min, $t_m$ = 2 min), so the machine is *constantly* stopped for tool change. PCD lasts longer per edge in absolute terms, but the **non-productive time** of tool change is what dominates. In an automated cell, fewer tool changes mean less operator intervention and higher OEE.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks Loss in Part (b):**
> 1. Writing only "PCD is best" without **cost numbers** — examiner deducts 2–3 marks.
> 2. Confusing "built-up edge" with "built-up layer" — they are the same phenomenon; KTU expects the term **BUE**.
> 3. Forgetting that **diffusion wear** is the controlling mechanism in aluminium cutting with ferrous tools.
> 4. Not linking the discussion back to the **10,000-piece batch size** — examiners look for *batch-volume awareness*.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Taylor's Equation** $VT^n = C$ is the **master key** — every CBA problem starts here.
- [x] $n$ values: HSS ≈ 0.10–0.15, Carbide ≈ 0.20–0.30, Ceramic ≈ 0.30–0.40, CBN/PCD ≈ 0.40–0.50. **Higher n = tool life is less sensitive to V** = wider operating window.
- [x] **Minimum Cost Speed** $V_c$ uses $(C_t + C_h)/C_o$; **Max Production Speed** $V_p$ uses $t_{ct}$. Do not mix them up.
- [x] **Cost per piece = $C_o t_m + (C_t + C_h) \cdot t_m / T$** — three terms: machine, tool, change overhead.
- [x] **High tool cost + low machine cost** → operate at **lower $V$** (save the tool).
- [x] **Low tool cost + high machine cost** → operate at **higher $V$** (save the machine).
- [x] **Coated tools** increase $C$ in Taylor's equation by 2–3× and $n$ by 0.05–0.10, raising both $V$ and $T$.
- [x] **CVD coatings** (TiN, TiCN) are thick and ideal for **roughing**; **PVD coatings** (TiAlN, AlCrN) are thin and ideal for **finishing + sharp edges**.
- [x] **PCD cannot be coated** — already carbon; coated with DLC only as outer anti-stick layer.
- [x] **CBN is for hardened steel** (≥50 HRC); using it on mild steel is **economic waste** and may cause chemical wear.
- [x] **Ceramics (Al₂O₃, SiAlON) are not for interrupted cuts** — low fracture toughness; catastrophic failure risk.
- [x] **The breakeven volume** for a premium tool is typically **3,000–5,000 components/year** in Indian MSME context.
- [x] **MRR = V·f·d/60** is the productivity metric; **$P_c = k_s \cdot MRR$** is the power metric; both feed into the cost.
- [x] For KTU numericals, **always state the unit** of $C$ (m/min) — examiners deduct 0.5 marks for omission.
- [x] The **derivative $\frac{dC_{pc}}{dV} = 0$** leads to $V_c$; a *second derivative* test confirms it's a minimum (positive $d^2C/dV^2$).
- [x] **Tool wear mechanisms** to know: abrasion, adhesion, diffusion, oxidation, thermal fatigue, chemical wear — each tool material resists a *subset* of these.
- [x] **"Coat or not?"** decision tree: high V + dry cutting → coat; low V + wet cutting → uncoated may suffice.
- [x] **Final check before submitting a KTU answer:** Are the units consistent? Is the sign of $(1/n - 1)$ positive? Is the $V_c$ reasonable (typically 50–400 m/min for production)?
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cutting Parameters - Speed, Feed, and Depth of Cut
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET522 - MECHANICS OF METAL CUTTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cutting Parameters - Speed, Feed, and Depth of Cut

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Cutting Parameters?

In any metal cutting operation (turning, milling, drilling, shaping, etc.), three primary **kinematic variables** govern the entire machining process. KTU 2024 Scheme refers to these as the **Primary Cutting Parameters** (sometimes called the "cutting regime" or "machining triad"). Together, they define the size, shape, and rate of the chip being produced.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** These three parameters are the foundation for calculating Material Removal Rate (MRR), cutting power, cutting force, and tool life. Mastery of these three variables is mandatory before tackling the Taylor tool life equation (Module 4).

The three primary cutting parameters are:

1. **Cutting Speed ($V$)** — *How fast the cutting edge sweeps across the workpiece.*
2. **Feed ($f$)** — *How much the tool advances per revolution or per tooth.*
3. **Depth of Cut ($d$ or $a_p$)** — *How deep the tool plunges into the workpiece.*

---

## 1.2 Cutting Speed ($V$) — Formal Definition

**Cutting speed** is defined as the **instantaneous linear (tangential) surface speed of the workpiece (in turning) or the cutting tool (in milling/drilling) measured at the point of contact with the cutting edge.**

$$V = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000} \quad \text{(m/min)}$$

Where:
- $V$ = Cutting speed in **meters per minute (m/min)**
- $D$ = Workpiece diameter (turning) or tool diameter (drilling/milling) in **millimeters (mm)**
- $N$ = Spindle rotational speed in **revolutions per minute (rpm)**

### Intuitive Analogy — Cutting Speed 🍕
> Imagine you are using a **pizza cutter** to slice through dough. The "cutting speed" is equivalent to *how fast you push the wheel along its circular path*. If you push the cutter very slowly, you get a clean, controlled slice but waste time. If you push it very fast, you cut quickly but risk tearing the dough. In machining, the *workpiece* plays the role of the dough, and the *tool edge* plays the role of the pizza wheel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Unit Reminder (KTU Exam):** Always express cutting speed in **m/min** (or m/s) and diameter in **mm**. The factor $1000$ in the denominator converts mm/min to m/min. Marks are often lost for unit mismatches in numerical problems.

---

## 1.3 Feed ($f$) — Formal Definition

**Feed** is defined as the **distance traveled by the cutting tool relative to the workpiece per revolution of the spindle (turning) or per tooth (milling) or per stroke (shaping).**

Common notations in KTU textbooks:
- $f$ = Feed per revolution in turning (**mm/rev**)
- $f_z$ = Feed per tooth in milling (**mm/tooth**)
- $v_f$ = Table feed (linear feed rate) in **mm/min**

### Intuitive Analogy — Feed 🍞
> Continuing the pizza analogy — if cutting speed is *how fast* the wheel moves, then **feed** is *how much the wheel is angled into the dough for each revolution*. A small feed = thin slice (low material removal, fine surface). A large feed = thick slice (high material removal, rough surface).

---

## 1.4 Depth of Cut ($d$ or $a_p$) — Formal Definition

**Depth of cut** is defined as the **one-sided penetration of the cutting tool into the workpiece, measured perpendicular to the machined surface.**

- In **turning**: $d$ = radial thickness removed from the workpiece (mm).
- In **milling**: $a_p$ = axial depth (mm) and $a_e$ = radial depth (mm).

### Intuitive Analogy — Depth of Cut 🪵
> If we extend the analogy to sawing a log of wood — cutting speed is the *blade speed*, feed is *how fast you push the log into the saw*, and depth of cut is *how deep you plunge the saw blade into the log* in a single pass. Just like you wouldn't try to saw through a thick log in one go (you'd do multiple shallow passes), machinists often prefer multiple shallow depth-of-cut passes over one deep pass for better tool life.

---

## 1.5 The Cutting Triad — Summary

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Controls... |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cutting Speed | $V$ | m/min | Heat generation & chip flow |
| Feed | $f$ | mm/rev (or mm/tooth) | Surface finish & chip thickness |
| Depth of Cut | $d$ | mm | Bulk material removal per pass |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight (Board Exam Favorite):** Of the three parameters, **Cutting Speed ($V$)** has the *largest* influence on **tool life**, while **Depth of Cut ($d$)** has the *smallest* influence on tool life. Feed has a moderate influence. This is the qualitative trend KTU expects students to know for theory questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Physical Interpretation of Each Parameter

### A. Cutting Speed ($V$)
- Measured at the **cutting edge/workpiece interface** (the so-called "tool tip" speed).
- For turning: $V$ is the speed of the *workpiece* surface.
- For drilling/milling: $V$ is the speed of the *cutter periphery*.
- Cutting speed directly affects the **cutting temperature**, which in turn affects **tool wear**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Concept:** A change in cutting speed is logarithmic in its effect on tool life. Doubling $V$ reduces tool life by approximately $2^5 = 32$ times (this is the essence of the **Taylor Tool Life Equation** covered in the next module).

### B. Feed ($f$)
- For **turning**: $f$ is feed per revolution (mm/rev).
- For **milling**: There are two levels — feed per tooth ($f_z$) and table feed ($v_f$).

$$v_f = f_z \cdot z \cdot N$$

Where $z$ = number of teeth on the milling cutter.

- **Effect of feed:**
  - Higher $f$ → **rougher surface finish** (higher $R_a$).
  - Higher $f$ → **higher cutting force** ($F_c \propto f^{0.75}$ approximately).
  - Higher $f$ → **higher Material Removal Rate (MRR)**.

### C. Depth of Cut ($d$)
- For **turning**: $d$ = (Initial diameter − Final diameter) / 2.
- For **milling**: $a_p$ (axial) and $a_e$ (radial) are used.
- **Effect of depth of cut:**
  - Linear effect on MRR.
  - Smallest effect on tool life compared to $V$ and $f$.
  - Greater effect on **chisel-edge and tool rigidity** considerations.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The table below contains every high-yield formula for Module 3 numerical problems. Memorize and verify unit conversions.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cutting Speed (Turning) | $V = \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000}$ | m/min | $D$ in mm, $N$ in rpm |
| 2 | Cutting Speed (Drilling) | $V = \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000}$ | m/min | $D$ = drill diameter |
| 3 | Spindle Speed | $N = \dfrac{1000 \, V}{\pi D}$ | rpm | Rearranged from #1 |
| 4 | Feed Rate (Table Feed) | $v_f = f_z \cdot z \cdot N$ | mm/min | Milling only |
| 5 | Feed per Rev (Turning) | $f$ | mm/rev | Direct input |
| 6 | Material Removal Rate (Turning) | $\text{MRR} = V \cdot f \cdot d$ | mm³/min | Most common formula |
| 7 | MRR (Milling) | $\text{MRR} = v_f \cdot a_p \cdot a_e$ | mm³/min | Where $a_p$ = depth, $a_e$ = width |
| 8 | MRR (Drilling) | $\text{MRR} = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot v_f$ | mm³/min | For solid drill |
| 9 | Feed per Tooth | $f_z = \dfrac{v_f}{z \, N}$ | mm/tooth | Rearranged from #4 |
| 10 | Average Chip Thickness | $h_{avg} = f \sin\phi$ | mm | Orthogonal cutting, $\phi$ = shear angle |
| 11 | Uncut Chip Thickness | $t_1 = f$ | mm | Turning, single point |
| 12 | Uncut Chip Width | $b$ | mm | Width of cut, $b = d$ in turning |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder:** All formulas above assume the **conventional cutting regime**. For unconventional operations (e.g., parting, grooving, threading), the geometry changes — but KTU 2024 Module 3 primarily tests **turning** with secondary focus on **drilling and milling**.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Why do these three parameters matter to a practicing engineer?

1. **Process Planning (CAM/CNC):** Every CNC program requires an explicit declaration of these three parameters (e.g., G96 for constant surface speed in turning).
2. **Cost Estimation:** MRR is the primary driver of machining time, which directly drives **hourly machine cost** and **operator cost**.
3. **Tool Life Management:** A 20% increase in $V$ can reduce tool life by ~50–60% — costing thousands in tooling.
4. **Quality Control:** Surface finish (Ra, Rz) is governed primarily by feed and nose radius — critical for aerospace and medical implants.
5. **Energy & Sustainability:** Specific cutting energy × MRR = Power consumed; optimizing these parameters directly reduces **kWh per part** and the carbon footprint of manufacturing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Standard Reference (ISO 3685):** ISO 3685 is the internationally recognized standard for "Tool-life testing with single-point turning tools." It uses **standardised cutting conditions** with $V$, $f$, and $d$ as independent variables.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relationship Between Cutting Speed ($V$) and Spindle Speed ($N$)

**Objective:** Prove the formula $V = \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000}$ from first principles.

### Step 1 — Geometric Setup
Consider a cylindrical workpiece of diameter $D$ mounted on a lathe spindle. The spindle rotates at $N$ revolutions per minute.

The **circumference** of the workpiece (i.e., the path traced by a point on the surface) is:

$$C = \pi D \quad \text{(mm per revolution)}$$

### Step 2 — Linear Distance per Minute
In one revolution, a point on the surface travels a distance equal to the circumference $C$. If the spindle makes $N$ revolutions per minute, the total linear distance traveled per minute is:

$$\text{Distance/min} = C \times N = \pi D N \quad \text{(mm/min)}$$

### Step 3 — Unit Conversion (mm → m)
Since the standard unit of cutting speed in KTU 2024 syllabus is **meters per minute (m/min)**, we divide by 1000 to convert millimeters to meters:

$$V = \frac{\pi D N}{1000} \quad \text{(m/min)} \quad \blacksquare$$

> **Logic Check:** If $D = 50$ mm and $N = 1000$ rpm, then $V = \pi \cdot 50 \cdot 1000 / 1000 = 50\pi \approx 157.08$ m/min. This is a realistic cutting speed for mild steel.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Material Removal Rate (MRR) for Turning

**Objective:** Derive the formula $\text{MRR} = V \cdot f \cdot d$ from geometric reasoning.

### Step 1 — Volume Removed per Revolution
In turning, the tool removes a thin ring of material. For one revolution:
- The tool advances axially by $f$ (feed).
- The tool penetrates radially by $d$ (depth of cut).
- The ring has circumference $\pi D_{avg}$ where $D_{avg}$ is the average diameter.

But a more direct and standard approach: in a single pass from $D_1$ (initial) to $D_2$ (final), the cross-sectional area of the chip is:

$$A_{\text{chip}} = f \cdot d \quad \text{(mm}^2\text{)}$$

### Step 2 — Volume Removed per Unit Time
The cutting edge sweeps past this cross-section at a linear rate of $V$ (m/min). Therefore the volumetric material removed per minute is:

$$\text{MRR} = V \cdot A_{\text{chip}} = V \cdot f \cdot d$$

Accounting for unit consistency (all in mm):

$$\text{MRR} = \frac{\pi D N}{1000} \cdot f \cdot d \quad \text{(mm}^3\text{/min)} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Average Chip Thickness in Turning (with Lead Angle Correction)

When a turning tool with a **side cutting edge angle** (lead angle, $\phi_s$) cuts material, the actual chip thickness differs from the nominal feed.

### Step 1 — Geometry
The uncut chip cross-section is a parallelogram with:
- Width = $b$ (uncut chip width) = $d / \sin\phi_s$
- Nominal thickness = $f \sin\phi_s$

### Step 2 — Average Chip Thickness
By definition, average chip thickness $h_{avg}$ is the area divided by width:

$$h_{avg} = \frac{f \cdot d}{b} = \frac{f \cdot d}{d / \sin\phi_s} = f \sin\phi_s \quad \blacksquare$$

> This derivation is essential for the **merchant circle analysis** (force analysis) covered later in KTU 2024.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Exam Style)

**Problem:** A mild steel workpiece of initial diameter $80$ mm is being turned on a lathe. The final diameter is $76$ mm, and the tool feed is $0.3$ mm/rev. The cutting speed recommended for the operation is $200$ m/min. Calculate:
- (i) Depth of cut $d$
- (ii) Spindle speed $N$
- (iii) Material Removal Rate (MRR)
- (iv) Time required to machine a length of $L = 200$ mm

**Solution:**

**Step (i) — Depth of Cut:**

$$d = \frac{D_1 - D_2}{2} = \frac{80 - 76}{2} = 2 \text{ mm}$$

**Step (ii) — Spindle Speed:**

The cutting speed is given at the **average diameter** (most common convention):

$$D_{avg} = \frac{80 + 76}{2} = 78 \text{ mm}$$

Rearranging the cutting speed formula:

$$N = \frac{1000 \, V}{\pi D_{avg}} = \frac{1000 \times 200}{\pi \times 78} = \frac{200000}{245.04} \approx 816.3 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step (iii) — Material Removal Rate:**

$$\text{MRR} = V \cdot f \cdot d = 200 \times 0.3 \times 2 = 120 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

**Step (iv) — Machining Time:**

Number of revolutions required for length $L$:

$$\text{Revs} = \frac{L}{f} = \frac{200}{0.3} = 666.67 \text{ revolutions}$$

Time:

$$T_m = \frac{\text{Revs}}{N} = \frac{666.67}{816.3} \approx 0.8166 \text{ min} \approx 49 \text{ s} \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note for Students:** Always compute cutting speed at the **average diameter** unless the problem explicitly says "constant surface speed mode" (CNC lathes use G96 which varies $N$ as diameter changes). For conventional lathes, $N$ is constant and $V$ changes as diameter changes.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Cutting Parameter Interactions and Outputs

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Workpiece Material and Geometry] --> B{Choose Operation Type}
    B -->|Turning| C1[Single Point Tool]
    B -->|Milling| C2[Multi Tooth Cutter]
    B -->|Drilling| C3[Twist Drill]
    C1 --> D1[Define V m per min]
    C2 --> D2[Define V m per min and fz mm per tooth]
    C3 --> D3[Define V m per min and vf mm per min]
    D1 --> E[Calculate Spindle Speed N rpm]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D1 --> F[Define Feed f mm per rev]
    D1 --> G[Define Depth of Cut d mm]
    F --> H[Compute Material Removal Rate MRR]
    G --> H
    D1 --> H
    H --> I[Output Process Outputs]
    I --> I1[Machining Time]
    I --> I2[Cutting Power]
    I --> I3[Surface Finish]
    I --> I4[Tool Life Estimate]
    E --> I1
    H --> I2
    F --> I3
    D1 --> I4
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Cutting Parameter Influence Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Cutting Speed V] --> O1[Cutting Temperature]
    P1 --> O2[Tool Wear Rate]
    P1 --> O3[Tool Life]
    P2[Feed f] --> O3
    P2 --> O4[Surface Finish Ra]
    P2 --> O5[Cutting Force Fc]
    P3[Depth of Cut d] --> O5
    P3 --> O6[Material Removal Rate MRR]
    P1 --> O6
    P2 --> O6
    O1 --> O2
    O2 --> O3
    O5 --> O7[Power Consumption Pc]
    O6 --> O7
    O4 --> Q1[Quality of Finished Part]
    O3 --> Q1
    O7 --> Q2[Cost per Component]
    O6 --> Q2
```

---

## 4.3 Schematic Description of Cutting Geometry (Verbal Block Diagram)

Since a true isometric drawing of a chip is difficult to render in Mermaid, the following block diagram describes the cutting geometry used in derivations:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Workpiece Rotating at N rpm] --> B[Cutting Edge of Tool]
    B --> C[Chip Flows Over Rake Face]
    C --> D[Shear Plane in Work Material]
    B --> E[Uncut Chip Thickness t1 equals f sin phi]
    B --> F[Uncut Chip Width b equals d by sin phi]
    B --> G[Chip Thickness t2 after shear]
    D --> H[Shear Angle phi Merchant Equation]
    H --> I[Cutting Force Fc and Thrust Force Ft]
    I --> J[Power P equals Fc times V]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** If asked to "draw a neat sketch" in the exam, the **Merchant force circle diagram** (which uses the chip geometry above) is one of the most frequently requested Module 3 figures. Practice drawing it with proper labels: $V$, $V_s$ (shear velocity), $V_c$ (chip velocity), $\phi$ (shear angle), $\alpha$ (rake angle), and $F$ forces.

---

## 4.4 Interrelationship Summary Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Primary Cutting Parameters] --> B[Cutting Speed V]
    A --> C[Feed f]
    A --> D[Depth of Cut d]
    B --> E[Critical: Largest Influence on Tool Life]
    C --> F[Moderate Influence on Tool Life]
    D --> G[Smallest Influence on Tool Life]
    B --> H[MRR Calculation]
    C --> H
    D --> H
    H --> I[Power Required]
    H --> J[Time Required]
    H --> K[Cost Optimization]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define cutting speed, feed, and depth of cut. State their standard units for a turning operation.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Cutting Speed ($V$):** The relative tangential velocity between the cutting tool and the workpiece surface at the point of contact. It represents the speed at which the material passes the cutting edge. For turning, $V = \pi D N / 1000$ in **m/min**.
- **Feed ($f$):** The distance moved by the tool relative to the workpiece per spindle revolution. Standard unit: **mm/rev**.
- **Depth of Cut ($d$):** The radial thickness of material removed from the workpiece in a single pass. Standard unit: **mm**.

These three parameters together determine the **Material Removal Rate (MRR)** and govern the chip geometry, cutting forces, power, and tool life of the operation. *(3 marks)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Why is cutting speed considered the most influential parameter on tool life among $V$, $f$, and $d$?**

**Model Answer:**

Cutting speed directly governs the **cutting temperature** at the tool-chip interface. The Taylor tool life equation (covered in the next module) shows that tool life varies **inversely and exponentially** with cutting speed ($V^{1/n}$ where $n \approx 5$ to 8). A small increase in $V$ produces a disproportionately large rise in tool wear.

In contrast, depth of cut has only a **linear** effect on tool life, and feed has a moderate effect (between the two). Therefore, the hierarchy of influence is: **Cutting Speed > Feed > Depth of Cut**. This is why CNC programmers must select $V$ carefully based on tool material and workpiece material. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern Reminder:** Each Module in the End Semester Examination (ESE) is allotted 14 marks. KTU 2024 Scheme uses **Internal Choice** — students answer **one** of the two questions. Sub-parts are typically (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Sample Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **three primary cutting parameters** in a turning operation. Derive the expression for **cutting speed** in terms of workpiece diameter and spindle speed. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A cylindrical workpiece of diameter $100$ mm is reduced to $94$ mm in one pass on a lathe. The spindle rotates at $400$ rpm and the tool feed is $0.25$ mm/rev. Calculate:
- (i) Cutting speed (m/min)
- (ii) Material Removal Rate (mm³/min)
- (iii) Time required to machine a length of $150$ mm *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to A(a):

**Step 1 — Definition of the three parameters:**

- **Cutting Speed ($V$):** Tangential surface speed at the cutting edge.
- **Feed ($f$):** Axial tool advance per revolution.
- **Depth of Cut ($d$):** Radial material thickness removed.

**Step 2 — Sketch (description):** Draw a cylindrical workpiece mounted between chuck and tailstock, with a single-point tool engaging the surface. Label:
- Workpiece diameter $D$
- Tool advancing axially by $f$ per revolution
- Tool radial penetration $= d$
- Chip flowing over the rake face

**Step 3 — Derivation of $V$:**

$$C = \pi D \quad \text{(mm per rev)}$$

$$V_{mm/min} = \pi D N$$

$$V_{m/min} = \frac{\pi D N}{1000} \quad \blacksquare$$

*(Total 7 marks: 2 for definitions, 2 for sketch, 3 for derivation)*

#### Model Solution to A(b):

**Step 1 — Average Diameter:**

$$D_{avg} = \frac{100 + 94}{2} = 97 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Cutting Speed:**

$$V = \frac{\pi D_{avg} N}{1000} = \frac{\pi \times 97 \times 400}{1000} = \frac{121,889.7}{1000} \approx 121.89 \text{ m/min}$$

**Step 3 — Depth of Cut:**

$$d = \frac{100 - 94}{2} = 3 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — MRR:**

$$\text{MRR} = V \times f \times d = 121.89 \times 0.25 \times 3 \approx 91.42 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

**Step 5 — Machining Time:**

Number of revolutions for $L = 150$ mm:

$$\text{Revs} = \frac{L}{f} = \frac{150}{0.25} = 600 \text{ revs}$$

$$T_m = \frac{600}{400} = 1.5 \text{ min} = 90 \text{ seconds} \quad \blacksquare$$

*(Total 7 marks: 1 setup, 2 cutting speed, 2 MRR, 2 time)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Sample Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Differentiate between **cutting speed** and **feed**. Explain how each of them affects **surface finish**, **cutting force**, and **tool life** in a turning operation. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** In a turning operation, the cutting speed is $180$ m/min, feed is $0.4$ mm/rev, and depth of cut is $2$ mm. If the workpiece diameter changes from $60$ mm to $54$ mm, calculate:
- (i) Spindle speed (in rpm) before and after the cut (assume constant spindle speed — find $N$ at average diameter).
- (ii) MRR.
- (iii) Average uncut chip thickness if the tool has a side cutting edge angle (lead angle) of $15°$. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to B(a):

| Aspect | Cutting Speed ($V$) | Feed ($f$) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Tangential surface speed | Axial tool advance per rev |
| Unit | m/min | mm/rev |
| Affects Surface Finish? | Slightly | **Directly — Major Effect** |
| Affects Cutting Force? | Moderately (via temp) | **Exponentially** ($F_c \propto f^{0.75}$) |
| Affects Tool Life? | **Strongest** influence | Moderate influence |

- **Surface Finish:** Higher feed → rougher surface (lower $R_a$).
- **Cutting Force:** Higher $V$ → lower force (shear angle increases); higher $f$ → higher force.
- **Tool Life:** Higher $V$ → drastic reduction (Taylor's law); higher $f$ → moderate reduction.

*(7 marks: 2 table, 5 explanation)*

#### Model Solution to B(b):

**Step 1 — Average Diameter:**

$$D_{avg} = \frac{60 + 54}{2} = 57 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Spindle Speed:**

$$N = \frac{1000 \times 180}{\pi \times 57} = \frac{180000}{179.07} \approx 1005.2 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 3 — MRR:**

$$\text{MRR} = V \times f \times d = 180 \times 0.4 \times 2 = 144 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

**Step 4 — Average Uncut Chip Thickness:**

$$h_{avg} = f \sin\phi_s = 0.4 \times \sin 15° = 0.4 \times 0.2588 = 0.1035 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{h_{avg} \approx 0.1035 \text{ mm}} \quad \blacksquare$$

*(Total 7 marks: 2 spindle speed, 2 MRR, 3 chip thickness)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Losing Mistakes on Cutting Parameter Problems:**
>
> 1. **Unit Mismatch in Cutting Speed:** Forgetting the factor **$1000$** and writing $V = \pi D N$ without unit conversion. This loses **1 mark** immediately.
> 2. **Wrong Diameter for $N$:** Using initial diameter $D_1$ instead of average diameter $D_{avg}$ for cutting speed calculation. Always state the assumption clearly.
> 3. **MRR Confusion:** Writing MRR in cm³/min or m³/min without conversion. KTU expects **mm³/min** consistently.
> 4. **Feed Direction Confusion:** Confusing feed per rev (turning) with feed per tooth (milling) and feed per minute (drilling). Read the problem statement carefully.
> 5. **Tool Wear Hierarchy:** Stating that *depth of cut* is the most influential parameter on tool life. This is **incorrect** — Cutting Speed is.
> 6. **Skipping Sketch:** For sub-parts that say "with neat sketch," even a basic labeled diagram fetches **at least 2 marks** (out of 7) in KTU valuation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Save this section for the night before the exam. It contains the absolute essentials.**

- [x] **Cutting Speed ($V$):** $V = \pi D N / 1000$ m/min. Most influential on tool life.
- [x] **Feed ($f$):** mm/rev (turning), mm/tooth (milling), mm/min (drilling).
- [x] **Depth of Cut ($d$):** $d = (D_1 - D_2)/2$ in turning. Least influential on tool life.
- [x] **MRR (Turning):** $\text{MRR} = V \cdot f \cdot d$ — always in **mm³/min**.
- [x] **Hierarchy of Influence on Tool Life:** $V > f > d$.
- [x] **Hierarchy of Influence on Surface Roughness:** $f > V > d$ (feed dominates finish).
- [x] **Average Diameter Convention:** Use $D_{avg} = (D_1 + D_2)/2$ unless G96 is specified.
- [x] **Uncut Chip Thickness:** $h_{avg} = f \sin\phi_s$ for turning with lead angle.
- [x] **MRR (Milling):** $\text{MRR} = v_f \cdot a_p \cdot a_e$ where $v_f = f_z \cdot z \cdot N$.
- [x] **MRR (Drilling):** $\text{MRR} = (\pi D^2/4) \cdot v_f$ for solid drills.
- [x] **Conversion:** 1 m/min = 1000 mm/min. Always keep units consistent.
- [x] **Time Formula:** $T_m = L / (f \cdot N)$ for turning; = $L / v_f$ for drilling/milling.
- [x] **Master Taylor's Equation next:** $V T^n = C$ — the natural extension of this module.
- [x] **Practical Rule of Thumb:** Doubling $V$ reduces tool life by a factor of **32** (for $n = 0.2$, exponent = 5).
- [x] **Industrial Standard:** ISO 3685 governs single-point turning tool life tests.

---

**End of Module 3 Notes — Cutting Parameters: Speed, Feed, and Depth of Cut**
*Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned), Revised Bloom's Taxonomy cognitive levels (Remember → Apply → Analyze), and ESE valuation patterns.*
