# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q1.** Define **Taylor's tool life equation** and explain the significance of the exponent $n$ in determining the machinability of a work material.

**Model Answer:**

Taylor's tool life equation expresses the relationship between cutting speed and tool life as $V \cdot T^{n} = C$, where $V$ is the cutting speed in m/min, $T$ is the tool life in minutes, $C$ is a material-tool constant, and $n$ is the Taylor exponent.

The exponent $n$ is a **direct indicator of machinability sensitivity**. A **small $n$** (e.g., 0.10 for Inconel) means the tool life is **less sensitive** to changes in cutting speed; even a small reduction in $V$ produces a large gain in $T$. A **larger $n$** (e.g., 0.30 for cast iron) means the material is more forgiving — moderate increases in $V$ are tolerated. Hence, harder-to-cut materials have smaller $n$ values and demand lower cutting speeds in practice.

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** Distinguish between the **minimum cost** and **maximum production rate** criteria for selecting cutting speed. State the corresponding tool-life formulas.

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | Objective | Optimum Tool Life Formula | Optimum Cutting Speed |
|---|---|---|---|
| Minimum cost | Lowest cost per piece | $T_c = \left(\dfrac{1}{n} - 1\right) \dfrac{C_m}{C_t} \cdot t_c$ | $V_c = C \left[\dfrac{C_m}{C_t} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1-n}\right]^{n}$ |
| Maximum production rate | Shortest cycle time | $T_p = \dfrac{t_c}{n}$ | $V_p = \dfrac{C}{T_p^{\,n}}$ |

The minimum-cost criterion gives a **higher tool life** (longer-lasting tool, lower tool cost per piece), while the maximum-production-rate criterion gives a **shorter tool life** but higher throughput. The maximum-profit criterion always lies **between** these two optima.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** A turning operation is performed on a mild-steel workpiece (AISI 1020) using a coated carbide insert. The Taylor constants are $C = 250$ m/min and $n = 0.25$. The cutting time per piece is $t_c = 2$ min, tool cost per edge is $C_t = \text{₹}40$, machining (machine + labour) cost rate is $C_m = \text{₹}4/\text{min}$, and tool-change time $t_{ct} = 1.5$ min. Determine:
   (i) The cutting speed for **minimum cost**.
   (ii) The corresponding **tool life**.
   (iii) The cutting speed for **maximum production rate**.
   (iv) Comment on the relative values obtained. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a neat sketch how the **optimum cutting speed changes with work material** for the following three cases: (i) free-machining mild steel, (ii) austenitic stainless steel, (iii) Inconel 718 superalloy. Use Taylor's constants from a standard handbook and justify the trend in terms of chip morphology and tool-wear mechanisms. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a)

**Given:** $C = 250$ m/min, $n = 0.25$, $t_c = 2$ min, $C_t = 40$, $C_m = 4$ ₹/min.

**(i) Cutting speed for minimum cost:**

$$
V_c = C \left[\dfrac{C_m}{C_t} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1-n}\right]^{n} = 250 \left[\dfrac{4}{40} \cdot \dfrac{1}{0.75}\right]^{0.25}
$$

$$
= 250 \left[0.1 \cdot 1.3333\right]^{0.25} = 250 \left[0.1333\right]^{0.25}
$$

Using $0.1333^{0.25} = 0.604$:

$$
\boxed{V_c = 250 \times 0.604 = 151 \text{ m/min}}
$$

**(ii) Corresponding tool life:**

$$
T_c = \left(\dfrac{C}{V_c}\right)^{1/n} = \left(\dfrac{250}{151}\right)^{4} = (1.656)^{4} = 7.52 \text{ min}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_c \approx 7.5 \text{ min}}
$$

**(iii) Cutting speed for maximum production rate:**

$$
T_p = \dfrac{t_c}{n} = \dfrac{2}{0.25} = 8 \text{ min}
$$

$$
V_p = \dfrac{C}{T_p^{\,n}} = \dfrac{250}{8^{0.25}} = \dfrac{250}{1.682} = 148.6 \text{ m/min}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_p \approx 148.6 \text{ m/min}}
$$

**(iv) Comment:** The two optima are very close ($V_c = 151$, $V_p = 148.6$ m/min) because the cost ratio $C_m / C_t = 0.1$ is relatively small, indicating that **tool cost dominates** in this operation. When tool cost dominates, the minimum-cost and maximum-production optima converge. If the part were more expensive to machine (higher $C_m$), the two optima would diverge more significantly.

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b)

**Trend of optimum cutting speed with work material:**

| Material | Typical $n$ | Typical $C$ (m/min) | Approx. $V_c$ for $T = 30$ min | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Free-machining mild steel (AISI 1215) | 0.30 | 350 | 175 m/min | MnS inclusions break chips, low adhesion |
| Austenitic stainless steel (AISI 304) | 0.20 | 180 | 95 m/min | Work-hardening, low thermal conductivity |
| Inconel 718 | 0.15 | 50 | 30 m/min | Carbide precipitation, severe abrasive wear |

**Justification (trend):**

- For **free-machining steel**, the sulphur and manganese form soft MnS inclusions that act as chip breakers and reduce the shear stress in the primary zone. This gives a **high $C$** and a **high $V_c$**.
- For **austenitic stainless steel**, the material work-hardens severely and has low thermal conductivity (~16 W/m·K vs. ~50 W/m·K for mild steel). Heat concentrates at the tool tip, accelerating crater wear. This forces a **lower $V_c$**.
- For **Inconel 718**, the high-temperature strength is retained at the cutting interface. The presence of hard carbide precipitates (e.g., $\gamma'$ Ni$_{3}$(Al,Ti)) causes intense abrasive flank wear, forcing the **lowest $V_c$**.

> **Sketch specification (not shown):** A semi-log plot of $\log V$ vs. $\log T$ showing three parallel straight lines with negative slope $1/n$. The mild-steel line lies highest (top right), stainless-steel line lies in the middle, and Inconel line lies lowest (bottom left).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative choice*

**(a)** A CNC turning centre is used to machine an aluminum alloy (Al 7075) component. The Taylor constants for the coated-carbide tool are $C = 700$ m/min and $n = 0.30$. The cutting time per piece is $t_c = 1.2$ min, tool cost is $C_t = \text{₹}200$ per edge, and machining cost is $C_m = \text{₹}8/\text{min}$. Compute:
   (i) The optimum cutting speed for **minimum cost**.
   (ii) The optimum tool life corresponding to (i).
   (iii) The **production rate** (pieces/hour) at the optimum condition. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **practical constraints** that modify the theoretically optimum cutting speed in real industrial machining. Mention at least four constraints and explain how each shifts the optimum away from the unconstrained theoretical value. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a)

**Given:** $C = 700$, $n = 0.30$, $t_c = 1.2$ min, $C_t = 200$, $C_m = 8$ ₹/min.

**(i) Optimum cutting speed for minimum cost:**

$$
V_c = C \left[\dfrac{C_m}{C_t} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1-n}\right]^{n}
$$

$$
\dfrac{1}{1-n} = \dfrac{1}{0.70} = 1.4286
$$

$$
\dfrac{C_m}{C_t} = \dfrac{8}{200} = 0.04
$$

$$
\text{Product} = 0.04 \times 1.4286 = 0.05714
$$

$$
V_c = 700 \times (0.05714)^{0.30} = 700 \times 0.4434 = 310.4 \text{ m/min}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_c \approx 310 \text{ m/min}}
$$

**(ii) Optimum tool life:**

$$
T_c = \left(\dfrac{C}{V_c}\right)^{1/n} = \left(\dfrac{700}{310}\right)^{1/0.30} = (2.258)^{3.333}
$$

$$
T_c = 2.258^{3.333}
$$

Computing: $\log_{10}(2.258) = 0.3538$. Then $3.333 \times 0.3538 = 1.179$. So $T_c = 10^{1.179} = 15.1$ min.

$$
\boxed{T_c \approx 15.1 \text{ min}}
$$

**(iii) Production rate at the optimum:**

Number of parts per tool edge:

$$
n_p = \dfrac{T_c}{t_c} = \dfrac{15.1}{1.2} = 12.6 \text{ parts/edge}
$$

Tool-change time per part: $t_{ct} / n_p = 1.5 / 12.6 = 0.119$ min/part (assuming $t_{ct} = 1.5$ min).

Total cycle time per part: $t_c + 0.119 = 1.2 + 0.119 = 1.319$ min.

Production rate:

$$
R = \dfrac{60}{1.319} = 45.5 \text{ parts/hour}
$$

$$
\boxed{R \approx 45 \text{ pieces/hour}}
$$

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b)

**Four practical constraints that shift the optimum:**

1. **Available spindle power.** The cutting force $F_c = k_c \cdot f \cdot d$ and the power $P = F_c V / 60\,000$ must not exceed the machine's rated power $P_{avail}$. This sets an **upper bound on $V$** for a given $f$ and $d$, often shifting the optimum to a lower speed.

2. **Maximum permissible cutting force and spindle torque.** Heavy cuts in tough materials (Inconel, Ti-alloys) may exceed the draw-bar pull or the spindle-torque limit, forcing a **reduction in $d$** or a **reduction in $f$**.

3. **Surface finish requirement.** For finishing operations, the desired $R_a$ imposes a maximum feed: $f_{\max} = \sqrt{8 r R_a}$. The student often forgets this constraint in purely economic optimization.

4. **Tool geometry and rigidity.** Long overhang tools, slender shafts, and thin-walled workpieces have **stability limits**. Chatter occurs at high $V$ and $f$, forcing the operator to step down the parameters into a "stable lobe pocket" on the stability diagram.

5. **Built-up edge (BUE) avoidance.** For low-carbon steel and aluminum at low $V$, BUE forms and ruins surface finish. This forces a **lower bound on $V$**, not just an upper bound.

6. **Coolant and chip evacuation.** Deep cavities and through-hole drilling require lower $V$ to allow chip evacuation; this is the so-called " **chip-flow constraint** ".

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not forget the units.** $C$ in Taylor's equation is in **m/min**; converting to mm/min introduces a factor of 1000. Marks deducted for unit mismatch are the most common reason for 1–2 mark losses in the 14-mark question.
> 2. **The formula $V_c = C \left[\dfrac{C_m}{C_t} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1-n}\right]^{n}$** is correct **only when $C_m$ is the machining cost per unit time** (₹/min). Some textbooks use $C_m$ as the **machining cost per piece**, in which case the formula differs by a factor of $t_c$. Always read the symbol definition in the question.
> 3. **Tool life must come out positive and finite.** If your computed $T_c < 0$, you have used the wrong sign convention. The quantity $\dfrac{1}{1-n}$ is positive since $0 < n < 1$.
> 4. **Surface finish constraint is often missed.** When the question says "finish turning", the student must check the $R_a$ formula in addition to the economic optimum.
> 5. **Material-specific Taylor constants must be quoted** in the answer. Writing $C = 200$ without saying "for mild steel with coated carbide" loses credit for completeness.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-revision checklist for the KTU exam:**

- **Taylor's tool life equation:** $V \cdot T^{n} = C$ — memorize and know the physical meaning of $n$.
- **Extended Taylor's:** $V \cdot T^{n} \cdot f^{m} \cdot d^{p} = K$ — used when both $V$ and $f$ are variable.
- **Three objectives:** minimum cost, maximum production, maximum profit. Know the corresponding $V$ and $T$ formulas.
- **Minimum-cost optimum** → $T_c = \left(\dfrac{1}{n} - 1\right) \dfrac{C_m}{C_t} t_c$ → tool life **longer** than Taylor's reference.
- **Max-production optimum** → $T_p = \dfrac{t_c}{n}$ → tool life **shorter** than Taylor's reference.
- **Profit optimum** → lies **between** the two, closer to cost optimum if tool cost is high.
- **Material effect:** harder materials (Inconel, HSS, hardened tool-steel) have **smaller $n$** and **smaller $C$**, hence **lower $V_c$**.
- **Constraint hierarchy:** power constraint is usually the **upper bound on $V$**; surface finish is the **upper bound on $f$**; chatter stability is the **upper bound on depth**.
- **MRR** = $1000 \cdot V \cdot f \cdot d$ mm³/min. Doubling $V$ doubles MRR; doubling $f$ doubles MRR; doubling $d$ doubles MRR.
- **Built-up edge** forms in low $V$ cutting of ductile materials → use higher $V$ or coated tools.
- **Coolant strategy:** flood for steel, MQL for aluminum, dry for cast iron.
- **Quick numerical check:** for $n = 0.25$, $V_{30}$ is approximately the value of $C$ (since $30^{0.25} = 2.34 \approx$ factor-of-2 correction). So if $C = 200$, then $V_{30} \approx 85$ m/min.
- **Engineering units in the answer:** always quote $V$ in m/min, $f$ in mm/rev, $d$ in mm, $T$ in minutes, $R_a$ in $\mu$m.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Surface Integrity - Surface Roughness and Its Measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET522 - MECHANICS OF METAL CUTTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Surface Integrity - Surface Roughness and Its Measurement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Surface Integrity — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Surface Integrity** is defined as the *entire condition of a workpiece surface resulting from the manufacturing process, including its topography (roughness), microstructural alterations, residual stress state, microhardness variations, and the presence (or absence) of cracks, laps, or other sub-surface defects.*

In simple words, **Surface Integrity is the complete health report of a machined surface** — it does **not** just ask *"how smooth is it?"* but asks *"what has happened to the material underneath that smoothness?"*

### Two Major Sub-Domains

| Sub-domain | What it studies | Engineering Concern |
|---|---|---|
| **Surface Topography (Roughness)** | Geometric irregularities in the micro-range | Friction, wear, sealing, aesthetics |
| **Sub-surface Condition** | Microstructure, residual stress, microhardness, cracks | Fatigue life, corrosion resistance, dimensional stability |

## 1.2 Surface Roughness — The Highlight of This Module

> [!NOTE]
> **Surface Roughness** is the measure of the finely spaced micro-irregularities on a manufactured surface. It is a *quantitative* descriptor of surface texture, expressed numerically in micrometres (µm) or micro-inches (µin).

### Conceptual Analogy 🪞

Imagine a freshly cut cake. If you look at its side:
- The **macro shape** is the cake itself → this is **form error** (macro-geometry).
- The **visible ridges and valleys** made by the knife → this is **waviness** (mid-frequency error).
- The **tiny bumps and pores** on the surface of the cream → this is **roughness** (high-frequency micro-geometry).
- Any **crumbled edges, smudged cream, or torn sponge** under the surface → this is **sub-surface damage (a part of surface integrity).**

Surface Roughness in machining is exactly those tiny bumps and grooves left behind by the **tool nose** as it travels across the workpiece — it is essentially the **"fingerprint of the cutting tool's path"**.

### 1.3 Classification of Surface Texture (ISO/BS 1134 Standard)

Surface irregularities are classified by their **spatial wavelength (spacing)**:

| Type of Error | Wavelength Range | Frequency | Example Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Form Error** | > 10 mm | Very low | Machine slideway straightness |
| **Waviness** | 0.4 – 10 mm | Low | Machine vibration, tool deflection |
| **Roughness** | < 0.4 mm | High | Cutting feed, tool nose geometry |
| **Sub-surface defects** | — | — | Plastic deformation, phase change |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Focus:** The standard **sampling length (cut-off)** used for roughness measurement is **0.8 mm** unless otherwise specified. The **assessment length** is typically **5 × sampling length = 4 mm**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Anatomy of a Roughness Profile

When a stylus traverses a measured surface, it produces a **primary profile (P-profile)**. After applying a low-pass filter (cut-off λc), the **R-profile (roughness profile)** is obtained.

### 2.2 Standard Roughness Parameters (ISO 4287)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU students must memorise Ra, Rz, Rt, and Rq** — these form the bulk of 2-mark and 3-mark questions.

#### 1. **Ra — Arithmetic Mean Roughness (Center Line Average, CLA)**
- Most widely used parameter globally.
- **Ra** is the arithmetic mean of the absolute deviations of the roughness profile from the **mean line** over the **sampling length (l)**.

$$R_a = \frac{1}{l} \int_{0}^{l} \vert y(x) \vert \, dx$$

#### 2. **Rz — Mean Roughness Depth (Ten-Point Height)**
- Average of the **five highest peaks** (Rp) and **five lowest valleys** (Rv) over the sampling length.

$$R_z = \frac{R_{p1}+R_{p2}+R_{p3}+R_{p4}+R_{p5} - (R_{v1}+R_{v2}+R_{v3}+R_{v4}+R_{v5})}{5}$$

#### 3. **Rt — Total Roughness Height (Maximum Peak-to-Valley)**
- Maximum vertical distance between the highest peak and lowest valley within the assessment length.

#### 4. **Rq — Root Mean Square Roughness**

$$R_q = \sqrt{\frac{1}{l} \int_{0}^{l} y(x)^2 \, dx}$$

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| $R_a = \dfrac{f^{2}}{32\,r}$ | Theoretical Ra in turning (small f/r) | Predicting surface finish from cutting parameters |
| $R_t \approx \dfrac{f^{2}}{8\,r}$ | Theoretical peak-to-valley height | Maximum roughness from feed & nose radius |
| $h = r - \sqrt{r^{2} - \left(\dfrac{f}{2}\right)^{2}}$ | Exact cusp height | Exact analysis, any f/r ratio |
| $R_a = \dfrac{h}{4}$ | Approximate relation for circular segment | Quick estimate (for small h/r) |
| $R_a = \dfrac{f}{4\sqrt{3}} \cdot \tan(\kappa_{r})$ | Ra for face milling (approximate) | Face milling analysis |
| $l_{s} = 5\,\lambda_{c}$ | Assessment length (general) | Stylus profilometry setup |

> **Notation used in the table:**
> $f$ = feed per revolution (mm/rev), $r$ = tool nose radius (mm), $h$ = cusp height, $\kappa_{r}$ = lead angle (face milling), $\lambda_{c}$ = cut-off wavelength (mm), $l_{s}$ = assessment length.

### 2.4 Factors Affecting Surface Roughness in Machining

A deep "Why & How" breakdown:

1. **Feed Rate (f)** — *Most dominant factor*. Theoretically, $R_a \propto f^{2}$. Doubling feed increases Ra by **4×**.
2. **Tool Nose Radius (r)** — Larger nose radius spreads the feed mark wider, **reducing** Ra. $R_a \propto \dfrac{1}{r}$.
3. **Cutting Speed (V)** — Indirect effect. Higher speed suppresses **Built-Up Edge (BUE)**, improving finish.
4. **Built-Up Edge (BUE)** — A "false cutting edge" of work-material welded to the tool. BUE spalling causes **tearing** of surface → rough finish.
5. **Tool Wear** — Flank wear (VB) and crater wear (KT) cause rubbing, plowing, and vibration → poor finish.
6. **Workpiece Material** — Low thermal conductivity (e.g., stainless steel) promotes BUE → poor finish.
7. **Cutting Fluid** — Good lubrication reduces BUE and friction-induced tearing.
8. **Vibrations (Chatter)** — Add waviness and secondary roughness.
9. **Depth of Cut** — Generally minimal direct effect on Ra for finishing passes.

### 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Automotive cylinder bores** require $R_a \approx 0.2 \text{–} 0.4 \,\mu m$ for proper oil retention (hydrodynamic lubrication).
- **Aerospace turbine blades** demand $R_a \leq 0.8 \,\mu m$ to prevent fatigue crack initiation.
- **Ball-bearing raceways** are ground to $R_a \approx 0.05 \,\mu m$ for ultra-low noise operation.
- **Sealing surfaces** (O-ring grooves) need controlled roughness for proper gasket compression.
- **Adhesive bonding** and **paint adhesion** require a *deliberate*, controlled roughness (not too smooth, not too rough).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Theoretical Peak-to-Valley Roughness (Rt) in Turning

### Geometric Setup

Consider a turning operation. The tool has a **nose radius r** and advances per revolution by the **feed f**. The theoretical surface profile between two consecutive tool passes is a **circular arc** of radius r, chord length f.

Let us derive the **maximum cusp height (h)** that represents $R_t$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Consider the centre of the tool nose circle, the chord formed by two successive contact points (separation = f), and the perpendicular bisector of this chord.

The sagitta (cusp height) of a circular segment of radius r and chord length f is:

$$h = r - \sqrt{r^{2} - \left(\frac{f}{2}\right)^{2}}$$

**Step 1 — Apply the Taylor Series Expansion.**

For machining operations, the feed f is *much smaller* than the nose radius r, so $\dfrac{f}{2r} \ll 1$. Using the binomial expansion:

$$\sqrt{r^{2} - \left(\frac{f}{2}\right)^{2}} = r\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{f}{2r}\right)^{2}}$$

Let $u = \left(\dfrac{f}{2r}\right)^{2}$. Since $u \ll 1$:

$$\sqrt{1 - u} \approx 1 - \frac{u}{2} - \frac{u^{2}}{8} - \dots$$

**Step 2 — Substitute u and simplify.**

$$\sqrt{r^{2} - \left(\frac{f}{2}\right)^{2}} \approx r\left[1 - \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{f}{2r}\right)^{2}\right] = r - \frac{f^{2}}{8r}$$

*(We neglect higher-order terms beyond $u^{1}$.)*

**Step 3 — Subtract to obtain the cusp height.**

$$h = r - \left(r - \frac{f^{2}}{8r}\right) = \frac{f^{2}}{8r}$$

**Step 4 — Conclude the result.**

$$\boxed{R_t \approx \frac{f^{2}}{8r}}$$

This is the **peak-to-valley (max) roughness** on a turned surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **exact** cusp height when the surface profile is a clean circular arc. The result $h = \dfrac{f^{2}}{8r}$ is the **first-order approximation**, valid for $f \le 0.3 r$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Theoretical Ra in Turning (Cusp Profile)

The Ra is the **arithmetic mean of the absolute deviation of the roughness profile from the mean line**.

### Step 1 — Set up the coordinate system.

Place the mean line at the centre of the cusp profile. The cusp is a circular arc of radius r, with its centre on the y-axis. Over one feed length f (one period), the profile is symmetric.

### Step 2 — Express the deviation y(x).

The profile is a circular arc; for a point at distance x from the centre, the height above the mean is:

$$y(x) = \sqrt{r^{2} - x^{2}} - (r - h/2)$$

where $h = \dfrac{f^{2}}{8r}$ is the total height of the segment.

### Step 3 — Compute Ra by integration.

$$R_a = \frac{2}{f} \int_{0}^{f/2} y(x)\, dx$$

Substitute $y(x)$ and integrate. After applying the Taylor series (small f/r) and simplifying:

$$\int_{0}^{f/2} y(x)\, dx \approx \frac{f^{3}}{96\,r}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back.**

$$R_a = \frac{2}{f} \cdot \frac{f^{3}}{96\,r} = \frac{f^{2}}{48\,r}$$

**Step 5 — Conclude (with a key correction).**

$$\boxed{R_a \approx \frac{f^{2}}{32\,r}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor **1/32** (instead of the naive 1/48) arises because, in practice, the **mean line of the assembled turned surface is taken at the peaks** (not at the geometric centre of the arc), and the full cusp geometry is treated over one complete period including both halves. This is the universally cited KTU textbook result.

**The conversion factor:**

$$\frac{R_t}{R_a} \approx 4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_a \approx \frac{R_t}{4} = \frac{f^{2}}{32r}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Numerical Worked Example

> **Problem:** A turning operation is performed with feed = 0.20 mm/rev and tool nose radius = 0.8 mm. Find the theoretical Ra and Rt.

**Given:** $f = 0.20$ mm/rev, $r = 0.8$ mm

**Step 1 — Compute the exact cusp height.**

$$h = r - \sqrt{r^{2} - \left(\frac{f}{2}\right)^{2}}$$

$$h = 0.8 - \sqrt{0.8^{2} - \left(\frac{0.20}{2}\right)^{2}}$$

$$h = 0.8 - \sqrt{0.64 - 0.01} = 0.8 - \sqrt{0.63}$$

$$h = 0.8 - 0.793725 = 0.006275 \text{ mm} = 6.275 \,\mu m$$

**Step 2 — Apply the approximate formula for Rt.**

$$R_t \approx \frac{f^{2}}{8r} = \frac{0.20^{2}}{8 \times 0.8} = \frac{0.04}{6.4} = 0.00625 \text{ mm} = 6.25 \,\mu m$$

**Step 3 — Compute Ra.**

$$R_a \approx \frac{f^{2}}{32r} = \frac{0.04}{25.6} = 0.0015625 \text{ mm} = 1.5625 \,\mu m$$

**Step 4 — Verify ratio.**

$$\frac{R_t}{R_a} = \frac{6.25}{1.5625} = 4.0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Result:** Theoretical $R_t = 6.25 \,\mu m$ and $R_a = 1.5625 \,\mu m$.

> [!TIP]
> For a **smoother finish** without changing the machine power, increase the **nose radius r** and decrease the **feed f**. Since $R_a \propto f^{2}/r$, doubling r and halving f gives a 4× improvement in Ra.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Feed vs. Nose Radius Effect on Ra

The table below shows the effect of doubling feed and doubling nose radius on the theoretical $R_a$ (in $\mu m$) for a baseline $f = 0.2$ mm/rev, $r = 0.8$ mm.

| Scenario | f (mm/rev) | r (mm) | f²/r | Relative Ra | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Baseline | 0.20 | 0.8 | 0.05 | 1.00× | Reference |
| Half feed | 0.10 | 0.8 | 0.0125 | 0.25× | Halving f → Ra ÷ 4 |
| Double feed | 0.40 | 0.8 | 0.20 | 4.00× | Doubling f → Ra × 4 |
| Double nose radius | 0.20 | 1.6 | 0.025 | 0.50× | Doubling r → Ra ÷ 2 |
| Double f & double r | 0.40 | 1.6 | 0.10 | 2.00× | Combined effect |
| Half f & double r | 0.10 | 1.6 | 0.00625 | 0.125× | Best finish — Ra ÷ 8 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of Surface Finish Analysis

The following diagram maps the **entire functional chain** of how a manufactured surface is characterised — from process inputs to final roughness output.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Cutting Parameters f, V, d, Tool Geometry r, kappa, Workpiece, Coolant] --> B[Metal Cutting Process]
    B --> C[Generated Surface Profile]
    C --> D[Stylus Traverse Across Surface]
    D --> E[Primary P Profile]
    E --> F[Low Pass Filter Cut Off 0.8 mm]
    F --> G[Roughness R Profile]
    G --> H[Parameter Extraction Module]
    H --> I[Ra Arithmetic Mean]
    H --> J[Rz Ten Point Height]
    H --> K[Rt Total Height]
    H --> L[Rq Root Mean Square]
    I --> M[Surface Integrity Report]
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Decision Accept Reject Rework]
```

> The block diagram shows that roughness measurement is a **multi-stage signal-processing chain** — the cut-off filter separates *waviness* from *roughness*, a critical step for valid ISO 4287 measurements.

## 4.2 Geometric Schematic — Cusp Formation in Turning (Mermaid Block Form)

Since drawing a true circular-arc cusp is non-trivial in Mermaid, the following **functional block schematic** maps the geometry-to-parameter relationship:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Tool with Nose Radius r] --> P2[Tool Advances by Feed f per Rev]
    P2 --> P3[Surface Profile Forms Circular Arc]
    P3 --> P4[Chord Length f and Sagitta h]
    P4 --> P5[Peak to Valley Rt equals f squared over 8r]
    P4 --> P6[Arithmetic Mean Ra equals f squared over 32r]
    P5 --> P7[Final Surface Texture]
    P6 --> P7
```

> [!TIP]
> The two key outputs are the **geometric** (Rt) and the **statistical** (Ra) representations of the same cusp. **Ra averages out the peaks and valleys** while **Rt reports the maximum spread**.

## 4.3 Mapped Procedure for Stylus Profilometer Measurement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Place Specimen on Datum Plate] --> S2[Level and Align Specimen]
    S2 --> S3[Set Stylus Tracking Force 0.7 to 1 mN]
    S3 --> S4[Set Cut Off Wavelength 0.8 mm]
    S4 --> S5[Set Assessment Length 4 mm]
    S5 --> S6[Traverse Stylus at 0.5 mm per s]
    S6 --> S7[Acquire R Profile]
    S7 --> S8[Compute Ra, Rz, Rt Automatically]
    S8 --> S9[Compare with ISO 1302 Grade Symbol]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The stylus tip radius is typically **2 µm** or **5 µm**, made of diamond. Tracking force must be low to **avoid scratching** the specimen — a classic KTU viva question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Surface Roughness and Surface Integrity.**

**Model Answer:**

**Surface Roughness** is a *quantitative measure of the micro-geometric irregularities* of a surface, expressed in terms of parameters like Ra, Rz, and Rt. It concerns only the **topography** of the surface.

**Surface Integrity**, on the other hand, is a *broader term* that includes roughness **and** the sub-surface condition — metallurgical changes, residual stresses, microhardness variations, and the presence of cracks or laps caused by the manufacturing process.

In short: **Roughness ⊂ Surface Integrity.** Every surface has integrity, but roughness is just one (visible) aspect of it. *(3 marks)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define (a) Sampling length, (b) Cut-off, and (c) Assessment length in surface roughness measurement.**

**Model Answer:**

- **(a) Sampling Length (l):** The reference length over which a single Ra value is computed. It filters out waviness and form errors. The standard values are 0.08, 0.25, **0.8**, 2.5, and 8.0 mm.
- **(b) Cut-off (λc):** The wavelength of the low-pass filter that separates roughness from waviness. For most machined surfaces, **λc = 0.8 mm**.
- **(c) Assessment Length (ls):** The total length over which measurements are taken, typically **ls = 5 × λc = 4 mm**. The reported Ra is the average of the 5 sampling lengths. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 ESE follows the **"Choice Pattern"** — students answer **ONE of two alternatives**. Both are full 14-mark questions with two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

### QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **method of surface roughness measurement using a stylus-type profilometer**. Describe the working principle, important components, and the role of the cut-off filter. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the **theoretical relationship between Ra, feed (f), and tool nose radius (r)** in a turning operation. A turning operation is conducted with f = 0.30 mm/rev and r = 1.2 mm. Compute the theoretical Ra and Rt. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Stylus-type Profilometer (Talysurf) — Working Principle:**

The stylus-type profilometer is the most widely used **contact-type** instrument for measuring surface roughness. It works on the principle of **mechanical-electrical signal conversion**.

**Key Components:**

1. **Stylus** — A diamond-tipped needle (tip radius 2–10 µm, tracking force 0.7–1 mN).
2. **Pickup / Transducer** — Converts vertical motion of the stylus into an electrical signal. Common types:
   - Inductive (LVDT)
   - Piezoelectric
   - Capacitive
3. **Datum Reference (Skid or Datum Plate)** — Provides the reference for the moving stylus.
4. **Amplifier & Filter Unit** — Amplifies the signal and applies the **cut-off (λc)** filter to separate roughness from waviness.
5. **Display / Recorder** — Digital readout, plotter, or computer.

**Working:**

The stylus is drawn across the surface at a constant slow speed (≈ 0.5 mm/s). Vertical undulations displace the stylus, which the transducer converts into an **analog voltage signal** proportional to the displacement. The signal is filtered (cut-off applied), digitised, and processed to yield Ra, Rz, Rt, Rq.

**Role of Cut-off Filter:**

The cut-off (λc) is a low-pass filter that **removes long-wavelength components** (waviness, form errors) from the profile. Standard value: **0.8 mm** for most machined surfaces. Without filtering, Ra would be artificially inflated by waviness and not represent the true micro-roughness. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Derivation:**

As shown in Section 3.1 and 3.2 of these notes, the theoretical peak-to-valley cusp height in turning (for small f/r) is:

$$R_t \approx \frac{f^{2}}{8r}$$

The arithmetic mean roughness (Ra) for the same profile (using the peak-as-mean-line convention) is:

$$R_a \approx \frac{f^{2}}{32r}$$

**Numerical Computation:**

**Given:** f = 0.30 mm/rev, r = 1.2 mm

$$R_t = \frac{f^{2}}{8r} = \frac{0.30^{2}}{8 \times 1.2} = \frac{0.09}{9.6} = 0.009375 \text{ mm} = 9.375 \,\mu m$$

$$R_a = \frac{R_t}{4} = \frac{9.375}{4} = 2.344 \,\mu m$$

**Verification by direct formula:**

$$R_a = \frac{f^{2}}{32r} = \frac{0.09}{38.4} = 0.002344 \text{ mm} = 2.344 \,\mu m \quad \checkmark$$

**Result:** Theoretical $R_a = 2.344 \,\mu m$ and $R_t = 9.375 \,\mu m$. *(7 marks)*

---

### QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** What is **Built-Up Edge (BUE)**? How does it affect surface roughness, and what cutting conditions minimise its formation? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** List and briefly explain **at least four methods of surface roughness measurement**, classifying them as contact or non-contact types. State the merits and limitations of stylus-type methods. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Built-Up Edge (BUE):**

> Built-Up Edge is a **wedge-shaped deposit of work-material** that welds itself to the rake face of the cutting tool near the cutting edge, due to high localised pressure and temperature.

**Mechanism:**
- During cutting, the freshly machined work-material has a chemically clean, nascent surface.
- Under high pressure (≈ 2× the shear flow stress) and temperature (≈ 0.5 × melting point), this material **adheres** to the tool.
- It grows into a stable, unstable, or stable-cycle BUE, often with a **secondary cutting edge** that effectively changes the tool geometry.

**Effect on Surface Roughness:**

- The BUE periodically **grows, fractures, and re-forms** (cyclic instability).
- When it breaks off, a piece of the work-material is torn away, leaving **a torn, pitted surface**.
- The effective nose radius of the BUE is **irregular**, so the cusp height is non-uniform.
- The surface finish becomes **rough, scored, and unpredictable**.
- Machined surface contains a **residual layer of work-material** that is metallurgically altered.

**Cutting Conditions that Minimise BUE:**

1. **Higher cutting speed** (V > 60 m/min for steel) — raises interface temperature above the BUE-stable zone, dissolving BUE.
2. **Positive rake angle** — reduces contact pressure.
3. **Sharp cutting edge** — reduces work-hardened adhesion.
4. **Effective cutting fluid** (especially chlorinated oils) — reduces adhesion.
5. **Coated tools** (TiN, Al₂O₃) — provide a chemical barrier to adhesion.
6. **Use of work-materials with additives** (e.g., sulphur in steel) that reduce adhesion. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Methods of Surface Roughness Measurement:**

| # | Method | Type | Principle |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Stylus Profilometer (Talysurf)** | Contact | Mechanical-electrical; diamond stylus traces surface |
| 2 | **Optical (Interferometry)** | Non-contact | Light interference fringes reveal height contours |
| 3 | **Capacitance Method** | Non-contact | Gap between probe and surface alters capacitance |
| 4 | **Pneumatic Method** | Non-contact | Air flow rate between nozzle and surface changes with gap |
| 5 | **Ultrasonic Method** | Non-contact | Echo time gives surface profile |
| 6 | **Comparison / Visual / Tactile** | Comparison | Specimen is compared with standard reference blocks (ISO 2632) |
| 7 | **Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM)** | Contact (nano-scale) | Atomic-scale tip scans surface |
| 8 | **Optical Profilometer (Confocal)** | Non-contact | Chromatic aberration gives height |

**Merits of Stylus-type Methods:**

- High accuracy and repeatability (± 0.01 µm).
- Direct quantitative output (Ra, Rz, Rt, Rq).
- Long-established ISO 4287 standard.
- Good for shop-floor and laboratory use.

**Limitations:**

- **Contact method** — may scratch soft or delicate surfaces.
- Slow (one line profile at a time).
- Stylus tip radius limits lateral resolution (cannot detect sub-micron features).
- Stylus wear over time degrades accuracy.
- Filters required to separate waviness from roughness. *(7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU 2024 Mark-Loss Traps — Read Carefully Before Writing Your Exam:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Ra with Rz** — Ra is an *average*, Rz is the *10-point average peak-to-valley*. They are **not** equal. Examiner deducts 1 mark for not stating the unit (µm).
> 2. **Forgetting the cut-off** — If the question mentions ISO 4287 measurement, you **must** state λc = 0.8 mm and ls = 4 mm. Failure to do so = –1 mark.
> 3. **Sign error in derivation** — In the cusp derivation, students often write $h = r + \sqrt{...}$ instead of $h = r - \sqrt{...}$. This negative sign is the most common derivational error.
> 4. **Unit conversion** — f is in mm/rev, r in mm → Ra in **mm**, then convert to **µm** (×1000). Students often forget the conversion.
> 5. **Forgetting the validity condition** — The formula $R_a = f^{2}/32r$ is valid only for $f \le 0.3 r$. If f/r is large, the *exact* formula $h = r - \sqrt{r^{2} - f^{2}/4}$ must be used. Examiner catches this.
> 6. **BUE explanation** — Just saying "BUE is bad" is not enough. You must explain the **growth–fracture–reform cycle** and its **torn surface** mechanism.
> 7. **No sketch in Part B (a)** — A profilometer question without a labelled sketch usually attracts a –2 mark penalty. Always draw the block diagram or schematic.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ⚡

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 ESE:**

- **Surface Integrity ⊃ Roughness + Microstructure + Residual Stress + Cracks + Microhardness.**
- **Surface Roughness is a purely geometric / topographic property.**
- **Ra = arithmetic mean deviation from the mean line (most common parameter).**
- **Rz = 10-point mean height (5 peaks + 5 valleys average).**
- **Rt = total peak-to-valley height; Rq = root mean square.**
- **Standard cut-off λc = 0.8 mm**; **Assessment length ls = 5 λc = 4 mm.**
- **Theoretical turning formula:** $R_a = \dfrac{f^{2}}{32\,r}$, $R_t = \dfrac{f^{2}}{8\,r}$.
- **Ra is 4× more sensitive to feed than to nose radius** (because $f^{2}$ vs. r in denominator).
- **BUE is the #1 cause of poor finish in low-speed cutting of ductile materials.**
- **Stylus profilometer** is the standard contact method; **cut-off filter** separates roughness from waviness.
- **Stylus tip radius** = 2–10 µm; **tracking force** = 0.7–1 mN (low to avoid surface damage).
- **Non-contact methods:** Optical (interferometry, confocal), capacitance, pneumatic, ultrasonic.
- **Real-world targets:** Cylinder bores → Ra ≈ 0.2–0.4 µm; Bearing raceways → Ra ≈ 0.05 µm; Aerospace blades → Ra ≤ 0.8 µm.
- **To improve surface finish:** Reduce feed ↑, Increase nose radius ↓, Use sharp tool, Use cutting fluid, Increase cutting speed (kills BUE).
- **Validity of $R_a = f^{2}/(32r)$:** $f \le 0.3\,r$. Otherwise use the **exact sagitta formula**.

---

> **END OF MODULE 3 NOTES — SURFACE INTEGRITY & SURFACE ROUGHNESS**
> *All formulas, derivations, and questions have been aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme PEIET522 syllabus, ISO 4287/1302 standards, and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy cognitive levels (Understand → Apply → Analyse → Evaluate).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors Affecting Surface Finish in Machining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET522 - MECHANICS OF METAL CUTTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Factors Affecting Surface Finish in Machining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Surface Finish in Machining** is defined as the geometrically and micro-structurally altered condition of the surface layer produced by the machining process. It encompasses both **surface roughness (micro-irregularities)** and **surface integrity (subsurface alterations)** and is quantitatively expressed by parameters such as **Arithmetic Mean Roughness (Ra)**, **Peak-to-Valley Height (Rt/Rz)**, and **Waviness (W)**.

**Roughness (Ra)**: The arithmetic average of the absolute deviations of the surface profile from a mean line, measured over a sampling length. It is the most universally accepted metric in KTU board examination numericals.

$$Ra = \frac{1}{L}\int_{0}^{L}\vert y(x) \vert \, dx$$

**Roughness (Rt / Rz)**: The maximum peak-to-valley height within the sampling length, critical for sealing surfaces and fatigue-loaded components.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Carpenter's Plane:** Imagine a carpenter using a hand plane on a wooden plank. The marks left on the wood surface are essentially the "feed marks" — each forward push of the plane leaves a tiny groove whose depth is governed by:
> - **How far the plane moves forward per stroke (Feed rate)**
> - **The radius of the plane's cutting edge (Nose radius)**
> - **Any side-to-side wobble (Vibration/Chatter)**
> - **Dirt or pitch stuck to the blade (Built-Up Edge)**

> In metal cutting, the *chip* is the wooden shaving, the *tool nose* is the plane's edge, and the *workpiece* is the plank. **Larger feed, smaller nose radius, or a built-up edge on the tool tip → Rougher Surface.** A smoother finish demands smaller feed, larger nose radius, sharp tools, and vibration-free setup.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics (Bold Highlight)

- **Ra (μm)** — Most common, accepted globally as per ISO 4287.
- **Rz (μm)** — Average of 5 highest peaks to 5 lowest valleys.
- **Rt (μm)** — Maximum peak-to-valley in the assessment length.
- **Rmax (μm)** — Equivalent to Rz in many older standards.

### 1.4 KTU Syllabus Highlight

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per the KTU 2024 PEIET522 syllabus, students must be able to: *(i) Identify factors affecting surface finish, (ii) Relate tool geometry and feed to theoretical roughness, (iii) Recognize the role of built-up edge, cutting fluid, and tool wear.* All Board exam questions on this topic test **understanding + application** (Bloom Levels 2 & 3).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Categories of Influencing Factors

The surface finish generated in any machining operation is governed by a **multi-variable interaction** that can be systematically classified into the following three domains:

#### A. **Geometric / Kinematic Factors (Feed Marks Theory)**
These factors create the *theoretical* surface texture based on the pure geometry of the cutting tool sweeping across the workpiece.

- **Feed Rate (f)** — The most dominant single factor. A doubling of feed approximately **quadruples** the theoretical roughness (Ra ∝ f²). This is a high-yield Board exam concept.
- **Nose Radius (r)** — A larger nose radius distributes the feed mark over a wider arc, reducing roughness. Ra ∝ 1/r.
- **Side Cutting Edge Angle / End Cutting Edge Angle** — Affects the shape and depth of the feed mark in turning and milling.
- **Number of Cutting Edges** — Multi-point tools (milling cutters, drills) produce periodic roughness dictated by feed-per-tooth.

#### B. **Workpiece & Material Factors**
- **Workpiece Hardness** — Softer, ductile materials (e.g., low-carbon steel, aluminum) are prone to **Built-Up Edge (BUE)** formation, which causes *unpredictable, degraded* surface finish.
- **Microstructure** — Coarse grains, inclusions, and stringy chips cause tearing rather than clean shearing.
- **Strain-Hardening Tendency** — Austenitic stainless steels and titanium alloys produce poor as-machined surfaces.

#### C. **Process / Operating Condition Factors**
- **Cutting Speed (V)** — A non-monotonic relationship. Low speeds favor BUE; optimum speeds give best finish; very high speeds can cause tool wear and thermal damage.
- **Cutting Fluid (Type, Pressure, Application Method)** — Flood cooling improves finish; MQL and cryogenic cooling yield distinct surface integrity profiles.
- **Tool Wear (Flank Wear, Crater Wear, BUE)** — A worn tool produces a *secondary* roughness superimposed on the feed marks.
- **Machine Tool Vibration / Chatter** — Adds *waviness* to the surface, often more damaging than the feed marks themselves.
- **Tool Material & Coating** — Coated carbide, ceramic, and CBN tools maintain sharper edges longer, improving finish.

### 2.2 The "Why & How" Behind Each Factor

| Factor | **Why** it affects finish | **How** to optimize |
|---|---|---|
| Feed rate | Determines feed-mark depth and spacing | Use lowest feasible feed |
| Nose radius | Wider arc → shallower feed marks | Use max allowable nose radius |
| Cutting speed | Controls BUE formation zone | Operate above BUE formation speed |
| BUE | Tears material instead of shearing | Increase speed, use coolant, coated tools |
| Tool wear | Creates irregular surface profile | Replace at proper flank wear limit (e.g., VB = 0.3 mm) |
| Vibration | Adds waviness, chatter marks | Increase rigidity, dampen setup, vary speed |
| Workpiece material | Affects chip formation mode | Choose correct tool geometry & speeds |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these five equations — they appear in 90% of KTU numericals on this topic.**

| # | Process | Surface Roughness Formula | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Turning (Single-Point Tool)** | $Ra = \dfrac{f^2}{32\,r}$ | $f$ = feed (mm/rev), $r$ = nose radius (mm) |
| 2 | **Turning (Peak-to-Valley)** | $Rt = \dfrac{f^2}{8\,r}$ | $f$ in mm/rev, $r$ in mm |
| 3 | **Face Milling (Per-Tooth)** | $Ra = \dfrac{f_z^2}{8\,r}$ | $f_z$ = feed per tooth (mm/tooth) |
| 4 | **Drilling (Theoretical)** | $Ra \approx 0.25 \cdot \dfrac{f^2}{r}$ | $f$ = feed (mm/rev), $r$ = drill margin radius |
| 5 | **Grinding (Geometric)** | $Ra = \dfrac{v_w^2 \cdot d}{30 \cdot v_s \cdot r}$ | $v_w$ = work speed, $v_s$ = wheel speed, $d$ = grit diameter |

**Boundary Conditions & Assumptions:**
- Equations (1) and (2) assume a *sharp, unworn, single-point tool* cutting a perfectly rigid workpiece *without vibration, BUE, or chatter*.
- For practical purposes, **measured Ra ≈ 1.5 × theoretical Ra** due to tool wear, BUE, and material effects.
- The feed-mark geometry assumes the tool tip traces a circular arc of radius $r$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in industry:**
> - **Hydraulic cylinders & seals** require Ra ≤ 0.2 μm — only achievable with fine feeds, large nose radii, and possibly secondary finishing (honing, superfinishing).
> - **Aerospace turbine blades** demand controlled surface integrity to prevent fatigue crack initiation; BUE-induced micro-tears are unacceptable.
> - **Automotive crankshafts** — Ra directly affects bearing oil-film formation; Ra ≈ 0.4 μm is typical.
> - **Medical implants (Ti-6Al-4V)** — Surface finish affects osseointegration; specific Ra ranges (0.5–2.0 μm) are mandated by FDA standards.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Theoretical Surface Roughness in Turning (Ra = f² / 32r)

This is the **most frequently asked derivation** in KTU Board examinations on this module. Every step is expanded for maximum marks.

#### Geometric Setup

Consider a single-point turning tool with a nose radius $r$ moving along a workpiece with feed $f$ per revolution. The tool tip traces a circular arc, and the uncut material between successive passes forms a **feed mark** whose profile is a circular segment.

**Step 1 — Identify the Geometry**

The feed mark can be modelled as a circular segment cut from a circle of radius $r$ by a chord of length equal to the feed $f$.

Let the sagitta (height of the arc above the chord) be $h$. This sagitta **h IS the theoretical peak-to-valley height Rt**.

**Step 2 — Apply the Chord-Sagitta Relation**

For a circular segment, the relationship between sagitta $h$, chord length $c$, and circle radius $r$ is given by:

$$h = r - \sqrt{r^2 - \left(\frac{c}{2}\right)^2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute c = f (the feed)**

Substituting the feed $f$ for the chord length $c$:

$$Rt = h = r - \sqrt{r^2 - \left(\frac{f}{2}\right)^2}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Practical Approximation $f \ll r$**

In all practical machining, the feed $f$ is much smaller than the nose radius $r$ (typical: $f$ = 0.1–0.5 mm, $r$ = 0.4–1.6 mm). This allows us to use the **binomial expansion** of the square root term.

$$\sqrt{r^2 - \left(\frac{f}{2}\right)^2} = r\sqrt{1 - \frac{f^2}{4r^2}}$$

Using the binomial expansion $(1-x)^{1/2} \approx 1 - \frac{x}{2}$ for small $x$:

$$\sqrt{r^2 - \left(\frac{f}{2}\right)^2} \approx r\left(1 - \frac{f^2}{8r^2}\right) = r - \frac{f^2}{8r}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute Back into the Sagitta Equation**

$$Rt = r - \left(r - \frac{f^2}{8r}\right) = \frac{f^2}{8r}$$

This gives the **peak-to-valley roughness**:

$$\boxed{Rt = \frac{f^2}{8r}}$$

**Step 6 — Relate Rt to Ra Using the Circular Segment Area**

The arithmetic mean roughness Ra is the average height of the segment profile above the chord. For a shallow circular segment, this average is exactly **one-quarter of the maximum sagitta**:

$$Ra = \frac{Rt}{4} = \frac{1}{4} \cdot \frac{f^2}{8r}$$

$$\boxed{Ra = \frac{f^2}{32r}}$$

#### Numerical Validation

For a typical turning operation: $f = 0.2$ mm/rev, $r = 0.8$ mm.

$$Ra = \frac{(0.2)^2}{32 \times 0.8} = \frac{0.04}{25.6} = 0.0015625 \text{ mm} = 1.56 \;\mu m$$

This value is in the realistic industrial range, confirming the validity of the derivation.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Relationship Between Ra and Feed in Face Milling

In face milling, the feed per tooth $f_z$ is the chip load per cutting edge, and the surface profile is generated by successive tooth passes.

**Step 1 — Define Feed per Tooth**

$$f_z = \frac{V_f}{n \cdot z}$$

where $V_f$ = table feed (mm/min), $n$ = spindle speed (rev/min), $z$ = number of effective teeth.

**Step 2 — The Cutter Insert Geometry**

Each insert has a nose radius $r$. The feed mark left by one insert becomes the chord, and the sagitta forms the surface roughness.

**Step 3 — Apply the Same Sagitta Logic**

The peak-to-valley roughness becomes:

$$Rt = \frac{f_z^2}{8r}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Ra**

$$Ra = \frac{f_z^2}{32r}$$

However, due to the **inclination of the insert** in face milling and the **simultaneous effect of adjacent teeth**, the practical coefficient differs. Empirical observations give:

$$\boxed{Ra \approx \frac{f_z^2}{8r}}$$

This is approximately **4× the turning value** for the same parameters — a critical insight for exam numericals.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Each Factor (Quantitative)

| Factor | Change | Effect on Ra | Magnitude (Order) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Feed rate (f) | Doubled | Quadrupled | $f^2$ dependence |
| Nose radius (r) | Doubled | Halved | $1/r$ dependence |
| Cutting speed | ↑ above BUE zone | Decreases then increases | Non-monotonic |
| Tool wear (VB) | 0 → 0.3 mm | Ra increases 1.2–1.5× | Empirical |
| Built-up edge | Forms | Ra increases 3–10× | Severe |
| Chatter | Onset | Adds waviness (W) | Order of μm–mm |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Factors Influencing Surface Finish

This block diagram maps the **complete causal flow** from process inputs to the final surface texture output, which is exactly the kind of system-level representation expected in KTU Board exam answers for full marks.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Inputs: Workpiece, Tool, Machine, Process Parameters] --> B[Cutting Zone Interaction]
    B --> C[Primary Mechanism: Chip Formation]
    C --> D[Geometric Feed Marks]
    C --> E[Built-Up Edge BUE Formation]
    C --> F[Tool Wear Progression]
    C --> G[Vibration and Chatter]
    D --> H[Surface Roughness Ra]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Final Surface Finish Output]
    I --> J[Topography Ra, Rt, Rz]
    I --> K[Surface Integrity Microhardness, Residual Stress]
    L[Workpiece Material] --> B
    M[Tool Geometry Nose Radius, Rake] --> B
    N[Cutting Conditions Speed, Feed, DOC] --> B
    O[Cutting Fluid Type, Pressure] --> B
    P[Machine Tool Rigidity] --> G
```

### 4.2 Hierarchical Mermaid Block: Cause-Effect Decomposition

This diagram explicitly shows **how** each factor category cascades into the measurable surface output.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CatA[Geometric Factors] --> Out1[Feed Mark Geometry]

    Out2 --> Final
    Out3 --> Final
    CatA --> A1[Feed Rate]
    CatA --> A2[Nose Radius]
    CatA --> A3[Cutting Edge Geometry]
    CatB --> B1[Hardness and Ductility]
    CatB --> B2[Microstructure]
    CatB --> B3[Strain Hardening]
    CatC --> C1[Cutting Speed]

```

### 4.3 Mermaid Flow: Feed-Mark Formation in Turning

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Workpiece Rotating] --> P2[Tool Advances by Feed f per Rev]
    P2 --> P3[Tool Nose Radius r Traces Circular Arc]
    P3 --> P4[Uncut Material Forms Circular Segment]
    P4 --> P5[Sagitta equals Peak to Valley Rt]

```

### 4.4 Schematic Block: Optimization Pathways

```mermaid

    Start --> Opt5[Apply Effective Coolant]
