```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Tip for Students:** In KTU Board exams, drawing a clear block diagram or tree-diagram like these earns 2–3 easy marks even if your written explanation is partial. Mermaid-style hierarchical charts are the modern equivalent of hand-drawn flow charts — examiners love them.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define surface roughness. List the various factors affecting surface finish in machining.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Surface roughness is a measure of the micro-irregularities on a machined surface, quantified by the arithmetic mean deviation $Ra$ of the profile from a mean line over a sampling length, expressed in micrometers (μm).

**Factors (1.5 marks — list any 4 with brief notes):**
1. **Feed rate** — most dominant; Ra ∝ f².
2. **Tool nose radius** — Ra ∝ 1/r; larger radius improves finish.
3. **Cutting speed** — affects Built-Up Edge (BUE); optimum gives best finish.
4. **Built-Up Edge (BUE)** — causes tearing, drastically degrades finish.
5. **Tool wear (flank wear VB)** — increases roughness progressively.
6. **Workpiece material** — ductile materials form BUE; brittle materials chip.
7. **Cutting fluid** — flood cooling improves finish; dry cutting can cause BUE.
8. **Vibration and chatter** — adds waviness to the surface profile.

---

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Explain briefly the role of built-up edge (BUE) in affecting surface finish during machining.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Built-Up Edge (BUE)** is a wedge of work-material that welds to the rake face of the tool near the cutting edge due to high local pressure and temperature. It forms preferentially at **low-to-medium cutting speeds** with **ductile work materials** (e.g., low-carbon steel, aluminum).

**Effects on surface finish (3 marks distributed):**
- The BUE acts as a *false cutting edge* with an irregular geometry → produces a *rough, unpredictable* surface with **tear-outs and scratches**.
- The BUE periodically breaks off, carrying away tiny fragments of tool material → causes **chatter-like marks** and **micro-pits**.
- **Surface finish is degraded by 3 to 10 times** compared to BUE-free cutting.
- **Prevention:** Increase cutting speed above the BUE formation zone, use coated tools (TiN, Al₂O₃), apply cutting fluid, reduce friction via proper rake angle.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module 3 Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Part B pattern: Each 14-mark question has **two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks**, and the student must answer **ONE full question** (with both sub-parts). Below are two complete alternative questions for the student's choice.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, derive the expression for theoretical surface roughness $Ra$ in a turning operation in terms of feed $f$ and nose radius $r$. State all assumptions. (7 marks)

**(b)** A turning operation is performed on a mild steel shaft with the following data: feed $f = 0.3$ mm/rev, nose radius $r = 1.2$ mm, cutting speed $V = 120$ m/min, depth of cut = 2 mm. Calculate: (i) Theoretical surface roughness $Ra$, (ii) Theoretical peak-to-valley roughness $Rt$, (iii) If the measured Ra is 1.5 times the theoretical value, find the measured Ra and comment on the possible causes. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (a):**

The derivation is provided in full in Section 3.1 above. For Board exam writing, the student should:

- Draw a labelled diagram of the tool nose radius sweeping across the workpiece with a chord of length $f$.
- State the sagitta equation $h = r - \sqrt{r^2 - (f/2)^2}$.
- Apply the binomial approximation for $f \ll r$.
- Arrive at $Rt = f^2 / 8r$ and $Ra = Rt/4 = f^2 / 32r$.

**Assumptions (must list):**
1. Tool is perfectly sharp with no wear.
2. No Built-Up Edge (BUE) formation.
3. Workpiece and tool are perfectly rigid (no vibration, no deflection).
4. Feed $f$ is much smaller than nose radius $r$.
5. Tool nose is a perfect circular arc.
6. No side cutting edge angle effect considered (orthogonal cutting assumption).

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:** $f = 0.3$ mm/rev, $r = 1.2$ mm.

**(i) Theoretical Ra:**

$$Ra = \frac{f^2}{32r} = \frac{(0.3)^2}{32 \times 1.2} = \frac{0.09}{38.4} = 0.00234 \text{ mm} = 2.34 \;\mu m$$

**(ii) Theoretical Rt:**

$$Rt = \frac{f^2}{8r} = \frac{(0.3)^2}{8 \times 1.2} = \frac{0.09}{9.6} = 0.009375 \text{ mm} = 9.375 \;\mu m$$

**(iii) Measured Ra:**

$$Ra_{measured} = 1.5 \times 2.34 = 3.51 \;\mu m$$

**Possible causes of the deviation (any 2 for full marks):**
- Slight tool wear (flank wear VB > 0.1 mm).
- Formation of partial BUE at V = 120 m/min (within BUE zone for mild steel).
- Slight machine tool vibration due to depth of cut.
- Material microstructural inhomogeneity.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**(a)** Discuss in detail the various factors affecting surface finish in machining operations. Classify them into three categories and explain the role of each. (7 marks)

**(b)** A face milling operation on an aluminum alloy workpiece uses a 100 mm diameter cutter with 8 inserts, each having a nose radius of 0.8 mm. The spindle speed is 800 rpm and the table feed is 480 mm/min. Calculate the theoretical surface roughness Ra. If a finer finish is required (Ra ≤ 1.0 μm), suggest two practical modifications. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (a):**

The student should produce a structured answer with three headings — **Geometric, Workpiece, Process Factors** — as outlined in Section 2.1. Each category contributes ~2–3 marks. Key points to include:

- **Geometric factors** (feed, nose radius, tool geometry) — theoretical basis Ra = f²/32r.
- **Workpiece factors** (hardness, ductility, BUE tendency) — material response.
- **Process factors** (speed, fluid, tool wear, vibration) — operational effects.

A simple classification table earns full marks.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:** $D = 100$ mm, $z = 8$ teeth, $r = 0.8$ mm, $n = 800$ rpm, $V_f = 480$ mm/min.

**Step 1 — Feed per tooth:**

$$f_z = \frac{V_f}{n \cdot z} = \frac{480}{800 \times 8} = \frac{480}{6400} = 0.075 \text{ mm/tooth}$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical Ra (face milling):**

$$Ra = \frac{f_z^2}{8r} = \frac{(0.075)^2}{8 \times 0.8} = \frac{0.005625}{6.4} = 0.000879 \text{ mm} = 0.879 \;\mu m$$

This is *already* below 1.0 μm, so the target is achievable with these parameters.

**Step 3 — Practical modifications (any 2 for full marks):**

If Ra ≤ 1.0 μm must be guaranteed despite wear/BUE:

1. **Reduce feed per tooth** to $f_z = 0.05$ mm/tooth → $Ra = (0.05)²/(8 \times 0.8) = 0.39 \;μm$.
2. **Increase nose radius** to $r = 1.6$ mm (if insert geometry allows) → $Ra = (0.075)²/(8 \times 1.6) = 0.44 \;μm$.
3. **Use coated inserts (TiAlN or diamond-like carbon)** to suppress BUE on aluminum.
4. **Apply flood coolant or MQL** to maintain stable cutting temperature.
5. **Replace inserts at VB = 0.2 mm** to maintain sharp edge.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Unit Mismatch:** Converting $Ra$ from mm to μm is MANDATORY. Forgetting to convert loses 1 mark per sub-question.
> - **Wrong formula coefficient:** Use $f²/32r$ for **turning**, $f_z²/8r$ for **face milling**. Confusing them is the #1 error.
> - **Skipping assumptions:** In derivation questions, examiners award 1–2 marks for stating the *binomial expansion assumption* ($f \ll r$) and the *sharp tool* assumption. Skipping them costs dearly.
> - **Ignoring BUE in numericals:** When asked for *measured* Ra, multiply theoretical by 1.5× and mention BUE/tool wear. The phrase "Measured Ra = 1.5 × Theoretical Ra" is the KTU expected answer.
> - **Drawing neat diagrams:** In 7-mark sub-parts, a labelled sketch of the feed-mark geometry is worth 2 marks on its own.
> - **Avoid over-simplification:** "Increase speed for better finish" is WRONG without specifying *optimum speed range*. Always say: "Increase speed *above the BUE formation zone but below the tool wear acceleration zone*."

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Read this 5 minutes before the exam:**

- **Surface Finish** = combination of **Surface Roughness (Ra, Rt, Rz)** + **Surface Integrity** (residual stress, microhardness, microstructural changes).
- **Ra** is the most common metric; **Rt = 4 × Ra** theoretically; practical ratio ≈ 4–5.
- **Most dominant factor is feed rate**: doubling feed → 4× Ra.
- **Nose radius** is the second most influential: doubling r → halves Ra.
- **Theoretical formulas** (must memorize cold):
  - Turning: $Ra = f²/32r$, $Rt = f²/8r$
  - Face milling: $Ra = f_z²/8r$
  - Drilling: $Ra \approx 0.25 f²/r$
- **Built-Up Edge (BUE)** is the *single biggest enemy* of surface finish in ductile materials; it forms at low-medium speeds and tears the surface.
- **Cutting speed** has a *non-monotonic* effect: low → BUE bad; optimum → best; very high → tool wear bad.
- **Tool wear** degrades finish progressively; replace at **VB = 0.3 mm** (general turning guideline).
- **Cutting fluid** (flood) improves finish; MQL gives intermediate results; dry is worst for ductile materials.
- **Vibration & chatter** add *waviness* (W) — sometimes more damaging than roughness itself.
- **Workpiece hardness & ductility** determine BUE susceptibility; harder + more brittle → better as-machined finish (but more tool wear).
- **Machine tool rigidity** affects finish through deflection and chatter; finite stiffness → wavy surface.
- **Measured Ra ≈ 1.5 × Theoretical Ra** is the standard KTU assumption in numericals.
- **Industrial targets** to remember: Hydraulic seals Ra ≤ 0.2 μm; Bearing surfaces Ra ≤ 0.4 μm; General machined Ra = 1.6–3.2 μm; Rough Ra > 6.3 μm.
- **Three category classification** (Geometric / Workpiece / Process) is the KTU-favoured answer structure — use it for full marks in 7-mark theory questions.
- **Derivation steps** to memorize: (1) Draw arc diagram, (2) Write sagitta equation, (3) Binomial approximation, (4) Get Rt, (5) Divide by 4 for Ra.
- **Sketch is essential** — feed-mark geometry diagram is worth 2 marks in any derivation question.
- **Optimum cutting speed** for best finish: typically 60–90 m/min for mild steel with HSS; 150–250 m/min with coated carbide.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Impact of Cutting Conditions on Surface Integrity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET522 - MECHANICS OF METAL CUTTING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Impact of Cutting Conditions on Surface Integrity # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Surface Integrity (SI)** is a comprehensive, all-encompassing metallurgical and topographical description of the condition of a workpiece surface produced by a manufacturing process. It is a broader and more rigorous concept than mere surface roughness, encompassing **surface topography**, **surface metallurgy** (microstructural alterations), **residual stress states**, and **subsurface deformation** induced by the machining operation. In the KTU 2024 scheme framework, surface integrity is considered a critical indicator of **functional performance**, directly governing fatigue life, corrosion resistance, wear behavior, and dimensional stability of machined components. It is formally divided into two primary domains: 1. **Surface Texture (Topography)** — Geometric irregularities evaluated via parameters such as **Ra (Arithmetic Mean Roughness)**, **Rz (Mean Peak-to-Valley Height)**, and **Rt (Total Peak-to-Valley Height)**, measured in micrometers (µm). 2. **Subsurface Integrity** — The metallurgical state beneath the machined surface, including: - **Plastic Deformation Zone** (depth: 1 µm – 500 µm) - **Residual Stresses** (tensile or compressive, in MPa) - **Micro-Hardness Changes** (work hardening) - **Microstructural Transformations** (recrystallization, phase changes) - **Micro-Cracks and Voids** - **Chemical Alterations** (intergranular attack, contamination) - **Thermal Damage Zones** (white layer / untempered martensite) ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: The "Ski Slope" on a Mountain Side** > > Imagine a pristine snowy mountain slope (the parent material) that has been freshly carved by a snowcat (the cutting tool). After the snowcat passes, the slope is no longer pristine — it has: > - **Visible tracks** (surface roughness — topography) > - **Compacted snow underneath** (work hardening, residual stress) > - **Some patches where the snow melted and refroze into ice** (white layer / phase transformation) > - **Tiny cracks from the heavy machine's vibration** (micro-cracks) > > Surface integrity is the *complete forensic report* of what the snowcat did to the snow — not just the visible tracks, but everything hidden below. **Just as a ski racer cares about both the visible grooming and the ice layer beneath, an engineer designing a turbine shaft cares about both Ra AND the residual stress profile.** ## 1.3 Standardized Metrics & Reference Standards > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Standardized Metrics (Mandatory for KTU Board Exams):** > - **Ra** — Arithmetic Mean Roughness (µm) — *ISO 4287/1997* — Most universally used > - **Rz** — Mean Peak-to-Valley Height (µm) — *DIN 4768 / ISO 4287* > - **Rt / Rmax** — Maximum Peak-to-Valley Height (µm) > - **Rq (Rrms)** — Root Mean Square Roughness (µm) > - **Rsk (Skewness)** — Profile symmetry indicator > - **Rku (Kurtosis)** — Peak sharpness indicator > - **Subsurface Residual Stress (σr)** — Measured in **MPa** via X-ray diffraction (XRD) or the hole-drilling method > - **Micro-Hardness (HV / HK)** — Measured in **Vickers / Knoop** scale > - **Plastic Deformation Depth (δp)** — Measured in **µm** via cross-sectional metallography The **white layer** (also called **untempered martensite layer** in ferrous alloys) typically measures **0.1 µm – 50 µm** in depth and exhibits hardness values **50%–100% higher** than the bulk parent material. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Two Domains of Surface Integrity — Hierarchical Breakdown ### Domain A: Surface Topography (Geometric) Surface topography in metal cutting is governed by three fundamental geometric sources: **(i) Geometrical / Ideal Surface Profile** Produced purely by the **nominal tool geometry** interacting with the feed motion. For turning, this is the canonical "feed mark" profile. **(ii) Built-Up Edge (BUE) and Built-Up Layer (BUL) Artifacts** Adhesion of workpiece material onto the cutting tool, which then "ploughs" the freshly cut surface, creating random, chaotic irregularities superimposed on the geometric profile. **(iii) Plastic Side Flow and Side Burr Formation** Material that does not separate cleanly as a chip, but instead flows plastically over the tool nose and freezes onto the workpiece flanks. ### Domain B: Subsurface Metallurgical State The mechanical and thermal energy deposited during cutting alters the material beneath the surface in a layered fashion: - **Layer 1 — White / Phase-Transformed Layer** (0.1–50 µm): Rapid heating above Ac1/Ac3 followed by rapid quenching produces untempered martensite, retained austenite, or over-tempered regions. - **Layer 2 — Severely Plastic-Deformed Layer** (5–500 µm): Heavy dislocation density, sub-grain refinement, and intense work hardening. - **Layer 3 — Mildly Affected / Recovery Zone** (up to 1000 µm): Lower dislocation density, partial recovery, modest residual stress. - **Layer 4 — Unaffected Bulk Substrate**: The original parent microstructure with its original residual stress state (often null, depending on prior thermo-mechanical history). ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table consolidates all board-exam-relevant formulas for the topic "Impact of Cutting Conditions on Surface Integrity." These equations are mathematically verifiable and form the analytical backbone of typical KTU 14-mark problems. | # | Parameter | Formula | Variables & Units | Engineering Significance | |---|-----------|---------|-------------------|--------------------------| | 1 | Theoretical Ra (turning) | $\displaystyle R_a^{th} = \frac{f^2}{32\,R}$ | $f$ = feed (mm/rev), $R$ = tool nose radius (mm) | Ideal geometric roughness ignoring BUE; achievable only with sharp, clean tools | | 2 | Theoretical Peak-to-Valley Rt | $\displaystyle R_t = \frac{f^2}{8\,R}$ | $f$ (mm/rev), $R$ (mm) | Useful for fatigue-critical design where deepest valley matters | | 3 | Rz (approx) | $\displaystyle R_z \approx 4 \cdot R_a$ | $R_a$ in µm | Empirical quick-conversion rule used in industry reports | | 4 | Power dissipated in shear plane | $\displaystyle P_s = F_s \cdot V_s$ | $F_s$ = shear force (N), $V_s$ = shear velocity (m/s) | Determines thermal energy entering workpiece — drives residual stress | | 5 | Specific cutting energy | $\displaystyle U = \frac{P}{Q} = \frac{F_c \cdot V}{f \cdot d \cdot V}$ | $U$ (J/mm³), $f$ (mm/rev), $d$ (mm) | Total energy per unit volume removed; partitions into chip + workpiece heat | | 6 | Fraction of heat to workpiece | $\displaystyle R_T = \frac{1}{1 + 1.13 \sqrt{\frac{\rho_c\,c_c\,k_c}{\rho_w\,c_w\,k_w} \cdot \frac{V \cdot t}{A}}}$ | $R_T$ dimensionless, $V$ (m/min), $t$ = uncut chip thickness (mm), $A$ = contact area (mm²) | Key driver of thermal damage; high $V$ and large $t$ push more heat into tool | | 7 | Cutting temperature (trigger formula) | $\displaystyle \theta = C_\theta \cdot V^{m_\theta} \cdot f^{n_\theta} \cdot d^{p_\theta}$ | $V$ (m/min), $f$ (mm/rev), $d$ (mm) | Empirical; exponents depend on tool-workpiece pair. High $V$ dominates. | | 8 | MRR (Material Removal Rate) | $\displaystyle \text{MRR} = f \cdot d \cdot V$ | $f$ (mm/rev), $d$ (mm), $V$ (m/min) | Productivity metric; high MRR often degrades surface integrity | | 9 | Tool-life (Taylor) | $\displaystyle V \cdot T^{n} = C$ | $V$ (m/min), $T$ (min), $n$ = Taylor exponent | Couples productivity with tool-wear-driven surface degradation | | 10 | Workpiece heat fraction (Loewen-Shaw) | $\displaystyle R_{ws} = \frac{0.4\, t^{0.5}}{V^{0.5}\, r^{0.25}}$ | $t$ = undeformed chip thickness, $V$ (m/min), $r$ = nose radius | Used in KTU numerical problems for residual-stress prediction | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Symbol Safeguard**: In the formulas above, the symbol `\vert` or `\mid` is used for absolute value or norm. Avoid the bare vertical bar `|` inside markdown table cells to prevent table-syntax breakage. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Aerospace turbine shafts** (Inconel, Ti-6Al-4V): require compressive residual stress at the surface to extend fatigue life — drives selection of low-stress grinding or low-speed honing. - **Automotive crankshafts** (forged steel): require controlled Ra (typically **0.4 µm – 0.8 µm**) to balance oil-retention with bearing-load capacity. - **Hydraulic spools and valves**: require ultra-low Ra (< **0.1 µm**) to prevent leakage and pressure-pulsation in fluid-power systems. - **Rolling-element bearings**: surface integrity governs the **L10 fatigue life** by 2nd–3rd power. - **Medical implants (Ti-6Al-4V, Co-Cr)**: residual stress and white-layer avoidance are critical for biocompatibility and fatigue endurance in the body. - **Cutting-condition optimization** in CAM (e.g., Mastercam, NX, CATIA) directly uses these formulas to recommend feeds, speeds, and tool geometries that meet SI specifications. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Theoretical Surface Roughness in Turning (Ra from Feed Marks) This is the single most important derivation for KTU 2024 Module 3 numericals. It links cutting condition (feed $f$) and tool geometry (nose radius $R$) directly to surface texture. ### Step 1 — Set Up the Geometry Consider a single-point turning tool with nose radius $R$ (mm) advancing by one feed $f$ (mm/rev) per revolution. The tool tip traces a circular arc of radius $R$, but because $R \gg f$, the arc is locally approximated as a circular "shoulder." The tool removes material in such a way that the **leftover ridge** (the feed mark left on the workpiece) has a cross-section that is a circular segment of radius $R$ and chord length $f$. ### Step 2 — Express the Peak-to-Valley Height (Rt) For a circular segment of radius $R$ and chord $f$, the sagitta (peak-to-valley height) is given by the standard circle geometry: $$ R_t \;=\; R - \sqrt{R^{2} - \left(\frac{f}{2}\right)^{2}} $$ ### Step 3 — Apply the Engineering Approximation $R \gg f$ In real machining, $R$ (typically 0.4 mm – 1.6 mm) is **much greater** than $f$ (typically 0.05 mm – 0.5 mm), so the term $(f/2)^2 \ll R^2$. We can apply the **binomial expansion**: $$ \sqrt{R^{2} - x^{2}} \;=\; R\sqrt{1 - \frac{x^{2}}{R^{2}}} \;\approx\; R\left(1 - \frac{x^{2}}{2R^{2}} - \frac{x^{4}}{8R^{4}} - \cdots\right) $$ Substituting $x = f/2$ and truncating after the second-order term: $$ \sqrt{R^{2} - \left(\frac{f}{2}\right)^{2}} \;\approx\; R - \frac{f^{2}}{8R} $$ ### Step 4 — Substitute Back into the Sagitta Equation $$ \begin{aligned} R_t &\;=\; R - \left(R - \frac{f^{2}}{8R}\right) \\[6pt] &\;=\; \frac{f^{2}}{8R} \end{aligned} $$ This is the **theoretical peak-to-valley roughness** due purely to geometric feed-mark action. ### Step 5 — Convert Rt to the Arithmetic Mean Ra For an ideal sinusoidal or circular-arc profile, the arithmetic mean Ra is exactly **one-quarter** of the peak-to-valley Rt: $$ \begin{aligned} R_a^{th} &\;=\; \frac{R_t}{4} \\[4pt] &\;=\; \frac{1}{4} \cdot \frac{f^{2}}{8R} \\[4pt] &\;=\; \frac{f^{2}}{32R} \end{aligned} $$ This is the famous KTU board-exam result. ### Step 6 — Practical Correction Factor In actual machining, **BUE, vibration, side flow, and tool wear** increase Ra beyond the theoretical value. A common empirical correction: $$ R_a^{actual} \;\approx\; C_{sf} \cdot \frac{f^{2}}{32R} $$ where $C_{sf}$ is a **surface factor** typically between **1.1 and 4.0**, depending on the tool-workpiece material combination and cutting conditions. ### Step 7 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) > **Problem (KTU 2024 Pattern):** A turning operation uses feed $f = 0.2$ mm/rev and a tool nose radius $R = 0.8$ mm. Calculate (a) theoretical peak-to-valley roughness Rt, (b) theoretical arithmetic mean roughness Ra, and (c) the actual Ra if the surface factor $C_{sf} = 1.75$. **Solution (a):** $$ R_t \;=\; \frac{f^{2}}{8R} \;=\; \frac{(0.2)^{2}}{8 \times 0.8} \;=\; \frac{0.04}{6.4} \;=\; 0.00625 \text{ mm} \;=\; 6.25\ \mu m $$ **Solution (b):** $$ R_a^{th} \;=\; \frac{f^{2}}{32R} \;=\; \frac{(0.2)^{2}}{32 \times 0.8} \;=\; \frac{0.04}{25.6} \;=\; 0.0015625 \text{ mm} \;=\; 1.5625\ \mu m $$ **Solution (c):** $$ R_a^{actual} \;=\; C_{sf} \cdot R_a^{th} \;=\; 1.75 \times 1.5625 \;=\; 2.734\ \mu m $$ This is well within the typical KTU answer range and demonstrates how cutting parameters govern achievable surface finish. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Coupling of Cutting Conditions to Residual Stress Sign and Magnitude Residual stress is governed by the **competition** between two energy-deposition mechanisms in the cutting zone: ### Step 1 — Mechanical Loading Component (Tensile Tendency) The **cutting force $F_c$** acting on the tool flank creates a **ploughing** action that plastically elongates the surface layer in the feed direction. When the tool passes, the plastically elongated layer is restrained by the underlying bulk, generating **tensile residual stress** parallel to the cutting direction. Mechanical contribution scales with: $$ \sigma_r^{mech} \;\propto\; \frac{F_c}{f \cdot d} \;\propto\; k_s \cdot t $$ where $k_s$ is the **specific cutting energy** (N/mm²) and $t$ is the uncut chip thickness. ### Step 2 — Thermal Loading Component (Compressive-to-Tensile Reversal) The **cutting temperature $\theta$** causes the surface layer to **expand**. If the surface is constrained by the cooler bulk substrate, the surface goes into **compression** during heating. On rapid cooling, the surface layer wants to **contract** but is restrained — converting into **tensile residual stress**. Thermal contribution: $$ \sigma_r^{therm} \;\propto\; -E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T \;+\; \text{plastic-strain relaxation} $$ where $E$ is Young's modulus, $\alpha$ is the coefficient of thermal expansion, and $\Delta T$ is the temperature rise. ### Step 3 — Net Residual Stress Superposition $$ \sigma_r^{net} \;=\; \sigma_r^{mech} \;+\; \sigma_r^{therm} $$ The **net sign** depends on which mechanism dominates: | Dominant Mechanism | Typical Cutting Conditions | Net Residual Stress | |--------------------|----------------------------|---------------------| | Mechanical (ploughing) | Low $V$, high $f$, dull tool, low $\theta$ | **Tensile** (undesirable) | | Thermal (rapid quench) | High $V$, low $f$, sharp tool, no coolant | **Tensile at extreme high $V$** (white layer risk) | | Balanced | Moderate $V$, moderate $f$, sharp tool, abundant coolant | **Mild compressive** (desirable) | > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for KTU Examiners:** This is why **finish-pass machining** uses lower cutting speed, sharp tools, smaller feeds, and copious cutting fluid — to engineer a thin layer of **compressive residual stress** that extends fatigue life. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Impact of Each Cutting Condition on Surface Integrity > [!IMPORTANT] > This matrix is **the most exam-relevant synthesis** in Module 3. Memorize the directional arrows; KTU questions often ask "predict the effect of X on Y." | Cutting Condition | ↑ Increase in Parameter → Effect on Ra | Effect on Residual Stress | Effect on White Layer | Effect on Micro-Hardness | Effect on BUE | |-------------------|---------------------------------------|---------------------------|----------------------|--------------------------|----------------| | **Cutting Speed (V)** | ↑ (modestly, but high V increases BUE at low V, then decreases it) | Shifts from compressive → tensile (very high V) | ↑↑ (severe) | ↑ (thermal softening wins at high V) | Peaks at 20–40 m/min for steel; suppressed above 60 m/min | | **Feed (f)** | ↑↑↑ (Ra ∝ f²) — most sensitive parameter | More tensile (mechanical ploughing) | Slight ↑ | ↑ (cold work) | Less BUE; smoother but rougher geometrically | | **Depth of Cut (d)** | Negligible direct effect on Ra | Minor effect; deeper cuts may increase bulk heat | Slight ↑ at high d+V combo | Slight ↑ | Marginal effect | | **Tool Nose Radius (R)** | ↓ (Ra ∝ 1/R) — use larger R for finer finish | Larger R → more compressive at low V; tensile at high V | Reduced | Reduced | Larger R reduces BUE (lower contact pressure) | | **Rake Angle (γ)** | Minor direct effect | Higher γ → lower cutting force → less tensile | Reduced | Reduced | Higher γ reduces BUE | | **Cutting Fluid** | Significant reduction (cools, lubricates, suppresses BUE) | Shifts toward compressive (faster quench) | Strongly reduced | Reduced | Strongly suppressed | | **Tool Wear (VB)** | ↑↑ (catastrophic above VB = 0.3 mm) | Strongly tensile (rubbing, heat) | ↑↑ | ↑↑ (rubbing) | BUE/BUL replaced by crater/flank wear | | **Workpiece Hardness (↑)** | ↓ (less BUE, less side flow) | Less tensile in absolute MPa | Reduced | Baseline shift | Strongly reduced | | **Use of Coated Tools (TiN, Al₂O₃, DLC)** | ↓ | Shifts toward compressive | Strongly reduced | Reduced | Strongly reduced | --- ## 3.4 Derivation 3: The Loewen-Shaw Heat-Fraction Model (for KTU Numericals) The **Loewen-Shaw model** is the KTU-preferred analytical tool for predicting how much heat enters the workpiece. Derivation steps: ### Step 1 — Define Heat Partitioning Total cutting power $P_c$ partitions into **chip heat $P_c^{chip}$** and **workpiece heat $P_c^{wk}$**: $$ P_c \;=\; P_c^{chip} \;+\; P_c^{wk} $$ ### Step 2 — Empirically-Motivated Form Based on dimensional analysis and experimental fits, Loewen and Shaw proposed: $$ R_{ws} \;=\; \frac{P_c^{wk}}{P_c} \;=\; \frac{0.4\, t^{0.5}}{V^{0.5}\, r^{0.25}} $$ where $t$ = uncut chip thickness (mm), $V$ = cutting speed (m/min), $r$ = tool nose radius (mm). ### Step 3 — Worked Example > **Problem:** Turning at $V = 200$ m/min, $f = 0.25$ mm/rev, $R = 0.8$ mm. Uncut chip thickness $t \approx f$ for small R. Find the workpiece heat fraction $R_{ws}$. **Solution:** $$ \begin{aligned} R_{ws} &\;=\; \frac{0.4 \times (0.25)^{0.5}}{(200)^{0.5} \times (0.8)^{0.25}} \\[4pt] &\;=\; \frac{0.4 \times 0.5}{14.142 \times 0.946} \\[4pt] &\;=\; \frac{0.20}{13.38} \\[4pt] &\;\approx\; 0.0149 \;\;(\text{or } 1.49\%) \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** Only ~1.5% of the cutting energy enters the workpiece. While small, this energy is highly concentrated at the surface and is the dominant driver of **thermal damage, white layer formation, and tensile residual stress**. The remaining ~98.5% leaves with the chip. ### Step 4 — Physical Interpretation $$ \boxed{\;R_{ws} \uparrow \;\text{when}\; t \uparrow,\; V \downarrow,\; r \uparrow\;} $$ - **Slower cutting + larger feed + larger nose radius → more heat in workpiece → more thermal damage.** - **Faster cutting + smaller feed + smaller nose radius → less heat in workpiece → better surface integrity (provided BUE is avoided).** # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Subsurface Alteration Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram) The following block diagram maps the layered architecture of the subsurface zone created by metal cutting, and how each cutting condition modulates each layer. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Cutting Tool Engagement Zone] --> B[Process Energy Input] B --> B1[Mechanical Energy
Ploughing Force Fc] B --> B2[Thermal Energy
Cutting Temperature theta] B1 --> C1[Plastic Deformation
10 to 500 um depth] B2 --> C2[White Layer Formation
0.1 to 50 um depth] C1 --> D1[Work Hardening
HV increases 30 to 100 percent] C2 --> D2[Phase Transformation
Untempered Martensite] D1 --> E[Residual Stress Field
Tensile or Compressive] D2 --> E E --> F1[Fatigue Life Impact] E --> F2[Corrosion Resistance] E --> F3[Dimensional Stability] G[Cutting Conditions Input] --> A G --> G1[Cutting Speed V] G --> G2[Feed f] G --> G3[Nose Radius R] G --> G4[Rake Angle gamma] G --> G5[Cutting Fluid] G --> G6[Tool Wear VB] G1 --> H1{BUE Regime?} H1 -->|Low V 20 to 40 m/min| I1[BUE Forms
Ra Degraded] H1 -->|High V above 60 m/min| I2[BUE Suppressed
Thermal Damage Risk] G2 --> J[Roughness Ra proportional f squared] G3 --> K[Roughness Ra inversely proportional R] G5 --> L[Suppresses BUE and White Layer] G6 --> M[Increases Ra Tensile Stress White Layer] ``` ## 4.2 Process Flow: Surface Integrity Formation Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Workpiece Bulk Material] --> B[Tool Engagement] B --> C[Primary Shear Zone] B --> D[Secondary Shear Zone Tool Chip] B --> E[Flank Contact Zone Tool Workpiece] C --> F[Chip Formation] D --> G[Heat Generation] E --> H[Ploughing Force] G --> I[Workpiece Surface Heating] H --> J[Plastic Side Flow] I --> K[White Layer and Residual Stress] J --> L[Surface Roughness and BUE Marks] K --> M[Final Surface Integrity State] L --> M M --> N{Quality Check} N -->|Pass| O[Functional Component] N -->|Fail| P[Reconditioning Required] ``` ## 4.3 Causal Chain: Cutting Parameter to Functional Failure ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Increase Cutting Speed V] --> B{Thermal Regime} B -->|Low to Moderate V| C[Lower Ra via BUE Suppression] B -->|Excessive V| D[White Layer Formation] D --> E[Tensile Residual Stress] E --> F[Reduced Fatigue Life] F --> G[Component Failure in Service] H[Increase Feed f] --> I[Ra increases with f squared] I --> J[Rougher Surface] J --> K[Poor Oil Retention or Seizure Risk] L[Reduce Nose Radius R] --> M[Ra increases as 1 over R] M --> N[Sharper Geometric Profile] N --> O[Higher Contact Pressure and BUE Risk] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are purely alphanumeric and prefixed (`A`, `B1`, `G5`, etc.). Labels are raw uppercase alphanumeric text — no markdown bold/italic tags inside quoted labels — to ensure clean rendering in KTU PDF exports and markdown viewers. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]** **"Define surface integrity. How is it different from surface roughness?"** **Model Answer:** Surface integrity is a comprehensive term that describes the entire condition of a machined surface, including its **topography, microstructure, residual stress state, micro-hardness, and chemical composition**, together with the depth and nature of any subsurface alterations. Surface roughness, in contrast, is only one component of surface integrity — it is a purely **geometric** descriptor of the microscopic peaks and valleys on the surface, quantified by parameters such as **Ra, Rz, and Rt**. In short, surface roughness is a **2D line-profile geometric metric**, whereas surface integrity is a **3D volumetric, multi-physics descriptor** of the surface and the underlying material. Two surfaces with identical Ra can have vastly different fatigue lives because their subsurface residual stresses and microstructures differ — a clear demonstration that surface integrity is the more complete and functionally relevant concept. --- ### Question A2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]** **"What is a white layer? How is it formed during machining and why is it undesirable?"** **Model Answer:** A **white layer** (also called **untempered martensite layer** in steels, or a thermally altered layer in non-ferrous alloys) is a hard, brittle, often crack-prone sub-surface zone that appears **white** under a metallurgical microscope because it resists chemical etching. It typically forms in **steels** when the surface temperature during cutting or grinding briefly exceeds the **upper critical temperature (Ac3, ~723 °C – 910 °C depending on carbon content)**, followed by **rapid self-quenching** by the cool bulk substrate, producing **untempered martensite** with hardness values often **80% – 100% higher** than the parent metal. The white layer is **undesirable** because: 1. It is **brittle** and prone to micro-cracking under service loads. 2. It contains **tensile residual stress**, severely reducing fatigue life. 3. It can spall or flake, accelerating wear in mating components. 4. It is **resistant to further machining** (drilling, tapping) in downstream operations. --- ## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Module 3 Internal Choice, Set 1]** **(a)** Derive an expression for the theoretical surface roughness (Ra) produced in a turning operation. State clearly the assumptions used. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** In a finish-turning operation on an AISI 1045 steel shaft, the cutting parameters are: cutting speed $V = 180$ m/min, feed $f = 0.15$ mm/rev, depth of cut $d = 0.5$ mm, and tool nose radius $R = 0.8$ mm. A coated carbide tool (TiN) is used with abundant flood coolant. Estimate: (i) the theoretical peak-to-valley roughness $R_t$, and (ii) the theoretical arithmetic mean roughness $R_a^{th}$. (iii) If the empirical surface factor is $C_{sf} = 1.6$, comment on whether the actual $R_a$ meets the design specification of $R_a \le 1.0\ \mu m$. **(7 Marks)** --- ### Model Solution — Question A #### Part (a) — Derivation of Theoretical Ra **Assumptions:** 1. Tool nose is perfectly sharp, no wear, no BUE. 2. The nose radius $R$ is much greater than the feed $f$ ($R \gg f$), so the circular nose arc is locally approximated by a shallow circular segment. 3. The material is rigid-plastic with no elastic recovery, no side flow, no vibration. 4. Pure orthogonal cutting geometry (turning approximated as orthogonal at the nose region). **Derivation:** The leftover feed-mark on the machined surface is a circular segment of radius $R$ and chord $f$. The peak-to-valley height (sagitta) is: $$ R_t \;=\; R - \sqrt{R^{2} - \left(\frac{f}{2}\right)^{2}} $$ Applying binomial expansion for $R \gg f$: $$ \sqrt{R^{2} - \left(\frac{f}{2}\right)^{2}} \;\approx\; R - \frac{f^{2}}{8R} $$ Therefore: $$ \begin{aligned} R_t &\;=\; R - \left(R - \frac{f^{2}}{8R}\right) \;=\; \frac{f^{2}}{8R} \\[4pt] R_a^{th} &\;=\; \frac{R_t}{4} \;=\; \frac{f^{2}}{32R} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Result:** $$ \boxed{\;R_a^{th} \;=\; \frac{f^{2}}{32R}\;} $$ **Engineering Commentary:** This formula reveals that Ra scales with the **square of feed** and **inversely with nose radius**. Hence, to halve Ra, one must halve the feed (or quadruple the nose radius). The strong $f^2$ dependence is why finish-pass operations always use very small feeds. --- #### Part (b) — Numerical Evaluation **Given:** $f = 0.15$ mm/rev, $R = 0.8$ mm, $C_{sf} = 1.6$, target $R_a \le 1.0\ \mu m$. **(i) Theoretical Rt:** $$ R_t \;=\; \frac{f^{2}}{8R} \;=\; \frac{(0.15)^{2}}{8 \times 0.8} \;=\; \frac{0.0225}{6.4} \;=\; 0.003516 \text{ mm} \;=\; 3.52\ \mu m $$ **(ii) Theoretical Ra:** $$ R_a^{th} \;=\; \frac{f^{2}}{32R} \;=\; \frac{0.0225}{25.6} \;=\; 0.000879 \text{ mm} \;=\; 0.879\ \mu m $$ **(iii) Actual Ra and Specification Check:** $$ R_a^{actual} \;=\; C_{sf} \cdot R_a^{th} \;=\; 1.6 \times 0.879 \;=\; 1.406\ \mu m $$ **Comparison with specification:** $$ R_a^{actual} = 1.406\ \mu m \;\;>\;\; R_a^{spec} = 1.0\ \mu m $$ **Conclusion:** The **actual Ra exceeds the design specification** by ~40.6%. The component **fails** the surface-finish requirement. **Remedial Recommendations (typical KTU answer continuations):** 1. **Reduce the feed** to $f' = f / \sqrt{C_{sf}} = 0.15 / \sqrt{1.6} \approx 0.119$ mm/rev to bring $R_a^{th}$ below 0.625 µm. 2. **Increase the tool nose radius** to $R' = R \cdot C_{sf} = 0.8 \times 1.6 = 1.28$ mm (e.g., use a 1.2 mm or 1.6 mm radius tool). 3. Ensure **sharp cutting edge** (no flank wear) and **clean flood coolant** to keep $C_{sf}$ closer to 1.0. --- ### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam – Module 3 Internal Choice, Set 2]** **(a)** Explain the mechanisms of **Built-Up Edge (BUE) and Built-Up Layer (BUL)** formation during machining. Discuss their impact on surface integrity. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** With the help of the **Loewen-Shaw heat-partition model**, explain how cutting speed, feed, and tool nose radius affect the fraction of heat entering the workpiece. How does this fraction in turn govern the formation of **white layer and tensile residual stress**? Use a worked example with $V = 150$ m/min, $f = 0.20$ mm/rev, $R = 1.2$ mm. **(7 Marks)** --- ### Model Solution — Question B #### Part (a) — BUE and BUL Mechanism **Built-Up Edge (BUE)** is a wedge-shaped accretion of **workpiece material** that welds onto the **rake face** of the cutting tool near the cutting edge, when the local interface temperature and pressure are within the **seizure range** of the tool-workpiece pair (typically 0.4 – 0.6 of the melting point). It is most prominent at **low-to-moderate cutting speeds** (20–40 m/min for carbon steels), with **low rake angles**, and with **ferrous workpieces of intermediate hardness** (180–300 HB). **Formation stages:** 1. **Initial adhesion**: As the tool engages, the freshly cut workpiece surface contacts the rake face under high pressure and frictional heat. 2. **Cold welding**: Atoms of the workpiece diffuse across the interface and form metallurgical bonds. 3. **Stabilization**: The BUE grows until the cutting force tears it off cyclically (instability). 4. **Cyclical detachment**: Fragments of BUE break off and become **embedded in the freshly machined surface** as random inclusions, while new BUE forms. **Built-Up Layer (BUL)** is a related but **more stable, thinner, and more continuous** layer on the **flank face** of the tool that does not cyclically detach but persists, slowly growing. **Impact on Surface Integrity:** - **Geometric degradation**: BUE acts as a "false tool" with a larger, irregular effective nose radius, producing a **rough, smeared, streaked surface** with Ra often 2–4× the theoretical value. - **Microstructural contamination**: BUE fragments embedded in the surface are **chemically and mechanically different** from the parent metal, creating subsurface **microvoids and inclusions**. - **Work hardening**: The severe plastic deformation at the BUE interface increases surface micro-hardness by 30–80%. - **Residual stress**: Highly tensile due to severe mechanical ploughing. - **Suppression strategies**: Use **higher cutting speeds** (> 60 m/min), **positive rake angles**, **coated tools** (TiN, Al₂O₃, DLC), **sharp edges**, and **effective cutting fluid** (sulphur-chlorine EP additives). --- #### Part (b) — Loewen-Shaw Model Application **Model Equation:** $$ R_{ws} \;=\; \frac{0.4\, t^{0.5}}{V^{0.5}\, r^{0.25}} $$ where $t$ ≈ feed $f$ (for small nose radii, uncut chip thickness ≈ feed). **Given:** $V = 150$ m/min, $f = t = 0.20$ mm, $R = r = 1.2$ mm. $$ \begin{aligned} R_{ws} &\;=\; \frac{0.4 \times (0.20)^{0.5}}{(150)^{0.5} \times (1.2)^{0.25}} \\[4pt] &\;=\; \frac{0.4 \times 0.4472}{12.247 \times 1.0466} \\[4pt] &\;=\; \frac{0.1789}{12.818} \\[4pt] &\;\approx\; 0.01396 \;\;(\text{or } 1.396\%) \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** Only ~1.4% of the cutting energy enters the workpiece, but this small fraction is **concentrated at the surface** and is the primary driver of: 1. **White Layer Formation**: If the surface temperature briefly exceeds the Ac3 (~750–900 °C for medium-carbon steels) and is then rapidly self-quenched by the cooler bulk, untempered martensite forms. 2. **Tensile Residual Stress**: The rapid thermal contraction upon cooling, restrained by the bulk, generates high tensile stress in the surface layer. **Effect of cutting conditions on $R_{ws}$:** | Parameter | Trend | Effect on $R_{ws}$ | Effect on white layer | Effect on residual stress | |-----------|-------|--------------------|-----------------------|---------------------------| | ↑ V (cutting speed) | $V$ in denominator, exponent 0.5 | **Decreases** | Decreases (less residence time) | Decreases | | ↑ f (feed) | $t$ in numerator, exponent 0.5 | **Increases** | Slight increase | More tensile (mechanical) | | ↑ R (nose radius) | $R$ in denominator, exponent 0.25 | **Slight decrease** | Reduced | More compressive at low V | **Key Engineering Insight:** The exponent on $V$ (−0.5) is **stronger** than the exponent on $R$ (−0.25), so **increasing cutting speed is the most effective lever** for reducing workpiece heat fraction. However, this must be balanced against BUE suppression and tool-life constraints. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit mismatch trap**: Students often mix mm and µm when substituting into the Ra formula. Express final Ra in µm. *Marks lost: 1–2 per occurrence.* > 2. **Binomial expansion error**: Forgetting to truncate correctly in the sagitta derivation. The correct expansion is $\sqrt{R^2 - (f/2)^2} \approx R - f^2/(8R)$, **not** $R - f^2/(4R)$. > 3. **Confusing Ra with Rt**: Ra is **one-quarter** of Rt for a circular-arc profile. Mixing them up is a 1-mark deduction. > 4. **Ignoring $C_{sf}$**: Quoting $R_a^{th}$ as the "final" answer without applying the surface factor loses ~2 marks in the comparison-with-specification step. > 5. **BUE mechanism vagueness**: Writing "BUE forms at high temperature" is insufficient. You must specify the **pressure-temperature window** and the **cyclical detachment** process. > 6. **White layer terminology**: Do not call it "oxide" or "carbide." The correct term is **untempered martensite** (in steels) or **thermally altered layer** (in non-ferrous alloys). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (for last-minute KTU exam review):** - [x] **Surface integrity ≠ surface roughness.** Roughness is one *component*; integrity is the *complete forensic report* (topography + metallurgy + stress + chemistry). - [x] **Master the derivation** $R_a^{th} = f^2 / (32R)$ and the sagitta geometry. - [x] **Ra is one-quarter of Rt** for an ideal circular-arc feed-mark profile. - [x] **Ra scales with $f^2$** — feed is the **most sensitive** cutting parameter for surface finish. - [x] **Ra scales with $1/R$** — larger nose radius yields finer finish (provided BUE is avoided). - [x] **BUE** forms in a specific **V-window** (20–40 m/min for steel), is suppressed by high V, positive rake, coated tools, and cutting fluid. - [x] **White layer** = **untempered martensite**, 0.1–50 µm deep, formed by rapid thermal cycle above Ac3 + self-quench. **Avoid by reducing V, using coolant, sharp tools.** - [x] **Residual stress sign** is a balance: **mechanical ploughing → tensile**, **thermal cycle → complex (compressive during heating, tensile on rapid quench)**. - [x] **Loewen-Shaw**: $R_{ws} = 0.4\, t^{0.5} / (V^{0.5}\, r^{0.25})$. **Higher V → less heat in workpiece** (exponent −0.5 dominates). - [x] **Tool wear (VB > 0.3 mm)** is catastrophic for surface integrity — rubbing generates heat, tensile stress, white layer, and BUE-like debris. - [x] **Cutting fluid** is **non-optional** for finish passes — it suppresses BUE, reduces white layer, and shifts residual stress toward compressive. - [x] **Coated tools** (TiN, Al₂O₃, CrN, DLC) dramatically improve surface integrity by reducing friction and thermal load at the tool-chip interface. - [x] **Depth of cut (d)** has minor direct effect on Ra but affects bulk heat and tool deflection. - [x] **Standard metric units**: Ra in **µm**, residual stress in **MPa**, micro-hardness in **HV**, depth in **µm**. Always annotate units in the answer. - [x] **Two surfaces with identical Ra can have vastly different fatigue lives** — this is the key argument for using surface integrity (not just roughness) as a design metric. - [x] **MRR** is a productivity metric; **surface integrity** is a quality metric. The two often trade off — finish passes use low MRR to achieve high SI. - [x] **Taylor's tool-life equation** $VT^n = C$ couples productivity with tool-wear-driven surface degradation — wear out = roughness up. - [x] **Subsurface zones** (white → severe plastic → mild recovery → bulk) are layered and graded, not sharp boundaries. > **End of Module 3 Notes — Impact of Cutting Conditions on Surface Integrity** > *Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) Outcome-Based Education framework. Aligned to Course Outcomes CO3 and CO4 of PEIET522.*

topics.title: Inspection Methods for Machined Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET522 - MECHANICS OF METAL CUTTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Inspection Methods for Machined Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Inspection** is the process of examining a machined component against established specifications, engineering drawings, and functional requirements using standardized measuring instruments and techniques. In the context of metal cutting and manufacturing engineering, it constitutes a critical pillar of **Quality Assurance (QA)** and **Quality Control (QC)** workflows.

According to **ISO 9001:2015** and **KTU 2024 Scheme** Production Engineering standards, inspection is defined as:

> "The activity of measuring, examining, testing, or otherwise determining the conformity of a manufactured product to specified requirements, followed by the recording of results."

The two principal paradigms governing the discipline are:

| Paradigm | Description | Application |
|----------|-------------|-------------|
| **Metrology** | The science of measurement, including all theoretical and practical aspects | Precision engineering, R&D |
| **Quality Control** | Operational techniques for fulfilling quality requirements | Shop-floor inspection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** Inspection of machined components is the *bridge* between **Manufacturing Process Capability** and **Functional Product Performance**. A perfectly designed cutting operation is rendered useless if the output component fails to meet dimensional, geometric, or surface integrity specifications.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of inspection as a **hospital's diagnostic process for a patient after surgery**:

- **The Surgeon (Machining Process):** Performs the operation using sharp tools (cutting inserts), precise machines (CNC/turning), and skilled hands.
- **The Post-Operative Checkup (Inspection):** The doctor uses a stethoscope (vernier caliper), blood test reports (CMM), and X-rays (surface profilometer) to verify the patient is healing correctly.
- **The Health Report (Quality Documentation):** Certifies whether the component is *fit for service* (within tolerance) or requires *rework/rejection*.

Just as a doctor cannot afford to skip post-operative diagnostics, a manufacturing engineer cannot release a machined component to assembly without dimensional and surface validation.

## 1.3 Objectives of Inspection in Machined Components

The primary objectives, in alignment with **KTU Module 3 Learning Outcomes**, are:

1. **Dimensional Verification** — ensuring length, diameter, depth, and angle are within design limits.
2. **Geometric Verification** — checking form (straightness, flatness, cylindricity), orientation (perpendicularity, parallelism), and location (concentricity, position).
3. **Surface Integrity Assessment** — measuring roughness, waviness, and lay patterns.
4. **Functional Verification** — confirming fit, clearance, and assembly compatibility.
5. **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** — feeding data back for process improvement.
6. **Cost Reduction** — preventing defective parts from progressing downstream.

## 1.4 Classification of Inspection Methods

```
INSPECTION METHODS
│
├── 1. Dimensional Inspection
│   ├── Linear (Length, Diameter, Depth)
│   └── Angular (Bevels, Chamfers, Tapers)
│
├── 2. Geometric Inspection (GD&T)
│   ├── Form Tolerances
│   ├── Orientation Tolerances
│   └── Location / Run-out Tolerances
│
├── 3. Surface Finish Inspection
│   ├── Qualitative (Visual, Comparison)
│   └── Quantitative (Stylus Profilometer, Optical)
│
├── 4. Comparative Inspection
│   ├── Mechanical Comparators
│   ├── Optical Comparators
│   ├── Pneumatic Comparators
│   └── Electrical/Electronic Comparators
│
└── 5. Specialized Inspection
    ├── Threads (Pitch, Effective Diameter)
    ├── Gears (Tooth Profile, Runout)
    └── Computer-Aided (CMM, Vision Systems)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference:** All linear and geometric inspections in Indian manufacturing industries are governed by **IS 919-1958** (recommendations for limits and fits) and **ISO 286-1:2010** (basis of the ISO system of limits and fits).

## 1.5 Fundamental Concepts: Limits, Fits & Tolerances

Before proceeding to instruments, the foundational vocabulary must be cemented:

| Term | Symbol | Definition |
|------|--------|------------|
| **Nominal Size** | D | The size of the part as per the basic design (e.g., 50 mm) |
| **Basic Size** | D | The reference size from which limits are derived |
| **Maximum Material Condition** | MMC | The condition where the part contains the maximum volume of material (upper limit for shaft, lower limit for hole) |
| **Least Material Condition** | LMC | The opposite of MMC (lower limit for shaft, upper limit for hole) |
| **Tolerance** | T | The total permissible variation in size, $T = D_{max} - D_{min}$ |
| **Allowance** | A | The minimum clearance (or maximum interference) between mating parts |
| **Upper Deviation** | ES (hole) / es (shaft) | $D_{max} - D$ |
| **Lower Deviation** | EI (hole) / ei (shaft) | $D_{min} - D$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fundamental Formula:**
> $$T = D_{max} - D_{min} = (D + \text{upper deviation}) - (D + \text{lower deviation})$$
>
> Equivalently:
> $$T = \text{upper deviation} - \text{lower deviation}$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Two Pillars: Accuracy vs. Precision

A **KTU-favorite** conceptual question concerns distinguishing:

- **Accuracy:** The closeness of a measurement to the *true value* of the quantity being measured.
- **Precision:** The closeness of *two or more measurements* to each other (repeatability).

> **Analogy:** A watch that shows 10:15:03 when the actual time is 10:15:05 is **accurate but imprecise** if subsequent readings fluctuate wildly. A watch that consistently shows 10:15:15 is **precise but inaccurate** (systematic error).

## 2.2 Instruments Classified by Range, Least Count & Application

The engineering logic of instrument selection follows a strict hierarchy:

| Instrument Type | Range (Typical) | Least Count | Application |
|-----------------|-----------------|-------------|-------------|
| **Steel Rule** | 0–1000 mm | **1.0 mm** | Rough measurement, layout work |
| **Vernier Caliper** | 0–300 mm | **0.02 mm** | OD, ID, depth measurement |
| **Vernier Height Gauge** | 0–600 mm | **0.02 mm** | Vertical measurements, marking |
| **Micrometer (External)** | 0–25 mm (set) | **0.01 mm** | Precision shaft diameter |
| **Micrometer (Internal)** | 5–30 mm | **0.01 mm** | Bore, slot measurements |
| **Dial Indicator (DTI)** | 0.8 mm typical | **0.01 mm** | Runout, alignment, comparison |
| **Bevel Protractor** | 0°–360° | **5' (5 arc-minutes)** | Angle measurement |
| **Sine Bar / Sine Centre** | 100–300 mm | Calculated | Accurate angular setup |
| **Optical Profile Projector** | Up to 500 mm | **0.001 mm** | Complex profile inspection |
| **Toolmaker's Microscope** | 150 × 100 mm | **0.001–0.005 mm** | Threads, tools, small features |
| **CMM (Coordinate Measuring Machine)** | 600 × 800 × 400 mm | **0.0001–0.001 mm** | 3D inspection, complex parts |
| **Surface Profilometer (Stylus)** | 0.5–12.5 mm | **0.001 µm (Ra)** | Surface roughness |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU 2024 ESE & University Examinations**

### A. Least Count Formulas

| Instrument | Least Count Formula | Example |
|------------|--------------------|---------|
| Vernier Scale | $LC = \dfrac{s}{n}$ where $s$ = smallest main scale division, $n$ = number of vernier divisions | $s = 1$ mm, $n = 50 \Rightarrow LC = 0.02$ mm |
| Micrometer | $LC = \dfrac{\text{Pitch}}{\text{No. of divisions on thimble}}$ | Pitch = 0.5 mm, divisions = 50 $\Rightarrow LC = 0.01$ mm |
| Vernier Height Gauge | Same as vernier caliper | $LC = 0.02$ mm |
| Bevel Protractor (Vernier) | $LC = \dfrac{1^\circ}{12} = 5'$ | One main scale div = $0.5^\circ$, 25 vernier divs = $12.5^\circ$ |
| Dial Test Indicator | Read directly from dial | $LC = 0.01$ mm or $0.001$ mm |
| Optical Comparator | $LC = \dfrac{\text{Main scale div}}{\text{Magnification}}$ | $s = 0.1$ mm, M = 100× $\Rightarrow LC = 0.001$ mm |

### B. Angular Measurement with Sine Bar

To set or measure an angle $\theta$ using a sine bar of length $L$ between rollers:

$$\sin\theta = \frac{h}{L}$$

where $h$ is the height of slip gauges stacked under one roller.

### C. Slip Gauge (Gauge Block) Formulas

- **Wringing film thickness:** ~0.0001 mm per mating surface (used in combination calculations)
- **Effective dimension of stack:** 
$$L_{eff} = L_{nominal} + (\text{No. of wringing surfaces}) \times 0.0001 \text{ mm}$$

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners often test whether students account for the **wringing film** in high-precision slip gauge combinations. Always state: *"L_eff = L_nominal + 0.0001 × (number of wrung surfaces)"*

### D. Surface Roughness Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula / Definition | Application |
|-----------|--------|----------------------|-------------|
| **Roughness Average** | $R_a$ | Arithmetic mean deviation from mean line | Most widely used |
| **Ten-Point Height** | $R_z$ | $\dfrac{(H_1 + H_2 + \ldots + H_5) - (V_1 + V_2 + \ldots + V_5)}{5}$ | Bearings, sealing surfaces |
| **Maximum Peak-to-Valley** | $R_t$ (or $R_{max}$) | Single largest peak-to-valley distance | Worn surface characterization |
| **RMS Roughness** | $R_q$ | $\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{L}\int_0^L y^2 \, dx}$ | Statistical roughness |

> **Note:** $R_a$ is computed as:
> $$R_a = \frac{1}{L}\int_0^L \vert y(x) \vert \, dx$$
> where $y(x)$ is the deviation of the profile from the mean line over the sampling length $L$.

### E. Limits, Fits & Tolerance Formulas

- **Tolerance:** $T = D_{max} - D_{min}$
- **Fundamental Deviation (Hole H-basis):** Lower deviation = 0
- **Fundamental Deviation (Shaft h-basis):** Upper deviation = 0
- **Maximum Clearance:** $C_{max} = D_{hole,max} - D_{shaft,min}$
- **Maximum Interference:** $I_{max} = D_{shaft,max} - D_{hole,min}$

### F. Comparator / Magnification Formulas

| Comparator Type | Magnification Formula |
|-----------------|----------------------|
| **Mechanical (Lever)** | $M = \dfrac{a}{b}$ (ratio of lever arms) |
| **Optical (Optical Lever)** | $M = \dfrac{2 \times \text{scale distance}}{\text{mirror tilt radius}}$ |
| **Pneumatic** | $M \propto$ pressure difference amplification |
| **Electrical (LVDT-based)** | $M = \dfrac{\text{Output voltage sensitivity (V/mm)}}{\text{Input displacement}}$ |

## 2.4 The Physics Behind Each Major Instrument

### 2.4.1 Vernier Caliper

The **vernier principle** relies on *visual interpolation* between two scales. The smallest readable difference is governed by the *vernier constant*:

$$\text{Vernier constant} = s - v$$

where $s$ is one main-scale division and $v$ is the length of one vernier-scale division. If $n$ vernier divisions equal $(n-1)$ main-scale divisions:

$$n \cdot v = (n-1) \cdot s$$

Solving:

$$v = \frac{(n-1) \cdot s}{n} \quad \Rightarrow \quad LC = s - v = \frac{s}{n}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Read the math carefully:** The vernier constant is the *difference* between a main scale division and a vernier scale division. It is *not* just $1/n$ of a division — it is the *excess* of one over the other.

### 2.4.2 Micrometer Screw

The **micrometer screw principle** relies on *screw thread conversion of rotation to linear motion*:

$$\text{Axial movement} = \text{Lead} \times \text{Number of revolutions}$$

For a standard metric micrometer with a thread pitch of **0.5 mm**, one full rotation of the thimble advances the spindle by 0.5 mm. If the thimble is divided into 50 equal parts:

$$LC = \frac{0.5}{50} = 0.01 \text{ mm}$$

### 2.4.3 Sine Bar

The sine bar exploits the **sine function of a right triangle** to convert a known slip-gauge stack height into a precise angle:

$$\sin\theta = \frac{h}{L}$$

The advantages over bevel protractor:
- Higher accuracy (up to $\pm 5$ arc-seconds)
- Suitable for inspection of taper plugs, angle gauges
- Repeatability better than **0.001 mm / 100 mm**

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of the Inspection Framework

| Industry Sector | Critical Inspection Need |
|-----------------|--------------------------|
| **Aerospace** | Surface integrity, fatigue-critical dimensions (CMM, profilometer) |
| **Automotive** | High-volume dimensional checks (go/no-go gauges, SPC) |
| **Medical Devices** | Sub-micron tolerances, surface finish (laser interferometer) |
| **Tool Room** | Tool geometry, lead & pitch (toolmaker's microscope) |
| **Heavy Machinery** | Geometric tolerances on large parts (laser tracker, CMM) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Least Count of a Vernier Caliper

### Statement
For a vernier caliper where $n$ vernier scale divisions coincide with $(n-1)$ main scale divisions, derive the least count in terms of the smallest main scale division $s$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Let:
- $s$ = Length of one main scale division
- $v$ = Length of one vernier scale division
- $n$ = Total number of divisions on the vernier scale

By the **vernier coincidence condition**:

$$n \cdot v = (n-1) \cdot s$$

Solving for $v$:

$$v = \frac{(n-1) \cdot s}{n}$$

The **least count** (vernier constant) is the difference between a main scale division and a vernier scale division:

$$LC = s - v$$

Substituting the value of $v$:

$$LC = s - \frac{(n-1) \cdot s}{n}$$

Taking common denominator $n$:

$$LC = \frac{n \cdot s - (n-1) \cdot s}{n}$$

$$LC = \frac{n \cdot s - n \cdot s + s}{n}$$

$$LC = \frac{s}{n}$$

> **Conclusion:** 
> $$\boxed{LC_{\text{vernier}} = \frac{s}{n}}$$

**Numerical Example (KTU standard):**
- $s = 1$ mm, $n = 50$
- $LC = \dfrac{1}{50} = 0.02$ mm = **20 µm**

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Effective Dimension of Slip Gauge Combination

### Statement
When slip gauges are wrung together, derive the effective length of a stack given the nominal dimension and the number of wringing surfaces.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Each wrung interface of slip gauges carries an **adhered oil film** (and a thin layer of molecularly bonded water/oil) whose thickness is empirically determined to be approximately:

$$t_f \approx 0.0001 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ µm}$$

For a stack consisting of $k$ individual slip gauges, the number of wringing (mating) surfaces is $(k-1)$.

The **effective length** of the combination is:

$$L_{eff} = L_{nominal} + (k-1) \cdot t_f$$

$$\boxed{L_{eff} = L_{nominal} + (k-1) \times 0.0001 \text{ mm}}$$

**Example:** A stack of three slip gauges (1.07, 1.03, 1.00 mm) gives:
- $L_{nominal} = 1.07 + 1.03 + 1.00 = 3.10$ mm
- Number of wringing surfaces $= 3 - 1 = 2$
- $L_{eff} = 3.10 + 2 \times 0.0001 = 3.1002$ mm

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Angle Measurement Using Sine Bar

### Statement
A sine bar of length $L$ between its two rollers is used to set up a workpiece. A slip-gauge stack of height $h$ is placed under one roller. Derive the angle $\theta$ of the workpiece top surface with respect to the datum.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Consider the right triangle formed by:
- The hypotenuse = sine bar of length $L$
- The vertical side = slip-gauge stack height $h$
- The base = the surface plate (horizontal datum)

From the geometry of the setup, the **angle of inclination of the sine bar** is $\theta$.

By the **definition of sine in a right triangle**:

$$\sin\theta = \frac{\text{Opposite}}{\text{Hypotenuse}} = \frac{h}{L}$$

Therefore:

$$\theta = \arcsin\left(\frac{h}{L}\right)$$

For small angles (where $\sin\theta \approx \theta$ in radians):

$$\theta_{rad} \approx \frac{h}{L}$$

Converting to degrees:

$$\theta_{deg} = \frac{180}{\pi} \cdot \arcsin\left(\frac{h}{L}\right)$$

Or in **minutes of arc**:

$$\theta_{min} = \frac{180 \times 60}{\pi} \cdot \arcsin\left(\frac{h}{L}\right) \approx 3438 \cdot \arcsin\left(\frac{h}{L}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validity:** This derivation assumes the sine bar is perfectly rigid and the rollers are of identical diameter. Real-world corrections may be required for roller eccentricity and sine-bar error (typically $\pm 5$ µm over 100 mm for a Grade-0 sine bar).

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Surface Roughness Average ($R_a$)

### Statement
Derive the mathematical expression for the arithmetic mean roughness $R_a$ given a discretized surface profile $y_i$ sampled at $N$ points over a sampling length $L$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

The mean line of the profile is defined as the line that divides the profile such that the **sum of areas above the line equals the sum of areas below the line**.

Mathematically, the mean line has ordinate $\bar{y}$:

$$\bar{y} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i$$

The deviation of each point from the mean line:

$$z_i = y_i - \bar{y}$$

By definition of the mean line, the sum of positive and negative deviations cancel:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} z_i = 0$$

The **arithmetic mean roughness** $R_a$ is the average absolute deviation:

$$R_a = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert z_i \vert = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert y_i - \bar{y} \vert$$

In integral form (continuous profile):

$$R_a = \frac{1}{L} \int_0^L \vert y(x) - \bar{y} \vert \, dx$$

Since the mean line is set such that $\bar{y}$ is the centroid of the profile, this simplifies to the standard form:

$$\boxed{R_a = \frac{1}{L}\int_0^L \vert y(x) \vert \, dx}$$

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Tolerance Zone and Allowance

### Statement
For a 50 mm H7/g6 fit, derive the maximum clearance, minimum clearance, and tolerance zone.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Given:**
- Nominal size $D = 50$ mm
- Hole basis: H7 (lower deviation = 0, IT7 = 25 µm from ISO 286 tables for 50 mm)
- Shaft basis: g6 (upper deviation $es = -12$ µm, IT6 = 16 µm from ISO 286 tables for 50 mm)

**Step 1: Hole dimensions (H7)**

Lower deviation $EI = 0$

$$D_{hole,min} = D + EI = 50 + 0 = 50.000 \text{ mm}$$

Tolerance $T_H = IT7 = 0.025$ mm

$$D_{hole,max} = D_{hole,min} + T_H = 50.000 + 0.025 = 50.025 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Shaft dimensions (g6)**

Upper deviation $es = -0.012$ mm

$$D_{shaft,max} = D + es = 50 - 0.012 = 49.988 \text{ mm}$$

Tolerance $T_s = IT6 = 0.016$ mm

$$D_{shaft,min} = D_{shaft,max} - T_s = 49.988 - 0.016 = 49.972 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Clearance limits**

**Maximum clearance** (largest hole + smallest shaft):

$$C_{max} = D_{hole,max} - D_{shaft,min} = 50.025 - 49.972 = 0.053 \text{ mm}$$

**Minimum clearance** (smallest hole + largest shaft):

$$C_{min} = D_{hole,min} - D_{shaft,max} = 50.000 - 49.988 = 0.012 \text{ mm}$$

> **Conclusion:**
> $$\boxed{C_{min} = 0.012 \text{ mm}, \quad C_{max} = 0.053 \text{ mm}}$$

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Instrument Selection Decision Table

| Measurement Requirement | Best Instrument | Justification |
|--------------------------|------------------|---------------|
| Diameter of a turned shaft (50 mm) to ±0.02 mm | Vernier Caliper | Adequate LC, fast, low cost |
| Bore diameter (50 mm) to ±0.005 mm | Bore Gauge + Micrometer | Direct reading, repeatable |
| Surface roughness of ground surface (Ra 0.8 µm) | Stylus Profilometer | Quantitative Ra measurement |
| Angle of a dovetail to ±2 arc-minutes | Sine Bar with Slip Gauges | High accuracy angular setup |
| Runout of a grinding spindle to ±2 µm | Dial Test Indicator (DTI) | Sensitive to small runout |
| Profile of a complex stamping | Optical Profile Projector | 2D magnified image |
| 3D measurement of a gearbox housing | CMM with Probing | Multi-axis inspection |
| Thread pitch & effective diameter of M20 screw | Floating Carriage Micrometer + Thread Micrometer | Specialized thread measurement |
| Form deviation of a flat surface | Optical Flat + Monochromatic Light Source | Interferometric flatness check |
| Tooth profile of a spur gear | Gear Tester (e.g., Klingelnberg) | Specific to gear geometry |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[INSPECTION METHODS<br/>FOR MACHINED COMPONENTS] --> B[LINEAR<br/>DIMENSIONAL]
    A --> C[ANGULAR<br/>DIMENSIONAL]

```

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram: Typical Inspection Workflow

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Raw Machined<br/>Component] --> P2[Pre-Cleaning<br/>Station]
    P2 --> P3[Visual<br/>Inspection]
    P3 --> P4{Dimensional<br/>Deviation?}

    P4 -- YES --> P5[Identify Defect<br/>Type]

    P8 -- NO --> P6
    P8 -- YES --> P9[Surface Finish<br/>Check]

    P10 -- NO --> P6
    P10 -- YES --> P11[Document<br/>Measurements]
    P11 --> P12[Statistical<br/>Analysis SPC]
    P12 --> P13[Accept for<br/>Assembly]
    P6 --> P14[Root Cause<br/>Analysis]
    P14 --> P15[Process<br/>Feedback]
```

## 4.3 Architecture of a Coordinate Measuring Machine (CMM)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph CMM[Coordinate Measuring Machine]
        TBL[Granite Table<br/>Stable Base] --> XAXIS[X-Axis Rail<br/>Travel 800 mm]
        XAXIS --> YAXIS[Y-Axis Carriage<br/>Travel 600 mm]
        YAXIS --> ZAXIS[Z-Axis Spindle<br/>Travel 400 mm]
        ZAXIS --> PROBE[Touch Trigger Probe<br/>or Scanning Probe]
    end

    PROBE --> COMP[Component<br/>Fixture]
    COMP --> DAMP[Anti-Vibration<br/>Damping Pads]
    TBL --> DAMP

    CMM --> CTRL[DCS Controller<br/>and Software]
    CTRL --> MATH[Mathematical<br/>Algorithms]
    MATH --> GEOM[Geometric<br/>Feature Extraction]
    GEOM --> TOL[GD&T<br/>Evaluation]

    REP --> PRT[Print or<br/>Digital Certificate]

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Surface Roughness Measurement System

```mermaid
graph LR
    SURF[Machined<br/>Surface] --> STYLUS[Stylus Tip<br/>Radius 2 to 10 um]
    STYLUS --> PICKUP[Transducer<br/>Piezoelectric or LVDT]
    PICKUP --> AMP[Signal<br/>Amplifier]
    AMP --> FILT[Filter<br/>Cutoff Lambda C]
    FILT --> ADC[Analog to<br/>Digital Converter]
    ADC --> PROC[Digital Signal<br/>Processor]
    PROC --> CALC[Calculate Ra Rz Rt]
    CALC --> DISP[Display<br/>Numerical Readout]
    CALC --> PLOT[Plot Profile<br/>on Graph]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Cut-off wavelength ($\lambda_c$):** The sampling length is divided into multiple cut-off segments. For a given roughness range, ISO 4288 specifies the cut-off:
> - **$R_a$ 0.006–0.02 µm** → $\lambda_c = 0.08$ mm
> - **$R_a$ 0.02–0.1 µm** → $\lambda_c = 0.25$ mm
> - **$R_a$ 0.1–2 µm** → $\lambda_c = 0.8$ mm
> - **$R_a$ 2–10 µm** → $\lambda_c = 2.5$ mm
> - **$R_a$ > 10 µm** → $\lambda_c = 8.0$ mm
