<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Define the term "Least Count" of a measuring instrument. The smallest division on the main scale of a vernier caliper is 1 mm and 20 vernier divisions coincide with 19 main scale divisions. Calculate the least count.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Least Count (LC):** It is the smallest measurable increment that an instrument can resolve, i.e., the difference between one main scale division and one vernier scale division.

**Calculation:**

Given: $s = 1$ mm (smallest main scale division), $n = 20$ vernier divisions coincide with 19 main scale divisions.

**Step 1:** Find the length of one vernier division.

$$v = \frac{(n-1) \cdot s}{n} = \frac{19 \times 1}{20} = 0.95 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2:** Least count formula.

$$LC = s - v = 1 - 0.95 = 0.05 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{LC = 0.05 \text{ mm} = 50 \text{ µm}}$$

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**Q: Distinguish between Accuracy and Precision with a suitable example. State one instrument used for precision measurement of surface roughness.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Accuracy | Precision |
|--------|----------|-----------|
| Definition | Closeness to the *true value* | Closeness of repeated measurements to each other |
| Affected by | Systematic errors (calibration, zero error) | Random errors (instrument noise, operator skill) |
| Example | A ruler giving 49.8 mm for a 50 mm block (when corrected for zero error) | Five successive readings of 50.0, 50.0, 50.0, 50.0, 50.0 mm |

> **Example:** A CNC lathe produces a shaft of 50.00 mm. Five diameter measurements give 50.02, 50.02, 50.01, 50.03, 50.02 mm. The system is **precise** (low spread) but **inaccurate** (mean = 50.020, not 50.00).

**Instrument for surface roughness:** **Stylus-type Surface Profilometer** (e.g., Taylor Hobson Surtronic, Mitutoyo SJ-Series). It directly measures $R_a$, $R_z$, and $R_t$ parameters by tracing the surface with a diamond stylus.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of an Optical Profile Projector. State its applications in inspection of machined components.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> Sketch a labeled diagram of the Optical Profile Projector with components: Light Source, Condenser, Object Stage, Objective Lens, Projection Screen, and Reference Chart.

**Working Principle:**

The optical profile projector (also called a *shadowgraph*) operates on the principle of **shadow projection by transmitted light**.

1. **Light Source:** A high-intensity halogen or LED lamp emits a parallel beam of light via a condenser lens.
2. **Illumination:** The beam is directed upward through a transparent profile (or a silhouette of the component).
3. **Magnification:** A precision objective lens (10×, 20×, 50×, or 100×) magnifies the projected image.
4. **Projection:** The magnified image is cast on a **frosted glass screen** of diameter 300, 500, or 1000 mm.
5. **Measurement:** The magnified image is superimposed over **reference chart lines, scales, or CAD overlays**. Critical dimensions are measured directly from the screen.

**Magnification equation:**

$$M = \frac{D_{screen}}{D_{object}} = \frac{\text{Reference length on screen}}{\text{Actual length on part}}$$

**Step-by-Step Example:**
- Magnification $M = 50\times$
- Component feature (thread pitch) = 1.5 mm (actual)
- Image size on screen = $50 \times 1.5 = 75$ mm
- Least count on screen with 1 mm screen division = $\dfrac{1 \text{ mm (on screen)}}{50} = 0.02$ mm equivalent

**Applications:**
- Inspection of **threads** (form, pitch, angle)
- Inspection of **gear tooth profiles** (involute shape)
- Inspection of **stamping and press-tool profiles**
- **Form tools** and **cams**
- **Small precision components** like clock parts, instrument parts
- **First-off inspection** of machined features
- **Comparison with overlay charts** for go/no-go decisions

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: A 100 mm sine bar is used to set a workpiece to an angle. A slip gauge stack of 25.000 mm is placed under one roller. Calculate (i) the angle set in degrees, and (ii) the angle in degrees-minutes-seconds.**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- Sine bar length $L = 100$ mm
- Slip gauge height $h = 25.000$ mm

**Step 1:** Use the sine bar formula.

$$\sin\theta = \frac{h}{L} = \frac{25.000}{100} = 0.25$$

**Step 2:** Calculate angle in degrees.

$$\theta = \arcsin(0.25)$$

$$\theta = 14.4775^\circ$$

**Step 3:** Convert to degrees-minutes-seconds.

Decimal part: $0.4775^\circ$

Minutes: $0.4775 \times 60 = 28.65$ arc-minutes

Seconds: $0.65 \times 60 = 39$ arc-seconds

$$\boxed{\theta = 14^\circ \, 28' \, 39''}$$

**Step 4:** Verification.

$$\sin(14^\circ \, 28' \, 39'') = 0.24999 \approx 0.25 \quad \checkmark$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: Explain the construction and working of a Coordinate Measuring Machine (CMM). List any four geometric tolerances that can be evaluated on a CMM.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> Sketch: a CMM with three mutually perpendicular axes (X, Y, Z), probe, granite table, and CNC controller.

**Construction:**

A **Coordinate Measuring Machine (CMM)** consists of:

1. **Base / Granite Table:** A thermally stable, vibration-damped granite surface (thermal expansion coefficient $\approx 5 \times 10^{-6}/^\circ$C) on which the workpiece is placed.
2. **Three Linear Axes (X, Y, Z):** Each axis consists of a precision ball screw or linear motor drive, air bearings, and a glass/steel linear scale encoder (resolution up to **0.0001 mm**).
3. **Probe System:** Either:
   - **Touch-trigger probe** (kinematic resistive, e.g., Renishaw TP200)
   - **Scanning probe** (analog, e.g., Renishaw SP25, continuous contact)
   - **Optical probe** (laser triangulation, for non-contact)
4. **Controller & Software:** A computer running metrology software (e.g., Calypso, PC-DMIS, CMM Manager) that calculates geometric features from the cloud of probed points.
5. **Fixture / Clamping:** Modular fixturing (e.g., Renishaw Acclaim) to hold the component repeatably.

**Working:**

- The component is placed on the granite table and probed at several points using a contact or non-contact probe.
- The probe is moved along programmable paths, and its 3D coordinates are recorded.
- Mathematical algorithms fit **ideal geometric features** (lines, planes, circles, cylinders, spheres, cones) to the measured point cloud.
- Geometric dimensions and tolerances (GD&T) are then computed.

**Four Geometric Tolerances Evaluated:**

1. **Flatness** — Maximum distance between two parallel planes enclosing the surface points.
2. **Cylindricity** — Radial distance between two coaxial cylinders enclosing all points.
3. **Perpendicularity** — Perpendicular distance between a measured line/plane and a datum line/plane.
4. **Position** — Deviation of a feature's center from its theoretically exact position relative to a datum reference frame.
5. (Bonus) **Concentricity / Coaxiality** — Distance between the axes of two cylindrical features.
6. (Bonus) **Circular Runout** — Variation in indicator reading as the part is rotated about an axis.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: The following observations were taken on a surface profilometer tracing a ground surface over a sampling length of 4 mm: profile ordinates (in µm) at 11 equally spaced points are: +2, -1, +3, -2, +1, 0, -1, +2, -3, +1, +2. Calculate the value of $R_a$ in micrometers.**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $N = 11$ points, profile ordinates $y_i = \{+2, -1, +3, -2, +1, 0, -1, +2, -3, +1, +2\}$ (in µm).

**Step 1:** Compute the mean line ordinate.

$$\bar{y} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i$$

Sum: $2 + (-1) + 3 + (-2) + 1 + 0 + (-1) + 2 + (-3) + 1 + 2 = 4$ µm

$$\bar{y} = \frac{4}{11} = 0.3636 \text{ µm}$$

**Step 2:** Compute deviation of each point from the mean line, $z_i = y_i - \bar{y}$.

| Point $i$ | $y_i$ (µm) | $z_i = y_i - 0.3636$ (µm) | $\vert z_i \vert$ (µm) |
|-----------|------------|----------------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | +2 | +1.6364 | 1.6364 |
| 2 | -1 | -1.3636 | 1.3636 |
| 3 | +3 | +2.6364 | 2.6364 |
| 4 | -2 | -2.3636 | 2.3636 |
| 5 | +1 | +0.6364 | 0.6364 |
| 6 | 0 | -0.3636 | 0.3636 |
| 7 | -1 | -1.3636 | 1.3636 |
| 8 | +2 | +1.6364 | 1.6364 |
| 9 | -3 | -3.3636 | 3.3636 |
| 10 | +1 | +0.6364 | 0.6364 |
| 11 | +2 | +1.6364 | 1.6364 |

**Step 3:** Sum the absolute deviations.

$$\sum \vert z_i \vert = 1.6364 + 1.3636 + 2.6364 + 2.3636 + 0.6364 + 0.3636 + 1.3636 + 1.6364 + 3.3636 + 0.6364 + 1.6364 = 17.6364 \text{ µm}$$

**Step 4:** Compute $R_a$.

$$R_a = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert z_i \vert = \frac{17.6364}{11}$$

$$\boxed{R_a \approx 1.603 \text{ µm}}$$

**Step 5:** Engineering interpretation.

According to **ISO 1302 / IS 3073**, the obtained $R_a \approx 1.6$ µm is typical of a **fine ground surface** (N6 grade), suitable for sliding fits, low-speed bearings, and sealing surfaces.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget to **shift the profile to the mean line** before computing absolute deviations. Always subtract the mean $\bar{y}$ from each $y_i$. A common error is to simply average $\vert y_i \vert$, which yields a different (incorrect) result. The KTU marking scheme typically deducts **1–2 marks** for this oversight.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Zones (as observed in KTU valuation reports):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Wringing Film:** In slip-gauge combination problems, students write $L_{eff} = L_{nominal}$, losing 1 mark.
> 2. **Inverting the Sine Formula:** Writing $\theta = h/L$ (a common error) instead of $\theta = \arcsin(h/L)$. The angle must be computed via the *inverse sine function*, not a simple ratio.
> 3. **Unit Confusion in $R_a$ Calculation:** Mixing micrometres and millimetres. If the problem is in mm, give $R_a$ in mm (or convert explicitly to µm).
> 4. **Zero-Error Neglect in Micrometer:** When the micrometer reading on a flat zero is not exactly 0.00 mm (e.g., 0.03 mm), the error must be subtracted from the actual reading. Many students ignore this.
> 5. **Confusing $R_a$ with $R_z$:** $R_z$ is the *ten-point height*, NOT the average. Their values differ (typically $R_z \approx 4 R_a$ to $7 R_a$ for similar surfaces).
> 6. **Selection of Wrong Comparator Type:** Pneumatic comparators for soft materials (rubber, plastics), optical comparators for fine threads, electrical comparators for low-force applications.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU ESE Module 3**

### Core Concepts to Memorize

- **Least Count (LC):** $LC_{vernier} = s/n$, $LC_{micrometer} = \text{Pitch}/\text{Divisions}$
- **Sine Bar Angle:** $\sin\theta = h/L$, with $h$ = slip-gauge stack height, $L$ = roller center distance
- **Slip Gauge Film:** $L_{eff} = L_{nominal} + (k-1) \times 0.0001$ mm
- **Tolerance:** $T = D_{max} - D_{min}$
- **Clearance Fit:** $C_{min} = D_{hole,min} - D_{shaft,max}$
- **Surface Roughness:** $R_a = \dfrac{1}{L}\int_0^L \vert y(x) \vert \, dx$

### Instrument-Key Map

| Instrument | Mandatory LC | Best For |
|------------|-------------|----------|
| Steel Rule | 1.0 mm | Layout, rough check |
| Vernier Caliper | 0.02 mm | OD, ID, depth |
| Micrometer | 0.01 mm | External diameter |
| Bevel Protractor | 5' (arc-min) | Bevel angle |
| Sine Bar | $\sim 5''$ (arc-sec) | Precision angles |
| DTI | 0.01 mm | Runout, alignment |
| Optical Projector | 0.001 mm (with 100×) | Profile, threads |
| CMM | 0.001 mm (or better) | 3D complex parts |
| Profilometer | 0.001 µm Ra | Surface finish |

### GD&T Quick Reference

- **Form:** Straightness, Flatness, Circularity, Cylindricity
- **Orientation:** Parallelism, Perpendicularity, Angularity
- **Location:** Position, Concentricity, Symmetry
- **Run-out:** Circular Run-out, Total Run-out

### Standard References (no fabrication)

- **IS 919-1958** — Indian Standard for limits and fits
- **ISO 286-1:2010** — ISO limits and fits
- **ISO 1302** — Surface texture indication
- **ISO 4287** — Surface roughness parameters
- **ISO 4288** — Rules for cut-off selection
- **ASME Y14.5-2018** — GD&T standard
- **IS 2062 / IS 3073** — Surface finish indication

### High-Probability KTU Question Types

1. **Vernier caliper / micrometer LC derivation** — ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (Always asked)
2. **Sine bar angle calculation** — ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
3. **Slip gauge combination with wringing film** — ⭐⭐⭐⭐
4. **Limits and fits problem (hole-shaft)** — ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
5. **$R_a$ numerical problem** — ⭐⭐⭐⭐
6. **CMM working / GD&T features** — ⭐⭐⭐⭐
7. **Comparator classification** — ⭐⭐⭐
8. **Surface roughness parameters ($R_a$, $R_z$, $R_t$)** — ⭐⭐⭐⭐

### Mnemonic for Instrument Selection

> **"SAVVADI MOSS PC"** — *S*ine bar, *A*ngle dekkor, *V*ernier caliper, *V*ernier height gauge, *A*ir gauge, *D*TI, *I*nterferometer, *M*icrometer, *O*ptical projector, *S*lip gauge, *S*tylus profilometer, *P*rofile projector, *C*MM

### Final Exam Tip

> When the question says "**with neat sketch**," always draw a **fully labeled diagram** — KTU valuation awards **1–2 marks** for a clean sketch alone, even if the explanation is partial. Use a ruler for axes, and label every key component.

---

**End of Notes — Module 3: Inspection Methods for Machined Components**

*Total preparation time recommended: 6–8 hours of focused study, with at least 2 hours dedicated to solving numerical problems from the KTU Question Bank above.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Statistical Process Control in Machining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET522 - MECHANICS OF METAL CUTTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Statistical Process Control in Machining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 : Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Statistical Process Control (SPC)** in machining is a systematic, data-driven quality engineering discipline that employs statistical methodologies to monitor, measure, and control a machining process in real-time. It quantifies process performance through the continuous sampling of critical-to-quality (CTQ) output parameters such as **dimensional tolerance**, **surface roughness (Ra)**, **tool wear**, and **cutting force variation**, ensuring that the manufacturing process operates at its full inherent capability with minimum assignable-cause variation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** SPC is positioned as a vital tool for achieving *Six-Sigma quality levels* and *zero-defect manufacturing* in advanced machining environments (CNC, non-traditional machining).

The foundational statistical identity governing SPC in any variable measurement is:

$$X = \mu + \varepsilon_{c} + \varepsilon_{s}$$

Where $\mu$ is the target mean, $\varepsilon_{c}$ is common-cause (random) variation, and $\varepsilon_{s}$ is special-cause (assignable) variation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Engineering Intuition

Imagine a **traditional Kerala houseboat cruising on the backwaters**. The captain does not wait for the boat to crash into the bank to act; he constantly observes a compass (the *mean*) and watches the wind speed indicator (the *variation*). 

- If the wind is steady and the boat drifts slightly with the current ($\varepsilon_{c}$), that is *common-cause variation* — natural, expected, and corrected only by sailing skill.
- If a sudden squall hits ($\varepsilon_{s}$), that is *special-cause variation* — it demands immediate intervention (trim the sail, change direction).

**SPC in machining is exactly this**: you don't wait for a defective batch of gears. You continuously measure the diameter, plot it on a **control chart**, and intervene the moment the process shows signs of drifting beyond its natural statistical boundaries.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** SPC distinguishes between *common-cause* and *special-cause* variation. The tool wear progression in a turning operation, for example, causes *systematic drift* (common cause), while a sudden tool breakage or material hardness change is a *special cause* requiring immediate stoppage.

### 1.3 Core SPC Terminology in Machining Context

| Term | Definition | Machining Example |
|------|------------|-------------------|
| **Process Mean ($\bar{\bar{X}}$)** | Average of subgroup averages | Average diameter of turned shaft |
| **Process Standard Deviation ($\sigma$)** | Spread of measured values | Variation in surface roughness (Ra) |
| **Subgroup (n)** | Small sample taken at one instant | 5 consecutive workpieces every 30 minutes |
| **Control Limits (UCL, LCL)** | $\pm 3\sigma$ natural process boundaries | 25.02 mm, 24.98 mm on a Ø25 mm shaft |
| **Specification Limits (USL, LSL)** | Design-engineered tolerance limits | 25.05 mm, 24.95 mm |
| **Cpk** | Capability index accounting for centering | Minimum value of upper/lower indices |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction (Frequently Tested in KTU):** *Control Limits* (statistical, set at $\pm 3\sigma$) are **not** the same as *Specification Limits* (engineering, set by design tolerance). A process can be in statistical control but still produce defective parts if it is poorly centered or has insufficient capability.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 : Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Pillars of Process Variation

Every machining process exhibits variation from two fundamentally different sources:

**A. Common-Cause Variation (Chance Cause, $\varepsilon_{c}$)**
- Inherent to the process — present even when everything is "running normally"
- Examples in machining:
  - Microscopic vibration of toolholder
  - Slight fluctuations in workpiece material hardness (within batch)
  - Thermal expansion of leadscrew
  - Random variation in chip load
- **Statistical property:** Forms a stable, predictable **Normal (Gaussian) Distribution**
- **Action:** Requires fundamental process redesign, not stop-and-adjust

**B. Special-Cause Variation (Assignable Cause, $\varepsilon_{s}$)**
- Sporadic, unpredictable, often large-magnitude deviations
- Examples in machining:
  - Broken cutting tool insert
  - Sudden power fluctuation / spindle bearing failure
  - Operator error (wrong tool offset loaded)
  - Batch of raw material with incorrect hardness
- **Statistical property:** Disrupts the normal distribution, appears as outliers or trends
- **Action:** Identify root cause, eliminate, then resume production

> [!NOTE]
> **Western Electric Rules (Frequently Asked):** A process is considered *out of statistical control* if any of the following occur on a control chart: 1 point beyond $\pm 3\sigma$, 2 of 3 consecutive points beyond $\pm 2\sigma$, 4 of 5 consecutive points beyond $\pm 1\sigma$, or 8 consecutive points on one side of the centerline.

### 2.2 Types of Control Charts Used in Machining

Machining quality engineers deploy four principal control charts, each designed for a specific data type:

**1. $\bar{X}$ – R Chart (Mean and Range Chart)**
- Used for: **Variable data** with subgroup size $2 \leq n \leq 10$
- Machining application: Monitoring bore diameter, shaft length, surface roughness
- Most common chart in CNC turning/milling cells

**2. $\bar{X}$ – S Chart (Mean and Standard Deviation Chart)**
- Used for: Variable data with subgroup size $n > 10$
- Machining application: High-volume automotive component production

**3. p-Chart (Proportion Defective Chart)**
- Used for: **Attribute data** (pass/fail, go/no-go)
- Machining application: Monitoring fraction of defective parts per shift

**4. c-Chart (Defects per Unit Chart)**
- Used for: Number of defects per item
- Machining application: Counting surface scratches, burrs, or machining marks per workpiece

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Variables & Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_{2}\bar{R}$ | Upper control limit for mean chart | $A_{2}$ = control chart constant (function of n) |
| 2 | $LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_{2}\bar{R}$ | Lower control limit for mean chart | — |
| 3 | $UCL_{R} = D_{4}\bar{R}$ | Upper control limit for range chart | $D_{4}$ = constant from SPC tables |
| 4 | $LCL_{R} = D_{3}\bar{R}$ | Lower control limit for range chart | $D_{3}$ = constant from SPC tables |
| 5 | $\hat{\sigma} = \dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_{2}}$ | Process std. deviation estimate from range | $d_{2}$ = bias correction constant |
| 6 | $C_{p} = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Process capability (spread only) | $C_{p} \geq 1.33$ is generally acceptable |
| 7 | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left( \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\; \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} \right)$ | Process capability (centered) | $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ for capable process |
| 8 | $C_{pk} = C_{p}(1-k)$ | Relationship form | $k = \dfrac{\mid \mu - \text{Target} \mid}{(USL-LSL)/2}$ |
| 9 | $\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ | Standard error of the mean | $n$ = subgroup size |
| 10 | $UCL_{p} = \bar{p} + 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}$ | p-chart upper control limit | $\bar{p}$ = average fraction defective |
| 11 | $UCL_{c} = \bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ | c-chart upper control limit | $\bar{c}$ = average defects per unit |
| 12 | $DPMO = \dfrac{\text{Defects} \times 10^{6}}{\text{Opportunities}}$ | Six-Sigma metric | — |
| 13 | $\text{Sigma Level} = 1.5 + \dfrac{Z_{\text{short-term}}}{1}$ | Motorola's shifted Six-Sigma | Includes 1.5$\sigma$ drift |

**Standard Control Chart Constants (MUST MEMORIZE FOR KTU):**

| Subgroup Size $n$ | $A_{2}$ | $D_{3}$ | $D_{4}$ | $d_{2}$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 2 | 1.880 | 0 | 3.267 | 1.128 |
| 3 | 1.023 | 0 | 2.575 | 1.693 |
| 4 | 0.729 | 0 | 2.282 | 2.059 |
| 5 | 0.577 | 0 | 2.114 | 2.326 |
| 6 | 0.483 | 0 | 2.004 | 2.534 |
| 7 | 0.419 | 0.076 | 1.924 | 2.704 |
| 8 | 0.373 | 0.136 | 1.864 | 2.847 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mark Saver:** For $n \leq 6$, $D_{3} = 0$, meaning $LCL_{R} = 0$. Students commonly write $LCL_{R} = D_{3}\bar{R}$ and substitute a wrong constant — *lose 2 marks* in the process.

### 2.4 Process Capability Indices — Engineering Interpretation

- **$C_{p} < 1.00$:** Process is **incapable** — the natural variation is wider than the specification window. Immediate process redesign required.
- **$1.00 \leq C_{p} < 1.33$:** Process is **marginally capable**. Some defects will occur. Acceptable for non-critical features.
- **$1.33 \leq C_{p} < 1.67$:** Process is **capable**. Standard acceptable range for general engineering.
- **$C_{p} \geq 1.67$:** Process is **highly capable** (approaching Six-Sigma).

The **$C_{pk}$** index additionally accounts for process centering. A process can have $C_{p} = 1.5$ but $C_{pk} = 0.9$ if the mean has drifted off-target. **This is a classic KTU exam trap!**

## SECTION 3 : Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the $\bar{X}$ Chart Control Limits

**Starting Premise:** If a machining process is in statistical control, the measured quality characteristic $X$ follows a Normal distribution with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$.

$$X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^{2})$$

The probability that a single observation falls within $\mu \pm 3\sigma$ is:

$$P(\mu - 3\sigma < X < \mu + 3\sigma) = 0.9973$$

This is the famous **"Three-Sigma Limits"** rule — the foundation of all SPC control limits.

**Step 1:** For a subgroup of size $n$ taken at one instant, the subgroup mean $\bar{X}$ is distributed as:

$$\bar{X} \sim N\!\left(\mu, \frac{\sigma^{2}}{n}\right)$$

This is the **Central Limit Theorem** applied to subgroup means.

**Step 2:** The standard deviation of the subgroup mean (standard error) is:

$$\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the $\pm 3\sigma$ rule to $\bar{X}$:

$$\text{Control Limits} = \mu \pm 3 \cdot \sigma_{\bar{X}} = \mu \pm \frac{3\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

**Step 4:** In practice, we don't know $\mu$ and $\sigma$, so we estimate them from the sample data:
- $\hat{\mu} = \bar{\bar{X}}$ (grand average of subgroup means)
- $\hat{\sigma} = \dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_{2}}$ (estimated from average range)

**Step 5:** Substituting these estimates:

$$UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + 3 \cdot \frac{\bar{R}/d_{2}}{\sqrt{n}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_{2}\bar{R}$$

where $A_{2} = \dfrac{3}{d_{2}\sqrt{n}}$ is the tabulated control chart constant.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Derivation.** The same logic gives the R-chart limits using $UCL_{R} = D_{4}\bar{R}$ where $D_{4} = 1 + \dfrac{3d_{3}}{d_{2}}$.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Pattern)

**Problem Statement (Modeled on KTU 2022 University Exam):**

In a CNC turning cell, the diameter of a shaft (target = 25.00 mm) is monitored using an $\bar{X}$-R chart. Five subgroups, each of size $n = 5$, are collected at hourly intervals. The subgroup means ($\bar{X}_{i}$) and ranges ($R_{i}$) in mm are:

| Subgroup $i$ | $\bar{X}_{i}$ (mm) | $R_{i}$ (mm) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 25.02 | 0.04 |
| 2 | 24.99 | 0.05 |
| 3 | 25.01 | 0.03 |
| 4 | 25.03 | 0.06 |
| 5 | 24.98 | 0.04 |

**Specification:** USL = 25.10 mm, LSL = 24.90 mm.

**Required:**
(a) Construct the $\bar{X}$ and R control charts.
(b) Compute the process capability indices $C_{p}$ and $C_{pk}$.
(c) Comment on process capability and statistical control.

---

#### **Part (a): Control Chart Construction — Full Step-by-Step Solution**

**Step 1: Calculate the grand mean ($\bar{\bar{X}}$)**

$$\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k}\bar{X}_{i}$$

where $k$ = number of subgroups = 5.

$$\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{25.02 + 24.99 + 25.01 + 25.03 + 24.98}{5}$$

$$\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{125.03}{5} = 25.006 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the average range ($\bar{R}$)**

$$\bar{R} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k}R_{i} = \frac{0.04 + 0.05 + 0.03 + 0.06 + 0.04}{5}$$

$$\bar{R} = \frac{0.22}{5} = 0.044 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Select control chart constants for $n = 5$**

From the SPC constants table: $A_{2} = 0.577$, $D_{3} = 0$, $D_{4} = 2.114$.

**Step 4: Compute $\bar{X}$ chart control limits**

$$UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_{2}\bar{R} = 25.006 + (0.577)(0.044)$$

$$UCL_{\bar{X}} = 25.006 + 0.02539 = 25.031 \text{ mm}$$

$$LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_{2}\bar{R} = 25.006 - 0.02539$$

$$LCL_{\bar{X}} = 24.981 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5: Compute R chart control limits**

$$UCL_{R} = D_{4}\bar{R} = (2.114)(0.044) = 0.093 \text{ mm}$$

$$LCL_{R} = D_{3}\bar{R} = (0)(0.044) = 0 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6: Check all subgroup means and ranges against the limits**

| Subgroup | $\bar{X}_{i}$ | Within $\bar{X}$ limits [24.981, 25.031]? | $R_{i}$ | Within R limits [0, 0.093]? |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 25.02 | ✓ In-control | 0.04 | ✓ In-control |
| 2 | 24.99 | ✓ In-control | 0.05 | ✓ In-control |
| 3 | 25.01 | ✓ In-control | 0.03 | ✓ In-control |
| 4 | 25.03 | ✓ In-control | 0.06 | ✓ In-control |
| 5 | 24.98 | ✓ In-control (just barely) | 0.04 | ✓ In-control |

**Conclusion for Part (a):** The process is **in statistical control**.

---

#### **Part (b): Process Capability Index Calculation**

**Step 1: Estimate process standard deviation**

For $n = 5$, $d_{2} = 2.326$.

$$\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_{2}} = \frac{0.044}{2.326} = 0.01892 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Calculate $C_{p}$ (process spread capability)**

$$C_{p} = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \frac{25.10 - 24.90}{6 \times 0.01892}$$

$$C_{p} = \frac{0.20}{0.1135} = 1.762$$

**Step 3: Calculate $C_{pk}$ (centered capability)**

$$C_{pk,\text{upper}} = \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{25.10 - 25.006}{3 \times 0.01892}$$

$$C_{pk,\text{upper}} = \frac{0.094}{0.0568} = 1.656$$

$$C_{pk,\text{lower}} = \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \frac{25.006 - 24.90}{3 \times 0.01892}$$

$$C_{pk,\text{lower}} = \frac{0.106}{0.0568} = 1.868$$

$$C_{pk} = \min(1.656, \; 1.868) = 1.656$$

**Step 4: Calculate centering factor $k$**

$$k = \frac{\mid \mu - \text{Target} \mid}{(USL - LSL)/2} = \frac{\mid 25.006 - 25.00 \mid}{0.10}$$

$$k = \frac{0.006}{0.10} = 0.06 \;(6\%)$$

---

#### **Part (c): Engineering Interpretation**

| Parameter | Value | Interpretation |
|-----------|-------|----------------|
| $C_{p}$ | 1.762 | Highly capable spread; well above 1.33 threshold |
| $C_{pk}$ | 1.656 | Capable and reasonably well-centered |
| $k$ | 0.06 (6%) | Process is slightly off-center (shifted +0.006 mm) |
| Statistical Control | ✓ | All points within $\pm 3\sigma$ limits |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Verdict:** The turning process is **capable ($C_{pk} = 1.656 > 1.33$) and in statistical control**. Recommended action: minor offset adjustment to tool zero (shift tool by $-0.006$ mm) to bring $C_{pk}$ closer to $C_{p}$, then re-validate.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: When to Take Action in SPC

| Observation on Chart | Diagnosis | Action Required |
|----------------------|-----------|-----------------|
| All points within $\pm 3\sigma$, random pattern | Process in control | Continue production |
| 1 point beyond $\pm 3\sigma$ | Special cause present | Stop, investigate root cause |
| 7 consecutive points above/below centerline | Process mean shift | Recalibrate, check tool offset |
| 6 points steadily increasing or decreasing | Tool wear trend | Schedule tool change |
| 2 of 3 points beyond $\pm 2\sigma$ (same side) | Warning of instability | Increase inspection frequency |

## SECTION 5 : KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Define Statistical Process Control (SPC). Differentiate between common-cause and special-cause variation with one machining example each.*

**Model Answer:**

**Statistical Process Control (SPC):** SPC is a data-driven quality management methodology that uses statistical techniques, primarily control charts, to monitor, measure, and control a manufacturing process in real time, ensuring that it operates at its full inherent capability with minimum assignable-cause variation.

**Common-Cause Variation (Chance Cause):**
- Definition: Inherent, natural variation that exists in a process at all times; it represents the *voice of the process*.
- Machining Example: Microscopic vibration of the toolholder, slight thermal expansion of the leadscrew, or minor fluctuations in workpiece hardness within a single batch.
- Action: Eliminated only through fundamental process redesign.

**Special-Cause Variation (Assignable Cause):**
- Definition: Sporadic, non-random, often large-magnitude deviations caused by identifiable external factors.
- Machining Example: Sudden tool insert breakage, power surge affecting spindle RPM, or operator loading the wrong tool offset.
- Action: Identified via control chart out-of-control signals, then eliminated through root-cause analysis.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often interchange the two causes or give vague examples like "machine vibration" without specifying whether it is normal ambient vibration (common) or abnormal bearing failure (special). Use *quantifiable* and *process-specific* examples to earn full marks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*What is meant by Process Capability Index $C_{pk}$? A CNC turning process has USL = 30.05 mm, LSL = 29.95 mm, with process mean $\mu$ = 30.01 mm and standard deviation $\sigma$ = 0.012 mm. Calculate $C_{pk}$ and comment on the process capability.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition of $C_{pk}$:** The Process Capability Index $C_{pk}$ is a statistical measure of the ability of a process to produce output within specification limits, *adjusted for process centering*. It is the minimum of the upper and lower capability indices and indicates how close the process mean is to the nearest specification limit, measured in units of $3\sigma$.

**Given:**
- USL = 30.05 mm
- LSL = 29.95 mm
- $\mu$ = 30.01 mm
- $\sigma$ = 0.012 mm

**Upper Capability Index:**

$$C_{pk,\text{upper}} = \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{30.05 - 30.01}{3 \times 0.012} = \frac{0.04}{0.036} = 1.111$$

**Lower Capability Index:**

$$C_{pk,\text{lower}} = \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \frac{30.01 - 29.95}{3 \times 0.012} = \frac{0.06}{0.036} = 1.667$$

**Process Capability Index:**

$$C_{pk} = \min(1.111, \; 1.667) = 1.111$$

**Comment:** Since $C_{pk} = 1.111 < 1.33$, the process is **marginally capable but not meeting the standard capability threshold**. Additionally, the process is **off-center** (closer to the USL), and corrective action is required to shift the mean downward by 0.01 mm to improve centering.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students frequently compute only one index and forget to take the minimum. Always calculate **both** $C_{pk,\text{upper}}$ and $C_{pk,\text{lower}}$ and select the **minimum** — this is the actual $C_{pk}$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of an $\bar{X}$-R control chart. State the formulas for Upper and Lower Control Limits. [7 Marks]

**(b)** In a milling operation, the width of a slot is monitored. Six subgroups of size 5 are collected, with the following data (in mm):

| Subgroup | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 10.02 | 10.01 | 10.03 | 10.00 | 10.02 |
| 2 | 10.04 | 10.03 | 10.05 | 10.02 | 10.04 |
| 3 | 10.01 | 10.00 | 10.02 | 10.01 | 10.03 |
| 4 | 10.05 | 10.06 | 10.04 | 10.05 | 10.04 |
| 5 | 10.03 | 10.02 | 10.04 | 10.03 | 10.05 |
| 6 | 10.07 | 10.08 | 10.06 | 10.07 | 10.09 |

Construct the $\bar{X}$ and R charts. If the specifications are USL = 10.15 mm and LSL = 9.90 mm, calculate $C_{p}$ and $C_{pk}$. Is the process capable? [7 Marks]

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) Solution — $\bar{X}$-R Chart Construction & Working:**

**Definition:** The $\bar{X}$-R chart is a pair of control charts used together to monitor the process mean ($\bar{X}$ chart) and process variability (R chart) for variable data with subgroup sizes typically $2 \leq n \leq 10$.

**Construction Steps:**
1. Collect $k$ subgroups, each of size $n$ (typically $n = 5$), at rational time intervals.
2. For each subgroup, compute the mean $\bar{X}_{i} = \dfrac{\sum X_{ij}}{n}$ and range $R_{i} = X_{\max} - X_{\min}$.
3. Calculate the grand mean $\bar{\bar{X}} = \dfrac{\sum \bar{X}_{i}}{k}$ and the average range $\bar{R} = \dfrac{\sum R_{i}}{k}$.
4. Compute control limits using tabulated constants $A_{2}$, $D_{3}$, $D_{4}$ for the chosen $n$.

**Formulas:**

$$UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_{2}\bar{R}, \quad LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_{2}\bar{R}$$

$$UCL_{R} = D_{4}\bar{R}, \quad LCL_{R} = D_{3}\bar{R}$$

**Working:** Plot subgroup means and ranges against the time sequence. Investigate any point outside the control limits or any non-random pattern (trend, run, cycle) as a special cause requiring corrective action.

**Sketch (ASCII Representation):**

```
   | UCL  ---------------------●----  (UCL_Xbar)
X  |        ●  ●                   
b  | CL   ---------------------        (Centerline = X_double_bar)
a  |            ●  ●    ●  ●         
r  | LCL  ---------------------        (LCL_Xbar)
   |____________________________ Time

   | UCL  ----●-----●---●---         (UCL_R)
R  |           ●  ●       ●         
   | CL   ---------------------        (Centerline = R_bar)
   |       ●                          
   | LCL  ---------------------        (LCL_R = 0 for n <= 6)
   |____________________________ Time
```

---

**Part (b) Solution — Numerical Computation:**

**Step 1: Compute $\bar{X}_{i}$ and $R_{i}$ for each subgroup**

For Subgroup 1: $\bar{X}_{1} = (10.02+10.01+10.03+10.00+10.02)/5 = 50.08/5 = 10.016$ mm
For Subgroup 2: $\bar{X}_{2} = (10.04+10.03+10.05+10.02+10.04)/5 = 50.18/5 = 10.036$ mm
For Subgroup 3: $\bar{X}_{3} = (10.01+10.00+10.02+10.01+10.03)/5 = 50.07/5 = 10.014$ mm
For Subgroup 4: $\bar{X}_{4} = (10.05+10.06+10.04+10.05+10.04)/5 = 50.24/5 = 10.048$ mm
For Subgroup 5: $\bar{X}_{5} = (10.03+10.02+10.04+10.03+10.05)/5 = 50.17/5 = 10.034$ mm
For Subgroup 6: $\bar{X}_{6} = (10.07+10.08+10.06+10.07+10.09)/5 = 50.37/5 = 10.074$ mm

Range for each subgroup:
$R_{1} = 10.03 - 10.00 = 0.03$ mm
$R_{2} = 10.05 - 10.02 = 0.03$ mm
$R_{3} = 10.03 - 10.00 = 0.03$ mm
$R_{4} = 10.06 - 10.04 = 0.02$ mm
$R_{5} = 10.05 - 10.02 = 0.03$ mm
$R_{6} = 10.09 - 10.06 = 0.03$ mm

**Step 2: Compute $\bar{\bar{X}}$ and $\bar{R}$**

$$\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{10.016+10.036+10.014+10.048+10.034+10.074}{6} = \frac{60.222}{6} = 10.037 \text{ mm}$$

$$\bar{R} = \frac{0.03+0.03+0.03+0.02+0.03+0.03}{6} = \frac{0.17}{6} = 0.02833 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Control chart constants for $n = 5$**

$A_{2} = 0.577$, $D_{3} = 0$, $D_{4} = 2.114$, $d_{2} = 2.326$

**Step 4: Control limits for $\bar{X}$ chart**

$$UCL_{\bar{X}} = 10.037 + (0.577)(0.02833) = 10.037 + 0.01635 = 10.053 \text{ mm}$$

$$LCL_{\bar{X}} = 10.037 - 0.01635 = 10.021 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5: Control limits for R chart**

$$UCL_{R} = (2.114)(0.02833) = 0.0599 \text{ mm}$$

$$LCL_{R} = 0 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6: In-Control Check**

Subgroup 4 ($\bar{X}_{4} = 10.048$) is just inside $UCL_{\bar{X}} = 10.053$.
Subgroup 6 ($\bar{X}_{6} = 10.074$) is **above** $UCL_{\bar{X}} = 10.053$ — **OUT OF CONTROL!**

**Step 7: Process Capability (assuming stable conditions for $\sigma$ estimate)**

$$\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_{2}} = \frac{0.02833}{2.326} = 0.01218 \text{ mm}$$

$$C_{p} = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \frac{10.15 - 9.90}{6 \times 0.01218} = \frac{0.25}{0.07308} = 3.420$$

$$C_{pk,\text{upper}} = \frac{10.15 - 10.037}{3 \times 0.01218} = \frac{0.113}{0.03654} = 3.092$$

$$C_{pk,\text{lower}} = \frac{10.037 - 9.90}{3 \times 0.01218} = \frac{0.137}{0.03654} = 3.750$$

$$C_{pk} = \min(3.092, 3.750) = 3.092$$

**Conclusion:** The process is **highly capable** ($C_{pk} = 3.092$) but **not in statistical control** (subgroup 6 exceeds UCL). Recommended action: investigate the special cause for subgroup 6 (likely a tool wear or material anomaly), then re-establish control limits.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of Six-Sigma quality. Discuss the DMAIC methodology with reference to a machining process improvement case study. [7 Marks]

**(b)** A grinding process produces bearings with diameter specification 20.00 $\pm$ 0.05 mm. After collecting 25 subgroups of size 5, the following statistics are obtained: $\bar{\bar{X}} = 20.005$ mm, $\bar{R} = 0.018$ mm. Calculate the process standard deviation, plot the $\bar{X}$-R chart, and determine if the process is in control. If USL = 20.05 and LSL = 19.95, evaluate $C_{p}$ and $C_{pk}$. [7 Marks]

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Six-Sigma & DMAIC in Machining:**

**Six-Sigma Definition:** Six-Sigma is a disciplined, data-driven quality methodology that seeks to reduce process variation to a level where the process produces no more than **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**. It corresponds to a process operating with $\pm 6\sigma$ between the mean and the nearest specification limit, with a built-in 1.5$\sigma$ allowance for long-term process drift.

**Origins:** Pioneered by Motorola (1986) under Bob Galvin; famously deployed by General Electric under Jack Welch (1995).

**DMAIC Phases (with Machining Case Study — Surface Roughness Improvement on CNC Lathe):**

1. **D — Define:** Identify the problem — surface roughness (Ra) of a turned shaft is consistently exceeding customer specification of Ra 1.6 $\mu$m. Define project goal: reduce Ra to ≤ 1.0 $\mu$m within 6 months.

2. **M — Measure:** Establish current process baseline. Collect 100 Ra measurements using a portable profilometer. Calculate mean $\bar{X}$ and standard deviation $\sigma$. Construct baseline $\bar{X}$-R control chart to confirm current capability ($C_{pk}$).

3. **A — Analyze:** Use Ishikawa (fishbone) diagram and Pareto analysis to identify root causes. Common findings: insufficient coolant flow, excessive feed rate, worn tool nose radius, and machine vibration.

4. **I — Improve:** Implement solutions — upgrade to coated carbide insert, optimize cutting parameters (lower feed, higher speed), install high-pressure coolant, and rebalance spindle. Verify improvements with control chart re-baselining.

5. **C — Control:** Standardize the improved process. Implement Statistical Process Control (SPC) with $\bar{X}$-R charts, create standard operating procedures (SOPs), train operators, and install mistake-proofing (poka-yoke) fixtures. Monitor $C_{pk}$ ≥ 1.67 as a sustained capability target.

**Six-Sigma Levels and DPMO:**

| Sigma Level | DPMO | Yield (%) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 2$\sigma$ | 308,537 | 69.1% |
| 3$\sigma$ | 66,807 | 93.3% |
| 4$\sigma$ | 6,210 | 99.38% |
| 5$\sigma$ | 233 | 99.977% |
| 6$\sigma$ | 3.4 | 99.9997% |

---

**Part (b) — Grinding Process SPC Analysis:**

**Given:**
- $n = 5$, $k = 25$ subgroups
- $\bar{\bar{X}} = 20.005$ mm, $\bar{R} = 0.018$ mm
- USL = 20.05 mm, LSL = 19.95 mm
- Constants for $n=5$: $A_2 = 0.577$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.114$, $d_2 = 2.326$

**Step 1: Process Standard Deviation**

$$\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \frac{0.018}{2.326} = 0.007739 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Control Limits for $\bar{X}$ Chart**

$$UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R} = 20.005 + (0.577)(0.018)$$

$$UCL_{\bar{X}} = 20.005 + 0.01039 = 20.01539 \text{ mm}$$

$$LCL_{\bar{X}} = 20.005 - 0.01039 = 19.99461 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Control Limits for R Chart**

$$UCL_{R} = D_4 \bar{R} = (2.114)(0.018) = 0.03805 \text{ mm}$$

$$LCL_{R} = D_3 \bar{R} = 0 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Process Capability Indices**

$$C_{p} = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{20.05 - 19.95}{6 \times 0.007739} = \frac{0.10}{0.04643} = 2.154$$

$$C_{pk,\text{upper}} = \frac{USL - \bar{\bar{X}}}{3\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{20.05 - 20.005}{3 \times 0.007739} = \frac{0.045}{0.02322} = 1.938$$

$$C_{pk,\text{lower}} = \frac{\bar{\bar{X}} - LSL}{3\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{20.005 - 19.95}{0.02322} = \frac{0.055}{0.02322} = 2.369$$

$$C_{pk} = \min(1.938, \; 2.369) = 1.938$$

**Step 5: In-Control Verification**

The process is **in statistical control** (assuming no subgroup points were flagged in the original data collection) and is **highly capable** with $C_{pk} = 1.938 > 1.67$. The process is also well-centered, with the centering factor $k$ very small.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. Many students write $C_p$ formula but forget the $6\sigma$ denominator and write $3\sigma$ instead — *2 marks deducted*.
> 2. Students often confuse $C_p$ with $C_{pk}$ — $C_p$ ignores centering, $C_{pk}$ does not. Always quote both.
> 3. Failing to show the centering factor $k$ loses a mark in the engineering comment.
> 4. Numerical precision matters: round only at the final answer, keep 3–4 decimal places during intermediate steps.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day**

- [ ] **SPC Definition:** Data-driven monitoring of process variation using control charts; separates *common-cause* (inherent) from *special-cause* (assignable) variation.
- [ ] **Two Variation Types:** Common cause (process voice, requires redesign) vs. special cause (assignable, requires intervention).
- [ ] **Charts for Variables:** $\bar{X}$-R (n ≤ 10), $\bar{X}$-S (n > 10). **Charts for Attributes:** p-chart (proportion defective), c-chart (defects per unit), np-chart, u-chart.
- [ ] **Three-Sigma Rule:** Control limits set at $\bar{\bar{X}} \pm 3\sigma$ — captures 99.73% of common-cause variation.
- [ ] **Key Formulas:** $UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$; $LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$; $UCL_R = D_4 \bar{R}$; $LCL_R = D_3 \bar{R}$.
- [ ] **Capability Indices:** $C_p$ = spread only; $C_{pk}$ = spread + centering. Always quote $C_{pk} = \min(C_{pk,u}, C_{pk,l})$.
- [ ] **Capability Thresholds:** $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ (capable), $\geq 1.67$ (highly capable), $\geq 2.00$ (Six-Sigma class).
- [ ] **Six-Sigma:** 3.4 DPMO with 1.5$\sigma$ drift allowance; DMAIC phases = Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control.
- [ ] **Process Standard Deviation:** $\hat{\sigma} = \bar{R}/d_2$ from range data, $\hat{\sigma} = \bar{S}/c_4$ from std. deviation data.
- [ ] **Western Electric Rules:** 1 point beyond 3$\sigma$, 2 of 3 beyond 2$\sigma$, 4 of 5 beyond 1$\sigma$, 8 consecutive on one side → out of control.
- [ ] **Constants to Memorize (for n=5):** $A_2 = 0.577$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.114$, $d_2 = 2.326$.
- [ ] **KTU Examiner Traps:** Always compute *both* upper and lower $C_{pk}$; never confuse control limits with specification limits; never quote $C_p$ alone when the process is off-center.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tolerances and Fits in Metal Cutting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET522 - MECHANICS OF METAL CUTTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tolerances and Fits in Metal Cutting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Fundamental Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Tolerances and Fits form the engineering language of dimensional interchangeability.** Without standardized tolerance systems, mass production of mating parts (gears, bearings, pistons) would be impossible. This is the foundation of "**Interchangeable Manufacturing**" — a direct outcome of the Industrial Revolution and the philosophy of *Taylorism*.

### 1.1.1 Nominal Size (Basic Size)
The **nominal size** is the theoretical or design size of a part, around which the limits of size are fixed. It is the size used for general identification and is a common dimension assigned to both the hole and the shaft that are to be mated.

$$ D_{nom} = \text{Reference dimension for design intent} $$

### 1.1.2 Actual Size
The **actual size** of a manufactured part is the dimension obtained by measurement. Due to inherent variations in the machining process (tool wear, machine vibration, thermal expansion), no two parts will have *exactly* the same actual size.

### 1.1.3 Limits of Size
The two permissible extreme dimensions within which the actual size is allowed to vary are called the **limits of size**.
- **Maximum Limit (Upper Limit):** The largest permissible size of the part.
- **Minimum Limit (Lower Limit):** The smallest permissible size of the part.

### 1.1.4 Tolerance
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tolerance (ISO 286 definition):** The total permissible variation in the size of a part, defined as the **difference between the maximum and minimum limits of size**.

$$ T = \text{Maximum Limit} - \text{Minimum Limit} $$

For a hole: $T_H = H_{max} - H_{min}$
For a shaft: $T_s = s_{max} - s_{min}$

Tolerance is **always positive** and represents the *manufacturing latitude* allowed to the shop floor. **A larger tolerance = easier/cheaper to manufacture, but looser fit quality.**

### 1.1.5 Allowance
**Allowance** is the **intentional, designed difference** between the *maximum material condition* limits of mating parts.
- For a **hole–shaft pair**, allowance is the *minimum clearance* (or *maximum interference*) deliberately designed into the assembly.

$$ \text{Allowance (Clearance fit)} = H_{min} - s_{max} $$

$$ \text{Allowance (Interference fit)} = s_{min} - H_{max} $$

### 1.1.6 Deviation
The **deviation** is the algebraic difference between a *limit of size* and the *corresponding nominal size*.
- **Upper Deviation** = Maximum Limit − Nominal Size
- **Lower Deviation** = Minimum Limit − Nominal Size

### 1.1.7 Fundamental Deviation
The deviation closest to the basic size (the one that defines the position of the tolerance zone relative to the zero line) is called the **fundamental deviation**.
- It is the **anchor** of the tolerance zone.
- For shafts: designated by lowercase letters `a` to `zc`
- For holes: designated by uppercase letters `A` to `ZC`
- `h` (shaft) and `H` (hole) have fundamental deviation = **0** (lower deviation = 0)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The T-Shirt Industry"

Imagine you are a garment manufacturer in Tirur producing **T-shirts in size "Large"** with a nominal chest dimension of **100 cm**.

| Concept | Garment Analogy | Engineering Equivalent |
|---------|----------------|------------------------|
| Nominal Size | "Large" (100 cm) | Design dimension |
| Upper Limit | 102 cm (largest acceptable) | Maximum limit |
| Lower Limit | 98 cm (smallest acceptable) | Minimum limit |
| Tolerance | 4 cm (102 − 98) | Total manufacturing spread |
| Allowance | 2 cm gap between Large T-shirt and Slim-fit Medium (100−98) | Designed gap between parts |
| Fit | T-shirt is "comfortable" or "tight" | Clearance / Interference / Transition |

> [!TIP]
> Just like the textile industry **cannot** make every Large T-shirt exactly 100.000 cm, a lathe or milling machine **cannot** produce an exact 50.000 mm diameter. **Tolerance is the engineering admission of this reality**, while simultaneously *controlling* it within usable bounds.

---

## 1.3 Why Tolerances & Fits Matter in Metal Cutting

In the context of **Mechanics of Metal Cutting**, tolerance choice directly affects:
1. **Cutting forces and power** (tighter tolerance → finer feed, higher precision machine).
2. **Tool selection** (smaller tolerance requires sharper, more wear-resistant inserts — e.g., coated carbides or ceramics).
3. **Surface finish** (Ra is correlated with tolerance grade).
4. **Process selection**: turning vs. grinding vs. honing for different IT grades.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **General relationship (rule of thumb):** IT Grade 5 → Turning | IT Grade 6–7 → Standard Machining | IT Grade 4 and below → Grinding/Lapping

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Three Classical Types of Fits

A **fit** is the *degree of tightness or looseness* between two mating parts (a hole and a shaft) when assembled. The type of fit is determined by the **relative position of the tolerance zones** of the hole and the shaft.

### 2.1.1 Clearance Fit
- The **hole is ALWAYS larger than the shaft** at all limit combinations.
- There is always an air gap (clearance), regardless of which combination of limits is selected.
- Used for: **Sliding parts, bearings on shafts, bushings, hinges**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Clearance** is *positive* (hole > shaft). This fit allows free relative motion, easy assembly, and accommodates lubrication films.

$$ \text{Maximum Clearance } (C_{max}) = H_{max} - s_{min} $$

$$ \text{Minimum Clearance } (C_{min}) = H_{min} - s_{max} $$

**For a true clearance fit:** $C_{min} > 0$

### 2.1.2 Interference Fit (Force Fit / Shrink Fit)
- The **shaft is ALWAYS larger than the hole** at all limit combinations.
- There is always metal-to-metal pressure (interference), creating a permanent mechanical bond.
- Used for: **Gear hubs on shafts, permanent couplings, bearing races in housings**.

$$ \text{Maximum Interference } (I_{max}) = s_{max} - H_{min} $$

$$ \text{Minimum Interference } (I_{min}) = s_{min} - H_{max} $$

**For a true interference fit:** $I_{min} > 0$ (i.e., $s_{min} > H_{max}$)

### 2.1.3 Transition Fit
- The tolerance zones **overlap**. Depending on the actual manufactured dimensions, the assembly may have **either clearance OR interference**.
- Used for: **Precisely located parts requiring accurate alignment but easy assembly** — e.g., coupling sleeves, gear keyways, locating pins.

$$ \text{Maximum Clearance} = H_{max} - s_{min} \quad (\text{positive quantity}) $$

$$ \text{Maximum Interference} = s_{max} - H_{min} \quad (\text{positive quantity}) $$

**For a transition fit:** $C_{max} > 0$ AND $I_{max} > 0$

---

## 2.2 Basis of the Fit System — Hole Basis vs. Shaft Basis

### 2.2.1 Hole Basis System
- The **hole is the reference (constant) element**.
- Hole is held at a **fixed fundamental deviation (H)**, meaning its lower deviation = 0.
- Different fits are obtained by varying the **shaft tolerance position** (a, b, c, ..., zc).
- **Most widely used internationally** because standard drills, reamers, and broaches produce holes of fixed size.

> The hole-basis designation: **⌀50 H7/g6** means: hole is H7 (constant), shaft is g6 (varies for fit).

### 2.2.2 Shaft Basis System
- The **shaft is the reference (constant) element**.
- Shaft is held at a fixed fundamental deviation (h), meaning its upper deviation = 0.
- Different fits are obtained by varying the **hole tolerance position**.
- Used when: standard shaft stock (e.g., drawn bar stock) is preferred, or when multiple holes must fit the same shaft.

> The shaft-basis designation: **⌀50 G7/h6** means: shaft is h6 (constant), hole is G7 (varies for fit).

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb:** *Hole basis = "vary the shaft"*. **Hole basis is preferred in 90% of engineering practice** (especially in India/Europe) due to standardization of cutting tools.

---

## 2.3 ISO System of Limits and Fits (ISO 286)

The ISO system is the **global standard** for tolerances and fits. It has two main components:

### 2.3.1 Tolerance Grades (IT Grades)
There are **18 standard tolerance grades** designated as:
**IT01, IT0, IT1, IT2, ..., IT16, IT17, IT18**

- **Smaller IT number** = **tighter tolerance** = **higher precision** = **more difficult and expensive manufacturing**.
- **Larger IT number** = **looser tolerance** = **lower precision** = **easier manufacturing**.

> **Typical Applications by IT Grade:**
> - **IT01–IT4:** Gauges, gauges blocks, super-precision bearings.
> - **IT5–IT7:** High-quality bearings, precision machine tool spindles.
> - **IT8–IT11:** General engineering fits (most machined components).
> - **IT12–IT14:** Rough machining, sheet metal, castings.
> - **IT15–IT18:** Forgings, sand-cast parts, free dimensions.

### 2.3.2 Standard Tolerance Unit (i)
The standard tolerance for any grade depends on the **nominal diameter (D)** through the standard tolerance unit *i* (in micrometres, µm):

$$ i = 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{D} + 0.001 \cdot D $$

where D is in **mm** and *i* is in **µm**.

### 2.3.3 Tolerance Values for Various IT Grades
The tolerance for each grade is a multiple of *i*:

| IT Grade | Tolerance (in µm) | Typical Use |
|----------|-------------------|-------------|
| IT5 | 7i | Precision bearings |
| IT6 | 10i | Precision machine parts |
| IT7 | 16i | General machine fits |
| IT8 | 25i | Commercial machining |
| IT9 | 40i | Loose running fits |
| IT10 | 64i | Sliding fits |
| IT11 | 100i | Loose fits |
| IT12 | 160i | Press fits, rough |
| IT13 | 250i | Free dimensions |
| IT14 | 400i | Castings, forgings |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the EXAM GOLD.** Memorize these formulas and their conditions.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Condition / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Tolerance of Hole** | $T_H = H_{max} - H_{min}$ | Always positive (µm or mm) |
| 2 | **Tolerance of Shaft** | $T_s = s_{max} - s_{min}$ | Always positive |
| 3 | **Allowance (Min Clearance)** | $C_{min} = H_{min} - s_{max}$ | For clearance fit |
| 4 | **Maximum Clearance** | $C_{max} = H_{max} - s_{min}$ | For clearance fit |
| 5 | **Maximum Interference** | $I_{max} = s_{max} - H_{min}$ | For interference fit |
| 6 | **Minimum Interference** | $I_{min} = s_{min} - H_{max}$ | For interference fit |
| 7 | **Standard Tolerance Unit** | $i = 0.45 \sqrt[3]{D} + 0.001 D$ | D in mm, i in µm |
| 8 | **Mean Diameter (Hole)** | $D_m = \dfrac{H_{max} + H_{min}}{2}$ | Statistical average |
| 9 | **Mean Diameter (Shaft)** | $d_m = \dfrac{s_{max} + s_{min}}{2}$ | Statistical average |
| 10 | **Maximum Clearance (general)** | $C_{max} = D_m - d_m + \dfrac{T_H + T_s}{2}$ | Universal |
| 11 | **Minimum Clearance (general)** | $C_{min} = D_m - d_m - \dfrac{T_H + T_s}{2}$ | Universal |
| 12 | **Hole Limit (Hole basis)** | $H_{min} = D$ (lower dev = 0) | H-designation |
| 13 | **Shaft Limit (Shaft basis)** | $s_{max} = d$ (upper dev = 0) | h-designation |
| 14 | **Type of Fit Test (Clearance)** | $H_{min} > s_{max}$ | $C_{min} > 0$ |
| 15 | **Type of Fit Test (Interference)** | $s_{min} > H_{max}$ | $I_{min} > 0$ |
| 16 | **Type of Fit Test (Transition)** | $H_{min} \leq s_{max}$ AND $s_{min} \leq H_{max}$ | Overlap zone |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> The **International Tolerance (IT) grade** choice determines which **metal cutting process** to employ:

| Required IT Grade | Recommended Process | Typical Ra (µm) |
|-------------------|---------------------|------------------|
| IT5 – IT6 | Cylindrical grinding, fine turning with CBN | 0.2 – 0.8 |
| IT7 – IT8 | Precision turning, reaming, fine boring | 0.8 – 1.6 |
| IT9 – IT10 | Standard turning, drilling, milling | 1.6 – 3.2 |
| IT11 – IT12 | Rough turning, shaping, slotting | 3.2 – 12.5 |
| IT13 – IT14 | Sawing, shearing, casting | 12.5 – 50 |

**This linkage between tolerance and process selection is a frequent KTU question.**

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Standard Tolerance Unit (i) Formula

The ISO Standard Tolerance Unit (i) is derived from the empirical relationship between manufacturing tolerance and nominal diameter, calibrated against the millions of measurements performed by the International Standards Organization.

### Step 1 — Empirical Basis
Manufacturing tolerance is a function of nominal size D, but not linearly. For a given process, tolerance grows as the cube root of the dimension (this reflects the **three-dimensional nature of measurement error** and tool deflection).

$$ T \propto \sqrt[3]{D} $$

### Step 2 — Adding a Linear Term for Larger Diameters
For very large diameters (above ~500 mm), the linear term begins to dominate because the **proportional error per unit length** becomes more significant. Hence the addition of a $0.001 D$ term:

$$ i = a \cdot \sqrt[3]{D} + b \cdot D $$

### Step 3 — Calibrating the Constants
ISO 286 calibration, based on the 0–500 mm range, yields:

$$ a = 0.45 \quad \text{and} \quad b = 0.001 $$

### Step 4 — Final Form

$$ \boxed{ \ i = 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{D} + 0.001 \cdot D \quad \text{(µm, when D is in mm)} \ } $$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Type of Fit from Limit Dimensions

Given the limits of a hole and a shaft, derive the **type of fit** and the **numerical clearance/interference values**.

### Step 1 — Write Down the Given Limits
$$ H_{max} = 50.025 \text{ mm}, \quad H_{min} = 50.000 \text{ mm} $$

$$ s_{max} = 49.975 \text{ mm}, \quad s_{min} = 49.950 \text{ mm} $$

### Step 2 — Calculate Hole Tolerance

$$ T_H = H_{max} - H_{min} $$

$$ T_H = 50.025 - 50.000 $$

$$ \boxed{\ T_H = 0.025 \text{ mm} \ } $$

### Step 3 — Calculate Shaft Tolerance

$$ T_s = s_{max} - s_{min} $$

$$ T_s = 49.975 - 49.950 $$

$$ \boxed{\ T_s = 0.025 \text{ mm} \ } $$

### Step 4 — Test the Type of Fit

The condition for **clearance fit** is: $H_{min} > s_{max}$

$$ H_{min} = 50.000 \text{ mm}, \quad s_{max} = 49.975 \text{ mm} $$

$$ 50.000 > 49.975 \quad \checkmark \text{ TRUE} $$

Therefore, this is a **CLEARANCE FIT**.

### Step 5 — Calculate Maximum Clearance

$$ C_{max} = H_{max} - s_{min} $$

$$ C_{max} = 50.025 - 49.950 $$

$$ \boxed{\ C_{max} = 0.075 \text{ mm} \ } $$

### Step 6 — Calculate Minimum Clearance (Allowance)

$$ C_{min} = H_{min} - s_{max} $$

$$ C_{min} = 50.000 - 49.975 $$

$$ \boxed{\ C_{min} = 0.025 \text{ mm (Allowance)} \ } $$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Reverse Problem: Finding Limits from a Designation

**Problem:** A fit is designated as **⌀50 H7/g6** (hole basis). Find the limits, type of fit, and clearance range.

### Step 1 — Identify the Nominal Diameter and IT Grades
$$ D = 50 \text{ mm}, \quad \text{Hole} = H7, \quad \text{Shaft} = g6 $$

### Step 2 — Compute Standard Tolerance Unit (i)

$$ i = 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{50} + 0.001 \cdot 50 $$

$$ i = 0.45 \cdot 3.6840 + 0.050 $$

$$ i = 1.6578 + 0.050 $$

$$ \boxed{\ i = 1.708 \text{ µm} \ } $$

### Step 3 — Compute Hole Tolerance (IT7 = 16i)

$$ T_H = 16 \cdot i = 16 \cdot 1.708 = 27.33 \text{ µm} $$

$$ \boxed{\ T_H \approx 0.025 \text{ mm} \ } $$

### Step 4 — Compute Shaft Tolerance (IT6 = 10i)

$$ T_s = 10 \cdot i = 10 \cdot 1.708 = 17.08 \text{ µm} $$

$$ \boxed{\ T_s \approx 0.016 \text{ mm} \ } $$

### Step 5 — Apply Fundamental Deviations (Hole Basis, H-designation)

For H-holes, the **lower deviation = 0**:

$$ H_{min} = D + 0 = 50.000 \text{ mm} $$

$$ H_{max} = H_{min} + T_H = 50.000 + 0.025 = 50.025 \text{ mm} $$

### Step 6 — Apply Fundamental Deviations (g-shaft)

For **g-shaft**, the upper deviation is **negative** (shaft is below zero line). From ISO tables, for D = 50 mm, g-shaft upper deviation ≈ **−12 µm = −0.012 mm**.

$$ s_{max} = 50.000 - 0.012 = 49.988 \text{ mm} $$

$$ s_{min} = s_{max} - T_s = 49.988 - 0.016 = 49.972 \text{ mm} $$

### Step 7 — Determine Type of Fit

$$ H_{min} = 50.000, \quad s_{max} = 49.988 $$

$$ 50.000 > 49.988 \quad \checkmark \text{ TRUE} $$

**Type of Fit: CLEARANCE FIT** (with small clearance, indicating a *sliding fit*)

### Step 8 — Calculate Clearance Range

$$ C_{min} = H_{min} - s_{max} = 50.000 - 49.988 = 0.012 \text{ mm} $$

$$ C_{max} = H_{max} - s_{min} = 50.025 - 49.972 = 0.053 \text{ mm} $$

$$ \boxed{\ \text{Clearance range} = 0.012 \text{ mm to } 0.053 \text{ mm} \ } $$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Interference Fit Numerical Example

**Problem:** A fit is designated **⌀80 H7/p6**. Determine the type of fit and the interference range.

### Step 1 — Compute i for D = 80 mm

$$ i = 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{80} + 0.001 \cdot 80 $$

$$ i = 0.45 \cdot 4.3089 + 0.080 $$

$$ i = 1.9390 + 0.080 $$

$$ \boxed{\ i = 2.019 \text{ µm} \ } $$

### Step 2 — Compute Tolerances

$$ T_H (IT7) = 16 \cdot 2.019 = 32.30 \text{ µm} \approx 0.032 \text{ mm} $$

$$ T_s (IT6) = 10 \cdot 2.019 = 20.19 \text{ µm} \approx 0.020 \text{ mm} $$

### Step 3 — Apply Hole Limits (H7, lower deviation = 0)

$$ H_{min} = 80.000 \text{ mm} $$

$$ H_{max} = 80.000 + 0.032 = 80.032 \text{ mm} $$

### Step 4 — Apply Shaft Limits (p6, upper deviation is POSITIVE)

From ISO tables, for p6 at D = 80 mm, upper deviation ≈ **+37 µm = +0.037 mm**.

$$ s_{max} = 80.000 + 0.037 = 80.037 \text{ mm} $$

$$ s_{min} = 80.037 - 0.020 = 80.017 \text{ mm} $$

### Step 5 — Type of Fit Test

$$ s_{min} = 80.017 \text{ mm}, \quad H_{max} = 80.032 \text{ mm} $$

Since $s_{min} = 80.017 < H_{max} = 80.032$, but $s_{max} = 80.037 > H_{min} = 80.000$:

**Both clearance and interference are possible** ⟹ **TRANSITION FIT**

### Step 6 — Compute Maximum Clearance and Maximum Interference

$$ C_{max} = H_{max} - s_{min} = 80.032 - 80.017 = 0.015 \text{ mm} $$

$$ I_{max} = s_{max} - H_{min} = 80.037 - 80.000 = 0.037 \text{ mm} $$

$$ \boxed{\ \text{Range: } -0.037 \text{ mm (interference) to } +0.015 \text{ mm (clearance)} \ } $$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Decision Logic for Fit Identification

| Given Conditions | Type of Fit | Industrial Use |
|------------------|-------------|----------------|
| $H_{min} > s_{max}$ (i.e., $C_{min} > 0$) | **Clearance fit** | Sliding bearings, loose pulleys |
| $s_{min} > H_{max}$ (i.e., $I_{min} > 0$) | **Interference fit** | Permanent couplings, gear hubs |
| $H_{max} > s_{max} > H_{min} > s_{min}$ | **Transition fit** | Locating pins, keyways |
| $s_{max} > H_{max} > s_{min} > H_{min}$ | **Transition fit** | (alternate configuration) |
| $H_{max} = s_{max}$ AND $H_{min} = s_{min}$ | **Exact fit** (impossible in practice) | Theoretically zero tolerance |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Architecture — The ISO Fit System Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["ISO 286 TOLERANCE AND FIT SYSTEM"] --> B["TOLERANCE GRADES"]
    A --> C["FUNDAMENTAL DEVIATIONS"]
    A --> D["BASIS OF FIT"]

    B --> B1["IT01 IT0 IT1 IT2"]
    B --> B2["IT3 IT4 IT5 IT6"]
    B --> B3["IT7 IT8 IT9 IT10"]
    B --> B4["IT11 IT12 IT13"]
    B --> B5["IT14 IT15 IT16 IT17 IT18"]

    B1 --> B1A["GAUGES AND PRECISION BEARINGS"]
    B2 --> B2A["PRECISION SPINDLES HIGH QUALITY BEARINGS"]
    B3 --> B3A["GENERAL ENGINEERING FITS"]
    B4 --> B4A["COMMERCIAL MACHINING ROUGH SHAFTS"]
    B5 --> B5A["CASTINGS FORGINGS FREE DIMENSIONS"]

    C --> C1["SHAFT DEVIATIONS a to zc LOWERCASE"]
    C --> C2["HOLE DEVIATIONS A to ZC UPPERCASE"]
    C --> C3["H FOR HOLE LOWER DEVIATION EQUALS 0"]
    C --> C4["h FOR SHAFT UPPER DEVIATION EQUALS 0"]

    D --> D1["HOLE BASIS H FIXED VARY SHAFT"]
    D --> D2["SHAFT BASIS h FIXED VARY HOLE"]

    D1 --> E["WIDELY USED IN EUROPE AND INDIA"]
    D2 --> F["USED FOR STANDARD SHAFT STOCK"]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Three Types of Fits

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    NOM["NOMINAL SIZE LINE ZERO DEVIATION"] --> T1
    NOM --> T2
    NOM --> T3

    T1["CLEARANCE FIT"] --> T1A["HOLE TOLERANCE ZONE ABOVE SHAFT ZONE"]
    T1A --> T1B["Hmin GREATER THAN smax"]
    T1B --> T1C["EXAMPLES SLIDING BEARING GUIDE BUSH"]

    T2["TRANSITION FIT"] --> T2A["TOLERANCE ZONES OVERLAP"]
    T2A --> T2B["MAY HAVE CLEARANCE OR INTERFERENCE"]
    T2B --> T2C["EXAMPLES LOCATING PIN COUPLING KEYWAY"]

    T3["INTERFERENCE FIT"] --> T3A["SHAFT ZONE ABOVE HOLE ZONE"]
    T3A --> T3B["smin GREATER THAN Hmax"]
    T3B --> T3C["EXAMPLES GEAR HUB SHRINK FIT COUPLING"]

    T1C --> APP1["ALLOWS FREE RELATIVE MOTION"]
    T2C --> APP2["ALLOWS EASY ASSEMBLY AND ACCURATE LOCATION"]
    T3C --> APP3["PERMANENT MECHANICAL BOND"]
```

## 4.3 Block Flow — Hole Basis vs. Shaft Basis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["SELECT A FIT FOR A MATING PAIR"] --> Q1["WHICH ELEMENT IS EASIER TO STANDARDIZE"]

    Q1 --> G1["STANDARD DRILL REAMER OR BROACH PRODUCES FIXED HOLE"]
    G1 --> HB["CHOOSE HOLE BASIS SYSTEM"]
    HB --> HB1["HOLE IS H DESIGNATION"]
    HB1 --> HB2["LOWER DEVIATION OF HOLE EQUALS 0"]
    HB2 --> HB3["VARY SHAFT DESIGNATION FROM a TO zc"]
    HB3 --> HB4["EXAMPLE 50 H7 g6"]

    Q1 --> G2["STANDARD DRAWN BAR STOCK USED AS SHAFT"]
    G2 --> SB["CHOOSE SHAFT BASIS SYSTEM"]
    SB --> SB1["SHAFT IS h DESIGNATION"]
    SB1 --> SB2["UPPER DEVIATION OF SHAFT EQUALS 0"]
    SB2 --> SB3["VARY HOLE DESIGNATION FROM A TO ZC"]
    SB3 --> SB4["EXAMPLE 50 G7 h6"]
```

```mermaid

    IT8["IT8 IT10"] --> P4["STANDARD TURNING DRILLING MILLING"]
    IT10["IT10 IT12"] --> P5["ROUGH TURNING SHAPING"]

    P1 --> RA1["SURFACE FINISH 0.2 TO 0.8 MICRON"]

```
