# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Module 3]

> **Q1.** Define the following terms with neat sketches:
> (a) Tolerance
> (b) Allowance
> (c) Deviation

#### Model Answer:

**(a) Tolerance:**
The difference between the maximum limit of size and the minimum limit of size of a part. It represents the total permissible variation in a dimension.

$$ T = \text{Maximum Limit} - \text{Minimum Limit} $$

**(b) Allowance:**
The intentional, prescribed difference between the maximum material condition limits of mating parts. It is the *minimum clearance* or *maximum interference* designed into the assembly.

$$ \text{Allowance} = H_{min} - s_{max} \quad \text{(for clearance fit)} $$

**(c) Deviation:**
The algebraic difference between a limit of size and the corresponding nominal (basic) size. Two types:
- Upper Deviation = Max Limit − Nominal Size
- Lower Deviation = Min Limit − Nominal Size

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Module 3]

> **Q2.** Differentiate between **hole basis system** and **shaft basis system** of fits. State which one is preferred and why.

#### Model Answer:

| Parameter | Hole Basis System | Shaft Basis System |
|-----------|-------------------|--------------------|
| Reference element | Hole (constant) | Shaft (constant) |
| Fundamental deviation of reference | H (lower deviation = 0) | h (upper deviation = 0) |
| Variable element | Shaft designation varies (a–zc) | Hole designation varies (A–ZC) |
| Example | ⌀50 H7/g6 | ⌀50 G7/h6 |
| Preferred in | Europe, India, ISO standard | Limited applications |

**Preferred: Hole Basis System** because:
1. Standard cutting tools (drills, reamers, broaches) are designed to produce holes of fixed size.
2. Holes are more difficult and expensive to manufacture than shafts, so holding the hole constant is more economical.
3. Wider range of standard tools available.

---

## 5.2 PART B — ESE Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]

> **Q A.** A hole and a shaft assembly is designated as **⌀60 H8/f7**.
> **(a)** Determine the limits of size for the hole and the shaft using ISO system.
> **(b)** Calculate the maximum and minimum clearance. State the type of fit with one engineering application.

#### Step-by-Step Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1:** Identify nominal diameter and IT grades.
$$ D = 60 \text{ mm}, \quad \text{Hole} = H8, \quad \text{Shaft} = f7 $$

**Step 2:** Calculate standard tolerance unit (i).

$$ i = 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{60} + 0.001 \cdot 60 $$

$$ i = 0.45 \cdot 3.9149 + 0.060 $$

$$ i = 1.7617 + 0.060 $$

$$ \boxed{\ i = 1.822 \text{ µm} \ } $$

**Step 3:** Calculate tolerances.
$$ T_H (IT8) = 25 \cdot 1.822 = 45.55 \text{ µm} \approx 0.046 \text{ mm} $$

$$ T_s (IT7) = 16 \cdot 1.822 = 29.15 \text{ µm} \approx 0.029 \text{ mm} $$

**Step 4:** Apply fundamental deviations.

For **H8 hole** (lower deviation = 0):

$$ H_{min} = 60.000 \text{ mm} $$

$$ H_{max} = 60.000 + 0.046 = 60.046 \text{ mm} $$

For **f7 shaft** (upper deviation is negative, from ISO tables at 60 mm: ≈ −30 µm = −0.030 mm):

$$ s_{max} = 60.000 - 0.030 = 59.970 \text{ mm} $$

$$ s_{min} = 59.970 - 0.029 = 59.941 \text{ mm} $$

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 5:** Test type of fit.
$$ H_{min} = 60.000 \text{ mm}, \quad s_{max} = 59.970 \text{ mm} $$

$$ 60.000 > 59.970 \quad \checkmark $$

**Type of Fit: CLEARANCE FIT** (specifically a *Running fit* with small clearances — H8/f7).

**Step 6:** Calculate maximum and minimum clearance.

$$ C_{max} = H_{max} - s_{min} = 60.046 - 59.941 = 0.105 \text{ mm} $$

$$ C_{min} = H_{min} - s_{max} = 60.000 - 59.970 = 0.030 \text{ mm} $$

**Engineering Application:** Suitable for **journal bearings in electric motors, lightly loaded shaft supports, and high-speed rotating shafts** where lubricant film and small thermal expansion must be accommodated.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Module 3]

> **Q B.** A 100 mm diameter shaft is to be assembled into a hole with a transition fit. The maximum interference is **0.040 mm** and the minimum clearance is **0.025 mm**. The hole tolerance is **0.054 mm**.
> **(a)** Determine the limits of size for both hole and shaft.
> **(b)** Sketch the tolerance zone diagram and identify the type of fit.

#### Step-by-Step Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
$$ D = 100 \text{ mm}, \quad I_{max} = 0.040 \text{ mm}, \quad C_{min} = 0.025 \text{ mm}, \quad T_H = 0.054 \text{ mm} $$

**Step 1:** Find the hole limits. Let $H_{min}$ be unknown.

$$ H_{max} = H_{min} + T_H = H_{min} + 0.054 $$

**Step 2:** Use the formula for maximum interference.

$$ I_{max} = s_{max} - H_{min} = 0.040 \text{ mm} $$

$$ s_{max} = H_{min} + 0.040 $$

**Step 3:** Use the formula for minimum clearance.

$$ C_{min} = H_{min} - s_{max} = 0.025 \text{ mm} $$

$$ H_{min} - s_{max} = 0.025 $$

Substituting $s_{max} = H_{min} + 0.040$:

$$ H_{min} - (H_{min} + 0.040) = 0.025 $$

$$ -0.040 = 0.025 $$

This is a **contradiction**, indicating I must reconsider the *sign convention*. The minimum clearance formula is:

$$ C_{min} = H_{min} - s_{max} $$

$$ 0.025 = H_{min} - s_{max} $$

Therefore $H_{min} = s_{max} + 0.025$.

**Step 4:** Re-apply the maximum interference formula.

$$ I_{max} = s_{max} - H_{min} = 0.040 $$

$$ s_{max} - (s_{max} + 0.025) = 0.040 $$

$$ -0.025 = 0.040 $$

This is *still* a contradiction. The conclusion is that the **given data is internally inconsistent** for a true transition fit. However, the **correct** interpretation is that the *nominal* dimensions should be inferred from the geometry.

**Step 5:** Re-approach by assuming a hole basis system with H designation, where $H_{min} = 100.000$ mm.

$$ H_{max} = 100.000 + 0.054 = 100.054 \text{ mm} $$

From $C_{min} = 0.025$:

$$ s_{max} = H_{min} - C_{min} = 100.000 - 0.025 = 99.975 \text{ mm} $$

From $I_{max} = 0.040$:

$$ H_{min} = s_{max} - I_{max} = 99.975 - 0.040 = 99.935 \text{ mm} $$

This contradicts $H_{min} = 100.000$ mm, so the **nominal size must be adjusted**.

**Step 6:** Properly assume a nominal size of 100 mm and choose a shaft such that **shaft is slightly smaller than hole** but with overlap.

Setting $H_{min} = 100.000$ mm, $H_{max} = 100.054$ mm.

From $C_{min} = 0.025 = H_{min} - s_{max}$:
$$ s_{max} = 99.975 \text{ mm} $$

From $I_{max} = 0.040 = s_{max} - H_{min}$:
$$ 99.975 - 100.000 = -0.025 $$

This negative sign confirms a **clearance zone**. The data set actually represents a **clearance fit** rather than transition. The corrected problem:

$$ C_{min} = 0.025 \text{ mm}, \quad C_{max} = H_{max} - s_{min} = 0.040 \text{ mm} \text{ (treating } 0.040 \text{ as } C_{max} \text{)} $$

For clearance fit:
$$ C_{min} = H_{min} - s_{max} = 0.025 $$
$$ C_{max} = H_{max} - s_{min} = 0.040 $$

$$ s_{max} = H_{min} - 0.025 = 100.000 - 0.025 = 99.975 \text{ mm} $$

$$ s_{min} = H_{max} - 0.040 = 100.054 - 0.040 = 100.014 \text{ mm} $$

Wait — $s_{min} = 100.014 > s_{max} = 99.975$ is impossible. Hence the **data must be re-interpreted**:

**Re-interpreting as a transition fit with $H_{min}$ and $H_{max}$ as unknowns:**

Let $H_{min} = 100.000$ mm, then $H_{max} = 100.054$ mm.

For transition fit, both $C_{min}$ and $I_{max}$ exist as positive quantities:

$$ C_{min} = H_{min} - s_{max} = 0.025 \Rightarrow s_{max} = 99.975 \text{ mm} $$

$$ I_{max} = s_{max} - H_{min} = 99.975 - 100.000 = -0.025 \text{ (negative, not valid)} $$

**Conclusion:** The given data is only consistent if interpreted as a **clearance fit** with $C_{min} = 0.025$ mm and the 0.040 mm being the tolerance sum. Thus:

**Hole limits:** $H_{min} = 100.000$ mm, $H_{max} = 100.054$ mm.
**Shaft limits:** $s_{max} = 99.975$ mm, $s_{min} = 99.975 - T_s$.

Given the standard interpretation, the shaft tolerance $T_s$ for a 100 mm f7 or g6 shaft is typically 0.022 to 0.029 mm, giving:

$$ s_{min} = 99.975 - 0.029 = 99.946 \text{ mm} $$

$$ C_{max} = 100.054 - 99.946 = 0.108 \text{ mm} $$

This yields a **clearance fit** (running fit), not transition.

**The problem, as stated, is geometrically inconsistent for a true transition fit.** In an exam, state this clearly and show your work.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks: Tolerance Zone Diagram**

For a transition fit, the diagram shows overlapping zones:

```
              |       Hole zone
              |  |<---------->|
              |  |             |
   ~~~~~~~~~~~|~~|~~~~~~~~~~~~~|~~~~~~~~~~  (nominal line, deviations)
              |  |   Shaft    |
              |  |   zone     |
              |  |<---------->|
   H_max      H_min         s_max   s_min
              ^                ^
              |________________|
              Tolerance zones OVERLAP
```

**Sketch Description (for your answer sheet):**
- Draw horizontal line for nominal size (zero deviation).
- Plot the hole tolerance zone (rectangle) straddling or above the line.
- Plot the shaft tolerance zone (rectangle) such that it partially overlaps the hole zone.
- Mark the points $H_{max}, H_{min}, s_{max}, s_{min}$ on the diagram.
- The overlap region indicates where both clearance and interference can occur.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Sign confusion in deviation tables:** For shafts `a` to `h`, the upper deviation is NEGATIVE (below the zero line). For shafts `j` to `zc`, the upper deviation is POSITIVE. Mixing these up is the #1 error.
>
> 2. **Hole vs. Shaft designations:** `H7` = hole with IT7 tolerance. `h7` = shaft with IT7 tolerance. **CASE MATTERS** — uppercase = hole, lowercase = shaft.
>
> 3. **Confusing $C_{min}$ and $C_{max}$:** $C_{min}$ is the *minimum* gap (worst case for free motion) = $H_{min} - s_{max}$. $C_{max}$ is the *maximum* gap (loosest fit) = $H_{max} - s_{min}$.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to include IT grade in tolerance calculation:** The tolerance is always a multiple of the standard tolerance unit $i$, specific to the IT grade (5i for IT5, 16i for IT7, 25i for IT8, etc.).
>
> 5. **Interference vs. Clearance sign convention:** When asked "find the interference," the answer is a positive number. If you get a negative, you may have swapped hole and shaft or mis-applied the formula.
>
> 6. **Units inconsistency:** Standard tolerance $i$ is in **µm**, but limits of size are in **mm**. Convert carefully: $1 \text{ mm} = 1000 \text{ µm}$.
>
> 7. **Process-tolerance mismatch:** Don't recommend grinding for IT12 or rough turning for IT5 — this shows lack of practical awareness.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Tolerances and Fits**

- ☐ **Nominal size** = design/reference dimension. **Actual size** = measured. **Limits of size** = max & min permissible.
- ☐ **Tolerance** = Max limit − Min limit. **Always positive.**
- ☐ **Allowance** = intentional designed difference for mating parts.
- ☐ **Upper deviation** = Max limit − Nominal size. **Lower deviation** = Min limit − Nominal size.
- ☐ **Fundamental deviation** = deviation of the limit *closest* to the basic size. H-holes and h-shafts have it = 0.
- ☐ **Three types of fits:** Clearance ($C_{min} > 0$), Interference ($I_{min} > 0$), Transition (overlap zone).
- ☐ **Hole basis** (preferred, H fixed, vary shaft) vs. **Shaft basis** (h fixed, vary hole).
- ☐ **ISO 286 has 18 IT grades:** IT01, IT0, IT1 to IT18. **Lower IT = tighter = harder to make.**
- ☐ **Standard tolerance unit:** $i = 0.45 \sqrt[3]{D} + 0.001 D$ (D in mm, $i$ in µm).
- ☐ **IT7 tolerance = 16i.** **IT8 = 25i.** **IT6 = 10i.** Memorize these.
- ☐ **Hole upper-case, shaft lower-case** in designations (H7, g6).
- ☐ **Clearance fit applications:** bearings, slides, hinges, light-duty shafts.
- ☐ **Interference fit applications:** permanent couplings, gear hubs, shrink fits.
- ☐ **Transition fit applications:** locating pins, keyways, accurate alignment.
- ☐ **Process correlation:** IT5–6 → grinding; IT7–8 → reaming/turning; IT10–12 → rough turning; IT13+ → casting/forging.
- ☐ **Units:** $1 \text{ mm} = 1000 \text{ µm}$. Always check unit consistency in numerical problems.
- ☐ **Sign convention:** shafts `a` to `h` have **negative** upper deviation; `j` to `zc` have **positive** upper deviation.

> **END OF MODULE 3 NOTES — TOLERANCES AND FITS IN METAL CUTTING**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Non-Traditional Machining Processes - EDM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET522 - MECHANICS OF METAL CUTTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Non-Traditional Machining Processes - EDM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Electrical Discharge Machining (EDM)** is a non-traditional, thermo-electric material removal process in which the desired shape is obtained by eroding the workpiece material through a series of discrete, controlled electrical sparks (discharges) that occur between a shaped electrode (tool) and the workpiece, both submerged in a dielectric medium. Material removal is achieved through **melting and vaporization** caused by the intense, localized heat of the spark, **not** by mechanical cutting forces.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, EDM is classified under **Non-Traditional Machining (NTM)** processes, specifically in the category of **Thermal Energy-Based** processes (along with Electron Beam Machining, Laser Beam Machining, and Plasma Arc Machining).

**Key distinguishing feature:** EDM removes metal purely by **thermal energy** of spark — there is **zero mechanical contact** between tool and workpiece.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Students must be able to (i) classify NTM processes, (ii) explain the working principle, mechanism, and process parameters of EDM, and (iii) derive the Material Removal Rate (MRR) expression for EDM using energy balance principles.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

**Analogy 1 — The "Lightning Sculptor":**
Imagine you have a block of hard granite (your workpiece) that is impossible to carve with a chisel because it is too hard and brittle. Now imagine a tiny, controlled bolt of lightning (the spark) that you can move across the surface of the granite. Each lightning strike vaporizes a microscopic crater of granite. By repeating thousands of these tiny lightning strikes in a pattern, you can "sculpt" any shape into the granite — even though you never touched it with a physical tool.

**Analogy 2 — Electric Arc Welding in Reverse:**
In welding, an electric arc melts metal to *join* two pieces. In EDM, the same principle of localized melting is used in a precisely controlled, repetitive, and microscopic way to *remove* metal.

**Analogy 3 — The Mosquito Bite:**
Think of each spark as a "bite" by a mosquito. The mosquito's proboscis (the electrode) doesn't push the skin — instead it transfers heat/saliva (the spark energy) that causes a tiny piece of skin to be destroyed. EDM works the same way: no force, only **heat-based erosion**.

> [!TIP]
> **Remember the "4 Ms" of EDM:** **M**elting, **M**icro-crater **M**aterial **M**echanism, all occurring in a **M**edium of dielectric fluid.

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Quantities

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Spark/Discharge Current | $I$ | Ampere (A) |
| Pulse Duration / Pulse-on time | $T_{on}$ | microseconds ($\mu s$) |
| Pulse-off time | $T_{off}$ | microseconds ($\mu s$) |
| Open Circuit Voltage | $V_{oc}$ | Volts (V) |
| Material Removal Rate | MRR | mm³/min |
| Tool Wear Rate | TWR | mm³/min |
| Surface Roughness | $R_a$ | µm (microns) |
| Dielectric strength | — | kV/mm |
| Workpiece density | $\rho$ | g/cm³ |
| Melting/Boiling temperature | $T_m$ | °C or K |

**Typical value:** Spark temperature is in the range of **8,000 °C to 12,000 °C** — about **one-quarter the temperature of the sun's surface**.



<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Historical and Process Background

EDM was invented in the 1940s by **Boris and Natalya Lazarenko** (Soviet scientists) while studying the destructive effects of electrical discharges on switch contacts. They discovered that controlled sparks could be used productively to shape metal.

### 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Mechanism

The EDM process can be understood as a sequence of four repeating events for every single spark:

**Step 1 — Approach & Ionization:**
The tool (cathode, −) and the workpiece (anode, +) are brought close to each other (typically **0.01 mm to 0.05 mm** gap, called the *spark gap* or *overcut*). The dielectric fluid between them is normally an insulator.

**Step 2 — Dielectric Breakdown:**
When the voltage exceeds the **dielectric strength** of the fluid (typically 10–30 kV/mm for EDM oil), the fluid ionizes and becomes a conductive plasma channel. This is called **dielectric breakdown**.

**Step 3 — Spark Discharge:**
A controlled electrical discharge passes through the plasma channel. The channel becomes extremely narrow (~0.01–0.3 mm diameter) and the current density reaches **$10^5$ to $10^7$ A/cm²**. Temperatures shoot up to **8,000–12,000 °C**.

**Step 4 — Material Removal:**
The intense heat at both the electrode and workpiece surfaces causes the metal to **melt and vaporize**. A small spherical crater forms on the workpiece. Some molten metal is also ejected by the **explosive force** of the collapsing plasma bubble (a phenomenon similar to cavitation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why dielectric fluid is needed:** (1) It acts as an insulator until the spark gap voltage is reached. (2) It cools the workpiece and electrode. (3) It flushes away the eroded debris (chips). (4) It prevents short-circuiting and excessive wear.

---

### 2.3 Process Parameters (The "Knobs" of EDM)

| Parameter | Effect on MRR | Effect on Surface Finish |
|---|---|---|
| **Discharge Current (I)** | ↑ I → ↑ MRR (almost proportional to $I^2$) | ↑ I → ↑ $R_a$ (rougher) |
| **Pulse Duration ($T_{on}$)** | ↑ $T_{on}$ → ↑ MRR (more energy per spark) | ↑ $T_{on}$ → ↑ $R_a$ |
| **Pulse-off time ($T_{off}$)** | More flushing → better stability | Better finish |
| **Open-circuit voltage ($V_{oc}$)** | Higher V → larger gap → more flushing | Slightly rougher |
| **Dielectric flow rate** | Better debris removal → stable MRR | Better finish |
| **Electrode material** | Cu/Graphite give high MRR | Cu gives best finish |

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is essential for solving any KTU numerical problem on EDM.**

| # | Formula | Description / Variables |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Volume of one crater} = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot T_{on}}{H_m}$ | Energy per spark / Heat required to melt unit volume |
| 2 | $H_m = \rho \cdot C_p \cdot (T_m - T_0) + \rho \cdot L_f$ | Specific melting energy (J/mm³) |
| 3 | $\text{MRR} = C \cdot V \cdot I \cdot f$ | Linear empirical MRR (C is process constant) |
| 4 | $\text{MRR} \propto I^2$ | Quadratic dependence on current |
| 5 | $\text{MRR} = \dfrac{\text{Volume removed}}{\text{Time (min)}}$ | Standard MRR definition |
| 6 | $TWR = \dfrac{\text{Volume of electrode worn}}{\text{Time}}$ | Tool Wear Rate (mm³/min) |
| 7 | $\text{Volumetric Wear Ratio} = \dfrac{TWR}{MRR}$ | Often expressed as % |
| 8 | $\text{Average Power} = V \cdot I \cdot \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{on}+T_{off}}$ | Considering duty cycle |
| 9 | $\text{Duty Factor} = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{on}+T_{off}}$ | Ratio of on-time to total cycle time |
| 10 | $R_a = K \cdot I^{0.4} \cdot T_{on}^{0.3}$ | Empirical surface roughness (approximate) |

**Where:**
- $V$ = Discharge voltage (V)
- $I$ = Discharge current (A)
- $T_{on}$ = Pulse-on time (s or µs)
- $f$ = Pulse frequency (Hz)
- $\rho$ = Density of workpiece (g/cm³ or kg/m³)
- $C_p$ = Specific heat (J/g·K)
- $T_m$ = Melting temperature (K or °C)
- $L_f$ = Latent heat of fusion (J/g)
- $C$ = Process constant (depends on material)
- $K$ = Empirical constant

---

### 2.5 Why MRR ∝ I² (The Core Physical Reasoning)

The energy of a single spark is given by:

$$E_{spark} = V \cdot I \cdot T_{on}$$

For a given voltage and pulse time, energy is linear in current. **However**, the volume of material removed is *not* linear in energy because:

1. Higher current → higher localized temperature → more material reaches **vaporization** state (not just melting).
2. Vaporization requires **~7× more energy** than melting.
3. The plasma channel cross-section also grows with current, but the depth grows faster (square-law).

This is why empirical MRR laws show $\text{MRR} \propto I^2$ for the **finishing** range and $\text{MRR} \propto I$ for **roughing** ranges.

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Die and mold making:** EDM is the industry standard for machining hardened tool steel dies (HRC 60+) with intricate cavities.
- **Aerospace:** Machining of **titanium**, **Inconel**, and **nickel-based superalloys** for turbine blades.
- **Automotive:** Fuel injection nozzles, gear cutting, prototype parts.
- **Medical:** Orthopedic implants (titanium hip joints), surgical instruments.
- **Electronics:** Micro-EDM for drilling micro-holes in printed circuit boards (PCBs) and semiconductor lead frames.
- **Defense:** Shaped charges and armor-piercing components.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Favorite:** "Why is EDM preferred for machining cemented carbide and tool steel?" — Because these materials are too hard for conventional cutting tools, and EDM's thermal mechanism can erode any *electrically conductive* material regardless of hardness.



<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Material Removal Rate (MRR) from Energy Balance

**Starting assumption:** Each spark removes one micro-crater. The energy required to remove unit volume of material is the **specific melting energy** $H_m$ (J/mm³).

**Step 1 — Energy released by one spark:**

$$E_{spark} = V \cdot I \cdot T_{on} \quad \text{(Joules)}$$

*Reasoning:* Electrical energy = Voltage × Current × Time. The dielectric breakdown momentarily turns the gap into a resistor, dissipating this energy as heat.

**Step 2 — Volume of material removed by one spark:**

Since only a fraction of the spark energy is actually used for melting (the rest is lost to dielectric, radiation, and electrode), we introduce an efficiency factor $\eta$:

$$V_{crater} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot T_{on}}{H_m} \quad \text{(mm³)}$$

*Reasoning:* Useful energy = efficiency × total energy. Useful energy = mass melted × energy per unit volume.

**Step 3 — Number of sparks per unit time:**

If the pulse frequency is $f$ (Hz), then in one second, $f$ sparks occur. In one minute:

$$N_{sparks/min} = 60 \cdot f$$

**Step 4 — Total volume removed per minute (MRR):**

$$\text{MRR} = V_{crater} \times N_{sparks/min}$$

Substituting:

$$\text{MRR} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot T_{on}}{H_m} \times 60 \cdot f$$

Since $T_{on} \cdot f$ = duty factor (number of pulses per second × pulse width), we can write:

$$\boxed{\text{MRR} = \dfrac{60 \cdot \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot f \cdot T_{on}}{H_m} \quad \text{(mm³/min)}}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Specific Melting Energy $H_m$

The total energy to heat 1 mm³ of metal from ambient $T_0$ to melting point $T_m$ and then fully melt it:

**Step 1 — Sensible heat (heating to melting point):**

$$Q_1 = m \cdot C_p \cdot (T_m - T_0) = (\rho \cdot V) \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$$

For unit volume ($V = 1$ mm³):

$$q_1 = \rho \cdot C_p \cdot (T_m - T_0)$$

**Step 2 — Latent heat of fusion:**

$$q_2 = \rho \cdot L_f$$

**Step 3 — Total specific melting energy:**

$$\boxed{H_m = \rho \cdot C_p \cdot (T_m - T_0) + \rho \cdot L_f \quad \text{(J/mm³)}}$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

> **Problem (Typical KTU 14-mark question):**
> In an EDM operation on a steel workpiece, the following data is given:
> Discharge voltage $V = 100$ V, Discharge current $I = 50$ A, Pulse-on time $T_{on} = 200$ µs, Pulse-off time $T_{off} = 50$ µs, Frequency $f = 1000$ Hz, Specific melting energy of steel $H_m = 4.5$ J/mm³, Efficiency $\eta = 0.30$.
> Calculate: (a) MRR (b) Average power dissipated (c) Energy per spark.

**Solution:**

**(a) MRR Calculation:**

$$\text{MRR} = \dfrac{60 \cdot \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot f \cdot T_{on}}{H_m}$$

Plug in values (note: $T_{on} = 200 \times 10^{-6}$ s):

$$\text{MRR} = \dfrac{60 \times 0.30 \times 100 \times 50 \times 1000 \times 200 \times 10^{-6}}{4.5}$$

$$\text{MRR} = \dfrac{60 \times 0.30 \times 100 \times 50 \times 1000 \times 0.0002}{4.5}$$

Computing the numerator step-by-step:

$$60 \times 0.30 = 18$$

$$18 \times 100 = 1800$$

$$1800 \times 50 = 90{,}000$$

$$90{,}000 \times 1000 = 9 \times 10^{7}$$

$$9 \times 10^{7} \times 0.0002 = 18{,}000$$

$$\text{MRR} = \dfrac{18{,}000}{4.5} = 4000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

**Result: MRR = 4000 mm³/min**

**(b) Average Power:**

$$\text{Duty Factor} = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}} = \dfrac{200}{200+50} = 0.8$$

$$\text{Average Power} = V \cdot I \cdot \text{Duty Factor} = 100 \times 50 \times 0.8 = 4000 \text{ W} = 4 \text{ kW}$$

**(c) Energy per spark:**

$$E_{spark} = V \cdot I \cdot T_{on} = 100 \times 50 \times 200 \times 10^{-6} = 1 \text{ J}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Tool Wear Ratio (TWR-based Analysis)

The Volumetric Wear Ratio (VWR) is defined as:

$$\text{VWR} = \dfrac{TWR}{MRR} \times 100 \text{ \%}$$

For **graphite electrodes** on steel, VWR is typically 1–5%. For **copper electrodes**, VWR is typically 0.5–2%. This is why graphite is preferred for roughing (high MRR tolerated) and copper for finishing (low wear preferred).



<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of EDM System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DC Pulse Power Supply] -->|Pulsed Voltage 80-300V| B[Servo Control Unit]
    B -->|Gap Monitoring Signal| C[Spark Gap Controller]
    C -->|Feed Control| D[Tool Electrode Z-axis Drive]
    D --> E[Tool Electrode Cathode]
    E -->|Spark Gap 0.01-0.05mm| F[Workpiece Anode]
    F --> G[Work Table with Dielectric Tank]
    G -->|Circulation| H[Dielectric Filter Unit]
    H -->|Clean Dielectric| G
    A -->|Return Path| F
```

**Functional description of the architecture:**

- The **DC Pulse Power Supply** generates high-frequency, low-duty-cycle voltage pulses.
- The **Spark Gap Controller** continuously measures the gap resistance and sends correction signals to the **Servo Control Unit**.
- The **Servo** moves the tool electrode down (or up) to maintain the ideal spark gap.
- **Dielectric fluid** flows through the work tank, carrying away eroded debris through the **Filter Unit**.

---

### 4.2 Process Flow Diagram of One Spark Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1: Tool and Workpiece approach] --> P2[Step 2: Dielectric fluid fills spark gap]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Voltage rises to breakdown level]
    P3 --> P4{Step 4: Breakdown occurs?}
    P4 -->|No| P1
    P4 -->|Yes| P5[Step 5: Plasma channel forms]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Spark discharge begins]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Temperature shoots to 8000-12000 C]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8: Workpiece material melts and vaporizes]
    P8 --> P9[Step 9: Plasma channel collapses]
    P9 --> P10[Step 10: Molten debris ejected by cavitation]
    P10 --> P11[Step 11: Dielectric flushes debris away]
    P11 --> P12[Step 12: Tool retracts to neutral position]
    P12 --> P1
```

---

### 4.3 Classification Block of EDM Variants

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    EDM[Electrical Discharge Machining EDM] --> DEDM[Die Sinker EDM Conventional]
    EDM --> WEDM[Wire EDM WEDM]
    EDM --> MicroEDM[Micro EDM]
    EDM --> HSMEDM[High Speed Milling EDM]
    DEDM --> D1[Uses shaped copper or graphite electrode]
    WEDM --> W1[Uses traveling brass or copper wire]
    MicroEDM --> M1[Used for micro holes below 200 microns]
    HSMEDM --> H1[Uses rotating electrode for 3D cavities]
```

---

### 4.4 Schematic of Spark Formation at Microscopic Level

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Tool Cathode] -->|Electron Emission| B[Plasma Channel Conductive Bridge]
    B -->|Ion Bombardment| C[Workpiece Anode]
    C --> D[Crater Formation on Workpiece]
    A --> E[Small Crater on Tool due to Ion Bombardment]
    B --> F[Heat Zone 8000-12000 C]
    F --> G[Melting and Vaporization]
    G --> D
    G --> E
```



<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (2-mark + 3-mark Conceptual)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, 3 marks]**
*Define EDM. Why is it classified as a non-traditional machining process?*

**Model Answer:**

Electrical Discharge Machining (EDM) is a non-traditional, thermo-electric material removal process in which material is removed by a series of rapidly recurring electrical discharges (sparks) between a tool (electrode) and the workpiece, immersed in a dielectric medium. The spark generates temperatures of **8,000 °C to 12,000 °C**, causing localized melting and vaporization of the workpiece.

**Why non-traditional:** (i) It uses **thermal energy**, not mechanical cutting force. (ii) The tool does **not physically contact** the workpiece. (iii) It can machine **electrically conductive materials regardless of hardness**. (iv) It is independent of mechanical properties like hardness, strength, or brittleness.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, 3 marks]**
*List any four process parameters of EDM and state their effect on Material Removal Rate (MRR).*

**Model Answer:**

| # | Parameter | Effect on MRR |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Discharge current (I) | ↑ I → ↑ MRR (almost ∝ $I^2$) |
| 2 | Pulse duration ($T_{on}$) | ↑ $T_{on}$ → ↑ MRR |
| 3 | Open-circuit voltage ($V_{oc}$) | ↑ V → ↑ spark gap → ↑ MRR slightly |
| 4 | Dielectric flushing pressure | Better flushing → stable MRR |
| 5 | Pulse frequency (f) | ↑ f → ↑ MRR |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice Pattern

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Alternative 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, 14 marks, Module 4 Choice]**

**Q.A (a)** Explain the working principle of EDM with a neat schematic. Discuss the role of dielectric fluid in detail. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The EDM process works on the principle of **spark erosion**. A shaped tool (electrode) and the workpiece are connected to a DC pulse power supply and submerged in a dielectric medium. The schematic of the EDM process is shown below:

```
       + (Anode)                  Dielectric
        |                          fluid
   ┌────┴────┐                 ┌──────────┐
   │Workpiece│ ◄── spark gap ──► │ Electrode │
   │ (Anode) │       (~25µm)    │ (Cathode)│
   └─────────┘                 └──────────┘
        |                           |
        +◄─── DC Pulsed Power ──────+
                  Supply
```

**Working Cycle:**

(i) The tool is fed towards the workpiece under servo control until the gap becomes very small (~0.025 mm).

(ii) The DC pulse supply applies a voltage (typically 80–300 V) across the gap.

(iii) The dielectric fluid ionizes, forming a plasma channel, and a spark jumps the gap.

(iv) Spark temperature (~10,000 °C) melts and vaporizes a small volume of metal on the workpiece, forming a crater.

(v) The molten debris is flushed away by the dielectric flow.

(vi) The voltage is cut off, plasma collapses, and the cycle repeats (thousands of times per second).

**Role of Dielectric Fluid (4 critical functions):**

1. **Insulator:** Prevents premature current flow until the spark gap is small enough for breakdown.
2. **Coolant:** Carries away heat from the workpiece and electrode, preventing thermal damage.
3. **Flushing medium:** Removes the eroded debris (chips) from the gap, preventing short circuits.
4. **Medium for plasma formation:** Provides the ions needed for the conductive plasma channel.

**Common dielectric fluids:** EDM oil (hydrocarbon), deionized water, kerosene.

---

**Q.A (b)** Derive the expression for Material Removal Rate (MRR) in EDM from energy balance principles. A steel workpiece is machined by EDM with the following data: V = 200 V, I = 30 A, $T_{on}$ = 100 µs, $T_{off}$ = 25 µs, f = 2000 Hz, $\eta$ = 0.25, $H_m$ = 5 J/mm³. Calculate the MRR. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation:**

Energy released per spark:

$$E_{spark} = V \cdot I \cdot T_{on}$$

Useful energy for material removal:

$$E_{useful} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot T_{on}$$

Volume removed per spark (energy balance: useful energy = volume × specific melting energy):

$$V_{crater} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot T_{on}}{H_m} \text{ mm}^3$$

Number of sparks per minute:

$$N = 60 \cdot f$$

Therefore, MRR:

$$\text{MRR} = \dfrac{60 \cdot \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot f \cdot T_{on}}{H_m}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $V = 200$ V, $I = 30$ A, $T_{on} = 100 \times 10^{-6}$ s, $f = 2000$ Hz, $\eta = 0.25$, $H_m = 5$ J/mm³.

$$\text{MRR} = \dfrac{60 \times 0.25 \times 200 \times 30 \times 2000 \times 100 \times 10^{-6}}{5}$$

Step-by-step:

$$60 \times 0.25 = 15$$

$$15 \times 200 = 3000$$

$$3000 \times 30 = 90{,}000$$

$$90{,}000 \times 2000 = 1.8 \times 10^{8}$$

$$1.8 \times 10^{8} \times 100 \times 10^{-6} = 1.8 \times 10^{4} = 18{,}000$$

$$\text{MRR} = \dfrac{18{,}000}{5} = 3600 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

**Result: MRR = 3600 mm³/min**

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative 2**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, 14 marks, Module 4 Choice]**

**Q.B (a)** Compare EDM with conventional machining processes. List six differences. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| # | Parameter | Conventional Machining | EDM |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Energy source | Mechanical (cutting force) | Thermal (electrical spark) |
| 2 | Tool-workpiece contact | Direct physical contact | No physical contact |
| 3 | Material hardness limit | Limited to ~HRC 40 | No limit (any conductive material) |
| 4 | Force on workpiece | High mechanical force | Negligible mechanical force |
| 5 | MRR vs Hardness | Decreases as hardness increases | Independent of hardness |
| 6 | Surface integrity | May cause residual stress, work hardening | Heat-affected zone (recast layer) |
| 7 | Application | Bulk material removal | Complex cavities, dies, micro-holes |
| 8 | Tool material | Harder than workpiece (carbide, HSS) | Softer than workpiece often (Cu, graphite) |
| 9 | Heat generation | Shear zone heat | Spark zone heat (10,000 °C) |
| 10 | Material limitation | Any material | Only electrically conductive materials |

---

**Q.B (b)** Explain the Wire-EDM (WEDM) process with a neat diagram. State any three advantages of WEDM over Die-sinker EDM. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Wire-EDM (WEDM) Working Principle:**

In WEDM, a **continuously traveling thin wire** (usually brass, 0.05–0.3 mm diameter) is used as the electrode instead of a shaped tool. The wire is fed from a spool, passes through the workpiece, and is wound on a take-up spool, all submerged in deionized water (dielectric).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Wire Spool] --> B[Tensioning and Guide System]
    B --> C[Upper Wire Guide]
    C --> D[Spark Zone with Workpiece]
    D --> E[Lower Wire Guide]
    E --> F[Take-up Spool]
    D --> G[X-Y CNC Table moves Workpiece]
    G --> H[2D Contour Machined]
```

**Process Details:**

- The wire travels at a constant speed (~2–10 m/min) to present a fresh electrode surface continuously.
- The workpiece is moved in X-Y by a CNC table to generate the desired 2D contour.
- The wire never touches the workpiece; cutting is purely by sparks across the wire-workpiece gap.
- Wire is consumed slowly; spool change occurs every few hours.

**Three Advantages of WEDM over Die-sinker EDM:**

1. **No need to manufacture a shaped electrode:** Especially for 2D profiles, die-sinker EDM requires a custom-shaped Cu/graphite electrode, which is expensive and time-consuming. WEDM uses a standard off-the-shelf wire.

2. **Higher accuracy and better surface finish:** WEDM can achieve tolerances of ±0.005 mm and $R_a$ below 0.2 µm, far superior to die-sinker EDM.

3. **Through-cutting capability:** WEDM can cut completely through thick workpieces (up to 300+ mm), whereas die-sinker EDM can only create blind cavities matching the electrode shape.

4. **Lower electrode cost per part:** Wire is cheap and continuous; shaped electrodes are costly and must be re-machined for wear.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — How students lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units:** $T_{on}$ must be converted from µs to seconds before substituting. Many students write `MRR = 60 × 0.25 × 200 × 30 × 2000 × 100 / 5` and get a wrong answer 1000× too large. **Always check: 100 µs = 100 × 10⁻⁶ s.**
> 2. **Confusing MRR with TWR:** MRR is for *workpiece* material removed; TWR is *electrode* material lost. Marks are awarded separately.
> 3. **Omitting the role of dielectric:** Many students mention dielectric as "coolant" only. Examiners want **all four functions** (insulator, coolant, flushing, plasma medium).
> 4. **Saying "EDM uses mechanical force":** This is the most common wrong statement. EDM has **zero mechanical cutting force**.
> 5. **Wrong assumption about hardness:** EDM works on electrically conductive materials, not on *all* hard materials. **Ceramics like Al₂O₃ are non-conductive and cannot be EDM-machined** (require laser or ultrasonic).
> 6. **Confusing $H_m$ with enthalpy:** $H_m$ is **specific melting energy per unit volume** (J/mm³), not per unit mass.
> 7. **Missing the schematic in 7-mark questions:** Even a hand-drawn schematic can fetch 1–2 marks. Always draw one.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Final Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print and memorize):**

- EDM = **Electrical Discharge Machining** = spark erosion = thermal NTM process.
- Spark temperature: **8,000 – 12,000 °C**.
- Spark gap: **0.01 – 0.05 mm** (typical 25 µm).
- Dielectric fluids: **EDM oil, kerosene, deionized water**.
- Tool electrode materials: **Copper (finishing), Graphite (roughing), Brass (WEDM)**.
- Workpiece requirement: **Must be electrically conductive** (steel, Ti, Inconel, WC, Cu — but not ceramics or plastics).
- No mechanical force, no tool-workpiece contact, no thermal distortion of bulk part (only micro recast layer).
- **MRR formula:** $\text{MRR} = \dfrac{60 \cdot \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot f \cdot T_{on}}{H_m}$
- **Specific melting energy:** $H_m = \rho \cdot C_p \cdot (T_m - T_0) + \rho \cdot L_f$
- **Energy per spark:** $E = V \cdot I \cdot T_{on}$
- **Duty factor:** $\tau = T_{on} / (T_{on} + T_{off})$
- **MRR ∝ I²** (empirical, finishing range).
- **Tool Wear Ratio (TWR/MRR):** Graphite ≈ 1–5%, Copper ≈ 0.5–2%.
- **Wire EDM** uses continuously moving brass wire (0.05–0.3 mm) for 2D contour cutting.
- **Applications:** Die/mold making, aerospace alloys, micro-drilling, surgical implants.
- **Advantages:** Independent of hardness, no burr, complex shapes, high accuracy.
- **Disadvantages:** Slow MRR, high energy consumption, recast layer, conductive materials only.
- EDM was invented by **Boris and Natalya Lazarenko** (1940s, USSR).
- One spark cycle = 4 phases: **Approach → Breakdown → Discharge → Debris ejection**.

---

**End of Module 4 — Introduction to Non-Traditional Machining Processes (EDM) Notes**
*Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme — Outcome-Based Education, NEP 2020 Aligned*


<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications and Advantages of Non-Traditional Machining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET522 - MECHANICS OF METAL CUTTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Applications and Advantages of Non-Traditional Machining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Non-Traditional Machining (NTM)** refers to a group of manufacturing processes that remove material by employing mechanisms **other than** the traditional wedge-based mechanical cutting action of a harder tool. These processes rely on **thermal, chemical, electrical, or electrochemical energy** — either singly or in combination — to atomize or dislodge material from the workpiece.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for *PEIET522 – Mechanics of Metal Cutting*, NTM processes are classified under **Module 4** and are formally defined as:

> "Manufacturing processes in which the **physical state of the tool** is not rigid (or there is no direct mechanical contact), and material removal is achieved through **non-conventional energy sources** such as electrical, chemical, thermal, or abrasive-fluid energy."

The three governing nomenclatures used in the KTU board are:

- **MRR** = Material Removal Rate (mm³/min or cm³/min)
- **TWR** = Tool Wear Ratio
- **SR** = Surface Roughness (µm or µm Ra)

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Invisible Sculptor"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition):**
> 
> Imagine you want to carve a delicate rose out of a block of ice. If you use a hammer and chisel (traditional machining), the ice will **crack, chip, and shatter** before you can shape the petals. But if you use a **warm water jet** (a non-traditional method), the water gently melts only where it touches, allowing you to sculpt smooth, intricate curves **without any mechanical impact**.
> 
> In the same way, Non-Traditional Machining is like using an *invisible sculptor* — it can shape:
> - **Super-hard materials** (carbides, ceramics, glass) that defeat a normal cutting tool
> - **Brittle materials** (silicon wafers) that would crack under tool pressure
> - **Ultra-thin, complex cavities** (micro-holes, slots, dies) that a rotating tool cannot reach

The "tool" in NTM may be:
- A **spark of electricity** (EDM)
- A **chemical solution** (ECM, CHM)
- A **focused beam of light/electrons** (LBM, EBM)
- A **high-pressure fluid jet** (WJM, AJM)
- A **vibrating abrasive slurry** (USM)
- A **plasma arc** (PAM)

---

## 1.3 Why NTM Becomes Necessary — The Limitation Trigger

Traditional machining fails when **one or more** of the following conditions exist:

| # | Condition | Example |
|---|-----------|---------|
| 1 | Workpiece **hardness > Tool hardness** | Tungsten carbide, Inconel, hardened tool steel (HRC 60+) |
| 2 | **Non-conductive / non-metallic** workpiece | Glass, ceramics, quartz, silicon, graphite |
| 3 | Requirement of **very small / intricate features** | Micro-holes < 0.5 mm, deep narrow slots |
| 4 | Requirement of **zero mechanical stress / no HAZ** | Brittle semiconductor wafers, aerospace alloys |
| 5 | **Complex 3D cavities** with sharp internal corners | Die-cavity, turbine blade cooling passages |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Favourite Question Trigger:** Whenever the examiner asks *"Why is NTM preferred for machining of hardened steel?"* — the standard KTU answer is: *"Because the **hardness of the workpiece exceeds the hardness of the available cutting tool**, and NTM removes material by **non-mechanical energy** (thermal/chemical/electrical) which is **independent of mechanical hardness**."*

---

## 1.4 Comprehensive Classification of NTM (For KTU Reference)

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups NTM into **four fundamental energy families**:

1. **Mechanical Energy Family** — AJM, WJM, USM
2. **Electrochemical Family** — ECM, ECG (Electrochemical Grinding)
3. **Thermal / Electrical Energy Family** — EDM (die-sinking & wire), LBM, EBM, PAM
4. **Chemical Energy Family** — CHM (Chemical Milling / Etching)

---

## 1.5 Key Takeaway — Definition Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In one sentence:** *NTM is any material-removal process in which **mechanical cutting force from a rigid tool is replaced** by thermal, chemical, electrical, or hybrid non-conventional energy — enabling machining of hard, brittle, exotic, and micro-feature workpieces that are beyond the capability of traditional chip-forming processes.*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Universal Advantages of NTM (The "Why" Framework)

Every NTM process, despite its internal mechanism, is preferred over conventional machining for **four universal reasons** that the KTU examiner expects as bullet answers:

### 1. **Material-Hardness Independence**
NTM does not depend on the workpiece being *softer* than the tool. It removes material regardless of the workpiece's mechanical hardness.

### 2. **Zero (or Minimal) Mechanical Cutting Force**
Because the "tool" is energy (spark, jet, beam, solution), there is no tool–workpiece mechanical contact → **no induced stress, no vibration, no chatter**.

### 3. **Capability for Micro & Complex Geometry**
NTM can produce features as small as a few microns, sharp internal corners, and complex 3-D cavities that a rotating tool cannot reach.

### 4. **Excellent Surface Integrity**
Since there is no mechanical rubbing, NTM typically produces surfaces **free of residual stress, work-hardening, and mechanical deformation** (though some thermal processes may leave a thin HAZ — Heat Affected Zone).

---

## 2.2 Process-Specific Advantages and Applications (KTU Module 4 Core Content)

### 2.2.1 Electrical Discharge Machining (EDM)

**Mechanism:** A controlled spark discharge between the tool (electrode) and workpiece in a dielectric fluid vaporizes and melts the workpiece material.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Fact:** EDM is the **most-frequently asked** NTM process in KTU university exams — at least one Part-A (3 mark) question per cycle.

| Parameter | Value / Description |
|-----------|---------------------|
| Workpiece Requirement | Must be **electrically conductive** |
| Tool Material | Copper, graphite, brass, tungsten |
| Dielectric Fluid | EDM oil, kerosene, deionized water |
| Surface Finish | Very good (Ra = 0.2 – 1.6 µm) |
| MRR | Low to moderate |
| Achievable Hole Diameter | As small as **0.05 mm** (micro-EDM) |

**KTU Standard Advantages of EDM:**

- Can machine **hardened steels** (HRC 60–70) directly after heat treatment → eliminates the need for soft machining + post-heat treatment
- No burr formation on the workpiece
- **No mechanical stress** on the workpiece (ideal for thin, fragile parts)
- Can produce **complex 3D cavities** with sharp internal corners
- **Wire-EDM** variant can cut intricate contours and tapered profiles

**Major Industrial Applications of EDM:**

1. **Die-making industry** — forging dies, die-casting dies, plastic moulds, extrusion dies
2. **Aerospace industry** — turbine blade cooling holes, blisks
3. **Tool room** — broaches, hobs, form tools, punches
4. **Medical devices** — surgical instruments, micro-implants
5. **Automotive** — fuel injector nozzles, gears, splines

---

### 2.2.2 Electrochemical Machining (ECM)

**Mechanism:** Controlled anodic dissolution of the workpiece in an electrolytic cell, governed by **Faraday's Law of Electrolysis.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Fact:** ECM is the **fastest NTM process** for deep cavities in hard metals and is the **#1 KTU answer** for *non-thermal, stress-free* machining.

| Parameter | Value / Description |
|-----------|---------------------|
| Workpiece Requirement | Must be **electrically conductive** |
| Tool Material | Brass, copper, stainless steel (does not get consumed) |
| Electrolyte | NaCl, NaNO₃, NaOH solution |
| Surface Finish | Excellent (Ra = 0.1 – 0.8 µm) |
| MRR | Very high |
| Heat Affected Zone | **None (cold process)** |

**KTU Standard Advantages of ECM:**

- **Zero tool wear** — tool never physically contacts the workpiece
- **Zero heat-affected zone** — purely electrochemical, no thermal damage
- **No residual stress** or work-hardening
- High MRR for **nickel-based superalloys** (Inconel, Waspaloy, Nimonic)
- Excellent for **complex 3D cavities, deep slots, turbine blade profiles**

**Major Industrial Applications of ECM:**

1. **Aerospace — turbine blade airfoil machining** (ECM is the industry standard)
2. **Die and mould making** for forged/cast parts
3. **Gun barrel rifling**
4. **Carbide and hardened-steel machining**
5. **Engines — deburring and port machining** (Electrochemical Deburring — ECD)
6. **Medical — machining of cobalt-chrome orthopedic implants**

---

### 2.2.3 Laser Beam Machining (LBM)

**Mechanism:** A **focused, high-power-density laser beam** (CO₂ or Nd:YAG or Fiber Laser) melts and vaporizes the workpiece material.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Fact:** LBM is **material-agnostic** — it can machine metals *and* non-metals (wood, plastic, fabric, ceramic) because the removal mechanism is **thermal vaporization**.

| Parameter | Value / Description |
|-----------|---------------------|
| Workpiece Material | **Any** — metal, non-metal, ceramic, plastic |
| Laser Types | CO₂ (10.6 µm), Nd:YAG (1.06 µm), Fiber (1.07 µm) |
| Power Density | 10⁶ – 10⁷ W/mm² |
| Hole Diameter | As small as 5 µm |
| HAZ | Small but **present** (thermal process) |

**KTU Standard Advantages of LBM:**

- **No tool wear** (the "tool" is light)
- Can machine **non-conductive materials** (glass, plastic, ceramic, wood, fabric)
- **Micron-level precision** (holes as small as 5 µm achievable)
- **Non-contact** — no mechanical stress
- Can drill **very deep holes** (depth-to-diameter ratio > 100:1)
- **High speed** for thin sheets

**Major Industrial Applications of LBM:**

1. **Electronics industry** — drilling micro-holes in printed circuit boards (PCBs)
2. **Aerospace** — drilling cooling holes in turbine blades, combustor liners
3. **Medical** — micro-machining of stents, surgical tools
4. **Automotive** — sheet-metal cutting, gasket cutting
5. **Jewellery & crafts** — engraving, cutting intricate patterns
6. **Textile industry** — cutting synthetic fabrics, composites

---

### 2.2.4 Electron Beam Machining (EBM)

**Mechanism:** A **focused beam of high-velocity electrons** (in a vacuum) strikes the workpiece, converting kinetic energy to **heat**, which melts and vaporizes the material.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Fact:** EBM **must be performed in a vacuum** — this is the most-asked distinguishing point in KTU viva and Part-A.

| Parameter | Value / Description |
|-----------|---------------------|
| Workpiece Material | Conductors and some non-conductors |
| Environment | **Vacuum** (10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ torr) |
| Power Density | Up to 10⁹ W/mm² (highest among NTMs) |
| HAZ | Very small |
| Beam Diameter | 10 – 25 µm |

**KTU Standard Advantages of EBM:**

- **Highest power density** of all NTM processes
- Extremely **small HAZ** (because beam is in vacuum — no oxidation)
- Very **precise** — micron-level accuracy
- Can weld dissimilar metals and perform **narrow, deep welds**

**Major Industrial Applications of EBM:**

1. **Micro-machining and micro-welding** in electronics
2. **Aerospace** — drilling micro-holes in turbine blades
3. **Nuclear industry** — welding of reactive metals (zirconium, titanium, beryllium)
4. **Semiconductor industry** — mask repair, thin-film patterning
5. **Joining of dissimilar metals** without filler

---

### 2.2.5 Ultrasonic Machining (USM)

**Mechanism:** A tool vibrates at **ultrasonic frequency (20–40 kHz)** in an abrasive slurry; the abrasive particles impact the workpiece and remove material by **micro-chipping**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Fact:** USM is the **only NTM that can machine glass and quartz economically** — a KTU-favourite one-liner.

| Parameter | Value / Description |
|-----------|---------------------|
| Workpiece Material | **Hard, brittle** — glass, ceramics, quartz, germanium, silicon |
| Abrasive | Boron carbide (B₄C), silicon carbide (SiC), aluminium oxide (Al₂O₃) |
| Frequency | 20 – 40 kHz |
| Amplitude | 15 – 50 µm |
| Tool Wear | Yes (slow) |

**KTU Standard Advantages of USM:**

- **No thermal damage** (purely mechanical impact)
- Ideal for **brittle, non-conductive** materials
- **No chemical / electrochemical** side effects
- High dimensional accuracy
- Can produce **square, round, and irregular holes** in glass and ceramics

**Major Industrial Applications of USM:**

1. **Electronics & semiconductors** — machining silicon, germanium, quartz wafers
2. **Optics industry** — machining lenses, prisms, optical flats
3. **Medical** — ceramic and zirconia implants
4. **Aerospace** — drilling holes in ceramic matrix composites
5. **Jewellery** — engraving on hard stones

---

### 2.2.6 Abrasive Jet Machining (AJM)

**Mechanism:** A high-velocity **stream of abrasive particles** (garnet, Al₂O₃) carried by a gas jet (air/N₂/CO₂) erodes the workpiece by **micro-cutting action**.

**KTU Standard Advantages of AJM:**

- Can machine **brittle and hard materials**
- **No thermal damage**
- Suitable for **cleaning, deburring, and etching** of surfaces
- Can cut **thin, brittle sheets** (germanium, silicon, mica)
- **No chemical or electrical** side effects

**Major Industrial Applications of AJM:**

1. **Cleaning of surfaces** — removal of paint, rust, scale
2. **Deburring and frosting** of glass
3. **Cutting thin brittle materials** — germanium, silicon, mica, glass
4. **Drilling micro-holes** in brittle wafers
5. **Artistic engraving** on glass, marble

---

### 2.2.7 Water Jet Machining (WJM / AWJM)

**Mechanism:** A **high-pressure water jet (or abrasive-mixed jet)** impinges on the workpiece and removes material by **erosion**.

**KTU Standard Advantages of WJM:**

- **No thermal damage** → ideal for heat-sensitive materials
- **No chemical or electrical** side effects
- Can cut **thick sections** of metal, stone, composites
- **Environmentally friendly** (only water + garnet abrasive)
- **Narrow kerf width** → less material waste

**Major Industrial Applications of WJM:**

1. **Stone and marble cutting** — granite, marble, slate
2. **Composites** — cutting carbon-fibre and glass-fibre composites
3. **Aerospace** — cutting of titanium and aluminium honeycomb panels
4. **Automotive** — interior trim, dashboard cutting
5. **Food industry** — cutting frozen foods, chocolates
6. **Ship-building** — thick steel plate profiling

---

### 2.2.8 Plasma Arc Machining (PAM)

**Mechanism:** A high-velocity **jet of ionized gas (plasma)** at temperatures up to **30,000 °C** melts and blows away the material.

**KTU Standard Advantages of PAM:**

- Very **high MRR** for thick metal plates
- Can cut **any electrically conductive metal** (including stainless steel, aluminium, copper)
- **Lower cost** than laser for thick-plate cutting
- **Portable and hand-held** versions available

**Major Industrial Applications of PAM:**

1. **Steel fabrication workshops** — cutting, beveling, gouging
2. **Ship-building** — thick steel plate profiling
3. **Construction** — cutting reinforcement bars
4. **Scrap metal cutting**
5. **Welding** (Plasma Arc Welding — PAW)

---

### 2.2.9 Chemical Machining (CHM)

**Mechanism:** A **chemical etchant** (e.g., FeCl₃, HNO₃) selectively dissolves the unmasked regions of the workpiece.

**KTU Standard Advantages of CHM:**

- **No thermal or mechanical stress** on the workpiece
- Can produce **very complex 2-D contours** without a tool
- Suitable for **very thin sheets** and large panels
- **Low tooling cost** (only a mask is required)
- No burr formation

**Major Industrial Applications of CHM:**

1. **Aerospace** — chemical milling of aircraft wing skins (weight reduction)
2. **Electronics** — printed circuit board (PCB) etching
3. **Sheet metal** — decorative panels, nameplates
4. **Sculpture and signage** — metal artworks, logos
5. **Medical devices** — etching of micro-channels

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The table below is the single most-important reference for solving any KTU numerical on NTM.** CRITICAL formatting note: all absolute-value bars are written as `\vert` so they do not break the markdown table syntax.

| # | Process | Governing Law | Key Formula | Units |
|---|---------|---------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | EDM | Energy balance | $\text{MRR} = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot \eta}{W}$ | mm³/min |
| 2 | EDM | Pulse-energy method | $\text{MRR} = \dfrac{k_{edm} \cdot V \cdot I}{t_p \cdot f}$ | mm³/min |
| 3 | EDM | Volumetric | $\text{MRR} = A \cdot v_{rec}$ | mm³/min |
| 4 | ECM | Faraday's Law | $\text{MRR} = \dfrac{A \cdot I \cdot \eta}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$ | mm³/s or cm³/min |
| 5 | ECM | Linear feed | $v_f = \dfrac{\eta \cdot I \cdot k}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$ | mm/s |
| 6 | LBM | Power density | $P_d = \dfrac{P}{\pi r_b^{\,2}}$ | W/mm² |
| 7 | LBM | Drilling time | $t_d = \dfrac{\pi d^{2} h}{4 \,\text{MRR}}$ | s |
| 8 | USM | MRR empirical | $\text{MRR} = k_u \cdot f \cdot A \cdot d_a$ | mm³/min |
| 9 | WJM | Kerf width | $w_k = 2 \, r_j + 2 \, \delta$ | mm |
| 10 | PAM | Cutting speed | $v_c = \dfrac{I}{t_c \cdot k_{pam}}$ | mm/min |
| 11 | EDM | Tool Wear Ratio | $\text{TWR} = \dfrac{V_{\text{tool removed}}}{V_{\text{work removed}}}$ | dimensionless |
| 12 | ECM | Specific Energy | $E_s = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{v_f \cdot A \cdot \rho}$ | J/mm³ |
| 13 | AJM | MRR | $\text{MRR} = k_{ajm} \cdot m_a \cdot v_p$ | mg/min |
| 14 | CHM | Etch rate | $R_e = \dfrac{T}{t_e}$ | mm/min |
| 15 | All | Overcut / Side Gap | $OC = \dfrac{\phi_{\text{hole}} - \phi_{\text{tool}}}{2}$ | mm |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The three most-likely numerical questions from this module are:
> 1. **EDM MRR** from current, voltage, pulse-on time
> 2. **ECM MRR / linear feed** from current using Faraday's law
> 3. **LBM power density** from laser power and beam radius

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of NTM

| Industry | Most-Used NTM | Why |
|----------|---------------|-----|
| Aerospace | ECM, EDM, LBM | Hard superalloys, complex blade cavities, micro cooling holes |
| Tool & Die | EDM, WJM | Hardened tool-steel dies, intricate cavities |
| Electronics | LBM, USM, CHM, EBM | Micro-holes, wafer dicing, PCB etching |
| Medical | EDM, LBM, USM | Micro-implants, stents, surgical instruments, ceramic implants |
| Automotive | EDM, PAM, ECM | Fuel injectors, gear dies, port deburring |
| Stone & Construction | WJM, AJM | Marble, granite, tile cutting |
| Ship-building | PAM, WJM | Thick steel plate profiling |

---

## 2.5 Selection Logic — Which NTM for Which Application?

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Decision Tree (memorize for design questions):**
> 
> 1. Workpiece **electrically conductive + very hard** → **EDM**
> 2. Workpiece **electrically conductive + complex 3D cavity + no thermal damage allowed** → **ECM**
> 3. Workpiece **non-conductive (glass, ceramic, plastic)** → **USM** (if brittle), **LBM** (if any)
> 4. **Very deep, narrow holes** (depth: dia > 10:1) → **LBM / EBM**
> 5. **Thick metal plate** cutting → **PAM / WJM**
> 6. **2D contour** on thin sheet without thermal damage → **CHM / WJM**
> 7. **Cleaning / deburring** of a surface → **AJM**
> 8. **Micro-welding of dissimilar metals** → **EBM**

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Section Goal:** Three exhaustive derivations that the KTU board most commonly sets as full 14-mark Part-B numericals. Every algebraic step is written out — no shortcuts.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Material Removal Rate in EDM from Energy Balance

### Statement
Derive an expression for the **MRR in EDM** in terms of the discharge energy, melting energy of the workpiece material, and the pulse-on time.

### Given
- Discharge voltage = $V$ (volts)
- Discharge current = $I$ (amperes)
- Pulse-on time per spark = $t_p$ (seconds)
- Pulse frequency = $f$ (Hz)
- Melting energy per unit volume of workpiece = $W$ (J/mm³)
- Efficiency of energy utilization = $\eta$ (decimal)

### Step 1 — Energy Released per Spark
Each electrical spark releases thermal energy equal to the product of voltage, current, and pulse duration:

$$
E_{spark} = V \cdot I \cdot t_p
$$

**Explanation (in words):** This is simply the electrical energy dissipated across the spark gap. Of this energy, only a fraction $\eta$ is effectively used for melting the workpiece; the rest is lost to the dielectric, the tool, and radiation.

### Step 2 — Energy Released per Second (Effective Energy)
With spark frequency $f$, the number of sparks per second is $f$. The **effective energy available for material removal per second** is:

$$
E_{sec} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot t_p \cdot f
$$

**Explanation (in words):** Each spark contributes $\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot t_p$ joules to melting. Multiplying by $f$ sparks per second gives the energy per second that actually melts the workpiece.

### Step 3 — Volume of Material Removed per Second
The melting energy required to remove 1 mm³ of workpiece is $W$ J/mm³. Therefore, the **volume removed per second** (which is the MRR in mm³/s) is:

$$
\text{MRR} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot t_p \cdot f}{W}
$$

### Step 4 — Converting to mm³/min (Standard KTU Unit)
Multiply by 60 seconds:

$$
\text{MRR} = \dfrac{60 \, \eta \, V \, I \, t_p \, f}{W} \quad [\text{mm}^3/\text{min}]
$$

### Final Result

$$
\boxed{\,\text{MRR}_{\text{EDM}} = \dfrac{60 \, \eta \, V \, I \, t_p \, f}{W}\,}
$$

**Engineering Meaning:** MRR increases linearly with voltage, current, frequency, and pulse duration, and decreases with the melting energy of the workpiece (which is why **aluminium has a high MRR** in EDM while **tungsten has a low MRR**).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Material Removal Rate in ECM from Faraday's Law

### Statement
Derive the **MRR in ECM** using Faraday's two laws of electrolysis, and express the linear feed rate of the tool.

### Given
- Current = $I$ (amperes)
- Atomic weight of workpiece = $A$ (kg/kmol or g/mol)
- Valency of dissolution = $n$ (number of electrons per atom dissolved)
- Density of workpiece = $\rho$ (kg/m³ or g/cm³)
- Electrochemical equivalent = $Z = A / (n F)$ (kg/C or g/C)
- Faraday's constant $F = 96{,}500$ C/mol
- Current efficiency = $\eta$ (decimal)
- Cross-sectional area of cut = $A_c$ (mm²)

### Step 1 — Faraday's First Law
The **mass of material dissolved** is directly proportional to the **charge passed**:

$$
m \;\propto\; Q
$$

In equation form:

$$
m = Z \cdot I \cdot t
$$

**Explanation (in words):** The greater the current, the greater the amount of metal dissolved from the anode (workpiece).

### Step 2 — Faraday's Second Law
If multiple substances are dissolved by the same charge, the masses dissolved are proportional to their **chemical equivalents**:

$$
m = \dfrac{A}{n F} \cdot I \cdot t
$$

This is the **electrochemical equivalent** $Z = A / (n F)$.

### Step 3 — Introducing Current Efficiency
In real ECM, not all current contributes to dissolution. Let $\eta$ be the **current efficiency** (typically 90–100% for active metals, 60–90% for passive metals). The effective mass is:

$$
m = \dfrac{\eta \cdot A \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}
$$

### Step 4 — Converting Mass to Volume
Volume removed = mass ÷ density:

$$
V = \dfrac{m}{\rho} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot A \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}
$$

### Step 5 — MRR in mm³/min
Divide by time $t$ (in seconds) to get volume per second, then convert to mm³/min by multiplying by 60 (and converting $\rho$ to consistent units — kg/m³ to g/cm³ requires a factor of $10^{-3}$, but KTU standard practice is to keep density in g/cm³ and express MRR in mm³/min using the simplified form):

$$
\boxed{\,\text{MRR}_{\text{ECM}} = \dfrac{60 \, \eta \, A_c \, I}{n \, F \, \rho}\,} \quad [\text{mm}^3/\text{min}]
$$

### Step 6 — Linear Feed Rate of the Tool
In steady-state ECM, the tool feeds forward at a rate exactly equal to the **recession rate of the workpiece**. The linear feed rate is MRR divided by the cross-sectional area of cut:

$$
v_f = \dfrac{\text{MRR}}{A_c} = \dfrac{60 \, \eta \, I}{n \, F \, \rho}
$$

**Final Result (Boxed):**

$$
\boxed{\,v_{f,\text{ECM}} = \dfrac{60 \, \eta \, I}{n \, F \, \rho}\,} \quad [\text{mm}/\text{s} \text{ or mm}/\text{min}]
$$

**Engineering Meaning:** In ECM, the **tool feed rate is independent of tool material, tool hardness, and the tool's geometric wear**. This is *the* fundamental reason ECM achieves zero tool wear and zero HAZ.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Power Density in LBM

### Statement
Derive the **power density** of a focused laser beam and show how it is used to determine the material-removal regime (cutting, drilling, or welding).

### Given
- Laser output power = $P$ (watts)
- Beam radius at focal point = $r_b$ (mm)
- Power density = $P_d$ (W/mm²)

### Step 1 — Cross-Sectional Area of the Focused Beam
A focused laser beam is circular at the focal point. The cross-sectional area is:

$$
A_b = \pi \, r_b^{\,2}
$$

**Explanation (in words):** A laser beam, when focused through a lens, converges to a near-circular spot. The smaller the spot, the more concentrated the energy.

### Step 2 — Definition of Power Density
Power density is the **power incident per unit area**:

$$
P_d = \dfrac{P}{A_b} = \dfrac{P}{\pi \, r_b^{\,2}}
$$

### Step 3 — Relating to MRR and Material Properties
For a workpiece to be removed, the absorbed power density must exceed the **threshold power density** for vaporization:

$$
P_d \;\geq\; P_{d,\text{threshold}} = \rho \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T \cdot v + L_v \cdot \rho \cdot v
$$

where $v$ is the cutting velocity and $L_v$ is the **latent heat of vaporization**.

**Explanation (in words):** The threshold depends on the density, specific heat, melting point, and latent heat of vaporization of the workpiece. A high $P_d$ allows rapid vaporization → high MRR.

### Final Result

$$
\boxed{\,P_d = \dfrac{P}{\pi \, r_b^{\,2}}\,} \quad [\text{W}/\text{mm}^2]
$$

**Engineering Meaning:** To *cut* a material with LBM, the power density must be **> 10⁶ W/mm²**. Below 10⁴ W/mm², the beam is used only for *welding*; below 10³ W/mm², it is used only for *heating*. This is the **single most-asked threshold question** in KTU Part-A.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — KTU Style (EDM MRR)

> [!TIP]
> **Problem:** In an EDM operation on tool steel, the discharge parameters are:
> $V = 60$ V, $I = 30$ A, $t_p = 200$ µs, $f = 5$ kHz, $\eta = 0.85$, and the melting energy of tool steel is $W = 4.2$ J/mm³.
> Calculate the MRR in mm³/min.

**Solution (exhaustive steps):**

**Step 1 — Substitute values into the EDM MRR formula:**

$$
\text{MRR} = \dfrac{60 \, \eta \, V \, I \, t_p \, f}{W}
$$

**Step 2 — Insert numerical values (with unit conversion: $t_p = 200 \, \mu s = 200 \times 10^{-6} \, s$):**

$$
\text{MRR} = \dfrac{60 \times 0.85 \times 60 \times 30 \times (200 \times 10^{-6}) \times (5 \times 10^3)}{4.2}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerator (compute step by step):**

$$
60 \times 0.85 = 51
$$

$$
51 \times 60 = 3060
$$

$$
3060 \times 30 = 91{,}800
$$

$$
91{,}800 \times 200 \times 10^{-6} = 91{,}800 \times 2 \times 10^{-4} = 18.36
$$

$$
18.36 \times 5 \times 10^{3} = 91{,}800
$$

**Step 4 — Divide by $W$:**

$$
\text{MRR} = \dfrac{91{,}800}{4.2} = 21{,}857.14 \;\; \text{mm}^3/\text{min}
$$

**Step 5 — Round and present:**

$$
\boxed{\,\text{MRR} \approx 21{,}857 \;\; \text{mm}^3/\text{min}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineer's sanity check:** This value (~22 cm³/min) is realistic for rough-cut EDM on tool steel — well within published industrial ranges of 5,000 – 50,000 mm³/min.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical — KTU Style (ECM Feed Rate)

> [!TIP]
> **Problem:** Calculate the linear feed rate of the tool in ECM machining of pure iron.
> Given: $I = 1500$ A, $n = 2$ (Fe dissolves as Fe²⁺), $\eta = 0.95$, $F = 96{,}500$ C/mol, $\rho_{Fe} = 7.86$ g/cm³.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Use the ECM feed rate formula:**

$$
v_f = \dfrac{60 \, \eta \, I}{n \, F \, \rho}
$$

**Step 2 — Insert values (treating $F \cdot \rho$ in C·g/cm³ form — note that $F$ already converts mol to charge, and $\rho$ converts g to cm³, so the units of $v_f$ become cm/min; the numerical magnitude is what we report, with conversion to mm/min by $\times 10$):**

$$
v_f = \dfrac{60 \times 0.95 \times 1500}{2 \times 96{,}500 \times 7.86}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerator:**

$$
60 \times 0.95 \times 1500 = 85{,}500
$$

**Step 4 — Denominator:**

$$
2 \times 96{,}500 \times 7.86 = 1{,}517{,}580
$$

**Step 5 — Compute $v_f$ in cm/min, then convert to mm/min:**

$$
v_f = \dfrac{85{,}500}{1{,}517{,}580} = 0.0563 \;\; \text{cm/min}
$$

$$
v_f = 0.0563 \times 10 = 0.563 \;\; \text{mm/min}
$$

**Step 6 — Present final answer:**

$$
\boxed{\,v_f \approx 0.56 \;\; \text{mm}/\text{min}\,}
$$

**Engineering meaning:** This extremely low feed rate is **typical of ECM** and is acceptable because ECM has **zero tool wear**, **zero HAZ**, and produces a **simultaneously finished surface** in a single pass.

---

## 3.6 Master Analytical Matrix — "Process vs Property" (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The matrix below is a one-page summary that the KTU board expects students to internalize for 14-mark comparison questions.**

| Property \ Process | EDM | ECM | LBM | EBM | USM | WJM | AJM | PAM | CHM |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Energy Type | Thermal/Electrical | Electrochemical | Thermal | Thermal | Mechanical | Mechanical | Mechanical | Thermal | Chemical |
| Workpiece | Conductor | Conductor | Any | Any | Hard Brittle | Any | Any | Conductor | Any |
| Tool Wear | Yes | **None** | None | None | Yes | Yes | Yes (nozzle) | Yes (electrode) | None |
| HAZ | Small | **None** | Small | Very small | None | None | None | Yes | None |
| Surface Finish | Good | **Excellent** | Good | Excellent | Good | Good | Good | Poor | Good |
| MRR | Medium | High | Medium | Low | Low | Medium | Low | **High** | Low |
| Micro-hole | Yes (micro-EDM) | Limited | **Yes (< 5 µm)** | **Yes** | Yes | Limited | Yes | No | No |
| HRC > 60 | **Yes** | **Yes** | Yes | Yes | Yes | No | No | Limited | Yes |
| Stressed Part | **Best** | **Best** | Good | Good | Good | Good | Good | Poor | Good |
| Vacuum | No | No | No | **Yes** | No | No | No | No | No |
| Typical Industry | Tool-room | Aerospace | Electronics | Nuclear | Optics | Stone | Cleaning | Ship-yard | Aerospace |
