# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q: List any three advantages of Non-Traditional Machining (NTM) over conventional machining processes.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — KTU key):**

1. **Material-Hardness Independence:** NTM can machine workpieces irrespective of their mechanical hardness. The material is removed by non-mechanical energy (thermal, chemical, electrical) and therefore does not require the workpiece to be softer than the tool. *(1 mark)*

2. **No Mechanical Cutting Force:** Because the "tool" in NTM is energy (spark, jet, beam, chemical), there is no physical contact and hence no induced mechanical stress, vibration, or chatter on the workpiece. This makes NTM ideal for machining **thin, fragile, and brittle** components. *(1 mark)*

3. **Capability for Micro and Complex Geometry:** NTM can produce features as small as a few microns (e.g., LBM holes down to 5 µm, micro-EDM holes down to 50 µm) and complex 3-D cavities with sharp internal corners that a rotating tool cannot reach. *(1 mark)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: Differentiate between EDM and ECM in terms of tool wear and heat-affected zone.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — KTU table-format expected):**

| Criterion | EDM | ECM |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| **Tool Wear** | Yes — tool electrode is gradually eroded (TWR typically 1–5%) | **No tool wear** — tool never physically contacts the workpiece |
| **Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ)** | Yes — small HAZ (~2–10 µm) due to spark heating | **No HAZ** — purely electrochemical, isothermal dissolution |
| **Surface Stress** | Small tensile residual stress from rapid heating-cooling | **Zero residual stress** — no thermal or mechanical loading |
| **Best for** | Hardened tool steel, micro-holes | Hard superalloys, turbine blade cavities |

*(1 mark for tool-wear contrast, 1 mark for HAZ contrast, 1 mark for a valid supporting point.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks with a 50% internal choice. Two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### 🔹 Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

**Q: With the help of a neat sketch, explain the principle, working, and applications of the Electrical Discharge Machining (EDM) process. Also list its advantages and limitations. (14 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Principle, Working & Sketch [7 marks]

**Principle (2 marks):**
EDM removes material by means of a **rapid series of discrete electrical sparks** between the tool (electrode) and the workpiece, which are separated by a small gap (~10–50 µm) and immersed in a **dielectric fluid**. Each spark produces a temperature of about **8,000–12,000 °C**, which locally melts and vaporizes a tiny volume of the workpiece.

**Working Steps (3 marks):**

1. The tool is connected to the **negative terminal** of a pulsed DC power supply; the workpiece is connected to the **positive terminal**.
2. A servo mechanism feeds the tool towards the workpiece until the gap is small enough to initiate a spark.
3. A **pulse generator** applies voltage across the gap, ionizing the dielectric and producing a spark.
4. The spark vaporizes a small crater of workpiece material and erodes a small amount of the tool.
5. The dielectric **flushes away the debris** (eroded particles) and **quenches the spark zone**, preventing short-circuiting.
6. The cycle repeats thousands of times per second, producing a cavity that is a **mirror image of the tool shape**.

**Neat Sketch Description (2 marks):**
*(Student should draw — a labelled schematic showing: tool electrode, workpiece, dielectric tank, pulsed DC source, servo feed mechanism, dielectric inlet/outlet, flushing nozzles, filtration unit.)*

#### Part (b) — Advantages, Limitations & Applications [7 marks]

**Advantages (3 marks):**

1. Can machine **hardened steels (HRC 60–70)** without softening first
2. **No burr** formation on the workpiece
3. **No mechanical stress** — ideal for thin, fragile parts
4. Can produce **complex 3-D cavities** with sharp internal corners
5. **Wire-EDM** can cut intricate contours in any conductive material

**Limitations (2 marks):**

1. **Slow** compared to conventional milling/turning for soft materials
2. **Workpiece must be electrically conductive**
3. **Tool wear** accumulates (TWR = 1–5% for graphite, 5–25% for copper)
4. **High energy consumption** and dielectric cost

**Major Applications (2 marks):**

1. **Die and mould making** — forging dies, die-casting dies, plastic injection moulds
2. **Aerospace** — turbine blade cooling holes, blisks, combustor liners
3. **Tool room** — broaches, hobs, form tools, punches
4. **Medical** — micro-machining of surgical implants and instruments

---

### 🔹 Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q: Explain the principle, working, and applications of Electrochemical Machining (ECM). Derive the expression for the Material Removal Rate (MRR) using Faraday's laws. (14 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Principle, Working & Applications [7 marks]

**Principle (2 marks):**
ECM removes material by **controlled anodic dissolution** in an electrolytic cell. The workpiece is the **anode** and the tool is the **cathode**; when a low-voltage (5–25 V) DC current is passed through an electrolyte (typically NaCl or NaNO₃), atoms of the workpiece lose electrons and dissolve into the electrolyte as metal ions, without any tool–workpiece contact.

**Working Steps (3 marks):**

1. The workpiece is connected to the **positive terminal** (anode); the tool to the **negative terminal** (cathode).
2. A high-velocity **electrolyte stream** is forced through the small gap (~0.1–1 mm) between tool and workpiece.
3. When current is passed, the workpiece dissolves according to Faraday's law. Hydrogen bubbles evolve at the tool.
4. The flowing electrolyte **carries away the dissolved metal ions and heat**, keeping the process stable.
5. The tool is **fed towards the workpiece** at a rate equal to the workpiece's recession rate, maintaining a constant gap.
6. The cavity produced is a **mirror image of the tool**, with a typical **side gap of 0.1–0.5 mm** (the "overcut").

**Applications (2 marks):**

1. **Aerospace** — machining of **turbine blade airfoil profiles** in nickel superalloys
2. **Die making** — complex forging-die cavities
3. **Gun barrel rifling**
4. **Carbide machining** of tungsten carbide tool bits
5. **Deburring** of cross-drilled holes (Electrochemical Deburring — ECD)

#### Part (b) — Derivation of MRR using Faraday's Laws [7 marks]

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Faraday's First Law:**
The mass of material dissolved is proportional to the charge passed:
$$m \propto Q \quad \Rightarrow \quad m = Z \cdot I \cdot t$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Faraday's Second Law:**
The mass dissolved is proportional to the **electrochemical equivalent** $Z = A / (n F)$:
$$m = \dfrac{A \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Introducing Current Efficiency $\eta$:**
$$m = \dfrac{\eta \cdot A \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Converting Mass to Volume (divide by density $\rho$):**
$$V = \dfrac{m}{\rho} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot A \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — MRR in mm³/min:**
$$\boxed{\,\text{MRR}_{\text{ECM}} = \dfrac{60 \, \eta \, A_c \, I}{n \, F \, \rho}\,}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Engineering Interpretation:**
- MRR is **independent of tool hardness** and **tool material** (because the tool does not dissolve)
- MRR is **directly proportional to current** — current is the main ECM control parameter
- For pure iron: $A = 55.85$ g/mol, $n = 2$, $\rho = 7.86$ g/cm³, $F = 96{,}500$ C/mol

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in NTM Questions (per KTU board pattern):**
> 
> 1. **Confusing EDM tool polarity:** Students frequently write "tool = positive." The **CORRECT** polarity is **tool = negative (−) cathode** and **workpiece = positive (+) anode**. (–1 mark)
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the vacuum in EBM:** Any answer that omits the "vacuum" requirement for Electron Beam Machining loses a key conceptual point. (–1 mark)
> 
> 3. **Wrong formula for ECM feed rate:** Mixing up linear feed rate ($v_f$) with MRR is the most common 2-mark error. MRR has units of **mm³/min**; feed rate has units of **mm/min**. (–2 marks if swapped)
> 
> 4. **Stating "ECM produces no heat":** Technically incorrect — there is Joule heating, but the flowing electrolyte removes the heat so quickly that the **workpiece temperature remains near ambient**. Say "no heat-affected zone" instead. (–1 mark)
> 
> 5. **In LBM, omitting laser type:** Always mention **CO₂ / Nd:YAG / Fiber** when asked to describe LBM. (–1 mark)
> 
> 6. **Writing AJM/WJM as "cutting" only:** Both processes also serve for **cleaning, deburring, and surface preparation** — a complete answer covers all three functions. (–1 mark)

---

## 5.4 Rapid Revision Checklist — "What to Remember Before the Exam"

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute High-Density Recap (memorize the night before):**
> 
> - **NTM definition:** Material removal by **non-mechanical** energy (thermal/chemical/electrical)
> - **EDM:** Spark-based; tool = (−), workpiece = (+); dielectric = EDM oil; for hardened steel, dies, micro-holes
> - **ECM:** Anodic dissolution; **zero tool wear, zero HAZ, no stress**; governed by **Faraday's law**; MRR ∝ current; for superalloys, turbine blades
> - **LBM:** Focused light beam; can machine **any material** (metal + non-metal); threshold = 10⁶ W/mm²; for micro-holes, electronics, PCB
> - **EBM:** Electron beam in **vacuum**; highest power density (10⁹ W/mm²); smallest HAZ; for nuclear, semiconductor, micro-welding
> - **USM:** Abrasive + ultrasonic vibration; for **glass, ceramics, quartz, silicon**; pure mechanical, no thermal damage
> - **WJM/AWJM:** High-pressure water ± abrasive; **no thermal damage**; for stone, composites, food, thick plates
> - **AJM:** Abrasive particles in gas jet; for **cleaning, deburring, frosting**
> - **PAM:** Plasma arc at 30,000 °C; for **thick metal plate** cutting in fabrication
> - **CHM:** Chemical etchant + mask; for **2-D contours, aircraft skin milling, PCB etching**
> - **Hardness independence** is the **single most important** advantage — write it in every NTM answer
> - **Selection rule of thumb:** Conductive + Hard → **EDM**; Conductive + No HAZ + Complex cavity → **ECM**; Non-conductive → **USM / LBM**; Thick plate → **WJM / PAM**
> - **MRR units:** mm³/min (KTU standard) — not mm³/s
> - **Faraday's constant:** $F = 96{,}500$ C/mol — memorize exactly
> - **LBM threshold power density:** 10⁶ W/mm² for cutting (memorize the order of magnitude)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — HIGH-SPEED AND ULTRA-PRECISION MACHINING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive (Module 4):** This module deals with the two extreme frontiers of modern machining technology — *productivity-driven* High-Speed Machining (HSM) and *accuracy-driven* Ultra-Precision Machining (UPM). These are not merely "faster" or "finer" versions of conventional machining; they are governed by fundamentally different physical phenomena.

### 1.1 High-Speed Machining (HSM) — Formal Definition

**High-Speed Machining (HSM)** is a material removal process in which the **cutting speed ($V_c$)** is elevated to a range where the **cutting temperature in the primary shear zone approaches the recrystallization temperature of the workpiece material**, resulting in a transition from *thermally-activated plastic flow* to *adiabatic shear-dominated* chip formation.

In quantitative terms (as adopted in industrial and academic practice, and consistent with KTU references):

| Workpiece Material | Conventional $V_c$ (m/min) | HSM Threshold $V_c$ (m/min) |
|---|---|---|
| Mild Steel | 30 – 60 | > 500 |
| Stainless Steel | 20 – 50 | > 300 |
| Aluminum Alloys | 100 – 300 | > **1500 – 5000** |
| Titanium Alloys | 20 – 50 | > 150 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Note (for KTU 2-mark questions):** The phenomenon was first observed by **C. Salmon (1929)**, who noted that cutting forces *decreased* above a certain critical speed. **E. Bergmann (1950s)** is credited with the modern scientific foundation of HSM, while **W. König** and **F. Klocke** later formalized the classification.

### 1.2 Ultra-Precision Machining (UPM) — Formal Definition

**Ultra-Precision Machining (UPM)** refers to a class of material removal processes capable of producing:
- **Dimensional accuracy** in the order of **nanometers (nm) to sub-micrometers (μm)**
- **Surface roughness ($R_a$)** typically in the range **< 0.1 μm (100 nm)**, often reaching **< 10 nm** for optical-grade surfaces

The most representative UPM process is **Single-Point Diamond Turning (SPDT)**, which uses a **single-crystal natural diamond tool** with an edge radius on the order of **10 – 100 nm** to machine non-ferrous materials on ultra-stiff, thermally-stable machines.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition for HSM — The "Potato Peeler at Racing Speed" Analogy:**
> Imagine peeling a potato with a slow, heavy stroke — the peel is thick, the knife digs deep, and your hand feels strong resistance. Now imagine the *same knife* moving so fast that the peel barely has time to deform before it snaps off. The chip is thinner, the force drops, and most of the heat travels *with the chip* rather than into the potato. **This is exactly what happens in HSM** — speed softens the workpiece thermally *during the cut itself*, and the chip carries the heat away.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition for UPM — The "Sculpting with a Single Molecule" Analogy:**
> Conventional machining is like carving wood with a chisel — the chips are large, and surface texture is visible. Ultra-precision machining is more like gliding a single, atomically sharp diamond edge across a mirror, removing material in layers measured in *nanometers*. The entire environment (temperature, vibration, air) must be as still as a deep-sea trench for this to work.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Recrystallization Temperature of Steel:** ≈ **0.4 – 0.5 × Melting Temperature (K)** → For mild steel ≈ **450 – 600 °C**
- **HSM speed range for Al:** **3000 – 5000 m/min** (i.e., **50 – 83 m/s**)
- **UPM tool edge radius:** **10 – 100 nm** (≈ **1000× smaller than a human hair**)
- **UPM achievable $R_a$:** **1 – 10 nm** for diamond-turned optics
- **Diamond tool wear rate on aluminum:** ≈ **0.1 – 1 μm per km of cut length** (extremely low)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of High-Speed Machining

In conventional cutting, the **primary deformation zone** (shear zone) dissipates energy primarily through **plastic work**, with a significant fraction of generated heat conducting *into the workpiece*. As $V_c$ increases, the *time available for heat conduction* per unit length of cut decreases. The **Peclet number** for the shear zone, defined as

$$Pe = \frac{V_c \cdot t_s}{\alpha}$$

where $t_s$ is the shear zone thickness and $\alpha$ is the thermal diffusivity of the workpiece, becomes very large ($Pe \gg 1$). This signals that **conduction becomes negligible during the cut**, and the process is effectively **adiabatic**.

### 2.2 Consequences of Adiabatic Shearing in HSM

| Phenomenon | Conventional Cutting | High-Speed Cutting |
|---|---|---|
| Heat partition to workpiece | 30 – 50 % | < 10 % |
| Heat carried by chip | 50 – 70 % | **> 80 – 90 %** |
| Shear angle $\phi$ | Moderate | **Increases** |
| Cutting force $F_c$ | Steady | **Decreases with speed** |
| Built-up edge (BUE) | Common | **Suppressed** |
| Surface integrity | Possible re-hardening | Improved |

### 2.3 Theoretical Basis: Why Cutting Force Decreases in HSM

The **shear strength $\tau_s$** of the workpiece is highly temperature-sensitive (decreases with rising $T$):

$$\tau_s = \tau_{s,0} \left[1 - \beta (T - T_0)\right]$$

In HSM, the adiabatic temperature rise in the shear zone drastically reduces $\tau_s$, and since the **cutting force component** is given by

$$F_c = \frac{\tau_s \cdot A_s}{\sin\phi \cdot \cos(\phi + \beta_c)}$$

where $A_s = \dfrac{t_1 \cdot b}{\sin\phi}$ is the shear area, a reduction in $\tau_s$ directly lowers $F_c$ (the **Salmon effect**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** In HSM, the **shear angle $\phi$ INCREASES** with cutting speed. This causes **chip thickness to DECREASE** (since $t_2 = t_1 \cdot r$, and the chip-thickness ratio $r = \dfrac{\sin\phi}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)}$ increases as $\phi$ grows). The chip becomes **thinner and more curled** at higher speeds.

## 2.4 The Physics of Ultra-Precision Machining

UPM is governed by the **minimum undeformed chip thickness (MUCT)** problem. The diamond tool edge is *not geometrically perfect* — it has a finite edge radius $r_\beta$. When the uncut chip thickness $t_1$ falls below a critical value

$$t_{1,\min} \approx 0.5 \, r_\beta$$

the tool simply **ploughs** the material rather than cutting it, generating elastic–plastic deformation, residual stresses, and poor surface finish.

> [!NOTE]
> This is why **UPM uses extremely sharp tools** ($r_\beta$ ≈ 10 – 100 nm) and **very small depths of cut** (typically < 10 μm), and why the **machine stiffness, thermal stability, and vibration isolation** must be extraordinary.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit / Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Pe = \dfrac{V_c \cdot t_s}{\alpha}$ | Peclet number — adiabatic criterion | $Pe \gg 1$ for HSM |
| 2 | $\tau_s = \tau_{s,0}\left[1 - \beta(T - T_0)\right]$ | Thermal softening of shear zone | $\beta$ = softening coefficient |
| 3 | $F_c = \dfrac{\tau_s \cdot t_1 \cdot b}{\sin\phi \cos(\phi + \beta_c)}$ | Cutting force in shear-plane model | N |
| 4 | $r = \dfrac{\sin\phi}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)}$ | Chip-thickness ratio (Ortiz) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $t_2 = \dfrac{t_1}{r}$ | Chip thickness after cut | mm |
| 6 | $\phi = 45^\circ + \dfrac{\beta_c}{2} - \dfrac{\alpha}{2}$ | Merchant's shear-angle equation | degrees |
| 7 | $U_s = \dfrac{F_c \cdot V_c}{Z \cdot w \cdot d}$ | Specific cutting energy | J/mm³ or N/mm² |
| 8 | $MRR = V_c \cdot f \cdot d$ | Metal Removal Rate | mm³/min |
| 9 | $t_{1,\min} \approx 0.5 \, r_\beta$ | Minimum chip thickness for cutting | mm |
| 10 | $R_a \approx \dfrac{f^2}{32 \, R}$ | Theoretical surface roughness (turning) | μm, $R$ = tool nose radius |
| 11 | $P_{spindle} = \dfrac{F_c \cdot V_c}{60 \, \eta}$ | Spindle power requirement | kW, $\eta$ = efficiency |
| 12 | $V_{HSM,steel} > 500$ m/min | HSM threshold for steel | empirical |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX-Safe Note:** In the table above, vertical bars in expressions like $\vert \beta \vert$ have been written as $\beta$ (the variable is itself a coefficient), and all equations use `\vert` or `\mid` equivalents inside the row to prevent markdown table breakage.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace industry:** HSM is used to machine thin-walled aluminum wing spars and titanium blisks, where low cutting force prevents *part deflection* (a major cause of scrap in monolithic aerospace components).
- **Optics manufacturing:** UPM/SPDT is the *only* practical method to produce aspheric infrared lenses and **Keck telescope mirror segments** (with sub-50 nm $R_a$).
- **Hard-disk substrates:** UPM is used to polish aluminum disk platters to $R_a <$ 1 nm so the read/write head can fly at < 10 nm altitude.
- **Die and mold making:** HSM of hardened tool steels (HRC > 55) using ball-end mills has *replaced* die-sinking EDM in many applications due to faster cycle times.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Cutting Speed and Shear Zone Temperature

We derive the **adiabatic temperature rise** in the primary shear zone, which is the cornerstone of HSM theory.

### Step 1 — Energy Dissipated in Shear

The plastic work done in the shear zone per unit time is:

$$P_s = F_s \cdot V_s$$

where $F_s$ is the shear force and $V_s$ is the shear velocity.

### Step 2 — Shear Velocity in Terms of Cutting Velocity

From the velocity diagram in orthogonal cutting:

$$V_s = \frac{V_c \cos\alpha}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)}$$

where $\alpha$ is the rake angle and $\phi$ is the shear angle.

### Step 3 — Shear Force Expression

From the Merchant force circle:

$$F_s = \frac{F_c \cos(\phi + \beta_c - \alpha)}{\cos\beta_c}$$

For simplicity in the **HSM regime** where $\beta_c$ (friction angle) tends to decrease and rake is positive, a simplified shear-force approximation is:

$$F_s = \tau_s \cdot A_s = \tau_s \cdot \frac{t_1 \, b}{\sin\phi}$$

### Step 4 — Adiabatic Heat Generation Rate

Substituting and recognizing that in the adiabatic limit, **all plastic work converts to heat** (Taylor–Quinney coefficient $\approx 0.9$ for metals):

$$\dot{Q}_{gen} = 0.9 \cdot \tau_s \cdot \frac{t_1 \, b}{\sin\phi} \cdot \frac{V_c \cos\alpha}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)}$$

### Step 5 — Heat Capacity of the Chip (Adiabatic Sink)

The chip mass flow rate is:

$$\dot{m}_{chip} = \rho \cdot t_2 \cdot b \cdot V_c = \rho \cdot \frac{t_1}{r} \cdot b \cdot V_c$$

with $\rho$ = density of workpiece, $r$ = chip-thickness ratio.

### Step 6 — Adiabatic Temperature Rise in the Shear Zone

Setting the generated heat equal to the chip's sensible heat rise ($\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$):

$$0.9 \cdot \tau_s \cdot \frac{t_1 \, b}{\sin\phi} \cdot \frac{V_c \cos\alpha}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)} = \rho \cdot \frac{t_1}{r} \cdot b \cdot V_c \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$$

### Step 7 — Simplification

Cancelling $t_1$, $b$, and $V_c$ from both sides:

$$\Delta T = \frac{0.9 \cdot \tau_s \cdot r \cdot \cos\alpha}{C_p \cdot \rho \cdot \sin\phi \cdot \cos(\phi - \alpha)}$$

### Step 8 — Final Compact Form

Since $r = \dfrac{\sin\phi}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)}$, we substitute:

$$\Delta T_{adiabatic} = \frac{0.9 \cdot \tau_s}{C_p \cdot \rho}$$

### Step 9 — Engineering Interpretation

This is the **maximum possible temperature rise** in the shear zone — the **adiabatic limit**. Substituting typical values for mild steel:

- $\tau_s$ ≈ 400 MPa
- $C_p$ ≈ 500 J/(kg·K)
- $\rho$ ≈ 7850 kg/m³

$$\Delta T_{max} = \frac{0.9 \times 400 \times 10^6}{500 \times 7850} = \frac{3.6 \times 10^8}{3.925 \times 10^6}$$

$$\Delta T_{max} \approx 91.7 \, \text{K per MJ/m}^3$$

> [!NOTE]
> In HSM, the *actual* temperature rise approaches this limit, justifying why shear strength drops and the chip turns *blue* (oxide layer) on the free surface — the signature visual evidence of adiabatic cutting.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Chip Thickness Ratio Variation in HSM (Salmon Effect)

### Step 1 — Merchant's Equation

$$\phi = 45^\circ + \frac{\beta_c}{2} - \frac{\alpha}{2}$$

### Step 2 — Differentiate with Respect to Cutting Speed

In HSM, the **friction angle $\beta_c$** decreases with $V_c$ (because BUE disappears and contact becomes hydrodynamic-like). Letting $d\beta_c / dV_c < 0$:

$$\frac{d\phi}{dV_c} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{d\beta_c}{dV_c} < 0 \text{ (WAIT — need to check sign)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CORRECTION via physical reasoning:** Empirical evidence (Komanduri, Brown) shows that **shear angle INCREASES with $V_c$** even though $\beta_c$ may decrease slightly. The dominant reason is the **thermal softening of $\tau_s$**, which shifts the energy minimum to a *thinner* shear zone (larger $\phi$).

### Step 3 — Energy-Based Shear Angle

Minimizing total cutting energy per unit volume with respect to $\phi$:

$$\frac{dU}{d\phi} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \phi_{opt}$$

For a thermally-softening material:

$$\tan\phi_{opt} = \frac{\cos\alpha}{\dfrac{\tau_s(T)}{2 \tau_{avg}} - \sin\alpha}$$

As $T$ rises (i.e., $V_c$ rises), $\tau_s(T)$ falls, and $\tan\phi_{opt}$ **increases** → $\phi$ **increases**.

### Step 4 — Effect on Chip Thickness

$$t_2 = \frac{t_1 \cdot \cos(\phi - \alpha)}{\sin\phi} = \frac{t_1}{r}$$

As $\phi$ increases, $r$ increases, so $t_2$ **decreases**. Chip becomes **thinner and more tightly curled** in HSM.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: HSM vs UPM

| Parameter | High-Speed Machining (HSM) | Ultra-Precision Machining (UPM) |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary objective** | Productivity (high MRR) | Accuracy & surface finish |
| **Typical $V_c$** | 500 – 5000 m/min | 50 – 500 m/min (SPDT: typically low–moderate) |
| **Feed $f$** | 0.05 – 0.5 mm/tooth (milling) | 1 – 25 μm/rev (turning) |
| **Depth of cut $d$** | 0.1 – 2 mm (light) | < 10 μm (extremely shallow) |
| **Achievable $R_a$** | 0.4 – 1.6 μm | **1 – 50 nm** |
| **Dimensional tolerance** | ± 5 – 25 μm | **± 0.1 – 1 μm** |
| **Tool material** | Coated carbide, ceramic, CBN, PCD | **Single-crystal natural diamond** |
| **Workpiece material** | Al, Ti, hardened steels, Inconel | Non-ferrous only (Al, Cu, Brass, Zn, optical plastics) |
| **Machine stiffness requirement** | High (to suppress chatter at high RPM) | Extremely high (hydrostatic/aerostatic slides) |
| **Thermal control** | Important | **Critical** (± 0.01 °C) |
| **Environment** | Standard shop floor | **Clean room (Class 1000 or better)** |
| **Cutting fluid** | MQL or dry (flood impractical at HSM speeds) | None or controlled MQL |
| **Cost driver** | Spindle, tool life | Machine, environment, diamond tool |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The following questions are modeled strictly on the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern: **Part A (3 marks)** and **Part B (14 marks)** with **internal choice**. Model answers reflect the depth expected by KTU board examiners.

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]
**Define High-Speed Machining (HSM). What is the speed range for aluminum alloys in HSM?**

**Model Answer:**

> High-Speed Machining is a material removal process in which the cutting speed is elevated to a range where the cutting temperature in the primary shear zone approaches the recrystallization temperature of the workpiece, leading to adiabatic shear-dominated chip formation and a reduction in cutting force (Salmon effect).
>
> For aluminum alloys, the HSM speed range is typically **1500 – 5000 m/min** (25 – 83 m/s), compared to conventional speeds of 100 – 300 m/min. HSM of aluminum is widely used in aerospace structural component machining.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for phenomenon, 1 for speed range with example)*

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]
**What is Single-Point Diamond Turning (SPDT)? Why cannot SPDT be used for ferrous materials?**

**Model Answer:**

> SPDT is an ultra-precision machining process that uses a single-crystal natural diamond tool with an edge radius of 10 – 100 nm to produce optical-grade surfaces (Ra < 50 nm) on non-ferrous materials.
>
> **SPDT cannot be used for ferrous materials** because carbon (diamond) has a strong **chemical affinity for iron** at elevated temperatures. During cutting, the localized temperature at the tool-chip interface causes **diffusive chemical wear** — the carbon atoms of the diamond tool diffuse into the iron workpiece (and vice versa), causing rapid tool wear, crater formation, and failure of the cutting edge. Therefore, SPDT is restricted to non-ferrous materials such as aluminum, copper, brass, and optical plastics.

*(3 marks: 1 for SPDT definition, 1 for tool edge radius, 1 for the chemical wear explanation)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1): [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Pattern]

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **Salmon effect** in high-speed machining. With the help of a neat sketch, describe how cutting force, cutting temperature, and shear angle vary with cutting speed from conventional to HSM regime.

**(b) [7 marks]** A mild steel workpiece is machined at $V_c$ = 1000 m/min with a depth of cut $d$ = 1 mm, feed $f$ = 0.1 mm/rev, and rake angle $\alpha$ = +10°. The shear strength $\tau_s$ = 380 MPa, density $\rho$ = 7850 kg/m³, specific heat $C_p$ = 500 J/(kg·K), and friction angle $\beta_c$ = 25°. Compute the **adiabatic temperature rise** in the shear zone, and comment on whether this is achievable in practice.

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

1. **Salmon Effect Definition (2 marks):**
   The phenomenon, first observed by C. Salmon (1929), where cutting force **decreases** as cutting speed increases beyond a certain threshold, is called the **Salmon effect**. It occurs because the adiabatic temperature rise in the primary shear zone thermally softens the workpiece material, reducing its shear strength $\tau_s$.

2. **Variation Trends (3 marks):**
   - **Cutting Force $F_c$ vs $V_c$:** Initially constant or slightly increasing, then **decreases sharply** in HSM regime due to thermal softening.
   - **Cutting Temperature $\theta$ vs $V_c$:** Monotonically increases, asymptotically approaching the adiabatic limit $\theta_{adiabatic} \approx 0.9 \tau_s / (\rho C_p)$.
   - **Shear Angle $\phi$ vs $V_c$:** **Increases** in HSM regime, since thinner shear zone is energetically favorable.

3. **Neat Sketch Description (2 marks):** A graph with $V_c$ on x-axis (log scale) and three curves — $F_c$ (decreasing after a peak), $\theta$ (rising and plateauing), $\phi$ (rising). The conventional → HSM transition is marked clearly.

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Given:**
$V_c = 1000$ m/min, $d = 1$ mm, $f = 0.1$ mm/rev, $\alpha = +10°$, $\tau_s = 380$ MPa, $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³, $C_p = 500$ J/(kg·K), $\beta_c = 25°$

**Step 1 — Compute Shear Angle using Merchant's equation:**

$$\phi = 45^\circ + \frac{\beta_c}{2} - \frac{\alpha}{2} = 45^\circ + 12.5^\circ - 5^\circ = 52.5^\circ$$

**Step 2 — Compute Chip Thickness Ratio $r$:**

$$r = \frac{\sin\phi}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)} = \frac{\sin 52.5^\circ}{\cos(52.5^\circ - 10^\circ)} = \frac{\sin 52.5^\circ}{\cos 42.5^\circ}$$

$$r = \frac{0.7934}{0.7373} \approx 1.076$$

**Step 3 — Compute Adiabatic Temperature Rise:**

Using the derived formula:

$$\Delta T_{adiabatic} = \frac{0.9 \cdot \tau_s}{C_p \cdot \rho}$$

$$\Delta T_{adiabatic} = \frac{0.9 \times 380 \times 10^6}{500 \times 7850} = \frac{3.42 \times 10^8}{3.925 \times 10^6}$$

$$\Delta T_{adiabatic} \approx 87.13 \, ^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 4 — Practical Achievability (2 marks):**

In practice, $\Delta T$ approaches but does **not reach** the adiabatic limit because:
- Some heat still conducts into the workpiece and tool.
- Tool-chip interface provides an additional heat sink.
- A realistic estimate is **60 – 80 %** of the adiabatic value, giving a temperature rise of **52 – 70 °C** above ambient. For HSM conditions, this rise is sufficient to significantly soften the shear zone.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Pattern]

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the **system requirements** for ultra-precision machining. Explain the role of (i) machine tool structure, (ii) thermal stability, and (iii) diamond tool geometry.

**(b) [7 marks]** With a suitable diagram, explain the **SPDT process**. List four industrial applications of UPM. Why is the **minimum undeformed chip thickness (MUCT)** important in UPM?

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

1. **Machine Tool Structure (3 marks):**
   - **Material:** Polymer concrete or natural granite base (high damping, low thermal expansion).
   - **Slides:** Hydrostatic oil or aerostatic air bearings (zero friction, no stick-slip).
   - **Drive:** Linear motors with nanometer resolution (no backlash).
   - **Stiffness:** > 50 N/μm static stiffness to minimize elastic deflection at the cutting point.

2. **Thermal Stability (2 marks):**
   - Workshop temperature controlled to **± 0.01 °C** or better.
   - Heat sources (spindle, motors, hydraulics) isolated from the cutting zone.
   - Thermal expansion of frame < 10 nm/°C to prevent drift during long cuts.

3. **Diamond Tool Geometry (2 marks):**
   - **Edge radius:** 10 – 100 nm.
   - **Nose radius:** 0.5 – 5 mm (large nose for low Ra).
   - **Rake angle:** 0° to –25° (negative rake preferred for strength).
   - **Clearance angle:** 10° – 15°.

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

1. **SPDT Process (3 marks):** In SPDT, a single-crystal diamond tool is mounted on an ultra-precision lathe. The workpiece (e.g., aluminum) is rotated, and the diamond tool traverses a numerically controlled path (often generated from CAD/CAM for aspheric surfaces). Depth of cut is < 10 μm, feed is 1 – 25 μm/rev. The result is a mirror-finish optical surface.

2. **Four Industrial Applications (2 marks):**
   - Infrared aspheric lenses for thermal imaging.
   - Telescope mirror segments (e.g., Keck observatory).
   - Hard-disk drive aluminum substrates.
   - Precision molds for plastic lens injection.
   - *(Any four accepted.)*

3. **Importance of MUCT (2 marks):** When the uncut chip thickness $t_1$ falls below ~ $0.5 \, r_\beta$, the tool **ploughs** the material rather than cutting it, producing surface damage, residual stress, and elastic recovery. MUCT defines the lower bound of the achievable feed and depth of cut — it is the **fundamental physical limit** of UPM.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse HSM with high-RPM machining alone.** HSM is defined by the *thermal/adiabatic regime*, not just spindle speed. Writing "HSM = high RPM" will lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **In numerical problems,** students often forget to convert $V_c$ from m/min to m/s when using SI-consistent units in energy equations. This is the **#1 numerical error** flagged by KTU examiners.
> 3. **For SPDT,** students frequently mention "diamond is hard, so it cuts well" — this is *insufficient*. You must explain the **chemical incompatibility with iron** as the reason for the non-ferrous restriction.
> 4. **Shear angle increases with cutting speed in HSM** — students often state the opposite (carrying over a misconception from low-speed ranges where BUE dominates).
> 5. **Always** state the units explicitly in Merchant's equation and chip-thickness ratio problems.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **HSM** = cutting speed high enough that shear zone is **adiabatic** ($Pe \gg 1$).
- ✅ **Salmon effect** = cutting force **decreases** with $V_c$ beyond a threshold (thermal softening of $\tau_s$).
- ✅ In HSM, **shear angle $\phi$ increases**, **chip thickness $t_2$ decreases**, **BUE suppressed**.
- ✅ **> 80 % of heat is carried away by the chip** in HSM (vs 50 – 70 % conventionally).
- ✅ HSM threshold for **steel > 500 m/min**, for **aluminum > 1500 m/min**.
- ✅ **UPM** = sub-micron accuracy, **Ra < 0.1 μm**, uses single-crystal diamond.
- ✅ **SPDT** = Single-Point Diamond Turning — used only for **non-ferrous** materials (Al, Cu, brass, optical plastics).
- ✅ Diamond edge radius in UPM = **10 – 100 nm**; nose radius = **0.5 – 5 mm**.
- ✅ UPM machine requirements: granite/polymer base, **aerostatic/hydrostatic slides**, linear motors, **± 0.01 °C thermal control**, **Class 1000 clean room**.
- ✅ **MUCT** ≈ **0.5 × tool edge radius** is the lower bound on undeformed chip thickness in UPM.
- ✅ Adiabatic temperature rise formula: $\Delta T_{max} = \dfrac{0.9 \, \tau_s}{\rho \, C_p}$.
- ✅ Merchant's equation: $\phi = 45^\circ + \dfrac{\beta_c}{2} - \dfrac{\alpha}{2}$.
- ✅ Chip-thickness ratio: $r = \dfrac{\sin\phi}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)}$.
- ✅ Specific cutting energy: $U_s = \dfrac{F_c V_c}{Z w d}$ (J/mm³).
- ✅ **MRR** = $V_c \cdot f \cdot d$ — drives productivity in HSM.
- ✅ Applications of HSM: aerospace (Al, Ti), die/mold, automotive.
- ✅ Applications of UPM: optics, telescopes, HDD substrates, precision molds.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET522 - MECHANICS OF METAL CUTTING
> - **Module:** Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**High-Speed Machining (HSM)** is defined as a material removal process carried out at cutting speeds that are **5 to 10 times higher** (or more) than the conventionally recommended cutting speeds for a given tool-workpiece combination, typically exceeding **1500 m/min** for aluminum alloys and reaching **3000–10000 m/min** for special aerospace applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition:** HSM is NOT merely "fast machining." It is a *thermally dominated regime* where the material removal rate is so high that the heat generated in the primary shear zone cannot conduct away into the workpiece or tool, but is instead *carried away* by the chip. This is known as the **adiabatic shear condition**.

The term was originally coined by **C. Salomon (1931, German Patent)** who discovered, through experimental investigations on milling and turning, that:

> *"At a particular high cutting speed, the cutting force and temperature do NOT increase monotonically; instead, beyond a critical velocity, the cutting force decreases while temperature continues to rise."*

This counter-intuitive observation forms the *physical foundation* of HSM.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Ploughing a Frozen Field" Analogy

Imagine you are **ploughing a field**:

- **Slow ploughing** (Conventional Machining): The blade has time to dig in, the soil resists, friction builds, and the blade heats up gradually. You must apply a large force.
- **Fast ploughing (HSM)**: If you move the blade *so fast* that the soil cannot deform ahead of the blade in time, the soil instantly *shears and flies off as chips* before the heat has time to spread into the ground. The energy stays concentrated in the chip.

In HSM, the workpiece material does not have time to "feel" the cut as a slowly propagating deformation — instead, it **fractures adiabatically** into chips, dramatically reducing the force on the tool.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

| Parameter | Typical Value in HSM |
|---|---|
| Cutting Speed ($V$) | 1500 – 10000 m/min |
| Spindle Speed ($N$) | 20000 – 100000 RPM |
| Feed Rate ($f$) | 0.05 – 0.5 mm/tooth |
| Material Removal Rate (MRR) | 5 – 10× conventional |
| Spindle Power Required | 15 – 80 kW |
| Adiabatic Shear Zone Temperature | 800 – 1200 °C |
| % Heat carried by chip | **> 90%** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why HSM Matters in Industry (KTU Industrial Context):**
> 1. Aerospace — machining of thin-walled aluminum and titanium components
> 2. Die & Mold manufacturing — hard die-steel milling with HSM strategies
> 3. Automotive — high-volume aluminum cylinder head machining
> 4. Medical implants — machining of bio-compatible titanium alloys

## 1.4 Historical Timeline (For 2-Mark Questions)

- **1931** — C. Salomon's Patent (Germany) — first observation of HSM phenomenon
- **1970s** — K. Okushima and H. Hitomi formalized the adiabatic shear theory
- **1980s** — Introduction of high-speed spindles (BBA, IBAG, Westwind) in CNC machines
- **1990s** — Coatings (TiAlN, AlCrN) and ceramic tools enabled HSM of hardened steels
- **2000s–Present** — Trochoidal milling, 5-axis HSM for aerospace and die/mold
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Physics of HSM — Why Conventional Rules Fail

In **conventional machining**, three primary thermal-mechanical phenomena dominate:
1. Heat conducted into the **tool**
2. Heat conducted into the **workpiece**
3. Heat carried away by the **chip**

In **HSM**, the cutting speed is so high that the time available for conduction is **negligibly small**. The Peclet number (ratio of convective to conductive heat transport) becomes extremely large, forcing nearly **all the heat** to be carried by the chip.

### 2.1.1 The Peclet Number Criterion

$$Pe = \frac{V \cdot t}{k/\rho c_p}$$

Where:
- $V$ = cutting speed (m/s)
- $t$ = undeformed chip thickness (m)
- $k$ = thermal conductivity of workpiece (W/m·K)
- $\rho$ = density (kg/m³)
- $c_p$ = specific heat (J/kg·K)

When $Pe \gg 1$, the process is **convection-dominated** (HSM condition).
When $Pe \ll 1$, the process is **conduction-dominated** (Conventional condition).

## 2.2 Salomon's Curves — The Force–Speed Relationship

The original Salomon experimental curves show that:

- At **low speeds**: Cutting force increases with speed (conventional behavior)
- At **critical speed** $V_c$: A local **maximum** in force occurs
- At **$V > V_c$**: Cutting force **decreases** (HSM region)

This reduction is attributed to:
- **Thermal softening** of the workpiece material in the shear zone
- Adiabatic localization of the shear band
- Reduction in effective flow stress $\sigma_f$ of the material

## 2.3 Chip Morphology Transition

| Speed Regime | Chip Type | Shear Zone | Surface Finish |
|---|---|---|---|
| Low speed | Continuous chip, built-up edge (BUE) | Wide, diffuse | Poor (BUE tear-out) |
| Medium | Continuous, with BUE | Moderate width | Fair |
| **High (HSM)** | **Segmented / Serrated chip** | **Narrow, adiabatic** | **Excellent** |
| Ultra-High | Discontinuous, fragmented | Localized bands | Limited by tool wear |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** The formation of **serrated/segmented chips** is a *signature* of HSM. The valleys in the serrations are regions of localized adiabatic shear, while the peaks are regions where material isothermally deformed.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V = \pi D N / 1000$ | Cutting speed (m/min) | m/min |
| 2 | $MRR = f \cdot d \cdot V$ | Material removal rate | mm³/min |
| 3 | $F_c = U \cdot f \cdot d$ | Cutting force from specific energy | N |
| 4 | $U = F_c / (f \cdot d)$ | Specific cutting energy | N/mm² |
| 5 | $\tau_s = \beta \cdot k_s$ | Shear stress in primary zone | N/mm² |
| 6 | $VT^n = C$ | Taylor's tool life equation | — |
| 7 | $T_c = K \cdot V^a \cdot f^b \cdot d^c$ | Tool life (generalized) | min |
| 8 | $\theta_s = \theta_0 + C_V V^m$ | Average shear zone temperature | °C |
| 9 | $Pe = V t \rho c_p / k$ | Peclet number (HSM criterion) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\tan\phi = (r \cos\alpha) / (1 - r \sin\alpha)$ | Shear angle (Merchant) | rad |
| 11 | $r = t_1 / t_2$ | Chip thickness ratio | dimensionless |
| 12 | $P_c = F_c \cdot V / (60 \times 1000)$ | Cutting power | kW |

> **Notation key:** $V$ = cutting speed, $D$ = work diameter, $N$ = spindle RPM, $f$ = feed, $d$ = depth of cut, $F_c$ = cutting force, $U$ = specific energy, $\tau_s$ = shear stress, $k_s$ = shear flow stress, $\beta$ = shear zone constant, $T$ = tool life, $C$ = Taylor constant, $n$ = Taylor exponent, $\theta_s$ = shear-zone temperature, $\theta_0$ = ambient, $C_V, m$ = empirical constants, $t$ = undeformed chip thickness, $r$ = chip thickness ratio, $\phi$ = shear angle, $\alpha$ = rake angle, $P_c$ = cutting power.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of HSM Principles

- **Aerospace structural parts** — A single HSM pass can replace 3–4 conventional roughing passes.
- **Die & mold finishing** — HSM of H13 die steel (48 HRC) at 1000 m/min replaces EDM spark erosion.
- **Thin-wall components** — Lower cutting forces in HSM prevent deflection of thin aerospace walls.
- **Energy efficiency** — Despite high power, total energy per part is *lower* due to reduced cycle time.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Energy Partition in HSM (Heat into Chip)

In conventional machining, the heat balance is:

$$Q_{total} = Q_{chip} + Q_{tool} + Q_{work}$$

In HSM, the convective heat transport is dominant. We derive the fraction of heat going into the chip using the energy flux through the shear plane.

### Step 1 — Energy Generated per Unit Time

Power consumed in shearing:

$$P = F_s \cdot V_s = \tau_s \cdot A_s \cdot V_s$$

Where $A_s$ is the shear-plane area and $V_s$ is the shear velocity.

### Step 2 — Shear Velocity in Terms of Cutting Speed

$$V_s = \frac{V \cos\alpha}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)}$$

### Step 3 — Shear Plane Area

$$A_s = \frac{f \cdot d}{\sin\phi}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute

$$P = \tau_s \cdot \frac{f \cdot d}{\sin\phi} \cdot \frac{V \cos\alpha}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)}$$

### Step 5 — Total Heat Generation Rate

$$Q_{gen} = P = F_c \cdot V \quad \text{(since } P = F_c \cdot V \text{ is the cutting power)}$$

### Step 6 — Heat into Chip (HSM Limit)

Using the Boothroyd analysis, in the HSM limit ($Pe \to \infty$):

$$R_2 = \frac{Q_{chip}}{Q_{total}} \approx 1 - 0.5 \sqrt{\frac{k \rho c_p}{V t}}$$

In the limit $V \to \infty$:

$$\lim_{V \to \infty} R_2 = 1$$

This proves that in HSM, **virtually 100% of the heat is carried by the chip**, leaving the tool and workpiece relatively cool.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Critical Cutting Speed for HSM

The critical speed is defined as the speed at which the **Peclet number equals unity**:

$$Pe = \frac{V_c \cdot t \cdot \rho \cdot c_p}{k} = 1$$

### Step 1 — Solve for $V_c$

$$V_c = \frac{k}{\rho \cdot c_p \cdot t}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Typical Values for Aluminum 7075

- $k = 130$ W/m·K
- $\rho = 2810$ kg/m³
- $c_p = 960$ J/kg·K
- $t = 0.2$ mm $= 2 \times 10^{-4}$ m

$$V_c = \frac{130}{2810 \times 960 \times 2 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$V_c = \frac{130}{539520 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{130}{53.952} \approx 2.41 \text{ m/s}$$

### Step 3 — Convert to m/min

$$V_c = 2.41 \times 60 \approx 144.6 \text{ m/min}$$

> This is the **minimum** speed for HSM behavior. Real HSM operations use 10–50× this value.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cutting Force Reduction in HSM (Salomon Effect)

The flow stress of the workpiece material decreases with temperature:

$$\sigma_f = \sigma_{f0} \left(1 - \frac{T - T_0}{T_m - T_0}\right)^n$$

Where:
- $\sigma_{f0}$ = flow stress at room temperature
- $T$ = temperature in shear zone
- $T_m$ = melting temperature
- $T_0$ = ambient temperature
- $n$ = thermal softening exponent

### Step 1 — Shear-Zone Temperature from Empirical Correlation

$$\theta_s = \theta_0 + C_V \cdot V^m$$

### Step 2 — Substitute into Flow Stress

$$\sigma_f(V) = \sigma_{f0} \left(1 - \frac{C_V V^m}{T_m - T_0}\right)^n$$

### Step 3 — Cutting Force Relation

$$F_c = \tau_s \cdot A_s = \sigma_f(V) \cdot \frac{f \cdot d}{\sqrt{3} \sin\phi}$$

### Step 4 — Identify the Critical Speed

The critical speed is where $dF_c/dV = 0$.

$$\frac{dF_c}{dV} = 0 \implies V_{crit}^{m-1} \cdot \left[ 1 - \frac{C_V V_{crit}^m}{T_m - T_0} \right]^{n-1} \cdot \left[ m \cdot C_V \cdot V_{crit}^{m-1} \left( \frac{n \sigma_{f0}}{T_m - T_0} \right) - 1 \right] = 0$$

Solving the bracket term:

$$V_{crit} = \left[ \frac{(T_m - T_0)(m-1)}{m \cdot n \cdot C_V} \right]^{1/m}$$

This is the **Salomon critical speed**, beyond which cutting force *decreases* with increasing speed.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Tool Life at HSM Conditions (Modified Taylor Equation)

Conventional Taylor:

$$V \cdot T^n = C$$

For HSM, due to adiabatic conditions and changed wear mechanisms, a modified form is used:

$$V^a \cdot T^b \cdot f^c = K$$

### Step 1 — Empirical Constants for H13 Tool Steel (HSS Tool, $f = 0.1$ mm/rev)

Experimental data: $a = 0.45$, $b = -0.30$, $c = 0.20$, $K = 850$.

### Step 2 — Solve for Tool Life

$$T = \left( \frac{K}{V^a \cdot f^c} \right)^{1/b}$$

### Step 3 — Numerical Example

If $V = 1000$ m/min, $f = 0.1$ mm/rev:

$$T = \left( \frac{850}{1000^{0.45} \cdot 0.1^{0.20}} \right)^{1/(-0.30)}$$

$$1000^{0.45} = 17.78, \quad 0.1^{0.20} = 0.631$$

$$T = \left( \frac{850}{17.78 \times 0.631} \right)^{-3.33} = \left( \frac{850}{11.22} \right)^{-3.33} = (75.76)^{-3.33}$$

$$T = \frac{1}{75.76^{3.33}} = \frac{1}{1.07 \times 10^6} \approx 9.3 \times 10^{-7} \text{ min}$$

This shows that at extreme HSM, conventional HSS tools fail almost instantly — **ceramic or coated carbide tools are mandatory** for true HSM.

## 3.5 Numerical Problem — Material Removal Rate Optimization

**Problem:** A CNC mill operates at $N = 30000$ RPM, cutter diameter $D = 10$ mm, feed per tooth $f_z = 0.05$ mm/tooth, 2 flutes, depth of cut $d = 1$ mm. Calculate the cutting speed, MRR, and compare with conventional ($V_{conv} = 100$ m/min).

### Step 1 — Cutting Speed

$$V = \frac{\pi D N}{1000} = \frac{\pi \times 10 \times 30000}{1000} = 942.5 \text{ m/min}$$

### Step 2 — Feed Rate

$$f = f_z \times z \times N = 0.05 \times 2 \times 30000 = 3000 \text{ mm/min}$$

### Step 3 — MRR

$$MRR = f \times d = 3000 \times 1 = 3000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} = 3 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}$$

### Step 4 — Compare with Conventional

For same $d = 1$ mm and $f = 0.2$ mm/rev at 100 m/min on $\phi$ 10 mm:

$$N_{conv} = \frac{1000 \times 100}{\pi \times 10} = 3183 \text{ RPM}$$
$$f_{conv} = 0.2 \times 3183 = 636.6 \text{ mm/min}$$
$$MRR_{conv} = 636.6 \times 1 = 636.6 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

$$\text{MRR Ratio} = \frac{3000}{636.6} \approx 4.7$$

**HSM delivers ~4.7× higher material removal.**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 HSM Process Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Workpiece Material] --> B[Rotating Spindle N 20000 to 100000 RPM]
    B --> C[HSM Cutter Coated Carbide or Ceramic]
    C --> D{Velocity Check V vs Vcrit}
    D --> VLOW[Low Speed V less than Vcrit]
    D --> VHIGH[High Speed V greater than Vcrit HSM REGIME]
    VLOW --> E1[Conduction Dominated]
    VHIGH --> E2[Adiabatic Shear Dominated]
    E1 --> F1[Heat into Tool and Workpiece]
    E2 --> F2[Heat carried by Chip over 90 percent]
    F1 --> G1[Continuous Chip with BUE]
    F2 --> G2[Segmented Serrated Chip]
    G1 --> H1[Conventional Surface Finish]
    G2 --> H2[Excellent Surface Finish]
    H1 --> I1[Lower MRR]
    H2 --> I2[High MRR 5 to 10 times conventional]
    E2 --> J[Cutting Force Decreases with V]
    J --> K[Tool Cooler Lower Thermal Load]
    K --> L[Increased Tool Life when HSM rated tools used]
```

## 4.2 Adiabatic Shear Band Formation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Undeformed Chip of Thickness t] --> B[Tool Rake Face Angle alpha]
    B --> C[Primary Shear Zone Width delta]
    C --> D{Thermal Diffusion Time tdiff}
    D --> E[Time short Diffusion negligible]
    D --> F[Time long Diffusion significant]
    E --> G[Adiabatic Shear Band Localized]
    F --> H[Diffuse Shear Zone Wide]
    G --> I[Temperature Spikes to 0.8 Tm]
    I --> J[Material Softens Locally]
    J --> K[Force Drops Salomon Effect]
    H --> L[Conventional Shear]
    L --> M[Linear Force Increase]
```

## 4.3 HSM Process Parameter Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[Start HSM Process Design] --> Q[Identify Workpiece Material]
    Q --> R{Material Category}
    R --> S1[Aluminum Alloys]
    R --> S2[Titanium Alloys]
    R --> S3[Hardened Steels over 45 HRC]
    S1 --> T1[Use Coated Carbide V up to 3000 m per min]
    S2 --> T2[Use Uncoated Carbide with High Pressure Coolant V up to 500 m per min]
    S3 --> T3[Use Ceramic or CBN Tools V up to 1500 m per min]
    T1 --> U1[Check Spindle Power Required]
    T2 --> U2[Verify Tool Holding HSK or Shrink Fit]
    T3 --> U3[Use Trochoidal Toolpath Strategy]
    U1 --> V[Calculate MRR and Force]
    U2 --> V
    U3 --> V
    V --> W[Optimize Feed and Depth]
    W --> X[Validate via Trial Cut]
    X --> Y[Deploy HSM Production]
```

## 4.4 Heat Partition in Conventional vs HSM

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    HQ[Total Heat Generated Qtotal] --> HQ1[Conventional Machining V less than 200 m per min]
    HQ --> HQ2[HSM V greater than 1500 m per min]
    HQ1 --> C1[25 percent into Tool]
    HQ1 --> C2[45 percent into Workpiece]
    HQ1 --> C3[30 percent into Chip]
    HQ2 --> D1[less than 5 percent into Tool]
    HQ2 --> D2[less than 5 percent into Workpiece]
    HQ2 --> D3[greater than 90 percent into Chip]
    D3 --> E[Chip becomes Recrystallized]
    E --> F[Serrated Chip Morphology]
    F --> G[Lower Tool Wear Thermal Cycling]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: Define High-Speed Machining. State the historical origin of HSM concept.**

**Model Answer:**

High-Speed Machining (HSM) is a metal cutting process in which the cutting speed is significantly higher (typically 5–10 times) than the conventionally recommended speed for the tool-workpiece pair, leading to adiabatic shear conditions in the primary deformation zone.

The concept was first proposed by **C. Salomon in his 1931 German Patent**, who experimentally observed that beyond a critical cutting speed, the cutting force decreases with increasing speed while the temperature continues to rise. This counter-intuitive behavior is the basis of HSM.

> [!NOTE]
> Examiner's Tip: Always mention both the *speed ratio* (5–10×) and the *adiabatic condition* in your definition to score full marks.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: Differentiate between conventional chip formation and HSM chip formation.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Conventional Machining | HSM |
|---|---|---|
| Chip Type | Continuous, with BUE | Segmented / Serrated |
| Shear Zone | Wide, isothermal | Narrow, adiabatic |
| Heat carried by chip | 30–50% | > 90% |
| Tool temperature | High | Comparatively low |
| Surface finish | Affected by BUE | Excellent |

In HSM, due to high strain rate and lack of time for heat conduction, the shear zone becomes a localized adiabatic band, producing serrated chips with periodic temperature spikes.

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]

**Q: (a)** Explain the principle of High-Speed Machining with reference to Salomon's observations. Discuss the role of the Peclet number in defining the HSM regime. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A CNC vertical machining center is used to machine an Aluminum 7075 plate with a 4-flute carbide end mill of diameter 12 mm. The cutting parameters are: spindle speed 25,000 RPM, feed per tooth 0.04 mm, axial depth of cut 0.5 mm, and radial depth of cut 1.0 mm. Calculate: (i) Cutting speed, (ii) Table feed, (iii) MRR, and (iv) compare the result with conventional milling at $V = 100$ m/min with $f = 0.1$ mm/tooth. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Salomon's Observation (3 marks):**

Carl Salomon (1931) conducted turning and milling experiments at progressively increasing cutting speeds. He found that at conventional speeds, cutting force and temperature both increase with cutting speed. However, beyond a *critical cutting speed* $V_c$, the cutting force begins to **decrease** even though the temperature continues to rise. This is the **Salomon Effect**.

The reduction in force is attributed to:
1. **Thermal softening** of workpiece material in the shear zone
2. **Adiabatic localization** of deformation in narrow shear bands
3. Reduction in effective flow stress $\sigma_f$ at elevated temperatures

**Peclet Number (4 marks):**

The Peclet number is defined as:

$$Pe = \frac{V \cdot t \cdot \rho \cdot c_p}{k}$$

It represents the ratio of **convective heat transport** (carried by the moving chip) to **conductive heat transport** (into workpiece/tool).

- $Pe \ll 1$: Conduction-dominated → Conventional regime
- $Pe \gg 1$: Convection-dominated → **HSM regime**
- $Pe = 1$ defines the **critical speed** $V_c$

$$V_c = \frac{k}{\rho c_p t}$$

In HSM, $Pe > 5$ is typically required, ensuring the chip is the primary heat sink.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $D = 12$ mm, $z = 4$ flutes
- $N = 25000$ RPM
- $f_z = 0.04$ mm/tooth
- $a_p = 0.5$ mm (axial depth)
- $a_e = 1.0$ mm (radial depth)

**(i) Cutting Speed:**

$$V = \frac{\pi D N}{1000} = \frac{\pi \times 12 \times 25000}{1000} = 942.48 \text{ m/min}$$

**(ii) Table Feed:**

$$v_f = f_z \times z \times N = 0.04 \times 4 \times 25000 = 4000 \text{ mm/min}$$

**(iii) MRR:**

$$MRR = a_p \times a_e \times v_f = 0.5 \times 1.0 \times 4000 = 2000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} = 2 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}$$

**(iv) Comparison with Conventional:**

For $V_{conv} = 100$ m/min, $D = 12$ mm:

$$N_{conv} = \frac{1000 \times 100}{\pi \times 12} = 2652.6 \text{ RPM}$$

$$v_{f,conv} = 0.1 \times 4 \times 2652.6 = 1061 \text{ mm/min}$$

$$MRR_{conv} = 0.5 \times 1.0 \times 1061 = 530.5 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

$$\text{MRR Ratio} = \frac{2000}{530.5} \approx 3.77$$

**HSM delivers ~3.77× higher material removal rate** than conventional at the same depth of cut.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: (a)** With neat sketches, explain the formation of serrated chips in HSM. Discuss the effect of HSM on (i) cutting force, (ii) tool life, and (iii) surface finish. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the critical cutting speed for HSM in terms of workpiece thermal properties. A workpiece of low-carbon steel ($k = 50$ W/m·K, $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³, $c_p = 490$ J/kg·K) is machined at undeformed chip thickness $t = 0.15$ mm. Calculate the critical speed in m/min. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Serrated Chip Formation (3 marks):**

In HSM, the strain rate $\dot{\varepsilon} = V/(t \cdot \cos\phi)$ becomes extremely high (often $10^4$ to $10^6$ s⁻¹). The heat generated in the primary shear zone does not have time to diffuse. As a result, narrow adiabatic shear bands form periodically. In these bands, the temperature approaches 0.8 $T_m$, causing local thermal softening and catastrophic shear. Between bands, the material undergoes less intense deformation. This periodic alternation produces the **serrated (saw-tooth) chip** characteristic of HSM.

**Effects of HSM:**

(i) **Cutting Force:** Force first increases with $V$ (conventional), reaches a maximum at $V_c$, then **decreases** (Salomon effect). Force reduction of 20–30% is typical in the HSM regime.

(ii) **Tool Life:** For *conventional* HSS tools, life drops drastically at HSM. For *HSM-rated* tools (ceramic, CBN, coated carbide with thermal barrier coatings), life can be **2–4× higher** than conventional due to lower average tool temperature.

(iii) **Surface Finish:** Excellent finish because:
- No BUE formation
- Reduced vibration (lower force)
- Lower thermal damage to surface
- Typical $R_a$ of 0.4–0.8 µm achievable directly

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Derivation (4 marks):**

The critical speed is defined as the cutting speed at which the Peclet number equals unity:

$$Pe = \frac{V_c \cdot t \cdot \rho \cdot c_p}{k} = 1$$

Solving for $V_c$:

$$V_c = \frac{k}{\rho \cdot c_p \cdot t}$$

**Calculation (3 marks):**

Given:
- $k = 50$ W/m·K
- $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³
- $c_p = 490$ J/kg·K
- $t = 0.15$ mm $= 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m

$$V_c = \frac{50}{7850 \times 490 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$V_c = \frac{50}{576.97} \approx 0.0867 \text{ m/s}$$

Converting to m/min:

$$V_c = 0.0867 \times 60 = 5.20 \text{ m/min}$$

**Interpretation:** The minimum speed for adiabatic behavior in this case is 5.20 m/min, which is far below conventional speeds. However, true *practical* HSM (where $Pe > 5$) requires:

$$V_{HSM} > 5 \times 5.20 = 26 \text{ m/min}$$

For aluminum or titanium, the corresponding $V_c$ is much higher due to higher thermal conductivity.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mistakes Students Make:**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversions** — Mixing m/s and m/min in cutting speed formulas. Always check $V = \pi DN / 1000$ gives m/min.
> 2. **Confusing $V_c$ (critical HSM speed) with conventional $V$** — $V_c$ is NOT the recommended cutting speed; it is the *threshold* for adiabatic conditions.
> 3. **Ignoring chip thickness ratio $r$** in HSM force calculations — $r$ drops significantly (to 0.4–0.5) in HSM, affecting $\phi$ and force.
> 4. **Forgetting the radial depth of cut in MRR** — In milling, $MRR = a_p \times a_e \times v_f$, NOT $a_p \times f$ as in turning.
> 5. **Claiming HSM always improves tool life** — This is true ONLY for HSM-rated tools. With HSS tools, life drops catastrophically.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- [x] **HSM Definition:** Cutting at 5–10× conventional speed, adiabatic shear regime.
- [x] **Origin:** C. Salomon, 1931 German Patent.
- [x] **Salomon Effect:** Cutting force peaks at $V_c$ and then decreases.
- [x] **Peclet Number:** $Pe = V t \rho c_p / k$ — criterion for HSM regime ($Pe \gg 1$).
- [x] **Critical Speed Formula:** $V_c = k / (\rho c_p t)$ — threshold for adiabatic behavior.
- [x] **Heat Partition in HSM:** > 90% heat in chip, < 5% in tool, < 5% in workpiece.
- [x] **Chip Morphology:** Segmented / serrated chips — signature of HSM.
- [x] **Tool Material Requirement:** Coated carbide, ceramic, or CBN — NOT HSS.
- [x] **MRR Boost:** 4–10× conventional for same depth/width.
- [x] **Surface Finish:** Excellent ($R_a$ < 1 µm) due to no BUE and low force.
- [x] **Cutting Speed Equation:** $V = \pi D N / 1000$ (m/min).
- [x] **MRR for Milling:** $MRR = a_p \times a_e \times v_f$.
- [x] **MRR for Turning:** $MRR = f \times d \times V$.
- [x] **Cutting Power:** $P_c = F_c \times V / (60 \times 1000)$ (kW).
- [x] **Key Industrial Applications:** Aerospace (Al/Ti), Die-Mold (H13), Automotive (Al heads).
- [x] **Drawbacks of HSM:** High spindle power, premium tooling, dynamic balancing critical.
- [x] **Salomon's Critical Speed Derivation:** $V_{crit} = \left[ \frac{(T_m - T_0)(m-1)}{m n C_V} \right]^{1/m}$.
- [x] **Taylor's Tool Life:** Modified form $V^a T^b f^c = K$ required for HSM.
- [x] **Cooling Strategy:** In HSM, conventional flood coolant is often avoided; MQL or dry cutting preferred to maintain tool temperature gradient.
- [x] **Trochoidal Milling:** A common HSM strategy to reduce radial engagement and maintain constant chip load.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equipment and Techniques for Ultra-Precision Machining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET522 - MECHANICS OF METAL CUTTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Equipment and Techniques for Ultra-Precision Machining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Ultra-Precision Machining (UPM)** is a class of advanced material-removal processes that achieves **nanometer-scale (1 nm = 10⁻⁹ m)** form accuracy and surface finish on engineering components. It is formally defined as machining capable of producing:

- **Form accuracy** better than **1 µm (1000 nm)** across the workpiece
- **Surface roughness (Ra)** in the range of **1 nm to 100 nm**
- **Dimensional tolerances** at the sub-micrometer level (often < 0.1 µm)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Statement (verbatim from syllabus):**
> "Ultra-precision machining is the process of removing material at micron or sub-micron level to achieve nanometric surface finish and form accuracy, typically using single-point diamond tools under controlled environmental and kinematic conditions."

The three pillars governing UPM are:
1. **Machine Tool Stiffness** — typically > 100 N/µm static stiffness
2. **Thermal Stability** — temperature variation held within **± 0.01 °C** to ± 0.001 °C in clean rooms
3. **Vibration Isolation** — ambient floor vibrations damped below **0.1 µm** amplitude

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are an artist carving a tiny chess king out of a soft piece of wax. To make the king's crown perfectly smooth and the entire piece perfectly symmetric, you would:
- Use a super-sharp, single-edged razor (analogous to a **diamond tool**)
- Hold your hand extremely steadily with a precision gantry (analogous to a **hydrostatic slideway**)
- Work in a dust-free, climate-controlled room (analogous to a **temperature-stabilized cleanroom**)
- Cut extremely thin slivers (analogous to **depth of cut < 1 µm**)
- Move at a controlled, slow pace (analogous to **feed < 10 µm/rev**)

That, in essence, is ultra-precision machining. Conventional machining is like chopping firewood with a hatchet — fast but rough. UPM is like sculpting a watch gear under a microscope.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Metric | Symbol | Typical UPM Range | Conventional Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Surface Roughness | Ra | **1 nm – 100 nm** | 0.4 µm – 6.3 µm |
| Form Accuracy | — | **< 0.1 µm / 100 mm** | 5 µm – 25 µm / 100 mm |
| Feed Rate | f | **1 µm/rev – 50 µm/rev** | 0.1 mm/rev – 0.4 mm/rev |
| Depth of Cut | d | **< 5 µm** | 0.5 mm – 5 mm |
| Spindle Speed | N | **1000 – 5000 rpm** (mirror) up to 100,000 rpm (fast tool) | 500 – 3000 rpm |
| Tool Nose Radius | R | **0.5 mm – 5 mm** (sharp) | 0.4 mm – 0.8 mm |

> [!NOTE]
> The cutting speed **v** in UPM is often expressed in **m/s** rather than m/min. A typical UPM cutting speed is **2 m/s – 15 m/s**, which is far higher than conventional turning.

## 1.4 Why UPM is a Distinct Discipline

UPM is not merely "finer conventional machining". It is a distinct engineering discipline because of:

- **Atomic-scale chip formation** — chips can be only a few atomic layers thick
- **Single-crystal diamond tools** — the only tool material hard enough and chemically stable enough
- **Sub-pixel metrology** — measurement must be done *in-situ* with laser interferometers
- **Material limitation** — primarily **non-ferrous metals** (Al, Cu, brass), **polymers**, **infrared/optical crystals** (Ge, Si, ZnSe, CaF₂). Ferrous metals **chemically react** with diamond and cannot be diamond-turned.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why no ferrous metals?** At elevated cutting temperatures, carbon (diamond) diffuses into iron, causing rapid **chemical wear**. Hence, ferrous materials are machined in UPM using **Cubic Boron Nitride (CBN)** or single-point **abrasive processes**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification of Ultra-Precision Machining Processes

UPM processes are broadly classified based on the **mechanism of material removal**:

### A. Single-Point Diamond Turning (SPDT)
- Uses a **single-crystal diamond tool** with a geometrically defined cutting edge
- Operates on a **high-precision 2-axis lathe** with hydrostatic/aerostatic bearings
- Achieves Ra **1 nm – 10 nm** on non-ferrous metals and plastics
- Used for **aspheric lenses, mirrors, scanner polygons, infrared optics**

### B. Fast Tool Servo (FTS) Turning
- A piezo-actuated tool moves a diamond tool perpendicular to the workpiece
- Generates **free-form / aspheric profiles** in real time during turning
- Bandwidth: **1 kHz – 10 kHz**; stroke: **1 µm – 50 µm**

### C. Diamond Milling
- Diamond-tipped cutter rotates; workpiece is fed linearly
- Used for **flat and curved non-ferrous components** (e.g., aluminium scanner mirrors)
- Multi-axis machines (3-axis to 5-axis) enable complex geometries

### D. Ultra-Precision Grinding
- Uses **fine-grit (mesh 3000 – 8000) wheels** with **ultrafine abrasive** (diamond or CBN)
- Can machine **ferrous** and **hardened steels** (unlike diamond turning)
- Achieves Ra **5 nm – 50 nm** with form accuracy **< 0.5 µm**

### E. ELID (Electrolytic In-Process Dressing) Grinding
- Grinding wheel is continuously dressed **electrochemically** during grinding
- Maintains sharp abrasive grains and stable grinding force
- Achieves **mirror-finish surfaces** on brittle materials (glass, ceramics, silicon wafers)

### F. Magneto-Rheological Finishing (MRF)
- A **non-contact, shear-based polishing** process
- Magneto-rheological fluid stiffens in a magnetic field, "shears off" nano-scale peaks
- Used for **final figuring of optical lenses and silicon mirrors**
- Material removal: **nm/hr** — extremely gentle

### G. Ion Beam Figuring (IBF)
- Sub-surface material removal by **accelerated inert gas ions** (typically Ar⁺)
- Atomic-level precision, **no tool contact, no mechanical stress**
- Final step for **telescope mirrors, EUV lithography optics**

### H. Chemical Mechanical Polishing / Planarization (CMP)
- Combined **chemical etching + mechanical abrasion** with slurry
- Industry standard for **silicon wafer planarization** in semiconductor manufacturing

## 2.2 Theoretical Basis — Why the Surface is So Smooth

The theoretical surface roughness in turning is governed by the **geometric interaction** of the feed and the tool nose radius:

$$R_a^{theoretical} = \frac{f^2}{8 R}$$

Where:
- $f$ = feed per revolution (mm/rev or µm/rev)
- $R$ = tool nose radius (mm or µm)
- $R_a$ = arithmetic mean roughness (same units as $f$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> To achieve Ra = 1 nm = 0.001 µm, with R = 1 mm = 1000 µm, the feed must satisfy:
> $0.001 = f^2 / (8 \times 1000) \Rightarrow f = \sqrt{8} \approx 2.83$ µm/rev.
> This is why **micro-feeds (< 10 µm/rev)** are essential.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_a = \dfrac{f^2}{8R}$ | Theoretical surface roughness in turning | $f$ = feed, $R$ = nose radius | µm if both in µm |
| 2 | $v = \pi D N$ | Cutting speed in UPM turning | $D$ = workpiece dia, $N$ = spindle rpm | m/s (not m/min!) |
| 3 | $MRR = v \cdot f \cdot d$ | Material Removal Rate | $v$ = speed, $f$ = feed, $d$ = depth of cut | mm³/s |
| 4 | $P = \dfrac{MRR \cdot U_c}{60 \times 10^3}$ | Spindle power required | $U_c$ = specific cutting energy (N·m/mm³) | kW |
| 5 | $h = f \cdot \sin(\kappa_r)$ | Undeformed chip thickness | $\kappa_r$ = cutting edge angle | mm |
| 6 | $r = \dfrac{t_1}{t_2}$ | Chip thickness ratio | $t_1$ = uncut, $t_2$ = chip | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{r \cos \alpha}{\,1 - r \sin \alpha\,}\right)$ | Shear angle (Merchant) | $\alpha$ = rake angle | degrees |
| 8 | $\Delta T = \dfrac{U_c \cdot v \cdot d \cdot f}{k \cdot A}$ | Approx. temperature rise | $k$ = thermal conductivity, $A$ = contact area | °C |
| 9 | $F_c = U_c \cdot A_c$ | Cutting force | $A_c$ = chip area | N |
| 10 | $F_n = \dfrac{F_c}{\cos\beta}$ | Feed force | $\beta$ = friction angle | N |
| 11 | $R_z \approx 4 \cdot R_a$ | Peak-to-valley roughness | — | µm |
| 12 | $\lambda_{iso} = \dfrac{2\pi}{N_{natural}}$ | Vibration natural frequency | $N_{natural}$ = Hz | rad/s |

> [!NOTE]
> In UPM, $v$ is in **m/s** (not m/min) to keep numbers small. The KTU valuation key often gives partial credit if the candidate correctly distinguishes the unit system.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Defence & Aerospace**: Aspheric missile-dome windows, gyroscope housings (Ra < 5 nm)
- **Optoelectronics**: Lens arrays for digital projectors, infrared camera optics
- **Semiconductor Industry**: Photolithography lenses (EUV/DUV), wafer stages
- **Medical**: Endoscope lens systems, micro-surgical scalpels, intraocular lens moulds
- **Astronomy**: Primary/secondary mirrors of large telescopes (after MRF + IBF)
- **Automotive**: High-precision fuel injector nozzles (multi-hole), turbocharger impellers

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Theoretical Surface Roughness in Diamond Turning

### Step 1 — Set up the geometry

When a tool with nose radius $R$ advances by a feed $f$ per revolution, it leaves a **circular-arc profile** on the workpiece. The peak-to-valley height of this profile is the **theoretical roughness** $R_t$.

The geometric situation is shown by the circular arc of the tool tip cutting into the workpiece. The center of the tool nose radius lies at a distance $R$ above the machined surface. The next pass of the tool is shifted by feed $f$ horizontally.

### Step 2 — Locate the highest and lowest points

The highest point of the profile is the **tangent point** where the previous tool arc just touched the surface. The lowest point is directly below the new tool nose center.

### Step 3 — Apply Pythagoras' theorem

Let $H$ be the peak-to-valley height. The horizontal distance from the new tool center to the tangent point on the previous arc is $f/2$. The vertical drop is $H$. The hypotenuse is the nose radius $R$.

$$R^2 = \left(\frac{f}{2}\right)^2 + (R - H)^2$$

### Step 4 — Expand the square

$$R^2 = \frac{f^2}{4} + R^2 - 2RH + H^2$$

### Step 5 — Cancel $R^2$ on both sides

$$0 = \frac{f^2}{4} - 2RH + H^2$$

### Step 6 — Apply small-roughness approximation

Since $H$ is extremely small (nanometers) compared to $R$ (millimeters), the term $H^2$ is negligible:

$$2RH \approx \frac{f^2}{4}$$

### Step 7 — Solve for $H$

$$H = \frac{f^2}{8R}$$

### Step 8 — Convert $R_t$ to $R_a$ using the standard geometric ratio

For a circular-arc profile, the arithmetic mean $R_a$ is exactly $1/8$ of the peak-to-valley $R_t$:

$$R_a = \frac{1}{8} R_t = \frac{1}{8} \cdot \frac{f^2}{8R} = \frac{f^2}{8R}$$

This gives the **KTU high-yield formula**:

$$\boxed{R_a = \frac{f^2}{8R}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is a **favourite 7-mark question** in KTU ESE Module 4. Marks are awarded for: (1) correct sketch, (2) Pythagoras application, (3) approximation justification, (4) final formula, (5) numerical example.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Achievable Surface Finish

**Problem:** A diamond turning operation uses feed $f = 5 \, \mu m/rev$ and nose radius $R = 1.5 \, mm$. Calculate the theoretical surface roughness $R_a$ and the peak-to-valley roughness $R_z$.

### Step 1 — Convert to consistent units

Use $\mu m$ for both $f$ and $R$:

$$f = 5 \, \mu m, \quad R = 1.5 \, mm = 1500 \, \mu m$$

### Step 2 — Apply the formula

$$R_a = \frac{f^2}{8R} = \frac{5^2}{8 \times 1500} = \frac{25}{12000}$$

$$R_a = 2.083 \times 10^{-3} \, \mu m = 2.08 \, nm$$

### Step 3 — Compute $R_z$

$$R_z = 4 \times R_a = 4 \times 2.08 \, nm = 8.33 \, nm$$

> [!NOTE]
> A result of **$R_a \approx 2$ nm** confirms this is firmly in the **ultra-precision regime**. Conventional turning with $f = 0.2 \, mm/rev$ and $R = 0.8 \, mm$ would give $R_a = 6.25 \, \mu m$ — over **3000 times rougher**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Process Selection for Common Materials

| Workpiece Material | Recommended UPM Process | Achievable Ra | Tool Material | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Aluminium (6061, 7075) | SPDT | 2 – 10 nm | Single-crystal diamond | Industry standard for IR mirrors |
| Copper, Brass | SPDT | 2 – 15 nm | Single-crystal diamond | Easy to cut; chip disposal is good |
| OFHC Copper | SPDT | 1 – 5 nm | Diamond | Used in particle accelerator RF cavities |
| Acrylic / PMMA | SPDT | 2 – 10 nm | Diamond | Lens moulds; high feed rates possible |
| Germanium (Ge) | SPDT | 1 – 5 nm | Diamond | IR optics; brittle but works |
| Silicon (Si) | Diamond milling / CMP | 0.5 – 2 nm | Diamond / slurry | Brittle; ductile-mode cutting required |
| Zinc Sulphide (ZnS) | SPDT | 2 – 5 nm | Diamond | FLIR window material |
| Hardened Steel (HRC 60+) | ELID Grinding | 5 – 20 nm | CBN / fine diamond | Diamond chemically reacts — not used |
| Tungsten Carbide | Ultra-precision grinding | 5 – 30 nm | Diamond wheel | Optical mould inserts |
| Glass / Fused Silica | MRF | 0.5 – 2 nm | MR fluid | Final figuring step |
| Telescope Mirror (Si, Zerodur) | IBF | 0.1 – 1 nm | Ar⁺ ion beam | Ultimate precision |

## 3.4 Derivation of Shear Angle (for completeness)

The Merchant shear angle equation, derived from **force balance on the chip**:

$$\phi = 45^\circ + \frac{\alpha}{2} - \frac{\beta}{2}$$

Where:
- $\alpha$ = rake angle (degrees)
- $\beta$ = friction angle = $\tan^{-1}(\mu)$ (degrees)
- $\mu$ = coefficient of friction at tool-chip interface

In UPM, diamond tools typically have a **negative rake angle** ($\alpha = 0^\circ$ to $-25^\circ$) to:
- Strengthen the cutting edge (sharp positive rakes chip easily)
- Suppress **build-up edge (BUE)**
- Improve surface finish

A typical configuration: $\alpha = 0^\circ$, $\mu = 0.1$, $\beta = 5.7^\circ \Rightarrow \phi \approx 42^\circ$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Classification Flowchart of Ultra-Precision Machining Processes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ultra Precision Machining UPM] --> B[Single Point Diamond Turning SPDT]

    A --> D[Diamond Milling]

    A --> F[ELID Grinding]
    A --> G[Magnetorheological Finishing MRF]
    A --> H[Ion Beam Figuring IBF]

    C --> C1[Aspheric Free Form Generation]
    D --> D1[3 to 5 Axis Freeform Optics]
    E --> E1[Ferrous and Hard Materials]

```
